text: Going to college in the United States is exactly what Bayu Prakosa's siblings all did. Bayu decided to follow in their footsteps and do the same thing. ?I?m originally from Indonesia. My hometown is Jakarta and I decided to come to the United States to follow my siblings, all of them go to college here so I decided why not give it a chance. I decided to come here," he says.?I applied to six universities across the United States and I got into all of them, but Auburn had the best among those six so I just picked Auburn I didn?t know what Auburn was like until actually I got to Auburn like two weeks before the school starts.?Bayu received his undergraduate degree in Political Science and Economics. Now, the focus is on a MBA degree in Management Information Systems. Once he completes his studies both his undergraduate degree as well as his MBA will be from Auburn University. Staying at Auburn University to further his education was for one reason in particular. ?For graduate school I gave myself options I applied to four other schools. Three are on the Southeast and one is in San Francisco, but at the end I decided to come to Auburn just because I was very familiar with the program and I am very familiar with the people here too.?

A group of nuns in a remote monastery have been gaining attention for their unique and delicious product - heavenly cheese. The nuns, who spend much of their days in prayer and meditation, have also taken up the art of cheese making as a way to support their community.Using traditional methods and high-quality ingredients, the nuns have been able to produce a variety of cheeses that have been praised for their exceptional taste and texture. Many have described the cheese as being truly heavenly, with a rich and creamy flavor that is unlike anything else on the market.The nuns have stated that they view their cheese making as a form of prayer in itself, with each batch being a labor of love and dedication to their craft. They hope that their cheese will not only bring joy to those who taste it, but also help to support their monastery and the work that they do.Local markets and shops have been quick to pick up the nuns' heavenly cheese, with many customers eagerly purchasing it to support the nuns and enjoy a taste of their unique product. As word continues to spread about the nuns' cheese making abilities, it is likely that demand for their heavenly cheese will only continue to grow.

Monday marks World Health Day for 2014. The World Health Organization is highlighting the annual observance with information about the increasing threat of vector-borne diseases. More than half the world's population is at risk from diseases such as malaria, dengue, leishmaniasis, Lyme disease, schistosomiasis and yellow fever, carried by mosquitoes, flies, ticks, water snails and other vectors. Vector-borne diseases affect the poorest populations, particularly where there is a lack of access to adequate housing, safe drinking water and sanitation. Malnourished people and those with weakened immunity are especially susceptible. More than one billion people are infected and more than one million die from vector-borne diseases every year. WHO says the diseases are entirely preventable. A newly published brief on vector-borne diseases outlines steps that governments, community groups and families can take to protect people from infection.WHO Director General Dr. Margaret Chan says "simple, cost-effective interventions like insecticide-treated bed nets and indoor spraying have already saved millions of lives." Dr. Lorenzo Savioli, director of WHO's Department of Control of Neglected Tropical Diseases, says "increased funds and political commitment are needed" to sustain existing vector-control tools and to conduct urgently needed research. Schistosomiasis, transmitted by water snails, is the most widespread of all vector-borne disease. It affects almost 240 million people worldwide and can be controlled through regular mass treatment of at-risk groups with a safe, effective medicine and access to safe drinking water and sanitation. WHO says in the past two decades many vector-borne diseases have re-emerged or spread to new parts of the world.

RANGOON — After months of delays, Burma's information ministry this week kept its pledge to lift part of its decades-old censorship policy. While reporters and editors grapple with the transition, many say a free press has not yet arrived. This week, newspapers in Burma will hit newsstands without ministry censorship for the first time since 1962. For the staff at The Myanmar Times, a Rangoon-based weekly, this is the first editorial meeting after the announced end of censorship. Editor Zaw Myint is elated, because not having to deal with censors will save time and improve the quality of reporting. "This is the same to my wedding day or something like that, but I will be more happy when we are granted as a daily license," he said. "Without censorship rules, with the chance to run our paper with a daily cycle, at that time I will be happier." State-run newspapers are still the only ones allowed to publish daily, but private publishers hope to be granted daily licenses. A released political prisoner and blogger who goes by the pen name Nay Phone Latt says online media in Burma remain unprotected. He says bloggers are still vulnerable to prosecution under electronic laws banning anti-government material on computers. "In the current media law, there is no place for the online media, so we are not safe," he said. "The electronic law still exists, so we are not safe according to this law." Controversial editor Kyaw Min Swe fears that the newly formed press council will not necessarily protect journalists and censorship will continue, in the form of legal threats. "They changed the system of PSB [Press Scrutiny Board] pre-censor to post-censor so that we have to submit after publication.

Auburn, Alabama - Bayu Prakosa, a dedicated and hardworking student from Indonesia, has made history at Auburn University by becoming the first student to receive both an undergraduate and graduate degree from the prestigious institution in the same year.Prakosa, who majored in Business Administration for his undergraduate degree and continued on to earn a Master's degree in Finance, is being hailed as a shining example of academic excellence and perseverance."I am truly grateful for the opportunities that Auburn University has provided me to further my education and achieve my goals," said Prakosa. "It has been a challenging journey, but with the support of my professors, classmates, and family, I was able to succeed."Prakosa's accomplishment has not gone unnoticed, with many praising his dedication and hard work. He has already been offered several job opportunities from top companies in the finance industry."I am confident that Bayu Prakosa will go on to achieve great things in his career," said Dr. Emily Jones, Prakosa's academic advisor. "His intelligence, work ethic, and determination set him apart from his peers."As Prakosa prepares to embark on the next chapter of his career, he hopes to inspire other students to pursue their dreams and never give up on their goals.Congratulations to Bayu Prakosa on his impressive academic achievement!

In New York, a collaborative effort between public and private organizations is underway to help address the issue of homelessness among veterans. Thousands of former service members have found themselves without a place to call home, facing various challenges as they try to navigate their way back into society.The city's Department of Veterans Services, along with non-profit organizations and businesses, have joined forces to provide housing, job training, and support services to these veterans. This initiative aims to not only provide immediate assistance but also to create long-term solutions to prevent veteran homelessness in the future.One of the main obstacles that homeless veterans face is the lack of affordable housing. By working with landlords and developers, the organizations involved in this effort are able to secure housing options for these individuals. Additionally, job training programs and employment opportunities are being offered to help veterans get back on their feet and regain their independence.The collaboration between public and private organizations in New York is a shining example of how communities can come together to support those who have served their country. By providing a helping hand to homeless veterans, these organizations are making a positive impact and changing lives for the better.

On November 9, 1989, the Berlin Wall that divided East and West Germany for nearly three decades finally fell, marking the symbolic end of the Cold War. The wall had been constructed in 1961 by the East German government to prevent its citizens from fleeing to the West.The fall of the Berlin Wall came after months of protests and pressure on the East German government to allow freedom of movement. Thousands of East Germans flooded through the checkpoints into West Berlin, where they were greeted with cheers and celebrations.The collapse of the Berlin Wall signified a new era of unity and cooperation between the East and West. It paved the way for the reunification of Germany in 1990 and contributed to the eventual dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.Today, the site of the Berlin Wall serves as a powerful reminder of the division and conflicts of the past, as well as a symbol of hope for a more peaceful and interconnected future.

A United Nations expert has revealed that North Korea is coercing its citizens to work abroad in order to earn much-needed cash for the regime. The report, released by the UN Special Rapporteur on North Korea, details how the North Korean government forces its citizens to work in countries such as Russia, China, and the Middle East, where they are subjected to harsh working conditions and their wages are heavily monitored and controlled.The report highlights the widespread human rights abuses that these North Korean workers face while working abroad, including confiscation of their passports, restricted freedom of movement, and limited access to basic necessities. Many of these workers are also reportedly forced to send a significant portion of their wages back to the North Korean government, effectively turning them into modern-day slaves.The UN Special Rapporteur has called for immediate action to address these abuses and to hold the North Korean government accountable for its actions. The international community has long been aware of the exploitation of North Korean workers abroad, but this latest report sheds new light on the extent of the problem and the urgent need for intervention.The North Korean government has denied these allegations, claiming that its overseas workers are treated well and are given fair wages. However, the evidence presented in the UN report suggests otherwise, raising serious concerns about the welfare of North Korean workers abroad and the need for greater oversight and protection.

With no laws to prosecute war criminals who live in the United States seeking safe haven after committing crimes against humanity abroad, Washington has begun to take action against criticism of its objections to the International Criminal Court (ICC). A Senate Judiciary subcommittee heard testimony this week to plug that loophole from a panel ranging from international lawyers to high-profile Darfur activists like American Olympic speed skater Joey Cheek and actress Mia Farrow. They are seeking stronger solutions for pursuing perpetrators still at large and speaking out against the Khartoum government?s efforts to shield government officials accused of perpetrating mass crimes of rape and murder in western Sudan. One of those testifying, American University law professor Diane Orentlicher, says that the atrocities being committed in Darfur illustrate that the time has come for the United States to take a more serious look at efforts to apprehend war criminals.?What is significant about crimes against humanity is that they?re atrocities that are targeted against civilians. They?re not simply acts of war, but the target is civilians themselves,? she points out.Although US support for prosecution of crimes against humanity dates back to World War Two and extends to curbing more recent upheavals in Rwanda, Yugoslavia, and Sierra Leone, there is no law on the books to prosecute persons responsible for mass atrocities that do not fall under the definition of genocide, torture or other select human rights violations. Professor Orentlicher says that the Bush administration made great strides to bridge this ambiguity with last year?s passage of the Genocide Accountability Act to steer American citizens, companies and government agencies away from conducting business with foreign regimes like Sudan that promote attacks against their own population.

India has urged Pakistan to spare the life of an accused spy who is facing execution. The accused, named Kulbhushan Jadhav, was arrested in Pakistan in 2016 and sentenced to death by a Pakistani military court in 2017.The Indian government has maintained that Jadhav was abducted from Iran and framed by Pakistani authorities. They have called the trial unfair and have repeatedly requested consular access to Jadhav, which has been denied by Pakistan.In a statement, the Indian Ministry of External Affairs said, "We once again call upon Pakistan to grant consular access to Mr. Jadhav immediately in compliance with the orders of the International Court of Justice."The case of Kulbhushan Jadhav has been a source of tension between India and Pakistan, who have a long history of political and territorial disputes.

Several journalists in the Maldives have recently been arrested for speaking out against the government, leading to concerns about press freedom in the island nation.The journalists, who were taken into custody for their critical reporting, have stated that the Maldives is no paradise for media professionals. They claim that there is a growing trend of censorship and suppression of dissenting voices in the country.One of the arrested journalists, who requested anonymity for fear of reprisals, spoke out about the challenges faced by media workers in the Maldives. "We are constantly under threat for doing our jobs and reporting the truth. The government is cracking down on anyone who dares to criticize its actions," the journalist said.International organizations have also raised concerns about the deteriorating press freedom situation in the Maldives. Reporters Without Borders recently ranked the Maldives 112th out of 180 countries in its Press Freedom Index, citing government interference and intimidation of journalists as key issues.The Maldivian government has denied allegations of targeting journalists and insists that it respects media freedom. However, the recent arrests have sparked outrage among press freedom advocates both locally and internationally.As the crackdown on journalists continues, many in the Maldives are calling for greater protection of media workers and respect for freedom of expression in the country. It remains to be seen how the government will respond to these calls for change.

India has issued a warning to Pakistan, stating that the recent spy case involving a Pakistani diplomat could have a negative impact on the relations between the two countries. The Indian government has accused a Pakistani embassy official in New Delhi of spying and has demanded that Pakistan take action against the individual. This incident has further escalated tensions between the two neighboring countries, who have a history of conflict and strained relations. The Indian Ministry of External Affairs has urged Pakistan to investigate the matter and take appropriate action to prevent any further deterioration of diplomatic ties. It remains to be seen how Pakistan will respond to India's warning and what the implications will be for the future of their relationship.

Several political leaders from around the world have been named in the Panama Papers, a massive leak of documents exposing offshore accounts and secret financial dealings. The Panama Papers, leaked in 2016, reveal how the rich and powerful use offshore companies to hide their wealth and avoid taxes.Among the political leaders implicated in the scandal are former prime ministers, presidents, and government officials.

Organizers of the vote by Iraqi expatriates say some two-thirds of eligable Iraqis living abroad have participated in the historic election so far.The International Organization for Migration said Sunday more than 186,000 Iraqis voted Friday and Saturday in the Middle East, North America, Australia and Western Europe.The three-day process runs until the end of Sunday across 36 cities in 14 nations, including Australia, Britain, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, Iran, Jordan, the Netherlands, Sweden, Syria, Turkey, United Arab Emirates and the United States.Some information for this report provided by AP, AFP.

The White House has revealed that some Bush administration staffers may have used their private Republican Party e-mail accounts to conduct government business, and it says those messages may have been improperly deleted.Congressional Democrats have been seeking the e-mails as part of their inquiry into the dismissals of eight federal prosecutors. White House spokesman Scott Stanzel says that some of those lost e-mails may have discussed the firings.Dozens of administration aides, including chief political advisor Karl Rove, had e-mail addresses set up through the Republican Party to use strictly for political work.Federal law prohibits government employees from engaging in political business using government-owned property.

Kanye West's latest album, "Donda," continues to dominate the charts and outsell his rival 50 Cent. The album, which was released in late August, has received widespread critical acclaim and has quickly climbed to the top of the charts in multiple countries.Despite 50 Cent's recent release of his own album, "Power of the Dollar," Kanye West's "Donda" has consistently outsold his competitor in both physical sales and streaming numbers. Fans have praised the album for its innovative production and introspective lyrics, further solidifying Kanye's status as one of the top artists in the music industry.With "Donda" still going strong on the charts, it seems like Kanye West has once again proven his dominance in the music world. Fans can expect to see the album continue to sell well and potentially break even more records in the coming weeks.

In a historic move, the Lutheran Church in Norway has voted in favor of allowing same-sex marriage within the church. The decision was made during the annual conference of the Church of Norway, where 88 out of 115 delegates voted in support of the resolution.This decision comes after years of debate within the church regarding the inclusion of LGBTQ+ individuals in the institution. The Lutheran Church in Norway has long been seen as progressive in its views on social issues, and this decision further cements its stance on equality and acceptance.The resolution will now allow same-sex couples to be married by church officials and hold weddings in church buildings. This marks a significant step forward for LGBTQ+ rights in Norway and has been met with celebration from advocates and members of the community.The Lutheran Church in Norway joins a growing number of churches around the world that have embraced same-sex marriage, signaling a shift towards greater acceptance and inclusion within religious institutions. This decision is expected to have a positive impact on LGBTQ+ individuals in Norway and beyond.

Thousands of residents in Arizona have been forced to evacuate as a massive wildfire threatens the city. The wildfire, which has been burning out of control for days, has already destroyed several homes and businesses in its path.Emergency responders are working tirelessly to contain the blaze and protect the remaining structures in the area. However, the high winds and dry conditions have made it difficult to control the spread of the fire.Evacuation orders have been issued for several neighborhoods, and residents have been urged to seek shelter in designated evacuation centers. Firefighters are also conducting door-to-door checks to ensure that everyone has safely evacuated the area.The Governor of Arizona has declared a state of emergency and requested additional resources from neighboring states to help fight the wildfire. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.Authorities are asking residents to stay vigilant and follow evacuation orders to ensure their safety. Our thoughts are with those affected by this devastating wildfire, and we hope for a swift containment of the blaze.

The White House has recently issued a statement acknowledging that official emails may have been lost due to employees using private email accounts for government business. This revelation has raised concerns about potential violations of record-keeping laws and transparency within the administration.Officials at the White House have stressed that they are working to rectify the situation and ensure that all official communications are properly archived in accordance with federal regulations. However, the use of private email accounts for government work has been a common practice among officials in the current administration, sparking criticism from transparency advocates and political opponents.The issue of email transparency has been a recurring topic in American politics, especially after high-profile scandals involving the use of private email servers by public officials.

A recent poll has revealed a growing dissatisfaction among Americans with the current system of government in the United States. The survey, conducted by a leading research firm, found that a significant majority of respondents feel that the current system is in need of major reforms.According to the poll results, many Americans feel that the current political system is plagued by corruption, gridlock, and inefficiency. There is a widespread belief that elected officials are more concerned with serving their own interests rather than the needs of the people they are supposed to represent.Furthermore, the poll found that a large number of Americans feel that the current two-party system is failing to adequately represent the diversity of opinions and beliefs within the country. Many feel that third-party candidates are unfairly marginalized and that the current system stifles true democracy.The poll results also show a strong desire among Americans for changes to the way elections are conducted, with many expressing support for measures such as campaign finance reform, term limits for elected officials, and the abolition of the Electoral College.Overall, the poll findings suggest that there is a hunger for change among the American public when it comes to the current system of government. It remains to be seen whether these sentiments will translate into concrete action and reforms in the future.

PENTAGON - The U.S. military service chiefs have sought a delay before allowing transgender people to enlist, but no final decision has been made on the issue, Pentagon chief spokesperson Dana White said.Transgender troops already in the military have been allowed to serve openly since July 2016.The U.S. armed forces were given until July 1 to develop policies to let transgender individuals who meet all the standards to join the military.The Associated Press reported earlier Friday that the chiefs were seeking a six-month delay.Deputy Secretary of Defense Robert Work has received recommendations from the Army, Navy, Air Force and Marine Corps and is having further conversations before submitting his own recommendation to Defense Secretary Jim Mattis.“Different services had different takes,” White said. “Some asked for time.”White said the recommendation will be made with a focus on how the U.S. military can best achieve lethality and readiness.Should the deputy secretary approve the delay, Mattis will need to make a final decision.

U.S. President Barack Obama is among dozens of heads of state who are in Colombia for the Summit of the Americas. The 33 presidents and prime ministers from Canada, the U.S., Caribbean, and Latin America are meeting in the resort town of Cartagena.President Obama is expected to use the summit as an opportunity to emphasize the importance of expanding economic and other ties across the region. While the U.S. president remains personally popular throughout the region, he faces growing discontent from his counterparts over a number of issues, including Washington's decades-long fight against the illicit drug trade and Cuba's continued absence from the summit.A growing number of leaders, including the summit's host, Colombian President Juan Manuel Santos, have called for decriminalizing drugs in order to reduce the demand fueling the violent drug cartels. Dan Restrepo, Mr. Obama's national security advisor for Latin America, says the president does not support decriminalization.There is also an increasing demand for the U.S. to end its five-decade-long embargo on Cuba and allow it to rejoin the Organization of American States. Ecuadorian President Rafael Correa is boycotting the summit to protest Cuba's absence and others are warning it should be the last without the communist-run island's participation. President Obama can also expect to be in the minority in his opposition to Argentina's claim to the British-controlled Falkland islands.

The fall of the Berlin Wall 25 years ago on November 9th, 1989, was an event that had momentous geopolitical ramifications.Most analysts and historians agree that former Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev played a pivotal role in the events leading up to the wall's demise and beyond. His policies of “perestroika” - restructuring - and “glasnost,” or openness, paved the way for the dissolution of communist power in Eastern Europe and ultimately led to the collapse of the Soviet Union.Robert Legvold from Columbia University said a key factor was Gorbachev’s decision that he would not use force to suppress reformist aspirations in Eastern Europe.“Increasingly he made it apparent to the East Europeans that the Soviet Union would not do what it had done many times in the past: 1953 in Berlin, 1956 in Hungary and Poland, 1968 in the Czech Republic and so on,” said Legvold.In July 1989, the so-called “Brezhnev Doctrine” was replaced by what one Gorbachev adviser described as the “Sinatra Doctrine,” based on the singer’s song My Way. In other words, the adviser said East European countries were now able to go their own way -- politically and economically.In early October, Gorbachev marked the 40th anniversary of East Germany by attending celebrations in East Berlin.Serge Schmemann, former Moscow and Bonn Correspondent for The New York Times, said Gorbachev was tough with East German leaders as tens of thousands of people marched with candles through the streets of the city.“A huge march through Berlin. And at that time, Gorbachev made it clear to [Erich] Honecker, the East German leader, that he was not going to prop him up - that if he doesn’t get with it he’s going to be dumped by history. So the message from Gorbachev was not just symbolic, it was right there - I mean they were announcing to the East Europeans that we’re not going to prop you up - and that was huge,” said Schmemann.A few weeks later Honecker was gone.

In a recent statement, US Senator Lindsey Graham has called for the establishment of a no-fly zone in Syria's Idlib province. This comes after a recent escalation of violence in the region, with airstrikes killing civilians and destroying vital infrastructure.Senator Graham has emphasized the need to protect innocent civilians from further harm and to prevent the further displacement of thousands of people in the region. He believes that a no-fly zone would help to create a safe space for civilians and provide humanitarian assistance to those in need.The situation in Idlib has been deteriorating rapidly, with many innocent lives at risk. The international community has been urged to take immediate action to address the crisis and prevent further bloodshed.It remains to be seen whether Senator Graham's call for a no-fly zone will be heeded, but it is clear that urgent action is needed to protect the people of Idlib and bring an end to the violence in Syria.

Formula One teams have persuaded the sport's ruling body to allow the numbers displayed on cars next season to reflect the 2001 championship standings.FIA, the world body, had triggered a minor controversy this week by announcing that beginning next year the numbers would no longer reflect where teams had finished the previous year, as had always been the case, but would follow the 2000 standings instead.Since the top three teams were the same this year as they were in 2000, it was the smaller teams which felt their achievements would not be recognized.For instance Sauber, which enjoyed its best season ever by finishing fourth in the constructors' standings, would have been forced to retain its 2000 numbers of 16 and 17 rather than a more exalted seven and eight.

Senegal's government has admitted it gave money to an International Monetary Fund official leaving the country in late September as what it called a "traditional African farewell gift". The IMF has returned the money, and Senegal has denied allegations of corruption.Senegal has confirmed it gave almost $200,000 to a departing IMF official last month, after previously denying the incident. In a statement released Monday, the IMF said its agent Alex Segura was given 100,000 euros and $50,000 as a "farewell present" after a dinner with Senegalese President Abdoulaye Wade the evening of Segura's departure from the country. Segura had spent three-years as the IMF resident representative in Senegal.According to the IMF statement, Segura did not realize the gift was a large sum of money until he was about to leave for Spain. Concerned about missing his flight and unsure of a safe place to leave the money in Senegal, the IMF said Segura took it with him on his flights to Paris and Barcelona, his final destination. Upon arrival, Segura turned the money over to the IMF, who said the money was then given to the Senegalese ambassador in Spain on October 6th via a security company.In a written statement released Tuesday, Senegalese President Abdoulaye Wade admitted he authorized the gift without specifying an amount. Mr. Wade said his aide-de-camp then made a mistake about the sum.At a news conference Tuesday in Dakar, the president refused to respond to questions about the incident, which has been dubbed "Seguragate" by local media.Mr. Wade said he would not respond to this question.

Iraqi activist and rescuer, Amina Hassan, is on a mission to end the cycle of hatred perpetuated by the Islamic State (IS) in Iraq. Hassan, a former teacher, has dedicated her life to rescuing children who have been orphaned or abducted by IS and providing them with a safe haven.Hassan founded the organization "Children of Hope" which focuses on rehabilitating children who have been exposed to violence and extremism. She believes that by providing these children with education, support, and love, they can overcome the trauma they have experienced and break the cycle of hatred that has plagued their country.In a recent interview, Hassan emphasized the importance of teaching these children about peace, tolerance, and coexistence. She believes that by instilling these values in the next generation, Iraq can move towards a more peaceful and harmonious society.Despite facing numerous challenges and threats from IS supporters, Hassan remains determined to continue her work. She hopes that by rescuing and rehabilitating children affected by the violence of IS, she can contribute to building a brighter future for Iraq.Hassan's efforts are commendable and serve as a beacon of hope in a country that has been torn apart by conflict and extremism. Her dedication to ending the cycle of hatred and violence in Iraq is truly inspiring and serves as a reminder of the resilience and strength of the human spirit.

The United States, Britain and France, launched military airstrikes in Syria that targeted a scientific research center, a chemical weapons storage facility and another storage facility that also included an important command post.U.S. Defense Secretary James Mattis said the “decisive” efforts were intended to send a “clear message” to the regime of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad for its suspected chemical attack against civilians last week and to deter him from doing it again.Mattis said at a briefing at the Pentagon late Friday that the targets were selected to inflict “long-term degradation” and “maximum damage” to Syria’s stockpile of chemical weapons.WATCH: U.S. Defense Secretary Mattis Briefs Reporters in Syria StrikesSorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline.The defense secretary said he is confident that chlorine was used in the chemical attack in the city of Douma last week that killed at least 40 people and sickened hundreds. He said he was also “not ruling out” the possibility that sarin was also used.Mattis said the poison gas Assad said he had gotten rid of “still exists.”The Syrian government has repeatedly denied any use of banned weapons.General Joseph Dunford, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said more details about the strikes will be available Saturday morning.Missile fire lights up the Damascus, Syria, sky, April 14, 2018.Associated Press reporters saw smoke rising from east Damascus and a huge fire could be seen from a distance to the east.

Indian officials say at least eight women have died after undergoing sterilization surgery at a government-run health camp in the central state of Chhattisgarh.Sonmani Borah, the commissioner for India’s Bilaspur district, said 24 of the more than 80 women who underwent the procedure on Saturday have been hospitalized. He said the women were sent home after their surgeries, but some were later admitted to a hospital in Bilaspur after falling ill. "Reports of a drop in pulse, vomiting and other ailments started pouring in on Monday from the women who underwent surgery," Borah said. Ramanesh Murthy, the medical superintendent at Chhattisgarh Institute of Medical Sciences, where some of the women were hospitalized, said the sterilization surgeries were minimally invasive, and should have taken less than five minutes. Local governments in India often offer incentives to couples volunteering for sterilization to try to control the country's billion-plus population. In this particular case, they were paid $10 each to undergo sterilization surgery in central India, according to The Wall Street Journal.

The White House is hosting a conference Friday to highlight ways the Obama administration has "helped create economic security for women," which coincides with the release of a report on women's progress under his administration.Opening the White House Forum on Women and the Economy, Valerie Jarrett, chair of the White House Council on Women and Girls, said President Barack Obama has taken "historic steps" to appoint more women to key positions and empowered them to drive policy promoting the interests of women and girls both at home and abroad."Women make up nearly half of the workforce, and they're the breadwinners for a growing number of families," said Jarrett. "So it's clear that the success of women in America is critical to the success and sustainability of our families, of our communities and of the national economy."Friday's conference comes at a key time for the president, a speaker at the forum, as he campaigns for re-election. Recent public opinion polls have shown women supporting Obama over Republican front-runner Mitt Romney.Asked Thursday about the political implications of staging an event focused on women during an election year, White House spokesman Jay Carney dismissed the idea that the forum is political."

The United States has recently imposed sanctions on Venezuela's First Lady, Cilia Flores, along with several other high-ranking government officials in the country. The move comes as a response to alleged corruption and human rights abuses committed by the Venezuelan government.According to the US Treasury Department, Flores and the other sanctioned individuals have been involved in corrupt activities and have contributed to the deteriorating human rights situation in Venezuela. The sanctions freeze any assets these individuals may have in the US and prohibit US citizens from conducting any business with them.This is not the first time the US has imposed sanctions on Venezuelan officials. In recent years, the US has targeted President Nicolas Maduro and his inner circle, as well as other government officials, in an effort to pressure the Venezuelan government to address the ongoing political and economic crisis in the country.The sanctions on the First Lady and other officials further escalate tensions between the US and Venezuela, which have been strained for years. The Venezuelan government has condemned the sanctions as an attempt to interfere in the country's internal affairs and has vowed to retaliate against what it perceives as unjust actions by the US.The international community will be closely watching how Venezuela responds to these latest sanctions and whether they will have any impact on the ongoing crisis in the country.

Italy has recently intensified its efforts to secure the release of journalist Giulia Sangalli, who was abducted in Afghanistan last month. Sangalli, a freelance journalist, was taken captive while reporting on the conflict in the region.Italian officials have been working closely with Afghan authorities and international partners to locate and negotiate the safe return of Sangalli. The Italian government has also reached out to local tribal leaders and community members in Afghanistan to gather information and support for the journalist's release.Sangalli's family and colleagues have been pleading for her safe return, emphasizing her dedication to reporting on important global issues. Italian Foreign Minister Luigi Di Maio has assured the public that every possible effort is being made to bring Sangalli back home.The abduction of journalists in conflict zones is a serious concern, as it hinders the ability of the media to report on crucial events and stories. Italy's determination to secure Sangalli's release serves as a reminder of the risks that journalists face in their pursuit of truth and information.As the situation continues to develop, Italy remains committed to ensuring the safety and well-being of all journalists working in dangerous environments. The efforts to secure Sangalli's release will continue until she is safely reunited with her loved ones.

The German Parliament has issued a strong condemnation of recent threats made by Turkish officials towards Greece and Cyprus. In a unanimous statement, members of the Bundestag expressed their support for the sovereignty and territorial integrity of both countries.The Turkish government has been increasing its aggressive rhetoric and actions in the Eastern Mediterranean, particularly in the disputed waters around Cyprus. Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan recently stated that Turkey will not back down in its claims to natural resources in the region, despite objections from the European Union and neighboring countries.In response, the German Parliament called on Turkey to de-escalate tensions and engage in dialogue to resolve disputes peacefully. The statement also emphasized the importance of upholding international law and respecting the rights of all countries in the region.The European Union has been closely monitoring the situation and has expressed solidarity with Greece and Cyprus. The escalating tensions in the Eastern Mediterranean pose a threat to stability in the region, and diplomatic efforts are underway to find a peaceful resolution.The German Parliament's condemnation of Turkish threats sends a clear message that aggressive behavior will not be tolerated, and that dialogue and diplomacy are the preferred methods for resolving conflicts. It remains to be seen how Turkey will respond to this international pressure, but for now, the focus remains on finding a peaceful solution to the escalating tensions in the Eastern Mediterranean.

Argentina is preparing for a return to the bond market after a long absence. The South American country has been working to reestablish its presence in the international financial markets following a series of economic challenges in recent years.The government of Argentina has announced plans to issue new bonds as part of its efforts to raise funds for various infrastructure projects and to refinance existing debt. The move is seen as a step towards rebuilding investor confidence in the country's economy and fiscal policies.Argentina has been in negotiations with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) to restructure its debt and stabilize its economy. The country's economy has been hit hard by high inflation, a weakening currency, and a lack of investor confidence.The return to the bond market is a positive sign for Argentina and could help attract much-needed foreign investment to support the country's economic recovery. Investors will be closely watching the government's actions and policies to ensure that they are on a sustainable path towards long-term growth and stability.Overall, Argentina's return to the bond market is a significant development that could have a major impact on the country's economy and its ability to attract foreign investment. It remains to be seen how successful the government will be in its efforts to rebuild investor confidence and achieve sustainable economic growth.

Tensions are rising in Iran as protests over water shortages continue to escalate, with Iranian police cracking down on demonstrators in several regions. The protests, which began earlier this month, have been fueled by anger over the lack of access to clean water in various parts of the country.The Iranian government has been struggling to address the water crisis, which has been exacerbated by drought and mismanagement of water resources. Many Iranians have been forced to rely on unclean or unsafe sources of water, leading to a public health crisis.In response to the protests, Iranian authorities have reportedly used tear gas and water cannons to disperse crowds and arrested dozens of demonstrators. The crackdown has drawn condemnation from human rights organizations, with calls for the Iranian government to respect the right to peaceful assembly and address the underlying issues causing the water shortages.As the situation continues to unfold, many Iranians are calling for urgent action to address the water crisis and improve access to clean water for all citizens. The Iranian government will need to take concrete steps to address the root causes of the water shortages and work towards finding sustainable solutions to ensure the well-being of its population.

NAIROBI, KENYA - August 7 is Kenya’s 9/11, the day of the worst terrorist attack in the country's history. On this day 20 years ago, a truck bomb destroyed the U.S. embassy in Nairobi, killing 213 people, most of them Kenyans. In Nairobi, hundreds gathered Tuesday to remember victims of the brutal attack.Pamela Olum was in the hospital the day of the embassy bombing. Three days earlier, she gave birth to her third child.She says her husband came to see her at Mater hospital and to pay the bill. He gave her 5,000 shillings for her 17,000 shilling bill and left for the bank to get the rest of the money. Olum says she never saw him again.Lawrence Olum was one of the people killed on August 7, 1998, when al-Qaida exploded truck bombs in front of the U.S. embassies in this capital city and in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. The attacks killed a total of 224 people and injured nearly 5,000.Sorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline.On Tuesday, about 200 people, including survivors, gathered at a memorial park in Nairobi to honor victims of the attack and offer prayers.Participants lit candles and laid flowers as signs of remembrance. Teddy Gianopulos came to honor her sister, who worked at the U.S. embassy at the time of the bombings. She was 51 when she was killed.“

WHITE HOUSE - White House officials on Monday continued to deflect criticism of President Donald Trump's firing of FBI Director James Comey and insisted the search to name his successor was moving quickly.Six days after Trump fired Comey, the firestorm over the decision raged on in Washington, with Democrats ramping up their calls for a special prosecutor to look into possible Russia-Trump ties.Although the extent of the political fallout remained unclear, the decision to fire Comey has become a distraction from the president's legislative agenda, which already was facing major roadblocks.Before being fired, Comey was overseeing an investigation into alleged Russian hacking of the 2016 presidential election, as well as into possible collusion between Moscow and the Trump campaign.Many Democrats think the firing amounted to obstruction of justice, and Trump himself acknowledged that he had the Russia investigation in mind when he fired Comey.President Donald Trump waves as he leaves after speaking at the 36th Annual National Peace Officers memorial service, May 15. 2017, on Capitol Hill in Washington.Not 'up to the job'On Monday, White House officials again dismissed the controversy as a partisan attack."

A group of former diplomats have issued a warning to Secretary of State Mike Pompeo, advising him against cutting the US Refugee Bureau. These ex-diplomats believe that such a move would have negative repercussions on America's reputation and national security.The US Refugee Bureau plays a crucial role in providing assistance and protection to refugees who are seeking asylum in the United States. By cutting funding or resources to this bureau, the ex-diplomats argue that the US would be turning its back on some of the world's most vulnerable populations.In a joint statement, the former diplomats emphasized the importance of upholding America's commitment to humanitarian values and international law. They also highlighted the role that refugees have played in contributing to the cultural and economic fabric of the United States.The warning comes amid reports that the Trump administration is considering reducing the budget of the US Refugee Bureau as part of broader efforts to cut government spending. Critics fear that such a decision could have far-reaching consequences, both at home and abroad.As the debate continues, it remains to be seen whether Secretary Pompeo will heed the advice of these ex-diplomats or move forward with plans to cut the US Refugee Bureau.

Belgian prosecutors say the top suspect in the November terrorist attack on Paris can be extradited to France.French Justice Minister Jean-Jacques Urvoas said Thursday he expects Salah Abdeslam to be sent to Paris within 10 days.Abdeslam's lawyer, Cedric Moisse, said his client agrees to be extradited."Abdeslam wants to make it known that he wants to cooperate with the French authorities. These are the words he wants to make known."Another one of his lawyers said after his March 18 arrest that Abdeslam would fight extradition to France. It is unclear why he changed his mind.Salah Abdeslam, a Belgian national French police are searching for in connection with Paris terror attacks. (Police Nationale Handout Photo)Abdeslam is accused of helping plan the November 13 terror attacks in Paris that killed 130 people at multiple locations.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday again assailed judicial review for illegal border crossers, contending that the migrants ought to immediately be sent back to their homelands."We want a system where when people come in illegally, they have to go," Trump told reporters at the White House. "We want strong borders and we want no crime."The American Civil Liberties Union said Sunday that the U.S. leader's call to end legal hearings for undocumented immigrants seeking asylum in the U.S. was unconstitutional. But Trump rejected that view in a pair of new Twitter comments."Hiring manythousands [sic] of judges, and going through a long and complicated legal process, is not the way to go - will always be disfunctional [sic]," he said. "People must simply be stopped at the Border and told they cannot come into the U.S. illegally."He contended that if children and their parents are sent home, "illegal immigration will be stopped in it's [sic] tracks - and at very little, by comparison, cost. This is the only real answer - and we must continue to BUILD THE WALL!"White House press secretary Sarah Sanders argued Monday that "it makes no sense that an illegal alien sets one foot on American soil and they would go through a three- to five-year judicial process to be removed from the country."She said there are designated points of entry that asylum seekers can use to apply for asylum."Anyone that goes to a point of entry seeking asylum will not be prosecuted.

A panel of experts testified before the Senate on Tuesday, highlighting the importance of Central Asia to US security interests. The experts emphasized the strategic location of the region, as well as the potential threats and opportunities it presents.Dr. Sarah Smith, a leading expert on Central Asian affairs, spoke about the growing influence of Russia and China in the region, and the need for the US to engage more actively to counter their influence. She also highlighted the potential for economic partnerships and the importance of stability in the region for US security.Another expert, Dr. John Davis, emphasized the need for the US to work closely with Central Asian countries to combat terrorism and promote regional stability. He pointed out that instability in Central Asia could have serious consequences for US security interests, including the spread of extremism and the disruption of vital supply routes.Overall, the experts stressed the importance of Central Asia to US national security and called for increased diplomatic and economic engagement in the region. They warned that neglecting Central Asia could lead to serious security challenges for the US in the future.

In a shocking revelation, a FIFA audit official has admitted to participating in bribery schemes as part of a US probe into corruption within the organization. The official, whose identity has not been disclosed, confessed to accepting bribes in exchange for influencing audit decisions and manipulating financial reports.This admission comes as a major blow to FIFA, which has been plagued by corruption scandals in recent years. The organization has long been criticized for its lack of transparency and accountability, with many accusing its officials of using their positions for personal gain.The US probe into FIFA's corruption practices has already led to the indictment and conviction of several high-ranking officials. This latest revelation is likely to further tarnish the organization's reputation and raise questions about the effectiveness of its internal controls and oversight mechanisms.FIFA has yet to comment on the admission made by the audit official, but critics are calling for a thorough investigation into the organization's financial practices and governance structure. The revelations highlight the need for greater transparency and accountability in international sports organizations to ensure that they uphold the highest standards of integrity and ethics.

STATE DEPARTMENT - VOA State Department Correspondent Pam Dockins interviewed Ambassador Bonnie Jenkins, coordinator for Threat Reduction Programs, at the Nuclear Security Summit in Washington on Friday.Question: Looking ahead, at the take-aways from summit ... what is going to be the U.S. catalyst going forward to help these countries meet some of their nuclear security goals?Bonnie Jenkins: One of things we did was working closely with countries to try and get them to move into areas of nuclear security. One of the things the summit has done has provided a catalyst for countries to move forward in terms of things needed to be done. ... Now summit ending in current format … other countries may want to do a summit, or the next president may want to do a summit, still use existing bilateral channels, working directly with countries, working with U.N., other international organizations, initiatives. … Even though (it's) over now, still feel other ways we can work with this country, including Russia, who is not here.Q: This is broader question, looking at possible nuclear terrorism as part of broader concern. … When groups such as Islamic State ... carry out attacks in the Middle East … what particular challenges does this present to the U.S. in effort to counter nuclear terrorism?Jenkins: Realize that we have to work very hard, and the work we have been doing … in terms of securing material, securing bio pathogens, securing precursors ... that we have to be even more diligent. … Certain groups out there who want to do harm, if get hands on weapons, materials, they would use them. … Think if nothing else, makes us realize how important situation is.

President Barack Obama's visit to Florida Tuesday highlighted the plight of an area that analysts say is a prime casualty of the nation's economic troubles. The real estate collapse and mounting job losses have left many families struggling in southwestern Florida.Residents of Lehigh Acres say few towns in America are in greater need of economic assistance than their Florida community.The construction and home-building boom that helped the town grow for years, has gone bust. Scores of construction workers and related employees are without jobs, and many homes sit vacant or unfinished. The mounting troubles have led to a record number of home foreclosures. Last year, 12 percent of homes in the region receive foreclosure notices.Local officials say the troubles have forced more and more families to seek public assistance. Charlotte Rae Nicely directs Lehigh Community Services, which offers food aid and financial counseling."In the last six months, I have seen more of the middle class family come in. I have seen men around 35-40 years old, coming in and asking for help. And they never had to ask for assistance before," Nicely said.As in many communities, local officials have drafted a list of stimulus measures they would like to implement with federal assistance. Their ideas focus mainly on generating construction jobs - repairing roads and water lines - and finishing work on abandoned homes.Nicely says she and other local officials hope the list will make into the president's hands during his trip to the nearby city of Fort Myers."We all know he is coming to talk about his stimulus plan and to try to get it passed," she says.

The Solar Impulse 2, a solar-powered aircraft, has embarked on its journey across the Atlantic Ocean. The plane took off from New York City and is headed towards Seville, Spain, in what is set to be the most challenging leg of its round-the-world flight. The aircraft is powered entirely by solar energy, with 17,000 solar cells built into its wings. The pilots, Bertrand Piccard and André Borschberg, will take turns flying the plane during the long journey, which is expected to take around three days. The goal of the Solar Impulse 2 mission is to promote clean energy and showcase the potential of renewable technology in aviation. The team behind the project hopes that the flight will inspire new innovations and help pave the way towards a more sustainable future.

The European Union has proposed visa-free travel for Ukrainian citizens, a move that could significantly ease travel restrictions for Ukrainian nationals. The proposal is part of the EU's efforts to strengthen ties with Ukraine and support the country's European integration aspirations.Currently, Ukrainian citizens are required to obtain a visa to travel to most EU countries, a process that can be time-consuming and expensive. The proposed visa-free travel would allow Ukrainians to enter the EU for short stays of up to 90 days within a 180-day period without the need for a visa.The proposal still needs to be approved by the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union before it can come into effect.

President-elect Joe Biden met with his team of presidential transition advisers on Monday to discuss priorities and plans for his upcoming administration. The meeting comes as Biden prepares to take office on January 20th, following his victory in the November election.The transition team includes a diverse group of experts and officials who will help guide Biden as he takes on the challenges of leading the country during a critical time. Biden has already announced some key appointments to his cabinet, including his picks for Secretary of State, Treasury, and Defense.During the meeting, Biden and his advisers discussed a wide range of topics, including the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, economic recovery, racial justice, and climate change. The team is working to ensure a smooth transition of power and to hit the ground running on day one of the new administration.Biden emphasized the importance of unity and working together to address the pressing issues facing the country. He also stressed the need for cooperation and bipartisanship in order to move the country forward.Overall, the meeting was seen as a positive step towards a successful transition of power and a signal of Biden's commitment to governing effectively and inclusively.

In Serbia, farmers have taken to blocking major roads in protest over what they claim is government neglect of the agricultural sector. The farmers are demanding better prices for their produce and more support from the government to help them compete in the market. The protests have caused major disruptions to traffic and commerce, with long lines of vehicles stuck on the blocked roads. The government has yet to respond to the farmers' demands, leaving the situation unresolved. Local authorities are urging both sides to engage in dialogue to find a solution and end the road blockades. It remains to be seen how the situation will unfold in the coming days.

Indian police have reportedly detained dozens of Tibetan exiles in the northern region of India in the lead up to Chinese President Hu Jintao's visit to the country. The detentions are seen as an attempt to prevent any potential protests by the Tibetan community during Hu's visit.Tibetan activists have long been critical of China's occupation of Tibet and have frequently staged protests against Chinese leaders when they visit foreign countries. The Indian government, which has a delicate relationship with China, is likely trying to avoid any diplomatic tensions during Hu's visit.Human rights groups have condemned the detentions, calling them a violation of the right to freedom of expression and peaceful assembly. They have urged the Indian government to release the detained Tibetan exiles and allow them to express their views peacefully.President Hu's visit to India is expected to focus on economic ties between the two countries, but the issue of Tibet is likely to loom large in the background. The Tibetan exiles have vowed to continue their struggle for independence and have called on the international community to support their cause.

In a historic move, Egypt, Ethiopia, and Sudan have signed an agreement for a new dam project in the region. The Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam has been a point of contention for years, as Egypt feared it would reduce its share of the Nile River water. However, after lengthy negotiations, the three countries have reached a compromise.The accord includes a commitment from Ethiopia to fill the dam at a slower pace during times of drought to ensure that Egypt and Sudan's water supply is not significantly impacted. This agreement marks a significant step towards regional cooperation and stability.The project is expected to provide much-needed electricity to Ethiopia and improve irrigation in the region. The signing of this accord is seen as a positive development for all three countries and could potentially pave the way for further cooperation in the future.

WASHINGTON - Sunday's mass shooting in Orlando, Florida — the worst in U.S. history — has refocused the U.S. presidential campaign on the threat of terrorism and especially how to stop so-called "lone wolf" attacks.Presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump's previous proposal for a ban on Muslims also is getting fresh attention, rekindling fears among some Republicans that he will be a divisive general election candidate."Well, he's a volatile candidate," said John Fortier of the Bipartisan Policy Center in Washington. "And I think there have been moments where he has seemed to be tamed and become more a part of the party and made peace with the [Republican] establishment and then times where his comments really bring those problems up again. So, I don't think we are going to see that change with Donald Trump. He is who he is."But some Republicans have cheered Trump's focus on calling out President Barack Obama and presumptive Democratic presidential nominee Hillary Clinton for refusing to use the term "radical Islamic terrorism." It has become a prime example of Trump's pledge to do away with political correctness.On Monday,Trump spoke to supporters in Manchester, New Hampshire, where he seemed to offer a new version of his plan to temporarily ban non-American Muslims from entering the U.S."When I'm elected, I will suspend immigration from areas of the world where there is a proven history of terrorism against the United States, Europe or our allies," Trump told cheering supporters.

A Myanmar judge on Monday found two Reuters journalists guilty of breaching a law on state secrets and jailed them for seven years in a landmark case seen as a test of progress toward democracy in the Southeast Asian country.Here are some reactions to the news.Scot Marciel, U.S. Ambassador to Myanmar:“I’m sad for Wa Lone and Kyaw Soe Oo and their families, but also for Myanmar. It’s deeply troubling for everybody who has struggled so hard here for media freedom. I think one has to ask, will this process increase or decrease the confidence the people of Myanmar have in their justice system.”Dan Chugg, British ambassador to Myanmar :“Speaking on behalf of the British government, but also on behalf of European Union member states, we are extremely disappointed by this verdict. Freedom of expression and rule of law are fundamental in a democracy, and this case has passed a long shadow over both today. The judge has appeared to have ignored evidence and to have ignored Myanmar law. This has dealt a hammer blow for the rule of law.”Stephane Dujarric, spokesman for United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres:“The secretary-general takes note with concern of the conviction and sentencing ... in Myanmar of journalists Wa Lone and Kyaw Soe Oo to seven years of imprisonment. He urges the authorities to review their decision. The right to freedom of expression and information is a cornerstone of any democracy. It is unacceptable that these journalists were prosecuted for reporting on major human rights violations against the Rohingya in Rakhine State.

In a recent clash in the Philippines, 18 soldiers and 5 militants were killed in a deadly confrontation. The fighting occurred in the southern part of the country, where government troops were engaging with an armed group believed to be linked to a terrorist organization.The Philippine military has been conducting operations against various insurgent groups in the region, with the goal of maintaining peace and stability. However, clashes like this serve as a stark reminder of the ongoing violence and conflict in the area.The loss of life on both sides is a tragic outcome of the conflict, and highlights the challenges faced by the authorities in combating insurgency and terrorism. The government has vowed to continue its efforts to eliminate threats to national security and protect the Filipino people from harm.As the situation develops, it is crucial for the government to address the root causes of the conflict and work towards a peaceful resolution that will bring an end to the violence and bloodshed in the region. The families of the fallen soldiers and militants are left to mourn their loved ones, as the nation grapples with the devastating consequences of the fighting.

FRESNO, CALIFORNIA - California officials demanded Monday that the Trump administration back off a plan to weaken national fuel economy standards aimed at reducing car emissions and saving people money at the pump, saying the proposed rollback would damage people's health and exacerbate climate change. Looming over the administration's proposal is the possibility that the state, which has become a key leader on climate change as Trump has moved to dismantle Obama-era environmental rules, could set its own separate fuel standard that could roil the auto industry. That's a change the federal government is trying to block."

BENTO RODRIGUES, BRAZIL - Mud and wastewater from burst dams at a Brazilian iron ore mine cut off drinking water and raised health and environmental concerns in cities more than 300 km (186 miles) downstream on Monday, amid increasingly dire search efforts in a village devastated by the mudslides.One of Brazil's worst mining disasters in recent memory left 25 people missing. Officials have confirmed two deaths since Thursday's tragedy and are working to identify two more corpses recovered on Sunday.Exhausted firefighters waded through waist-deep mud as thick as wet concrete among the tangled remains of Bento Rodrigues, a 600-person village that stood in the shadow of the broken dams in an area about a six-hour car ride north of Rio de Janeiro.Intense flooding swept through towns as far as 100 km (62 miles) downstream after the dams burst at two reservoirs holding mining waste.

Despite the ongoing withdrawal of US and NATO troops from Afghanistan, NATO forces have stated that it is "business as usual" for their remaining troops in the country.NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg emphasized that NATO's mission in Afghanistan is to train, advise, and assist Afghan security forces, and that this mission continues despite the reduction in troops.The decision to withdraw US and NATO troops comes as part of a larger effort to end the nearly 20-year-long war in Afghanistan. While some NATO allies have announced the complete withdrawal of their troops, others, including the United Kingdom and Turkey, have pledged to continue their presence in the country.Stoltenberg also highlighted NATO's commitment to ensuring that Afghanistan does not become a safe haven for international terrorists, emphasizing the importance of continued support for Afghan security forces.As the situation in Afghanistan continues to evolve, NATO troops remain focused on supporting Afghan security forces and maintaining stability in the region.

In China, the deadly respiratory virus known as Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome (SARS) is not showing signs of fading away. Despite efforts to contain the spread of the virus, new cases continue to be reported in various parts of the country.The outbreak of SARS has caused widespread fear and concern among the population, as the virus is highly contagious and can be fatal in severe cases. Health authorities are urging the public to practice good hygiene and follow preventive measures to avoid contracting the virus.The Chinese government has been criticized for its handling of the outbreak, with some accusing officials of withholding information and downplaying the severity of the situation. However, government officials have stated that they are doing everything in their power to control the spread of the virus and protect the health of the population.As the situation continues to develop, it is crucial for the public to stay informed and take necessary precautions to stay safe. The fight against SARS is far from over, and it is important for everyone to remain vigilant and proactive in preventing further spread of the virus.

In a rare show of unity, both Democratic and Republican candidates running for office in the upcoming election have voiced support for prison reform in the United States. This issue, which has long been a divisive topic among politicians, is now seeing bipartisan agreement as candidates from both sides of the political spectrum recognize the need for change.Prison reform has become a pressing concern in recent years as the US prison population continues to grow at an alarming rate. The current system is plagued by overcrowding, harsh sentencing laws, and limited access to rehabilitation programs. Many believe that these factors contribute to high rates of recidivism and perpetuate a cycle of incarceration.Both Democratic and Republican candidates have proposed various solutions to address these issues, including reducing mandatory minimum sentences, expanding access to education and vocational training programs for inmates, and implementing alternative sentencing options for non-violent offenders. The goal is to create a more fair and effective criminal justice system that focuses on rehabilitation and reducing the likelihood of reoffending.While there may still be disagreements on specific policies and approaches, the fact that both parties are recognizing the need for reform is a positive step forward. With support from both sides of the aisle, there is hope that meaningful changes can be made to improve the US prison system and ultimately reduce rates of incarceration in the country.

In a recent statement, a top Democrat has expressed concerns over Moscow's cyber capabilities, suggesting that Russia has closed the cyber gap with the United States. This revelation comes amidst growing tensions between the two nations, particularly in the realm of cybersecurity.The Democrat, who wished to remain anonymous, warned that Russia's advancements in cyber technology pose a significant threat to the US's national security. "We have underestimated the extent to which Moscow has been able to develop their cyber capabilities," the source stated.The statement has sparked debate among lawmakers and security experts, with some calling for increased measures to counter Russia's cyber operations. The US government has been ramping up efforts to bolster its cybersecurity defenses in recent years, but many fear that it may not be enough to combat the threat posed by Russia.As tensions continue to rise between the two countries, it remains to be seen how the US will respond to Moscow's alleged closing of the cyber gap. With cybersecurity playing an increasingly important role in modern warfare, this development could have far-reaching implications for both nations.

BRUSSELS - There is a still a concern that Britain may leave the European Union without a deal to smooth the way, the bloc's chief executive said Saturday, urging Britain to take advantage of a six-month delay to work out the details of its departure.European Commission President Jean-Claude Juncker made the comments in an interview with German newspaper FUNKE Mediengruppe, a week after EU leaders gave Britain six months more to exit the EU."Nobody knows how Brexit will end. This is creating great uncertainty. There is still a fear that there will be a hard Brexit without any withdrawal treaty arrangements," Juncker said, citing the long-term negative impact on Europe's economy.Even though the extension to Oct. 31 offers little clarity on when, how or even whether Brexit will happen, Britain should use the time wisely, he said."I hope that the British will make use of this time and not waste it again. We cannot keep on putting off the withdrawal date indefinitely. The best solution would be for the British to adopt the Withdrawal Agreement during the extra time that has been agreed," Juncker said.The withdrawal deal negotiated by Prime Minister Theresa May with the EU has been rejected three times by the British Parliament.Juncker, who is scheduled to meet U.S.

Austria's president on Wednesday swore in new, technocratic ministers to replace politicians from the far-right Freedom Party who left the government following a scandal surrounding its leader.With his government rocking, it remains unclear whether Chancellor Sebastian Kurz will survive a no-confidence vote next week.Austria faces early elections in September after the conservative Kurz's coalition with the Freedom Party collapsed. Heinz-Christian Strache quit as vice chancellor and party leader after a video leaked to two German publications showed him appearing to offer favors to a purported Russian investor during a boozy meeting on the Spanish island of Ibiza two years ago.President Alexander Van der Bellen urged the interim ministers to contribute to Austria taking "a constructive role" in the European Union at a crucial time for the 28-nation bloc.The newcomers include Interior Minister Eckart Ratz, who formerly headed one of Austria's top courts; Defense Minister Johann Luif, a military officer; and Infrastructure Minister Valerie Hackl, the head of Austria's air traffic control agency.Parliament is to vote Monday on a small opposition party's no-confidence motion against Kurz, which would succeed if both the center-left Social Democrats and the Freedom Party — the second- and third-biggest parties — support it. Their stance isn't yet clear, and Kurz said he has been holding talks with other parties."

BRASÍLIA - Right-wing President-elect Jair Bolsonaro said on Tuesday he plans to tackle the overhaul of Brazil's fiscally burdensome pension system with piecemeal reforms that can pass Congress, starting with an increase in the minimum age of retirement.He said reforms should start with the public social security system and advance gradually to make sure they pass Congress."The idea is to start with the (minimum) age, attack the privileges and take it forward," Bolsonaro said at a news conference, warning that the problem with the cost of the pension system was growing every year."We cannot allow Brazil to reach the situation that Greece reached to do something about it," he said.Brazil's next president said he planned to start by raising the minimum age of retirement for everyone by two years, but keeping the gender age gap, building on a proposal made by incumbent President Michel Temer. He gave few details.Currently, Brazilian men can retire after 35 years of contributions and women after 30 years.

Kenyan police have shot dead 21 people in a slum in the capital, Nairobi, after two police officers were killed on Monday night. The police say the dead were members of the outlawed Mungiki sect, which has been blamed for a recent upsurge in violence, including the beheading of six people in Kenya last month. For VOA, Katy Migiro has this report from Nairobi.The Kenyan police sent for reinforcements after two of their officers were killed and two injured in Nairobi slums on Monday night. Police say the reinforcing officers became involved in a shootout with about 100 people in the slum who were trying to obstruct their operation. A total of 21 people are believed to have been killed in the gun battle. Kenya police believe that those who killed the officers and were in turn themselves killed in the shootout were members of the notorious Mungiki sect, which has been linked to several gruesome murders in Kenya in recent months. Mburu Gitu, secretary to the government-funded Kenya National Commission on Human Rights, says that while the police are entitled to use force if their lives are in danger, they also have a constitutional duty to respect human rights and civil liberties. Gitu says every suspect must be presumed innocent until proven guilty in a court of law."I think what one needs to do is to call upon the police even as they respond to the crisis which clearly Mungiki is presenting to them, that they need to do that in a manner that is even handed," he said.

NEW YORK - Warner Bros. chief Kevin Tsujihara, one of the highest ranking Hollywood executives to be felled by sexual misconduct allegations, stepped down from the studio Monday following claims that he promised roles to an actress with whom he was having an affair.WarnerMedia chief executive John Stankey announced Tsujihara's exit as chairman and chief executive of Warner Bros., saying his departure was in the studio's ``best interest.'' "Kevin has contributed greatly to the studio's success over the past 25 years and for that we thank him," said Stankey. "Kevin acknowledges that his mistakes are inconsistent with the company's leadership expectations and could impact the company's ability to execute going forward." Earlier this month, WarnerMedia launched an investigation after a March 6 Hollywood Reporter story detailed text messages between Tsujihara and British actress Charlotte Kirk going back to 2013. The messages suggested a quid pro quo sexual relationship between the aspiring actress and the studio head in which he made promises that he'd introduce her to influential executives and she'd be considered for roles in movies and television. In a memo to Warner Bros. staff on Monday, Tsujihara said he was departing "after lengthy introspection, and discussions with John Stankey over the past week." "It has become clear that my continued leadership could be a distraction and an obstacle to the company's continued success," said Tsujihara. "The hard work of everyone within our organization is truly admirable, and I won't let media attention on my past detract from all the great work the team is doing." Tsujihara's attorney, Bert H.

ISLAMABAD - Pakistan has dismissed as “unfortunate” Afghan allegations that Islamabad is behind a recent spike in attacks aimed at disrupting the April 5 presidential polls and blocking Kabul’s efforts for a peace deal with the Taliban. Afghan authorities have suggested that an attack on a luxury hotel in Kabul and attacks on electoral commission offices in recent days have been carried out by “foreign intelligence agencies.” On Sunday, Afghan President Hamid Karzai spoke with U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry by phone, urging Washington to help the situation by putting pressure on Pakistan’s spy agency. Pakistan's advisor on foreign policy and national security, Sartaj Aziz, denied the allegations, suggesting they are part of election-season politics in Afghanistan. In an interview with VOA, Aziz said that Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif’s government has tried to improve relations with Kabul. He said Pakistani authorities believed they had convinced President Karzai it has no favorites in Afghanistan and strictly adheres to a policy of non-interference. Aziz claims the diplomatic outreach succeeded in connecting with many Afghan political factions, including leaders of the former Northern Alliance, and had improved overall relations.
