But the latest allegations are a setback. “It is rather unfortunate because there is no justification for it. What do we get out of disrupting the elections? For us, a smooth transition in Afghanistan is absolutely critical because without peace and stability in Afghanistan Pakistan cannot be stable. So, therefore it is important that this thing is reviewed,” said Aziz, referring to the leveling of accusations. Aziz also said that Pakistan is taking all possible steps to prevent anyone from trying to undermine the Afghan presidential vote and is ready to deal with any government that emerges in Kabul after the elections.

Former CIA Director John Brennan is threatening to sue President Donald Trump to stop him from stripping security clearances from other officials who criticize him."If my clearances -- and my reputation, as I'm being pulled through the mud now -- if that's the price we're going to pay to prevent Donald Trump from doing this against other people, to me, it's a small price to pay," Brennan told NBC television's Meet the Press Sunday."I am going to do whatever I can personally to try to prevent these abuses in the future and if it means going to court, I will do that," he added.Trump revoked Brennan's security clearance last week because the president said he had to do something about what he calls the "rigged" investigation into alleged collusion between his campaign and Russian election interference.Trump said he believes Brennan, who served during the administrations of former Presidents George W. Bush and Barack Obama, is one of those responsible for the investigation. Brennan was among a group of intelligence officials who spoke with Trump before his inauguation about evidence of Russian meddling in the 2016 presidential election.The president also said he plans to or is thinking about stripping nine other current and former senior intelligence officials of their clearances.More than 75 U.S. intelligence officers have spoken out, saying they have the right to criticize and administration without having to pay a penalty.Brennan, CIA director during President Barack Obama's second term, has been a familiar face on television talk shows as one of Trump's severest critics.He called Trump's behavior at the joint press conference with Russian President Vladimir Putin in Helsinki "treasonous."

Scientists have made a groundbreaking discovery with the finding of the earliest fossil of iridescent dinosaur feathers, shedding new light on the behavior of these prehistoric creatures.The fossil, which dates back to the Cretaceous period around 100 million years ago, was found in Burmese amber. The iridescent feathers are believed to have belonged to a dinosaur species related to modern birds.The discovery of iridescent feathers suggests that these dinosaurs may have used their colorful plumage for purposes such as communication, camouflage, or attracting mates. This finding challenges previous beliefs that dinosaurs were dull and drab in appearance.Researchers are excited about the new clues that these feathers provide about the behavior of dinosaurs and the evolution of feathers in general. The study of iridescent dinosaur feathers opens up new possibilities for understanding the diversity and complexity of these ancient creatures.This discovery highlights the importance of studying fossils in various forms, including those preserved in amber, to uncover new information about the lives of dinosaurs. The findings from this study could have far-reaching implications for our understanding of dinosaur behavior and evolution.
In a surprising turn of events, former President Barack Obama has seen a surge in delegates following an endorsement from former presidential candidate John Edwards.

WASHINGTON - The U.S. National Security Agency collected more than 151 million records of Americans' phone calls last year, even after Congress limited its ability to collect bulk phone records, according to an annual report issued on Tuesday by the top U.S. intelligence officer.The report from the office of Director of National Intelligence Dan Coats was the first measure of the effects of the 2015 USA Freedom Act, which limited the NSA to collecting phone records and contacts of people U.S. and allied intelligence agencies suspect may have ties to terrorism.It found that the NSA collected the 151 million records even though it had warrants from the secret Foreign Intelligence Surveillance court to spy on only 42 terrorism suspects in 2016, in addition to a handful identified the previous year.

NAIROBI - The East African bloc mediating peace talks for South Sudan adjourned the negotiations on Monday to allow its envoys to try to get the talks back on track after the opposition refused to take part in the latest round. During the adjournment, special envoys from the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD) will hold "broad consultations with South Sudanese stakeholders, the leaders of the IGAD region, the African Union the United Nations Security Council and other friends and partners of South Sudan," IGAD said in a statement. They will also follow up on efforts that are being made to deploy teams in South Sudan to monitor a ceasefire that has struggled to take hold. The so-called Monitoring and Verification Mechanism (MVM) was agreed to when the first cessation of hostilities agreement was signed in January, but the deployment of monitoring teams and regional troops to protect them was halted when fighting picked up again a few months later. The two sides in the South Sudan conflict have met several times in Addis Ababa for IGAD-led peace talks. The latest round, which opened on Friday, was supposed to bring parties besides the government and opposition to the negotiating table. The opposition boycotted the talks because, it said, a request to IGAD that groups in exile and internally displaced persons be included in the negotiations fell on deaf ears.

Italian researchers have made a significant breakthrough in robotics with the development of a lighter and cheaper robotic hand. The innovative technology is expected to revolutionize the field of prosthetics and robotic assistance.The new robotic hand, developed by a team of engineers at a research institute in Italy, is designed to be more affordable and accessible to a wider range of users. This advancement will make it easier for individuals with limb differences or disabilities to access high-quality robotic prosthetics.One of the key features of the new robotic hand is its lightweight design, which allows for greater dexterity and precision in tasks. The hand is also equipped with advanced sensors and artificial intelligence algorithms, making it more intuitive and responsive to user movements.The researchers hope that this breakthrough will pave the way for more affordable and advanced robotic prosthetics in the future. The technology has the potential to improve the quality of life for millions of individuals worldwide who rely on prosthetic devices for everyday tasks.The team is currently working on further refining the design of the robotic hand and conducting clinical trials to test its effectiveness. With continued research and development, the researchers aim to bring this groundbreaking technology to market in the near future.

Three scientists have been awarded a prestigious prize of $500,000 for their groundbreaking work in the field of cancer therapy. The recipients, Dr. Sarah Johnson, Dr. Liu Wei, and Dr. Alejandro Martinez, were recognized for their collective efforts in developing a new treatment that has shown promising results in fighting various forms of cancer.The prize, known as the Innovations in Cancer Research Award, was established to honor individuals who have made significant contributions to the advancement of cancer treatment and research. Dr. Johnson, Dr. Wei, and Dr. Martinez were selected by a panel of esteemed judges for their innovative approach to targeting cancer cells and minimizing the side effects often associated with traditional treatments.In a statement released by the award committee, Dr.

Israeli archaeologists have discovered a mark from the seal of biblical King Hezekiah, who helped build Jerusalem into an ancient metropolis.The circular inscription, on a piece of clay less than a centimeter (0.4 inch) long, may very well have been made by the king himself, said Eilat Mazar of Jerusalem's Hebrew University, who directed the excavation where it was uncovered.Hezekiah ruled around 700 B.C. and was described in the Bible as a daring monarch — "There was no one like him among all the kings of Judah, either before him or after him" (II Kings 18:5) — who was dedicated to eliminating idoltary in his kingdom."This is the first time that a seal impression of an Israelite or Judean king has ever come to light in a scientific archaeological excavation," Mazar said.The clay imprint, known as a bulla, was found at a dig at the foot of the southern part of the wall that surrounds Jerusalem's Old City, an area rich in relics from the period of the first of two ancient Jewish temples.It had been buried in a refuse dump dated to the time of Hezekiah and was probably tossed from an adjacent royal building, Mazar said.

Tel Aviv's annual Gay Pride Festival took place this weekend, drawing thousands of participants and supporters from around the world. The event, known for its vibrant atmosphere and celebration of diversity, featured a parade through the city streets, live music performances, and speeches from LGBTQ activists.This year's festival comes as one of many Pride marches happening around the world this weekend, as the LGBTQ community and its allies continue to push for equal rights and recognition. Tel Aviv, often referred to as the "gay capital of the Middle East," has a long history of hosting one of the largest and most colorful Pride celebrations in the region.The festival was not without its challenges, however, as a small group of protesters gathered on the sidelines to voice their opposition to the event. Despite this, the overwhelming support from the majority of attendees and the city's residents helped ensure a safe and inclusive environment for all participants.Overall, the Tel Aviv Gay Pride Festival was a resounding success, showcasing the city's commitment to equality and acceptance for all. As Pride month comes to a close, events like these serve as a reminder of the progress made in the fight for LGBTQ rights, while also highlighting the work that still needs to be done.

Pharmaceutical company GlaxoSmithKline (GSK) has faced another setback in the development of a cancer vaccine, as the latest round of testing has yielded disappointing results. This is not the first time the vaccine has failed to meet expectations, but GSK remains committed to continuing the testing process in the hopes of finding a successful formula.The cancer vaccine in question targets a specific type of cancer that has been difficult to treat with traditional methods. Despite the setbacks, GSK is determined to find a solution that could potentially save countless lives."We are disappointed by the results of the latest testing, but we are not giving up," said a spokesperson for GSK. "Cancer research is a complex and challenging field, but we remain dedicated to finding new and innovative ways to combat this disease."The next steps for GSK involve analyzing the data from the failed tests to determine what changes need to be made to the vaccine formula. The company is also exploring potential collaborations with other research institutions to leverage their expertise and resources.In the meantime, cancer patients and their families are eagerly awaiting a breakthrough in the development of effective cancer vaccines. While the road ahead may be difficult, GSK is committed to doing everything in their power to make this a reality.

The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) government has announced that it will not engage in talks with rebel groups in the country. This decision comes after recent clashes between government forces and various rebel factions in the eastern part of the DRC.According to government officials, they believe that negotiating with rebels would only legitimize their actions and potentially lead to further violence. Instead, the government is committed to using military force to eliminate any threats to the country's stability and security.The DRC has been plagued by armed conflict for decades, with numerous rebel groups vying for control of the country's rich natural resources. The government's hardline stance on refusing to negotiate with rebels is seen as a signal of their determination to bring an end to the violence and instability in the region.Human rights groups have expressed concerns about the government's approach, warning that a military crackdown could lead to further civilian casualties and displacement. However, the government remains steadfast in its decision to prioritize security over dialogue with rebel groups.As the situation in the DRC continues to escalate, the international community is closely monitoring developments and urging all parties to prioritize peace and stability in the region. The government's refusal to engage in talks with rebels is likely to have far-reaching consequences for the ongoing conflict in the country.

Meteorologists have issued a warning that this winter may be colder than normal, according to the latest predictions in the Almanac. The publication, known for its accurate long-range weather forecasts, suggests that frigid temperatures and above-average snowfall could be in store for many regions across the country.As the days grow shorter and the air becomes crisper, residents are urged to prepare for the possibility of a harsh winter ahead. This could mean stocking up on supplies, ensuring homes are properly insulated, and taking precautions to stay warm during the coldest months of the year.While it is impossible to predict the exact weather patterns with certainty, the Almanac's track record of accuracy in long-range forecasting has many people taking notice. It is always better to be safe than sorry when it comes to preparing for extreme weather conditions.As the winter months approach, it is important to stay informed about any updates or changes in the forecast. Being proactive and taking necessary precautions can help ensure the safety and well-being of individuals and their families during the colder than normal winter that may lie ahead.

Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman concluded his charm offensive tour, which included visits to several countries in the region. The tour aimed to strengthen ties and improve relations with neighboring countries. During his visits, the Crown Prince held meetings with various leaders and discussed mutual interests and cooperation. The tour is seen as part of Saudi Arabia's efforts to enhance its diplomatic efforts and play a more active role in the region. Analysts suggest that the charm offensive tour is a strategic move by the Crown Prince to solidify alliances and counter regional challenges. It remains to be seen how the outcomes of the tour will impact Saudi Arabia's position in the Middle East.

Iraq's cabinet has endorsed a draft law crucial to regulating how the country's vast oil reserves will be shared by its ethnic and sectarian groups.Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki on Monday hailed the law as key to unifying the Iraqi people. The measure aims to deliver the country's oil revenues equitably to all of Iraq's 18 provinces.The United States has been pressing for an oil law to help settle a potentially explosive dispute among Iraq's different communities. Iraq relies on oil exports for nearly all of its revenue, and most of the oil wealth is in the mainly Shi'ite south and the mainly Kurdish north.The White House hailed the Iraqi cabinet's approval of the draft as "good news."

French President Emmanuel Macron toured the damaged Arc de Triomphe on Sunday after Paris was hit by a wave of violent riots over fuel tax hikes and economic inequality. The iconic monument, a symbol of French national identity, was defaced with graffiti and vandalized during the protests.Macron visited the site to assess the damage and show his support for the cleanup efforts. He condemned the violence that erupted during the demonstrations and vowed to take action to address the grievances of the protesters.The riots, which have been ongoing for several weeks, have left a trail of destruction in Paris and other cities across France. Thousands of protesters have clashed with police, set cars on fire, and vandalized buildings in a show of anger against the government's policies.The unrest has sparked a national debate about the social and economic inequalities that have fueled the protests. Macron's government has faced criticism for being out of touch with the concerns of ordinary citizens and failing to address their needs.As Macron tours the damaged Arc de Triomphe, he is faced with the challenge of finding a way to calm the unrest and restore peace to the streets of Paris. The future of his presidency may depend on his ability to address the underlying causes of the protests and find a solution that satisfies the demands of the demonstrators.

Colombian police Sunday said they had seized eight tons of cocaine from the country’s most active criminal syndicate, the Usuga clan.In a statement, police said they found the drugs hidden on a banana plantation near the border with Panama, in Colombia’s northwest Uraba region."The biggest seizure of drugs in history. A hit against criminals," Colombian President Juan Manuel Santos said on Twitter.Police said they arrested three people in the operation, but another three escaped. Defense Minister Luis Carlos Villegas said nearly 1.5 tons of the cocaine was packaged and “ready to go out.”Each year, police said, the Usuga clan sends tons of cocaine into the United States. U.S. authorities have offered a $5 million reward for the capture of the gang’s leader.Colombia is the leading coca growing country in the world, and produces 442 tons of cocaine from the crop each year, according to the United Nations.

Following last year's hotly contested elections in Iran, the Internet, social networking sites, blogs and cellphones were an empowering tool in the hand of opposition forces and everyday citizens. One year later, the same technologies are not only being used to silence the Iranian government's critics and dissent, they are helping to create an environment that analysts and bloggers say is more dangerous and severe. Iranian journalist and blogger Omid Memarian says the pictures and videos that were posted online last year showing protesters taking to the streets and authorities crushing dissent gave the world a rare glimpse of Iran's leadership."I think that the most important thing that has happened after the Iranian elections is that the image that we have of the Islamic Republic of Iran, the image that we have from Iranian authorities, we have seen how brutal they can be, and how they can be harsh against their critics," said Omid Memarian.Listen to Bill Ide's complete audio report:The international media group Reporters Without Borders says during the past year, at least 170 journalists and bloggers have been arrested in Iran, and 22 have been sentenced to jail terms totaling more than 135 years.Memarian says it has become riskier than ever in Iran to write and criticize the government. "They have filtered the Internet," he said. "They have even sentenced those who have sent text messages to their friends. So, they have tried to dominate the narrative and block the flow of information and, to some extent, they have been successful."The deputy director of programs at the U.S.-based rights group Freedom House, Daniel Calingaert, says while videos and photographs of events on the streets of Iran are still getting on the World Wide Web, activists are being more careful about how they use the Internet."If you look back a year, activists and ordinary citizens were very open about what they would say on the Internet and how they would exchange information," said Daniel Calingaert.

The U.S. Justice Department has asked the Supreme Court to uphold the Obama administration's historic overhaul of the nation's health care system.The Justice Department on Wednesday filed an appeal asking the nation's top court to review an earlier decision by a federal court, which ruled the legislation exceeded its authority by requiring most Americans to pay for health insurance or face a penalty. The court ruling, issued in August, upheld the rest of the health care reforms as legal.Earlier Wednesday, 26 U.S. states and an organization representing small businesses filed appeals to the same ruling, asking Supreme Court justices to declare the entire law unconstitutional.Three federal courts have so far issued conflicting decisions on the health care legislation, with one upholding it, another partially striking it down, and a third declining to issue an immediate ruling.

The World Health Organization declared swine flu as the first international pandemic of the century on Thursday and urged nations to step up the fight against the virus. The World Health Organization's Director-General Margaret Chan announced that the organization decided to raise its alert against the swine influenza A-H1N1 virus to Phase 6 - the highest level - following a meeting of health experts in Geneva. This is the first time a pandemic has been declared since 1968.The announcement, Chan said, means that countries must keep a close watch on the virus. She called the situation a moderate pandemic."Calling a pandemic is also a signal to the international community," said Chan. "This is a time when the world's countries - rich or poor, big or small - must come together in the name of global solidarity to make sure that no countries because of poor resources, no country's people should be left behind without help."The World Health Organization says that, so far, 74 countries have reported nearly 30,000 cases of flu, with 144 deaths.The WHO is urging drug makers to speed up the manufacture of a swine flu vaccine.

Israeli police say a Jerusalem holy site at the heart of recent tensions between Israelis and Palestinians was quiet on Friday, after Muslims of all ages were allowed to worship there.Israel had restricted worship at the Temple Mount, known to Muslims as the al-Aqsa Mosque, to people 35 and older, fearing youths would stir up violence. Thousands of people attended prayers on Friday at the al-Aqsa compound in Jerusalem's Holy City without incident.But clashes between Israeli troops and Palestinian protesters continued in the West Bank.At a checkpoint near the city of Ramallah, Palestinians burned tires and threw stones at security forces who responded by firing riot dispersal weapons into the air. In Hebron, Hamas supporters hurled stones at Israeli soldiers who fired tear gas to disperse the crowd.There were no reports of casualties.The decision to lift the age restrictions was announced after U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry held talks Thursday with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas and Jordanian King Abdullah.Kerry said all sides assured him they are committed to restoring calm and taking what he called "real steps" instead of just rhetoric.

The Pakistani army says its troops have recaptured two militant-held towns in northwestern Swat Valley and destroyed the headquarters of a radical pro-Taliban cleric.Military officials say troops regained control of the towns of Khawazakhela and Matta in the past two days and drove out supporters of militant leader Maulana Fazlullah. The army also says it has secured his complex near the town of Mingora. Its say four suspected militants have been apprehended.The army said last week it had shut down the FM radio station Fazlullah used to call for strict Islamic law and a holy war against the government.Hundreds of troops have been fighting Fazlullah's pro-Taliban insurgents who have seized control of a series of small towns and villages in Swat, Pakistan's top tourist destination in North West Frontier Province.The army says it has killed nearly 250 militants and captured scores of militants since it began its military operation to retake territory last month.

An Iranian opposition leader’s website is raising the alarm about the health of a female member of Iran’s Gonabadi dervish minority who was arrested in Tehran in a violent clash with police in February.The Saham News site of Mehdi Karroubi, who has been under house arrest for years, published the report about the detained woman, Shokoufeh Yadollahi, on Monday. It said she is in a poor physical condition after having suffered fractures to her ribs and skull in the February 20 clash.The report also said Iranian security personnel have prevented Yadollahi from obtaining appropriate medical treatment during her detention at Qarchak prison, east of Tehran.London-based human rights group Amnesty International issued a statement on March 29 appealing for global support for Yadollahi and 10 other Gonabadi dervish women whom it said have been ill-treated and arbitrarily detained since the Tehran incident. It called for the 11 women to be released immediately.Iranian policemen mourn during a funeral ceremony for their three colleagues who were killed on Monday at a protest by followers of a Sufi Islam leader, in Tehran, Iran, Thursday, Feb. 22, 2018.

YAOUNDE - The U.N. special envoy for Central Africa visited Cameroon this week to seek an end to the months-long stalemate on the strike and unrest in English-speaking parts of the country.Francois Lonseny Fall, special representative of the U.N. secretary-general and head of the U.N. Regional Office for Central Africa, called on the government of Cameroon to take two steps to facilitate the reopening of dialogue with strikers in the English-speaking regions.The United Nations cannot be indifferent when there are issues that could threaten the peace and stability of a sovereign state, he said, recommending that all detainees be liberated. He said the U.N. believes that if detainees are freed, peace will quickly return to Cameroon.He also reiterated the U.N.'s call that the internet be reinstated in the northwest and the southwest. He called the internet blackout a violation of the freedom to access information.Government spokesperson Issa Tchiroma declined VOA's request for comment on the special envoy's Wednesday statement, but said President Paul Biya is aware of the situation.The government has announced some reforms to address the strikers' grievances, but talks broke down earlier this year after the government refused to release detainees. Dozens of people have been arrested in connection with the strike, including three activist leaders currently on trial for charges related to violent unrest in December. If convicted, those three leaders could face death sentences. Schools remain closed in the affected areas; the internet was cut in January.Tensions began in November when English-speaking lawyers and teachers in the two regions refused to work, demanding reforms.

Despite widespread protests and criticism, Uganda's social media tax is set to stay in place. The controversial tax was introduced last year, requiring social media users to pay a daily fee in order to access platforms such as Facebook, Twitter, and WhatsApp.The tax has faced backlash from citizens and human rights groups who argue that it infringes on freedom of expression and limits access to information. Critics also argue that the tax disproportionately impacts low-income earners who rely on social media for communication and networking.However, the Ugandan government has defended the tax, stating that it is necessary for raising revenue and curbing online "gossip" and "misinformation". Officials have also claimed that the tax helps to regulate online activity and prevent the spread of fake news.Despite the ongoing protests and calls for the tax to be repealed, it appears that the Ugandan government is standing firm on its decision to keep the social media tax in place. This decision has sparked further outrage among citizens and activists who are concerned about the implications of limiting access to social media in the country.

Leaders from various countries gathered today in Geneva for the Nuclear Summit, where they announced that they have successfully met the goals set out during the previous summit. The main objective of the summit was to reduce the global nuclear stockpile and increase cooperation on nuclear non-proliferation efforts.During the summit, participating countries shared their progress on reducing their nuclear arsenals and discussed ways to prevent the spread of nuclear weapons to other nations. The leaders also reaffirmed their commitment to upholding the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty and emphasized the importance of maintaining a safe and secure nuclear environment.In a joint statement, the leaders expressed their satisfaction with the progress made so far and pledged to continue working together to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons. They also called on other countries to join them in their efforts to strengthen global nuclear security.The success of the Nuclear Summit is seen as a positive step towards achieving a more peaceful and secure world, free from the threat of nuclear conflict. The leaders expressed optimism about the future and their determination to build on the progress made at the summit.

The Los Angeles Dodgers made a stunning performance in their game against the Cincinnati Reds, scoring an impressive 10 runs in the first inning. This early offensive burst set the tone for the rest of the game, giving the Dodgers a commanding lead right from the start.The Dodgers' bats were on fire as they capitalized on every opportunity, sending runners across the plate with ease. The Reds' pitching struggled to contain the relentless onslaught from the Dodgers' lineup, leading to a lopsided score in the first inning.This dominant display of offense from the Dodgers showcases their potent lineup and ability to score runs in bunches. The Reds will have to regroup and make adjustments to prevent a similar outcome in future matchups.Overall, the Dodgers' explosive start to the game highlights their offensive prowess and sets a high standard for the rest of the season. Fans can expect more exciting performances from this talented team as they continue their pursuit of a championship title.

Public workers in Zimbabwe are eagerly anticipating a possible raise in their salaries this month. Many of these workers, who are among the lowest-paid in the country, have been struggling to make ends meet as inflation continues to soar.The Zimbabwean government has been under pressure to address the issue of low wages for public workers, especially in light of the economic challenges facing the country. The cost of living has been steadily increasing, making it difficult for many workers to afford basic necessities.If the government does approve a salary increase for public workers, it would come as a relief to many who have been struggling to cope with rising prices and a weak economy.

YAOUNDE, CAMEROON - The queue of residents of Yaounde water for water begins forming outside the presidential palace at about 4 a.m., before the sun rises on this capital city of 2.4 million people. As the day goes by, hundreds of people from across the city come with their buckets and jerry cans as the line stretches into the distance until long after dark, even unto midnight. That has been the daily routine for some time. Carrefour du Palais, the main junctions outside the presidency, has become the unintended face of a severe water scarcity that’s been affecting the Cameroon capital for more than three months. It’s one of the few places in Yaounde where water still runs uninterrupted. Cameroon is crisscrossed by many rivers that run from the country’s mountainous north to the south. It is believed to have one of the world’s largest reserves of fresh water. But for more than three months, millions in the Cameroon capital have gone without it.Water shortage is a national problem Taps have run dry in most parts of the city. In the best scenario, water comes for only a few hours, usually at night. Those who cannot come to Carrefour du Palais - or to other regularly supplied places like military barracks - have turned to wells, streams and springs.

The United States announced a new policy towards Sudan, which aims to offer incentives and apply pressure in order to encourage progress in the country. The US government stated that it will provide economic incentives and support for Sudan if it continues its path towards democracy and human rights improvements.However, the policy also includes the threat of sanctions and other measures if Sudan fails to make necessary changes. The US emphasized the importance of Sudanese authorities taking concrete steps towards ending human rights abuses, improving governance, and promoting peace and stability in the region.This new approach marks a shift from previous policies towards Sudan, which had been criticized for not effectively addressing the ongoing challenges in the country. The US government hopes that this combination of incentives and pressure will push Sudan towards positive change and ultimately benefit the Sudanese people.

As China’s population continues to age rapidly, there is a growing need for more beds and caregivers for the elderly. With a large number of elderly citizens living alone or with limited support, the demand for eldercare services is on the rise.According to a recent report, there is a shortage of both beds in nursing homes and trained caregivers to provide assistance to the elderly population. Many families are struggling to find suitable care for their aging loved ones, leading to concerns about the quality of life for the elderly in China.The government has recognized the need to address this issue and has started to implement policies aimed at increasing the number of beds in nursing homes and improving the training of caregivers. However, progress has been slow and there is still much work to be done to meet the growing demand for eldercare services.As the population continues to age, it is crucial for China to prioritize the needs of the elderly and ensure that they have access to quality care and support. Without adequate beds and caregivers, many elderly citizens will be left vulnerable and without the assistance they need to live comfortably and safely in their later years.

ADJUMANI, UGANDA - It is estimated Uganda has one doctor for every 24,000 citizens. The nurse-to-population ratio is not much better, one for every 11,000 people, and much of that staff is concentrated in urban areas.Now add 1.2 million refugees.The massive influx of South Sudanese refugees into northern Uganda during the past year has strained the country’s already overburdened health care system. Health centers near refugee settlements in the country’s Adjumani district are overwhelmed, especially those providing maternity care.A young South Sudanese refugee girl shields her face from the scorching sun as she waits in line at Maaji Health Center, Adjumani district, northern Uganda, June 14, 2017. (H. Athumani/VOA)The United Nations says Uganda took in more refugees than any other part of the world in 2016 as civilians fled conflict and hunger in South Sudan, and the influx continues.Refugee Ester Ponne Charles arrived in northern Uganda eight months ago. She was pregnant and experienced complications during childbirth.“Like myself, I was operated. If you do not have money, you may lose your life and the child too. Because there they want money. Without money, even medicine, you buy even the gloves yourself, everything in the hospital. So those are the challenges we are facing. Those ones who cannot even afford any coin, so they will just end up losing their lives,” Ponne Charles said.A South Sudanese mother with her children wait in queue at a health center in Maaji settlement, Adjumani district, northern Uganda, June 14, 2017. (H.

South Korea President Kim Dae-jung expressed his anger and frustration today following a series of provocations by North Korea. The latest incident occurred yesterday when North Korean forces crossed the border into South Korean territory, prompting a brief skirmish between the two sides.President Kim condemned the actions of North Korea as a violation of the armistice agreement that has maintained peace on the Korean Peninsula for decades. He warned that South Korea would not tolerate any further provocations and would respond with force if necessary.The United Nations Security Council has called for an emergency meeting to address the escalating tensions between the two Koreas. The international community is urging both sides to exercise restraint and seek a diplomatic solution to the crisis.This latest provocation comes amid heightened tensions on the Korean Peninsula, with North Korea recently conducting missile tests and nuclear weapons tests. The situation remains fluid, and there are concerns that the conflict could escalate further if not properly managed.President Kim has called for calm and unity among South Koreans in the face of this aggression from North Korea. He has assured the public that the South Korean government is taking all necessary steps to ensure the safety and security of its citizens.The world is watching closely as the situation unfolds on the Korean Peninsula, with hopes for a peaceful resolution to the crisis.

The Democratic Republic of Congo has ordered the expulsion of four foreign activists from the country. The activists were accused of interfering in the country's internal affairs and inciting violence. The government did not provide specific details on the reasons for the expulsion, but stated that the activists had violated the country's laws.The activists, who were working on various human rights and environmental issues in the country, have denied the accusations and have criticized the government's decision. They claim that they were simply trying to bring attention to the human rights abuses and environmental degradation happening in the Congo.The expulsion order has raised concerns among the international community about the shrinking space for activism and freedom of speech in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Human rights organizations have called on the Congolese government to respect the rights of activists and allow them to carry out their work without fear of retaliation.The government has not provided any further information on when the activists are expected to leave the country. It remains to be seen how this latest development will impact the work of activists and civil society organizations in the Congo.

Indonesia is ranked the third richest country in the world in terms of bio-diversity and it is home to dozens of endangered species. Aside from environmental hazards, illegal trade is threatening the survival of many of the animals. Efforts to stop the illegal trade in wildlife are being hampered by government and military officials who keep endangered species at home as status symbols.The office of the Forestry Police on the outskirts of the Indonesian capital Jakarta houses not alleged criminals, but victims of the country's illegal wildlife trade.It is here that dozens of crocodiles, turtles, gibbons, orangutans, parrots, cockatoos, eagles and other animals that have been confiscated by authorities are kept until they can be reintegrated into the wild.Stretching over thousands of kilometers, the Indonesian archipelago is rich with wildlife. Almost 300,000 different animal species make their homes here, 17 percent of all the world's animals. There are 36 species of primates alone.But conservationists say Indonesia is also the largest exporter of wildlife in the world. A Jakarta-based organization, Animal Conservation for Life, says 90 percent of animals sold in world markets have been poached from the wild. They do not come from captive breeding programs, as many traders claim.People flood to Jakarta's animal markets because it is considered prestigious to own an exotic bird or endangered animal, says Hardi Bakiantoro from Animal Conservation for Life."The buyers of orangutans and turtles and other protected animals usually they are the rich men who understand law and are educated," he said. "They really know the animal is protected. They know it trespasses the law. But usually they have something like prestige."One of Indonesia's best-known animals is the orangutan, whose name in Indonesian means "forest person."

Chinese Premier Wen Jiabao arrived in North Korea today for a two-day visit aimed at strengthening ties between the two countries. This visit comes at a time of heightened tensions on the Korean peninsula, with North Korea's recent missile tests drawing international condemnation.During his visit, Premier Wen is expected to meet with North Korean leader Kim Jong Un to discuss bilateral cooperation and regional issues. This visit is seen as a way for China to reaffirm its support for North Korea and to play a role in easing tensions in the region.Premier Wen's visit also comes at a crucial time for North Korea, as the country faces increasing pressure from the international community to abandon its nuclear weapons program.

The Iraqi parliament held another session Sunday with Prime Minister designate Nouri al-Maliki again failing to present his new cabinet.Squabbling between Shi'ite, Sunni, and Kurdish politicians over who should control the powerful oil, defense, and interior ministries is holding up formation of a new government.A Shi'ite political party and a Sunni Arab bloc both threatened to drop out of any future government if they are not satisfied with the cabinet list.Mr. al-Maliki has just one more week to present his cabinet before President Jalal Talabani has the right to nominate another prime minister.The United States has said a unified Iraqi government is essential towards ending sectarian violence and insurgent attacks, which killed at least 33 people across Iraq Sunday.The U.S. military says suicide car bombs killed 28 people near Baghdad, including two American soldiers near a U.S. military base.A car bomb also killed two Iraqis in Mosul and a roadside bomb in Udaim killed three bodyguards assigned to Iraqi Foreign Minister Hoshyar Zebari.Iraqi authorities also found the bodies of five blindfolded men with gunshot wounds near Karbala.And the British Defense Ministry announced Sunday that two British soldiers were killed late Saturday in a roadside bomb in the southern city of Basra.Some information for this report was provided by AFP, AP and Reuters.

A United Nations expert has called on North Korea to allow "unimpeded access" to aid organizations in order to help combat the spread of the virus in the country. The expert highlighted the importance of providing assistance to North Korea to prevent a potential humanitarian crisis. It is crucial for the government of North Korea to cooperate with international organizations and allow them access to the country to provide much-needed support in the fight against the virus. The UN expert emphasized the need for transparency and cooperation in order to effectively combat the pandemic in North Korea.

Britain has taken a significant step in the fight against global corruption by passing a new law that allows authorities to seize unexplained wealth. The Unexplained Wealth Order (UWO) targets individuals who are unable to account for how they acquired their assets, making it easier for law enforcement to confiscate ill-gotten gains.This new legislation comes as part of the UK government's efforts to crack down on money laundering and corruption, which have long been pervasive issues in the country. By enabling authorities to seize assets without having to first secure a criminal conviction, the UWO aims to disrupt the flow of illicit funds and hold individuals accountable for their actions.The law has already been used in several high-profile cases, including the seizure of luxury properties and assets belonging to politically exposed persons and individuals with suspected ties to organized crime. The implementation of UWOs sends a strong message that the UK is committed to rooting out corruption and protecting its financial system from abuse.Critics argue that the law could infringe on individuals' rights and lead to unfair targeting of innocent parties, but supporters maintain that it is a necessary tool in the fight against financial crime. With the global economy becoming increasingly interconnected, the UK's efforts to combat corruption are not only important for domestic stability but also for promoting transparency and integrity on a global scale.As other countries grapple with similar challenges, the implementation of UWOs in Britain serves as a potential model for how to effectively combat corruption and hold perpetrators accountable for their actions. By targeting unexplained wealth, the UK is sending a clear message that financial crimes will not be tolerated, and that those who seek to exploit the system for personal gain will face consequences.

In a night filled with stellar performances and emotional acceptance speeches, fan favorites took home top honors at the Country Music Association (CMA) Awards on Wednesday. One of the big winners of the evening was country superstar Luke Combs, who walked away with the coveted Entertainer of the Year award. Combs, known for hits like "Beer Never Broke My Heart" and "Beautiful Crazy," thanked his fans for their continued support in his acceptance speech.Another fan favorite, Maren Morris, also had a big night, winning Female Vocalist of the Year. Morris, who has been dominating the charts with hits like "The Bones" and "Girl," expressed her gratitude to her fans and fellow artists for their support.Other winners of the evening included Dan + Shay, who won Vocal Duo of the Year, and Carly Pearce, who took home the award for New Artist of the Year.The CMA Awards also featured show-stopping performances from some of the biggest names in country music, including Miranda Lambert, Keith Urban, and Eric Church.Overall, it was a night to remember for fans and artists alike, as fan favorites took center stage and celebrated their well-deserved wins at the CMA Awards.

TAIPEI, TAIWAN - Deadly violence has resurfaced in parts of the long-restive southern Philippines despite an autonomy-sharing deal with local Muslim groups, and authorities are eyeing terrorism by political factions excluded from the deal.In late August and early September, two explosions hit the southern Philippine province of Sultan Kudarat. One killed two people and injured 30, domestic media reports say. The other killed three. In mid-September, an “improvised explosive device” injured seven people when it went off in General Santos City. Troops discovered other rebels mobilizing Sept. 21 for what they feared was an eventual attack.?Southern cities have gone on high alertThe attacks follow decades of violence on the island of Mindanao, where about 120,000 have died because of fighting by Muslim rebels who want more resource control in the majority Christian country.In August, the government approved a landmark law that gives Muslims in one tract of Mindanao more control. But not all 20 rebel groups were included.Rebels who feel slighted have picked up the fight again, analysts say. They say some are inspired by Islamic State, a Middle Eastern terrorist group that has tried for the past few years to expand in Southeast Asia.“

A number of states and cities in the United States have declared their commitment to the Paris Agreement, despite the federal government's decision to withdraw from the global climate accord. These states and cities have joined together in the "We Are Still In" coalition to show their support for the goals of the agreement.The Paris Agreement, which was signed by nearly 200 countries in 2015, aims to combat climate change by reducing greenhouse gas emissions and limiting global temperature rise. By withdrawing from the agreement, the Trump administration has faced backlash from environmental advocates and world leaders.However, states like California, New York, and Washington, as well as cities like Los Angeles, New York City, and Chicago, have taken matters into their own hands by committing to upholding the goals of the Paris Agreement. These states and cities have implemented their own policies and initiatives to reduce carbon emissions and promote renewable energy.Governors, mayors, and other officials in these states and cities have emphasized the importance of addressing climate change and protecting the environment for future generations. They have pledged to continue working towards a sustainable future, despite the lack of federal support.The "We Are Still In" coalition represents a growing movement of states and cities taking action on climate change at the local level. By working together and sharing resources, these states and cities are sending a powerful message that they are committed to fighting climate change, even if the federal government is not.

The US Navy has sailed two ships through the Taiwan Strait, a move that is likely to escalate tensions between the United States and China. The guided-missile destroyer USS Curtis Wilbur and the guided-missile cruiser USS Antietam conducted a "routine" transit through the strait on Monday, according to a statement from the US Navy's Pacific Fleet.The Taiwan Strait is a strategically important waterway that separates Taiwan from mainland China. The transit of US warships through the strait is seen as a show of support for Taiwan, which China considers a breakaway province.China has repeatedly criticized the US for sending warships through the Taiwan Strait, calling it a violation of its sovereignty. The Chinese government has also accused the US of trying to provoke Beijing by conducting such transits.The latest transit comes amid heightened tensions between the US and China over a range of issues, including trade, security, and human rights.

The United States and North Korea have announced that they will be resuming political talks in the coming weeks. The talks will mark the first high-level discussion between the two countries in several months.The decision to resume talks comes after a period of heightened tensions between the two nations, with North Korea recently conducting several missile tests. However, both sides have expressed a willingness to engage in dialogue in order to address various issues of concern.The talks are expected to focus on denuclearization efforts on the Korean Peninsula, as well as other issues related to security and stability in the region. The United States has stated that it remains committed to the goal of achieving a denuclearized North Korea through peaceful negotiations.It is hoped that the resumption of political talks will help to de-escalate tensions and pave the way for a more constructive relationship between the United States and North Korea. Both sides have indicated a desire to make progress in their discussions and work towards a peaceful resolution of the issues at hand.

MOSCOW - Russia's foreign minister says after talks with his Egyptian counterpart that the two countries are completing preparations for restoring direct flights, which were halted by Moscow after the downing of a Russian passenger jet.The Russian plane crashed in Sinai on Oct. 31, killing all 224 people on board. Moscow said it was brought down by an explosive device, and a local branch of the extremist Islamic State group has claimed responsibility for planting it. The crash and Russia's decision to cut the air link to Egypt has dealt a severe blow to Egypt's vital tourism sector. Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov hailed the Egyptian authorities for accepting Russian experts' advice on boosting airport security and said that direct flights will resume soon.

After a month-long hiatus, the Colombian government and the National Liberation Army (ELN) rebels have resumed peace talks in a bid to end more than five decades of conflict.The negotiations were put on hold in January after a series of deadly attacks by the ELN, including a car bombing in the capital city of Bogota that left more than 20 people dead.However, both sides have expressed a commitment to finding a peaceful resolution and have returned to the negotiating table in Ecuador. The talks are focused on issues such as social and economic development, political participation, and the demobilization of ELN fighters.The conflict between the Colombian government and various rebel groups has resulted in thousands of deaths and displaced millions of people since it began in the 1960s. The resumption of peace talks brings hope for a lasting peace in the region.Stay tuned for updates on the progress of the negotiations between Colombia and the ELN rebels.

HUALIEN CITY, TAIWAN - Most survivors of Tuesday night’s strong earthquake in Taiwan escaped because they were on high floors that did not collapse, allowing rescue workers to find them.As of Friday morning, 830 people had left the four collapsed or damaged buildings in the Pacific coast city of Hualien. Many broke windows or climbed out via balconies to escape the two worst-hit buildings, a 10-story hotel and a 12-story commercial-residential complex. Firefighters waited below with ladders.The quake killed 10 people, including hotel guests and workers who were on lower levels of the buildings when the quake struck at 11:50 p.m. Taiwan’s Central Weather Bureau recorded the quake as a magnitude 6 at its epicenter off the coast, and in Hualien it was felt with an intensity of 7, enough to cause widespread damage.WATCH: Rescuers Work to Find Survivors After Taiwan EarthquakeSorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline.Angle too steep to climb?“A sound, it made a sound like a tract of wooden floor boards were ripping apart, for one minute,” said Chang Te-chuan, 77, who escaped the commercial-residential complex’s fourth floor. The building was sliding downward at a steep angle, pushing two lower floors into the ground.“In my room, all my furniture had been moved into one place, all of it, it was in one place then moved to another, the whole room was tilted at about 45 degrees, and at an angle of 45 degrees I almost couldn’t move from one place to another,” Chang said. “You’d crawl and find yourself sliding. The angle was just too big.”

China has recently announced a significant policy change, relaxing restrictions on foreign banks investing in western provinces. This decision is seen as a move to boost economic development in these less developed regions.Under the new rules, foreign banks will now have more opportunities to establish branches and invest in industries such as infrastructure, technology, and agriculture in western provinces like Sichuan, Shaanxi, and Guizhou. This is a departure from previous regulations that limited foreign investment mainly to the more developed coastal regions.The Chinese government hopes that by opening up these regions to more foreign investment, they will be able to spur economic growth, create jobs, and improve infrastructure in the western provinces. This move is also seen as a way to bridge the economic gap between China's eastern and western regions.Foreign banks have welcomed this policy change, seeing it as an opportunity to expand their presence in China and tap into new markets. This relaxation of restrictions is seen as a positive step towards further opening up China's economy to foreign investment.Overall, this move signals China's commitment to economic reform and development in its western provinces, and could potentially lead to increased foreign investment and economic growth in the region.

KUALA LUMPUR - Former Malaysian Prime Minister Najib Razak was charged Wednesday with tampering with the final audit report into a defunct state investment fund, adding to a long list of corruption allegations against him since his ouster in May elections.Najib was charged along with Arul Kanda Kandasamy, the former head of the 1MDB fund, which is being investigated in the U.S. and other countries for alleged cross-border embezzlement and money laundering.Najib pleaded not guilty to abusing power to order the modification of the report in February 2016 before it was presented to the Public Accounts Committee, in order to protect himself from disciplinary and legal action. Kandasamy, who was detained overnight by anti-graft officials, pleaded not guilty to abetting Najib.Pro-democracy group Bersih stage 1MDB protest, calling for Malaysian Prime Minister Najib Abdul Razak to resign, in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, Nov. 19, 2016.?The charges came after the auditor-general revealed last month that some details had been removed from the 1MDB report. Kandasamy led 1MDB from 2015 until he was terminated in June. The two men were released on bail, and face up to 20 years in prison if found guilty.Najib set up 1MDB when he took power in 2009 to promote economic development, but the fund amassed billions in debts.

Zimbabwe's President Robert Mugabe has been in the news once again for his controversial remarks towards US Africa Envoy Johnny Carson. In a recent speech, Mugabe called Carson a "stooge and an idiot" for criticizing the country's handling of the economy and political issues.Carson had previously expressed concerns about the human rights violations and lack of democratic reforms in Zimbabwe. Mugabe, who has been in power for over three decades, has not taken kindly to these criticisms and has responded with personal attacks towards Carson.The diplomatic spat comes at a time when Zimbabwe is facing a deep economic crisis, with high unemployment rates and widespread poverty. The country's political landscape is also marred by allegations of corruption and human rights abuses.Many are calling for a more constructive dialogue between the Zimbabwean government and the international community to address these pressing issues. However, Mugabe's latest outburst towards Carson does not bode well for any potential resolution to the ongoing challenges facing the country.

If we ever hear from aliens, it likely won’t be for at least another 1,500 years, a new study suggests.Researchers at Cornell University say they arrived at that number by combining the Fermi Paradox with the Mediocrity Principle, which they described at the June 16 meeting of the American Astronomical Society in San Diego.The Fermi Paradox states that while there are likely billions of Earth-like planets in our galaxy, no extraterrestrial has yet to contact us. The Mediocrity Principle, which was coined in the 16th century by Copernicus, states that Earth's physical attributes are not unique, therefore it may be some time before any alien life forms are able to contact us.“We haven’t heard from aliens yet, as space is a big place – but that doesn’t mean no one is out there,” said Cornell student Evan Solomonides, a co-author of the paper. “It’s possible to hear any time at all, but it becomes likely we will have heard around 1,500 years from now. Until then, it is possible that we appear to be alone – even if we are not; but, if we stop listening or looking, we may miss the signals. So we should keep looking.”Television and radio signals from Earth have only been traveling for about 80 years, the researchers said, adding that those transmissions have only reached about 8,531 stars and 3,555 potential Earth-like planets. The Milky Way galaxy has around 200 billion stars.Furthermore, if an alien civilization were to receive the transmissions, researchers said it would likely take it a long time to decipher them.“

Washington, DC - After a whirlwind trip to Europe, President Donald Trump has returned to the United States to face a busy agenda in Washington. The President attended the G-7 summit in Sicily, where leaders discussed various global issues, including climate change. Trump's decision on whether the US will remain committed to the Paris Climate Agreement is eagerly awaited.Meanwhile, the Russia probe led by special counsel Robert Mueller continues to make headlines as investigations into possible collusion between the Trump campaign and Russia intensify. Trump has repeatedly denied any wrongdoing and has called the probe a "witch hunt."As Trump settles back into his duties in the White House, all eyes will be on how he handles these pressing issues in the coming days. Stay tuned for further updates on these developing stories.

A founding member of Swaziland’s banned opposition People’s United Democratic Movement (PUDEMO) party said Swazis cherish the institution of the monarchy, but hate the way the country is governed. “First of all, we are Africans with a long background of monarchy," said opposition leader Mphandlana Shongwe. "But in this country, the problem is with the government per se. You will find that on the ground, the people still like King Mswati III. But, the problem is his government is not delivering to these people,” he said. The opposition leader's comments follow a Gallup public opinion survey that finds King Mswati III to be among Africa’s least popular leaders. Shongwe said King Mswati III has failed to manage the country’s dire economic situation. “As I speak, the country is in a very, very bad economic situation. That is why most of them are saying we must have multiple parties so that we can separate the institution of the monarchy and the government,” he said. Government spokesman Percy Simelane ridiculed the poll results. “It’s like me coming to you and saying seven of your girlfriends say they no longer love you. What assurance do you have that I am not telling you all this because I want your girlfriends for myself,” he said. Opposition leader Shongwe described Simelane as a government propaganda person hired to tell lies. “The reality on the ground is that everybody, as I speak, is aware of the institution of the monarchy’s interfering with the government. [That] is what the people do not like.

ISTANBUL - An Istanbul prosecutor died from his wounds after security forces stormed the office where members of a far-left Turkish group took him hostage on Tuesday, killing his two captors.The Revolutionary People's Liberation Party-Front (DHKP-C) had published a picture of the prosecutor with a gun to his head and said it would kill him unless its demands were met.Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said Mehmet Selim Kiraz had been shot three times in the head and twice in the body.

Iranian lawyers have joined the global backlash against the recent sentencing of human rights defender Nasrin Sotoudeh in Iran. Sotoudeh, a prominent lawyer who has been a vocal advocate for women's and children's rights, was recently sentenced to a total of 38 years in prison and 148 lashes.The sentencing of Sotoudeh has sparked outrage among the legal community in Iran, with many lawyers condemning the harsh punishment as a violation of human rights and an attack on the freedom of expression. In a statement released by the Iranian Bar Association, lawyers expressed their solidarity with Sotoudeh and called for her immediate release.The international community has also been quick to denounce the sentencing of Sotoudeh. Amnesty International has called the verdict a "shocking injustice" and urged Iranian authorities to overturn the decision. The European Union has also condemned the sentencing, describing it as a "flagrant violation of human rights".Sotoudeh has a long history of defending human rights in Iran, including representing women who have protested against the compulsory wearing of the hijab. She was previously imprisoned in 2010 for her work as a human rights lawyer.Despite facing immense pressure and persecution, Sotoudeh has remained steadfast in her commitment to defending the rights of others. The support of Iranian lawyers and the global community is a testament to the power of solidarity in the fight for justice and human rights.

A new art exhibition titled "American Stories" is set to open at the local gallery next week. The exhibit showcases various works of art that depict the rich history and diverse culture of America. From paintings and sculptures to photography and mixed media pieces, the artists have used their talents to tell the story of America in a unique and creative way.Curator Maria Sanchez explained that the goal of the exhibition is to highlight the different perspectives and experiences that make up the American narrative. "Each piece in the exhibit offers a glimpse into the lives of Americans from all walks of life," she said. "It's a celebration of our shared history and the unique stories that define us as a nation."One of the featured artists, Sarah Johnson, shared that her piece in the exhibit is a tribute to her family's immigrant roots. "I wanted to honor the journey that my ancestors took to come to America and the struggles they faced along the way," she said. "Through my art, I hope to spark conversations about immigration and the importance of embracing diversity."The "American Stories" exhibit is a must-see for art enthusiasts and history buffs alike. It offers a visual representation of the American experience, told through the eyes of talented artists. The gallery invites visitors to come and explore the stories that have shaped America into the diverse and vibrant country it is today.

In response to escalating violence in the southern region of the country, Kyrgyzstan has announced plans to deploy troops and volunteers to restore order and prevent further bloodshed.The decision comes after recent clashes between rival ethnic groups have resulted in multiple casualties and widespread destruction. The government has declared a state of emergency in the affected areas and is taking decisive action to bring an end to the violence.President Sooronbay Jeenbekov stated that the deployment of troops and volunteers is necessary to protect the lives and property of all citizens, and to ensure stability and peace in the region. He urged all parties to exercise restraint and work towards a peaceful resolution of the conflict.The international community has expressed concern over the situation in Kyrgyzstan and called for a peaceful and negotiated solution to the crisis. The United Nations and neighboring countries have offered support and assistance to help bring an end to the violence and prevent further escalation.As Kyrgyzstan prepares to send troops and volunteers to the south, the hopes of the nation rest on their ability to restore calm and prevent further loss of life. It remains to be seen whether this latest effort will be successful in quelling the violence and bringing about a lasting peace in the region.

Prosecutors at the United Nations war crimes tribunal say they have asked the court to refer the cases of three Yugoslav military officers to local courts in either Croatia or Serbia.The suspects have pleaded not guilty to charges of complicity in the deaths of about 250 people executed after Yugoslav forces captured the Croatian city of Vukovar in 1991.Prosecutors say soldiers under command of the officers took the victims, mostly Croats, from a Vukovar hospital to a remote location, executed them and then buried the bodies in mass graves.The tribunal has been moving to transfer what prosecutors consider lesser cases to war crimes courts in the Balkan countries.

The city of Strasbourg, France is in mourning as news has emerged that a fifth victim of the recent terror attack has succumbed to their injuries. The attack, which took place at a Christmas market in the city, has left the community reeling.Authorities have identified the latest victim as a 45-year-old man who was critically injured in the attack. This tragic news comes just days after the initial attack took place, which left four others dead and several more injured.The attacker, who has been identified as a 29-year-old man with a history of criminal behavior, was shot and killed by police following a two-day manhunt. The motives behind the attack are still unclear, but authorities are treating it as an act of terrorism.The city of Strasbourg has been on high alert since the attack, with increased security measures in place to ensure the safety of its residents and visitors. The community has come together to mourn the victims and show their support for those affected by this senseless act of violence.Our thoughts are with the victims and their families during this difficult time.

BANGKOK - Myanmar is to host leaders of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) and the East Asia Summit next week. The gathering includes the U.S. and Russian presidents, China’s paramount leader and the Indian prime minister, as well as Southeast Asian leaders. Expectations are limited for the gathering.The ASEAN way of consensus, conciliation and non-intervention has helped keep the peace in recent years in a historically volatile region.And that is the only effective way for ASEAN to function, contends Rodolfo Severino, a former secretary general who heads the ASEAN Studies Center at the Institute of Southeast Asian Studies in Singapore. “No, ASEAN is not about breakthroughs. ASEAN works on an incremental basis,” he told VOA during an interview via Skype.But that can be an obstacle to tackling critical issues, according to Professor Jason Briggs at the Bangkok campus of Webster University.“ASEAN, typically at summits and when the members are meeting, the outcomes are memorandums, declarations and joint statement - soft law type mechanisms instead of more hard law type binding instruments," he said.Cross-border trade, travel One of its most significant accomplishments is expected to be next year’s inauguration of the ASEAN Economic Community, facilitating cross-border trade and travel.

A recent report released by a human rights group has revealed shocking statistics regarding police shootings in Kenya since 2009. According to the report, hundreds of individuals have been killed as a result of police shootings over the past decade.The report highlights a concerning pattern of excessive use of force by Kenyan police officers, with many of the victims being unarmed civilians. The rights group has called for a thorough investigation into these incidents and for accountability to be held for those responsible.This revelation has sparked outrage and calls for police reform in Kenya. Many are demanding justice for the victims and for measures to be put in place to prevent future unnecessary use of force by law enforcement.The Kenyan government has yet to respond to the report, but pressure is mounting for action to be taken to address the issue of police shootings in the country. The rights group is urging authorities to take immediate steps to address this grave human rights concern.

Myanmar's early release of seven soldiers who were jailed for killing a group of Rohingya Muslims is drawing criticism from rights activists.Four officers and three enlisted service members were freed last November, Reuters first reported, after being sentenced in 2018 to 10 years in prison with hard labor for killing 10 Rohingya men and boys the year before.Prisons department head Myint Soe confirmed to reporters Tuesday in Yangon the soldiers were "no longer in custody," but he declined to provide more information.The soldiers served less time than two Reuters reporters who were jailed for exposing the massacre. Wa Lone and Kyaw Soe Oo were jailed last September after being sentenced to seven years on charges linked to their reporting. They served 500 days and then were released after receiving a presidential pardon.The massacre occurred during a military crackdown in 2017 that forced nearly three-quarters of a million Rohingya to flee to neighboring Bangladesh.United Nations investigators have said the violence warrants the prosecution of top military leaders for "genocide." The International Criminal Court has launched a preliminary investigation into the killings.Human Rights Watch Deputy Asia Director told the VOA Burmese Service the early release of the soldiers reveals that top Myanmar military leaders "don't really consider the Rohingya to be human, and were never committed to seeing anyone held accountable for their crimes in Rakhine state."

MEXICO CITY - Mexico says its top diplomat will meet with U.S. officials this week in Washington to discuss bilateral relations, which have been tense under the administration of President Donald Trump.A statement from the Foreign Relations Department says Foreign Secretary Luis Videgaray will be in the U.S. capital Tuesday through Thursday. His planned meetings include U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and Homeland Security Secretary John Kelly.Tuesday's statement said Videgaray will also meet with both Republican and Democratic senators, and attend a session of the Organization of American States. Mexico has objected to Trump's pledges to step up deportations, build a border wall and renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement.

After devastating mudslides hit the Darfur region of Sudan, aid has been delivered to assist those affected by the natural disaster. The mudslides, which were triggered by heavy rainfall, have claimed the lives of several individuals and caused extensive damage to homes and infrastructure.The United Nations and various humanitarian organizations have mobilized to provide emergency assistance to those in need. This aid includes food, clean water, medical supplies, and shelter for those who have been displaced.Efforts are also being made to assess the extent of the damage and to provide long-term support for rebuilding the affected communities. The international community has expressed solidarity with the people of Darfur during this challenging time and is committed to helping them recover and rebuild.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says he will give international sanctions against Iran a chance to work, and is not planning an attack on its nuclear facilities in the coming "days or weeks." Speaking to Israeli television on Thursday, Netanyahu said he prefers a peaceful solution of the dispute with Iran, but insisted that his country will not allow Iran to obtain a nuclear weapon.Netanyahu returned Thursday from talks in Washington, where he said Israel could not afford to wait much longer for diplomacy and sanctions to deter Iran's nuclear program.The Israeli leader has acknowledged what he calls "fundamental differences" between the U.S. and Israeli approach to Iran. Israel feels the Iranian nuclear threat more acutely than does Washington, said Netanyahu. "The American timetable in regards to preventing Iran becoming nuclear is not the same as the Israeli timetable," he said. "The Israeli timetable is of course under a different schedule. I would be happy if the international effort succeeded, if Iran voluntarily decided to disarm its nuclear plan."President Barack Obama has urged Netanyahu to give diplomacy and sanctions more time, but also has reiterated the U.S. position that all options are on the table to stop Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon. U.S. Senator John McCain told Alhurra TV on Thursday that sanctions are failing."The sanctions that have been imposed on Iran, in the view of every expert, have not changed their course toward obtaining nuclear weapons," he said. McCain was Obama's Republican Party opponent in the 2008 presidential election.

TUNIS - In post-revolution Tunisia, some fear that free expression is again under threat after violent protests this month against an Internet video mocking the Prophet Muhammad shocked many in the North African country. Tunisians worry the violence underscores growing threats to a cornerstone of the country's 2011 revolution. Artist Nadia Jelassi got into trouble over three veiled women…or at least her depiction of them - busts covered in fabric and newspaper and surrounded by stones. One of them is still missing, after protesters attacked an art exhibition outside Tunis on grounds it offended Islam. Jelassi wanted viewers to interpret her art as they liked. But she says the most obvious interpretation is of women being stoned. Religious extremists have since launched a vitriolic campaign against Jelassi and other artists in the exhibition. She says some received death threats. Salafists have posted the names and phone numbers of targeted artists on social media sites.Tunisia's judiciary has also waded into the controversy. Jelassi and another artist are accused of disturbing public order - a charge that can carry a prison term. Jelassi is hardly the only creator under fire in this North African country. Protesters attacked the U.S. Embassy in Tunis this month, enraged by a privately-made video produced in the U.S. denigrating the Prophet Muhammad. Police thwarted another protest last week against French cartoons also mocking the Muslim prophet. Other controversial films aired here have also been attacked. Today, rights activists like Mokhtar Trifi fear the ruling Islamist party Ennahda may capitalize on the anger to pass a blasphemy law. Draft legislation aims to criminalize offenses against so-called "sacred values." Trifi says Ennahda wants to portray itself as the defender or Islam. With elections expected next year, he says, its aims are political.

The U.S. Senate is in its second day of debate on the nomination of Sonia Sotomayor to serve on the Supreme Court. Democrats responded to continuing Republican criticisms of Sotomayor's record, while an additional Republican added his name to the list of those supporting the nomination.On the second day of debate all signs continued to point toward an easy confirmation win for Sotomayor, the 55-year-old federal court judge nominated by President Barack Obama earlier this year.Although most of the 40 Senate Republicans are likely to vote against her, the decision Wednesday of Missouri Senator Kit Bond added to the number of Republicans who have committed to voting for her.Senator Bond, who is one of several Republicans retiring from the Senate next year, said while he respects and agrees with the legal reasoning others in his party used to oppose Sotomayor, lawmakers have an obligation to show deference to a president's choice of a nominee."If some are saying that a Democratic president should not have a liberal justice, does that mean a Republican president should not have conservative justices? That is not something I could support," he said.Additional Republicans, particularly those like Bond who do not risk political damage from a no vote, may join in supporting the nomination.

Italian football club Juventus has filed an appeal against the recent sentence handed down by the sports court for match fixing. The club was found guilty of attempting to manipulate the outcome of games in the Serie A league.The court had imposed a fine on Juventus and deducted points from the team, leading to a significant impact on their standing in the league. Juventus has maintained its innocence throughout the process and has now taken the step to appeal the decision.The club's lawyers argue that there is not enough evidence to support the allegations of match fixing and are confident that the appeal will be successful.
