The future of Juventus in the Serie A league hangs in the balance as the appeal process unfolds.

The Trump administration on Tuesday slapped financial sanctions on four members of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro’s inner circle, including his wife and the nation’s vice president, on allegations of corruption.As part of the actions, the U.S. barred Americans from doing business with and will seize any financial assets in the U.S. belonging to First Lady Cilia Flores, Vice President Delcy Rodriguez, Communications Minister Jorge Rodriguez and Defense Minister Vladimir Padrino.“We are continuing to designate loyalists who enable Maduro to solidify his hold on the military and the government while the Venezuelan people suffer,” Treasury Secretary Steven T. Mnuchin said in a statement. “Treasury will continue to impose a financial toll on those responsible for Venezuela’s tragic decline, and the networks and front-men they use to mask their illicit wealth.”Over the past two years the Trump administration has sanctioned dozens of individuals, including Maduro himself, on allegations of corruption, drug trafficking and human rights abuses.But until now it had spared key leaders like Delcy Rodriguez, as well as the U.S.-trained Padrino, believing they occupy seats of power and could play a key role in an eventual transition.David Smilde, a Tulane University professor who has spent more than two decades living and working in Venezuela, said Tuesday’s actions would seem to suggest the U.S. has given up trying to sow division within the government in the hopes it could force a democratic transition from within.“This clearly breaks from that strategy, said Smilde. “If everyone is sanctioned then it could end up uniting the government.”

In the news today, tensions continue to rise in the South China Sea as multiple countries lay claim to disputed territories. The United States has expressed concern over China's growing military presence in the region, while China insists on its sovereign rights.In other news, North Korea has conducted another missile test, further escalating tensions in the region.

Argentina's former President Carlos Menem has been sentenced to seven years in prison for embezzlement. Menem, who served as president from 1989 to 1999, was found guilty of embezzling funds during his time in office. The court determined that Menem had diverted public money to offshore bank accounts. This is not the first time Menem has faced legal troubles, as he was also convicted of arms smuggling in the past. The sentencing of a former president is a rare occurrence in Argentina and is seen as a significant step in the country's efforts to combat corruption at the highest levels of government.

An Islamic State commander accused of rape has surrendered to authorities. The commander, whose name has not been disclosed, was reportedly a high-ranking member of the terrorist group and has been accused of committing multiple acts of sexual violence against women and girls in the areas under the group's control.According to local sources, the surrender of the commander comes after intense pressure from both the authorities and within the Islamic State itself. The group has been facing increasing criticism for its human rights abuses, including the widespread use of sexual violence as a weapon of war.The surrender of the accused commander is seen as a significant development in the fight against the Islamic State and its brutal tactics. It is hoped that his capture will provide valuable information that can be used to hold other perpetrators accountable and bring justice to the victims of these heinous crimes.Authorities are now conducting investigations into the accusations against the commander and are working to ensure that he faces justice for his alleged crimes. The surrender also serves as a reminder of the urgent need to address the issue of sexual violence in conflict zones and hold those responsible accountable for their actions.

In a major blow to the US government, a federal appeals court has ruled against their latest attempt to halt a lawsuit brought by a group of young people over climate change. The lawsuit, which was originally filed in 2015, alleges that the government has violated the constitutional rights of the plaintiffs by failing to take action on climate change.The decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals means that the case will continue to move forward, with the government being forced to defend its environmental policies in court. The plaintiffs, who range in age from 10 to 21, argue that they will face severe consequences from climate change if action is not taken immediately.This latest ruling is just one of many legal battles taking place around the world as young people demand action on climate change from their governments. The youth plaintiffs in this case have received support from environmental groups and activists, who see this as a crucial moment in the fight against climate change.The US government has tried multiple times to have the case dismissed, arguing that the courts do not have the authority to intervene in matters of environmental policy. However, this latest ruling shows that the courts are taking the case seriously and are willing to hear the arguments of the young plaintiffs.The fight for climate action is far from over, but this ruling is a significant step forward for the youths who are demanding action to protect their future.

A devastating earthquake struck Indonesia, leaving at least 75 people dead and trapping thousands more under collapsed buildings. The 6.5 magnitude quake hit the island of Sulawesi, causing widespread destruction in the city of Mamuju. Rescue teams are working tirelessly to save those who are still trapped, while emergency services are struggling to provide aid to the injured. The Indonesian government has declared a state of emergency and called for international assistance to help deal with the aftermath of this tragedy. Our thoughts are with the victims and their families during this difficult time.

In an effort to reduce food waste and extend the shelf life of produce, a team of researchers has developed a new technology using "fruity films" made from natural ingredients. These films are designed to create a protective barrier around fruits and vegetables, preventing moisture loss and reducing the growth of mold and bacteria.The fruity films are composed of plant-based materials such as pectin, chitosan, and essential oils, which are known for their antimicrobial properties. When applied to the surface of produce, the films form a breathable layer that allows gases to pass through while keeping out harmful microorganisms.According to the researchers, early trials have shown promising results in extending the freshness of various fruits and vegetables, including strawberries, cucumbers, and avocados. Not only do the fruity films help to preserve the quality of the produce, but they also offer a sustainable alternative to traditional plastic packaging.With food waste being a significant issue globally, innovations like fruity films could have a meaningful impact on reducing the amount of discarded produce. As the technology continues to be refined and tested, it holds the potential to revolutionize the way we protect and preserve fresh fruits and vegetables.

The top 5 songs for the week ending April 1 have been revealed, showcasing the latest hits in the music industry.1. "Stay" by The Kid LAROI and Justin Bieber continues to hold the top spot with its infectious melody and catchy lyrics.2. "Montero (Call Me By Your Name)" by Lil Nas X remains a strong contender in the second position with its bold and provocative sound.3. "Peaches" by Justin Bieber featuring Daniel Caesar and Giveon follows closely behind, maintaining its popularity on the charts.4. "Levitating" by Dua Lipa featuring DaBaby continues to captivate listeners with its upbeat tempo and energetic vibe.5. "Save Your Tears" by The Weeknd rounds out the top 5, showcasing the artist's signature blend of R&B and pop.These songs are dominating the airwaves and streaming platforms, providing listeners with a mix of genres and styles to enjoy. Stay tuned for next week's rankings to see if these hits continue to reign supreme.

The Philippine military said Sunday that 18 of its soldiers were killed and more than 50 others were wounded in a 10-hour firefight with Abu Sayyaf extremists in the southwestern island province of Basilan.A military spokesman said at least five militants died and 20 others were wounded in Saturday's fighting. He said the dead included a Moroccan national.The Philippine Daily Inquirer reported that an entire military platoon "was wiped out" and that four of the soldiers were beheaded. The fighting was described as the worst single day of government combat casualties in the region this year.Authorities said the military force had been hunting the militants in Basilan and nearby Joso island for the past two weeks, in a push to free as many as 18 captive foreigners, including several Canadians and a European kidnapped from a local beach resort last year.Abu Sayyaf later released videos in which some of the captives pleaded for their governments to secure their freedom. Militants had also threatened to behead their prisoners if their ransom demands were not met by Friday.Military authorities quoted by The Associated Press said many of the militants engaged Saturday were armed with grenade launchers, and that they were able to reinforce their ranks quickly as the fighting raged near the Basilan towns of Tipo Tipo and Al-Barka.Abu Sayyaf — a splinter grouping of the now-disbanded Moro Islamic Front — was founded in 1991 with funding from al-Qaida.

A deadly bombing struck an election rally in Afghanistan, killing at least 20 people and injuring dozens more. The attack took place in the city of Charikar, where supporters had gathered to listen to a candidate speak ahead of the upcoming parliamentary elections. The bomb went off near the stage, causing chaos and panic among the crowd.No group has claimed responsibility for the bombing yet, but the Taliban has been known to carry out attacks on political gatherings in the past. The incident has raised concerns about the security situation in Afghanistan as the country prepares for the elections.Local authorities are working to identify the victims and provide medical assistance to the injured. The government has condemned the attack and vowed to track down those responsible.The Afghan people are already facing violence and instability, and this latest bombing has added to their fears and concerns. The upcoming elections are seen as a crucial moment for the country's democracy, but the security challenges remain a major obstacle to a smooth and peaceful electoral process.

In a recent statement, Sri Lanka has called for truce monitors to continue their mission in the country. The monitors, who are tasked with overseeing the ceasefire agreement between the government and Tamil Tiger rebels, play a crucial role in maintaining peace and stability in the region.The call for the truce monitors to stay the course comes at a critical time, as tensions have been escalating in certain parts of Sri Lanka. It is imperative that both parties adhere to the ceasefire agreement and work towards a peaceful resolution of the conflict.Sri Lanka's plea for the truce monitors to continue their efforts is a clear indication of the government's commitment to finding a lasting solution to the conflict. It is hoped that all parties involved will cooperate with the monitors and work towards a peaceful and sustainable resolution.The international community has also been urged to support Sri Lanka in its efforts to maintain peace and stability in the region.

The US government is bracing itself for the potential fallout from the release of a long-awaited report on the CIA's use of torture tactics during the post-9/11 era. The report, which is expected to shed light on the extent of the agency's involvement in the controversial practices, has sparked concerns about possible backlash from the international community.Many fear that the report's findings could further damage America's already strained relationships with key allies and reignite debates over the legality and morality of the CIA's actions. The government has reportedly been preparing for the release of the report for months, with officials working to formulate a coordinated response to the expected fallout.While some have argued that the report's release is necessary for accountability and transparency, others worry about the potential consequences of reopening old wounds and dredging up painful memories of a dark chapter in US history. The US government is keen to manage the fallout from the report in a way that minimizes damage to America's reputation and national security interests.

Barbie, the world's most iconic doll, is venturing into coding skills in her latest career as a robotics engineer.The new doll, launched Tuesday, aims to encourage girls as young as seven to learn real coding skills, thanks to a partnership with the kids game-based computing platform Tynker, toymaker Mattel said.Robotics engineer Barbie, dressed in jeans, a graphic T-shirt and denim jacket and wearing safety glasses, comes with six free Barbie-inspired coding lessons designed to teach logic, problem solving and the building blocks of coding.The lessons, for example, show girls how to build robots, get them to move at a dance party, or do jumping jacks.According to U.S. Department of Commerce statistics, 24 percent of STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Math) jobs were held by women in 2017.Barbie has held more than 200 careers in her almost 60-year life, including president, video game developer and astronaut.Tynker co-founder Krishna Vedati said in a statement that the company's mission to empower youth worldwide made Barbie an ideal partner "to help us introduce programming to a large number of kids in a fun engaging way."

A recent study conducted in China has revealed that the Covid-19 virus may not be as deadly as other coronaviruses such as SARS and MERS. Researchers found that while Covid-19 is highly contagious, the mortality rate is lower compared to previous outbreaks.The study, published in the Journal of Infectious Diseases, analyzed data from over 44,000 confirmed cases of Covid-19 in China. The findings showed that the overall fatality rate for the virus is around 2.3%, significantly lower than the 10% mortality rate for SARS and 34% for MERS.Experts believe that the lower mortality rate of Covid-19 could be attributed to improved healthcare infrastructure and earlier detection and treatment of the virus. However, they caution that the situation could change as the virus continues to spread globally.Despite the relatively lower mortality rate, health officials are still urging the public to take precautionary measures such as practicing good hygiene, wearing masks, and following social distancing guidelines to prevent the further spread of the virus.As the world continues to grapple with the Covid-19 pandemic, researchers are working tirelessly to better understand the virus and develop effective treatments and vaccines to combat it. The findings of this study offer some hope that Covid-19 may not be as deadly as initially feared, but it is crucial for everyone to remain vigilant and take necessary precautions to protect themselves and others.

The United States budget deficit has reached a six-year high, according to recent reports. The deficit for the fiscal year ending in September totaled $984 billion, marking the highest level since 2012.The rise in the budget deficit can be attributed to a combination of factors, including increased government spending and the impact of tax cuts passed by the Trump administration. The deficit is expected to continue growing in the coming years, with some estimates projecting it to exceed $1 trillion by 2020.Critics of the current administration have raised concerns about the growing deficit, warning that it could have negative implications for the economy in the long term. Rising deficits can lead to higher interest rates, inflation, and reduced investment in public services.The Trump administration has defended its fiscal policies, arguing that the tax cuts have boosted economic growth and will ultimately lead to increased government revenues. However, with the deficit reaching a six-year high, there is growing pressure on policymakers to address the issue and work towards balancing the federal budget.

As temperatures continue to rise in Houston, many residents are finding relief from the scorching summer heat by heading underground. The city's extensive network of underground tunnels, known as the Tunnel System, provides a cool and air-conditioned escape from the sweltering heat above ground.Spanning over 6 miles and connecting various office buildings, restaurants, shops, and hotels in downtown Houston, the Tunnel System offers a convenient and comfortable way to navigate the city while avoiding the intense heat and humidity outside.With temperatures often reaching well over 90 degrees Fahrenheit during the summer months, many Houstonians have opted to spend their days underground, enjoying a climate-controlled environment as they go about their daily activities.While some may argue that spending time in the tunnels disconnects residents from the vibrant street life above ground, others appreciate the respite from the oppressive heat and humidity that often accompanies a Houston summer.Whether grabbing lunch, running errands, or simply exploring the interconnected tunnels, Houstonians have found a unique way to beat the heat this summer. And with temperatures expected to remain high in the coming weeks, it's likely that many more residents will continue to seek refuge underground until the cooler temperatures of fall arrive.

Mortar shells wounded at least seven people in and around the presidential compound Thursday in Mogadishu, Somalia, while the Cabinet was meeting inside.At least three mortars struck a residential neighborhood near the palace.“A mortar shell hit near a house in Wardhigley district close to the presidential compound. We transported four injured civilians to the hospital,” Mohamed Aden, an ambulance driver told VOA.No Cabinet members were wounded as Prime Minister Hassan Ali Khaire was holding the new government’s first meeting with ministers to talk about security and the drought.No one has claimed the responsibility for the shelling, but Somali militant group al-Shabab often attacks government targets.In Washington, the Pentagon announced Thursday that President Donald Trump has given it more authority to launch airstrikes against al-Shabab without having to justify its actions.The U.S. is supporting Somali and African Union forces in their fight against the Islamic terrorists.“

Mozambique is gearing up for a crucial election on Wednesday, where citizens will have the opportunity to choose their next leader. The country has faced numerous challenges in recent years, including political instability and natural disasters. The upcoming election is seen as a critical moment for Mozambique to chart a path towards stability and progress.Several candidates are vying for the presidency, each with their own vision for the future of the country. Voters are eager to cast their ballots and make their voices heard. The election is being closely watched by international observers, who are hoping for a peaceful and transparent process.Mozambique has a history of electoral violence, but there is hope that this election will be different. Security measures have been put in place to ensure the safety of voters and prevent any disruptions. The outcome of the election will have far-reaching implications for the country and its people.As the day of the election approaches, Mozambicans are preparing to exercise their democratic right and shape the future of their nation. The eyes of the world will be on Mozambique on Wednesday as the country heads to the polls.

After years of suspension due to a widespread doping scandal, Russia is on the verge of making its return to international sports. The World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) announced that it has partially cleared the path for Russia's reinstatement by lifting the suspension of the Russian Anti-Doping Agency (RUSADA).This decision comes after RUSADA met the requirements set by WADA for its reinstatement, including acknowledging the findings of the McLaren Report which detailed widespread state-sponsored doping in Russia. While WADA has acknowledged the progress made by RUSADA, it has also warned that Russia still has work to do to fully comply with anti-doping regulations.The potential reinstatement of Russia into international sports has been met with mixed reactions. Some view it as a positive step towards reintegration, while others remain skeptical of Russia's commitment to clean competition. The final decision on Russia's return will be made by the WADA Executive Committee at its next meeting.If Russia is allowed to compete again, it will have a significant impact on the world of sports, particularly in events such as the Olympics and World Championships. It remains to be seen how the international sports community will react to Russia's potential return, and whether Russia can prove its commitment to clean competition moving forward.

November 11, 2008 is Veterans Day in the United States. This year marks the 90th anniversary of the end of World War I, which was fought from 1914 to 1918. VOA's Deborah Block recently met perhaps the last living U.S. soldier who took part in what was known as the "Great War." Frank Buckles is believed to be the last known surviving American veteran from the first world war. He is 107 years old. As a young soldier in the U.S. Army, he was an ambulance driver in France helping wounded American and French soldiers. France and the United States were allies during the war. Buckles was honored recently at the French Embassy in Washington.Jean-Marie Bockel, the Secretary of Veteran Affairs in France, thanked him and other Americans for their service in World War I. Buckles was presented with the Legion of Honor, the highest award in France for military or civil service.Buckles was fortunate that he was not injured during the war. An estimated 200,000 US soldiers were wounded; 50,000 American soldiers died on French soil alone. Buckles was only 16 years old, an underage recruit, when he talked his way into the U.S. Army looking for adventure. He wanted to go to the frontlines of the war. Instead he went first to England where he was a driver for dignitaries. "Something was happening every day," Buckles said. "I was right there."The stage was set for World War I after a breakdown of old alliances among European powers and an arms race led by Germany. The war started after Germany invaded Belgium. Wary of foreign entanglements, the United States did not enter the conflict until 1917.

Taipei, Taiwan - A Taiwanese politician's visit to the controversial Yasukuni Shrine in Tokyo, Japan has sparked criticism and debate in both countries.The Yasukuni Shrine honors Japan's war dead, including convicted war criminals from World War II. The shrine has been a source of tension between Japan and its neighbors, particularly China and South Korea, who view it as a symbol of Japan's militaristic past.The Taiwanese politician, who has not been named, visited the shrine as part of a private trip to Japan.

PARIS, FRANCE - Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has visited Juno Beach in Normandy, one of Canada's most important World War II sites.On June 6, 1944, thousands of Canadian troops stormed the beach with British and American soldiers on D-Day's amphibious invasion of the northern French region. Trudeau arrived with his family Monday and walked along the beach in the sun, as wind blew over French and Canadian flags that stood side-by-side in the sand. The visit comes a day after more than 20,000 people, most of them Canadians, attended a solemn ceremony at Vimy to commemorate a World War I battle that remains etched on Canada's national identity.

The Philippines police have arrested a suspected member of the terrorist group Abu Sayyaf, who had been working as security guard in the capital.The police said Friday that Arabani Jakiran was arrested Thursday at the plush Manila condominium where he worked as a security guard. Jakiran is suspected of taking part in the abduction of hospital staff in the southern island of Basilan in 2001. Some of the captives were killed as police hunted down the kidnappers.Abu Sayyaf, which claimed to be fighting for a Muslim homeland in the southern Philippines, became best known for a series of kidnappings for ransom. Police and Southeast Asia terrorism experts say the group has links to both the al-Qaida network led by Osama bin Laden and the Jemaah Islamiyah Islamic militants based in Indonesia.Police officials say the arrest was part of a new crackdown on members of Abu Sayyaf and members of Jemaah Islamiyah living in the country.Also Friday, police in Manila blocked a planned march to the U.S. Embassy to protest the killing of Osama bin Laden earlier this week in Pakistan by U.S. Navy SEALs.

On International Women's Day, African women experienced mixed results in their pursuit of gender equality and empowerment.While there were notable achievements to celebrate, such as the increased participation of African women in politics and leadership positions, there were also significant challenges that continue to hinder progress.One of the key successes was the election of Samia Suluhu Hassan as the first female President of Tanzania, following the death of former President John Magufuli. This historic moment marked a significant step towards gender equality in the region.Additionally, the African Union launched the African Women Leaders Network, a platform that aims to promote the leadership and participation of women in decision-making processes across the continent.However, despite these advancements, many African women still face barriers to equal opportunities, such as gender-based violence, limited access to education and healthcare, and cultural norms that restrict their freedom and autonomy.On International Women's Day, it is important to reflect on the progress made towards gender equality in Africa, while also recognizing the work that still needs to be done to ensure that all African women have equal rights and opportunities.

Former US Senator Richard Lugar, a widely respected foreign policy expert, has passed away at the age of 87. Lugar served as a Republican senator from Indiana for 36 years, from 1977 to 2013. During his time in the Senate, Lugar was known for his expertise in foreign affairs and was a strong advocate for nonproliferation of nuclear weapons. He played a key role in the dismantling of thousands of nuclear warheads in the former Soviet Union.Lugar's bipartisan approach to foreign policy earned him respect on both sides of the political aisle.

In the midst of ongoing clashes in the West Bank, Jerusalem witnessed peaceful prayers on Thursday. Despite the tension and violence in nearby areas, worshippers gathered at the Al-Aqsa Mosque for evening prayers without any incidents. This comes after days of violent confrontations between Israeli forces and Palestinian protesters in the West Bank, resulting in several injuries and arrests. The peaceful prayers in Jerusalem serve as a reminder of the importance of unity and faith in times of turmoil. Leaders on both sides have called for calm and dialogue to prevent further escalation of the conflict. The situation remains tense, but the peaceful prayers offer a glimmer of hope for a peaceful resolution to the ongoing unrest in the region.

A 12-year-old girl from Iowa has taken it upon herself to make a positive impact in her community by founding an organization aimed at helping children make a difference. Lily Johnson, a seventh-grader at Maple Elementary School, started the organization called "Kids for Change" with the goal of empowering children to take action and create change in their own communities."I believe that kids have the power to make a difference, no matter how small," said Lily. "I wanted to create a platform where children could come together to support each other and work towards common goals."Since its founding last year, Kids for Change has organized several initiatives such as beach clean-ups, food drives, and fundraising events for local charities. The organization has also partnered with schools and community centers to spread awareness about important issues affecting children."We are so proud of Lily and the work she is doing with Kids for Change," said Lily's mother, Sarah Johnson. "It's inspiring to see a young girl with such passion and dedication to making a difference in the world."Lily hopes that Kids for Change will continue to grow and inspire other children to take action in their communities. "I want every child to know that they have the power to create positive change," she said.

A group of teenagers from the United Kingdom have found a unique way to connect with Palestinian refugees - through their shared love of Korean pop music.The teenagers, who are part of a charity organization dedicated to helping refugees, have been organizing virtual K-pop dance parties and language exchange sessions with young Palestinians living in refugee camps. The initiative has not only provided a platform for cultural exchange but has also fostered friendship and understanding between the two groups."I never imagined that K-pop could bring us together in such a meaningful way," said Sarah, one of the UK teenagers involved in the project. "It's amazing to see how music can transcend borders and language barriers."The Palestinian teenagers have also expressed their gratitude for the initiative, noting that it has given them a sense of connection and belonging despite their difficult circumstances.The group plans to continue organizing virtual events and activities to further strengthen the bond between the UK teenagers and Palestinian refugees. Their hope is to spread a message of unity and solidarity through their shared love of music.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights says the Islamic State extremist group executed 33 people this week -- the first mass executions carried out by the militants this year.The British-based watchdog group says the killings were carried out in the eastern countryside of Deir Ezzor early Wednesday morning.The Observatory says its activists witnessed the executions, but says it is unknown if those killed were fighters on either side of the six-year civil war, or civilians who had been arrested by IS forces and sentenced to die.The Observatory says it is the largest mass execution carried out by IS since two groups of 15 persons were killed in two separate executions in between September and October of last year.

The United States and Norway have announced a new partnership aimed at combating the rapid progression of world deforestation. The two countries have pledged to work together to implement sustainable forestry practices, protect vital ecosystems, and promote reforestation efforts.Deforestation is a global issue that has significant environmental consequences, including habitat loss, biodiversity depletion, and increased greenhouse gas emissions. It is estimated that around 18 million acres of forest are lost each year, which has a profound impact on the health of our planet.By joining forces, the US and Norway hope to lead by example and encourage other countries to prioritize the protection of forests. This partnership will also focus on supporting indigenous communities that rely on forests for their livelihoods, as well as implementing innovative technologies to monitor and combat deforestation.Both countries recognize the urgent need to address deforestation and are committed to taking concrete actions to preserve the world's forests for future generations. The collaboration between the US and Norway is a positive step towards combating this pressing global issue and creating a more sustainable future for all.

The task of urging holdout rebels to sign the Darfur Peace Agreement has fallen to the African Union, which brokered the deal in Abuja, Nigeria. It is a difficult job, and across the region, AU peacekeepers are working relentlessly to entice the rebels into accepting the Darfur deal.Colonel Richard Lourens and his team of a dozen African Union troops and observers are flying deep into rebel-held territory.This is not a combat mission. Instead, it is an attempt to coax holdout Darfuri rebels into accepting a peace agreement that they do not want.Rebels from a holdout faction of the Sudan Liberation Army have agreed to meet Lourens outside of the tiny village of Debbus.But the meeting begins poorly as the heavily armed rebels say they have decided that they don't want to speak to Lourens.The colonel is clearly frustrated."I want them to understand that I am here to assist and I am here to help," he said. "And they must understand that there is a peace process going on. And I want to come and help them, but they must help me to help them."These rebels are aligned with Abdel Wahid Moahmed Nur, a commander with the Sudan Liberation Army (SLA) who says the May 5 Darfur Peace Agreement does not offer Darfuris enough.Nur wants more compensation money for victims of this brutal war and a Darfuri vice president.Since the signing of the accord, violence has intensified between supporters of Nur and Minni Minnawi, the SLA commander who did sign the agreement.The rebels in this region call Minni Minnawi a traitor for accepting the peace deal.Rebel Commander Ibrahim Abdallah is one of the first to air his concerns."This is not peace," he said. "Minni has no ground here, no civilians, no forces, nobody in Darfur.

European aerospace giant Airbus has accused the Polish government of misleading them for months over a failed deal to buy military helicopters.In a statement released on Tuesday, Airbus said that the Polish authorities had given them incorrect information during negotiations for the purchase of 50 Caracal helicopters, which ultimately fell through in 2016.According to Airbus, the Polish government had assured them that the deal was still on track, despite facing delays and funding issues. However, the contract was later cancelled by Poland, citing concerns over the cost and the need for a competitive tender process.The botched deal has reportedly cost Airbus millions of euros in lost revenue and damages, and the company is now seeking compensation from the Polish government.The Polish Ministry of Defence has denied the accusations, stating that they had acted in good faith throughout the negotiations and that Airbus was aware of the risks involved in the deal.This latest development has strained relations between Airbus and Poland, with the aerospace company threatening legal action if a resolution cannot be reached.The controversy highlights the challenges faced by multinational companies in dealing with government contracts, and the importance of transparency and communication in such negotiations.

Six Iraqi policemen were killed in an attack by unknown gunmen in the city of Baghdad today. The gunmen reportedly opened fire on a police checkpoint in a busy area of the city, resulting in the deaths of the officers. The attack has raised concerns about the ongoing security challenges faced by Iraqi security forces as they continue to combat terrorism and insurgency in the country. The Iraqi government has condemned the attack and vowed to bring the perpetrators to justice.This is not the first time that Iraqi policemen have been targeted by militants in the country. Despite efforts to improve security measures, attacks on security forces continue to pose a threat to stability in Iraq. The identities of the gunmen responsible for the attack are currently unknown, and investigations are ongoing to determine the motive behind the attack. The families of the slain policemen have been notified, and funerals are expected to take place in the coming days.

In a recent report released by the US Department of Justice, it has been revealed that the National Security Agency (NSA) continued to collect Americans' phone records even after a law change was implemented to restrict such surveillance activities.The report indicates that the NSA collected a significant number of phone records from American citizens in the months following the implementation of the USA Freedom Act in 2015. This law was intended to limit the NSA's ability to collect and store data on individuals without a warrant.Despite the legal restrictions imposed by the USA Freedom Act, the NSA reportedly found ways to exploit loopholes in the law to continue its collection of phone records. This revelation has raised concerns among privacy advocates and civil liberties groups about the government's ability to circumvent legal protections put in place to safeguard individuals' privacy rights.The report comes at a time of heightened scrutiny over government surveillance practices, particularly in light of recent revelations about the NSA's mass collection of data through programs like PRISM. The findings of this report are likely to reignite debates over the balance between national security and individual privacy rights in the digital age.The NSA has yet to respond to the report, but it is clear that the issue of government surveillance and data collection will continue to be a topic of discussion and debate in the coming months.

The island nation of Comoros is set to hold a referendum in July to decide on whether or not to impose presidential term limits. This decision comes after President Azali Assoumani, who has been in power since 2016, proposed the idea in an effort to promote democracy and prevent leaders from holding on to power indefinitely.The referendum will give the citizens of Comoros the opportunity to vote on whether or not to limit the number of terms a president can serve. Currently, there are no term limits in place, allowing presidents to potentially stay in power for decades.Opposition parties have expressed support for the referendum, seeing it as a step towards more democratic governance in the country. However, some critics argue that the proposed term limits are a way for President Assoumani to consolidate power and ensure his own re-election.The referendum is scheduled to take place on July 30th, and the outcome will have significant implications for the future of Comoros and its political landscape. Voters will have the chance to have their voices heard and shape the direction of their country.

South African relief groups are expressing deep concern for the victims of anti-foreigner violence in the country. The recent wave of attacks targeting foreign nationals has left many individuals and families displaced, injured, and traumatized.The violence, which erupted in various parts of South Africa, has been fueled by xenophobic sentiments and tensions within local communities. Foreign-owned businesses have been looted and destroyed, while many foreign nationals have been physically assaulted and forced to flee their homes.Relief groups are working tirelessly to provide assistance to the victims of the violence, including shelter, food, medical care, and counseling services. However, they are facing challenges in reaching all those affected and ensuring their safety and well-being.The government has condemned the violence and has promised to take action to address the root causes of xenophobia in the country. Officials have called for unity and tolerance among all South Africans, regardless of their nationality or background.As the situation continues to unfold, relief groups are calling for increased support and solidarity from the international community to help those affected by the anti-foreigner violence in South Africa. The safety and well-being of all individuals, regardless of their nationality, must be protected and prioritized.

The Trump administration has intensified its efforts to crack down on so-called sanctuary cities that refuse to comply with federal immigration authorities, sending letters to nine jurisdictions asking for proof that they are working with immigration enforcement.The U.S. Justice Department said in the letters Friday that it would withhold federal law enforcement grant money unless the jurisdictions can document cooperation. The department hands out more than $2 billion each year to communities across the country to support anti-crime efforts.The letters were sent to the state of California; Cook County, Illinois; and the cities of Chicago, New Orleans, Philadelphia, Las Vegas, Miami, Milwaukee and New York.FILE - Protester Jennifer Smith-Camejo holds a sign during an anti-Trump and anti-Gimenez rally in downtown Miami, Jan. 31, 2017.The jurisdictions all have policies that limit the amount of information that local law enforcement can provide to federal immigration authorities about people in custody who are in the country illegally.In a statement Friday, the Justice Department said the cities are "crumbling under the weight of illegal immigration and violent crime," and mentioned an increase in murders in Chicago as well as gang violence in New York City.It also accused local officials of being more concerned about protecting undocumented immigrants than capturing criminals, citing the recent arrest of MS-13 gang members in California."And just several weeks ago in California?

Washington, D.C. - Following President Trump's announcement of a breakthrough in trade negotiations with China, lawmakers in the United States are seeking more information about the agreement. The details of the trade deal, which Trump described as a "phase one" agreement, remain vague, leading to calls from both Democrats and Republicans for more transparency. Many lawmakers are eager to understand the specifics of the agreement and how it will impact American farmers, workers, and consumers.Critics of the president have expressed skepticism about the deal, questioning whether it will address long-standing issues such as intellectual property theft and forced technology transfers. They argue that without more concrete details, it is difficult to assess the true impact of the agreement.In response to the calls for more information, the Trump administration has promised to provide additional details about the deal in the coming days. As the negotiations continue, lawmakers will be closely monitoring the progress and seeking to ensure that any agreement reached with China is in the best interests of the American people.

The PGA Tour has announced that it will be moving a golf tournament from President Donald Trump's resort in Miami, Florida to Mexico City. The decision comes amid controversy surrounding Trump's comments on immigration and border security.The tournament, which was originally scheduled to take place at Trump National Doral in Miami, will now be held at Club de Golf Chapultepec in Mexico. The move has sparked mixed reactions, with some praising the decision as a stand against Trump's divisive rhetoric, while others criticize the PGA Tour for taking a political stance.In a statement, the PGA Tour explained that the decision to move the tournament was made in the best interest of the sport and its fans. The organization emphasized that its priority is to provide a welcoming and inclusive environment for all players and spectators.This move comes as various businesses and organizations have begun distancing themselves from Trump's brand in light of his controversial statements and policies. The PGA Tour's decision to relocate the tournament is likely to further fuel the ongoing debate over the intersection of politics and sports.

The United States Ambassador to Russia, John Sullivan, expressed optimism about the potential for improved relations between Washington and Moscow following Vladimir Putin's recent election victory. In a press conference on Monday, Sullivan highlighted the importance of dialogue and cooperation between the two countries."We have a unique opportunity to reset our relationship with Russia," Sullivan stated. "President Putin's reelection provides a chance for us to work together on shared goals, such as combating terrorism and addressing global security challenges."Sullivan's comments come amid ongoing tensions between the US and Russia, particularly over issues such as election interference, the conflict in Ukraine, and the war in Syria. However, the Ambassador believes that both countries can find common ground and build on areas of mutual interest."We may not always see eye to eye with the Russian government, but that doesn't mean we can't find ways to collaborate," Sullivan added. "I am hopeful that we can move forward in a positive direction and strengthen our ties with Moscow in the coming months."While the road to improved US-Russia relations may be long and challenging, Sullivan's optimism signals a willingness on the part of the US to engage with Russia in a constructive manner. As Putin begins his fourth term as President, the international community will be watching closely to see how the relationship between the two countries evolves.

The number of immigrant detentions in the United States has been steadily increasing, along with accusations of human rights violations against those detained.According to recent reports, the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has been detaining immigrants at a higher rate than in previous years. This surge in detentions has raised concerns about the treatment of immigrants in custody, with many alleging violations of their rights.Immigrant advocacy groups have been speaking out against the conditions in detention centers, citing overcrowding, lack of access to basic necessities, and reports of physical and verbal abuse by ICE officials. In response, ICE has denied any wrongdoing and maintains that they are following protocol in their handling of detained immigrants.The increase in detentions and allegations of rights violations come at a time of heightened scrutiny of the U.S. immigration system, with many calling for reforms to address the treatment of immigrants in custody. As the debate over immigration policy continues, the issue of immigrant rights and detention practices is likely to remain a topic of concern for many in the United States.

In a disturbing discovery, hundreds of bodies have been found in mass graves near a former Islamic State bastion in Syria. The graves were uncovered in the city of Albu Kamal, which was once under the control of the extremist group. The bodies are believed to be those of civilians and possibly fighters who were killed during the occupation of the city.Local authorities are working to identify the bodies and determine the cause of death. The discovery of these mass graves serves as a grim reminder of the atrocities committed by the Islamic State during their reign of terror in the region. It also highlights the ongoing challenges faced by Syria in the aftermath of the group's defeat.The Syrian government has vowed to investigate the mass graves and bring those responsible for the killings to justice. The United Nations and human rights organizations have also called for a thorough investigation into the matter. The uncovering of these graves is a tragic reminder of the toll that conflict and extremism have taken on the Syrian people.
Lebanese troops have killed six Islamic militants in clashes near the northern city of Tripoli.The fighting erupted outside the village of Qalamoun Thursday.

The U.S. financial crisis continued to dominate the presidential campaign Friday, as both major party candidates for the White House spoke about what they would do to deal with the market turmoil. VOA National correspondent Jim Malone has more from Washington. Republican candidate John McCain spoke in Wisconsin and said the U.S. central bank should stop bailing out failed financial institutions, and should return to the main job of managing the money supply and guarding against inflation.McCain said that, if elected president, he would set up a Mortgage and Financial Institutions Trust, MFI for short, which he says would help homeowners avoid foreclosure."The MFI will restore investor and market confidence, build sound financial institutions, assist troubled institutions and protect our financial system, while minimizing taxpayer exposure," he said.McCain also ramped up the attacks on his Democratic opponent, Senator Barack Obama. McCain said Obama's ties to the failed mortgage lenders Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae, including campaign contributions, make him part of the problem."We've heard a lot of words from Senator Obama over the course of this campaign," he said. "But maybe, just this once, he could spare us the lectures, and admit to his own poor judgment in contributing to these problems."

Iran's oil exports have experienced a significant decline, leading to a trade slump with both Germany and the United States. The ongoing sanctions imposed on Iran by the US have heavily impacted the country's economy, particularly its oil industry.According to reports, Iran's oil exports have dropped to their lowest levels in decades, with Germany and the US reducing their imports significantly. This decline in trade has further strained Iran's already struggling economy, leading to widespread economic challenges for the country.The US has been ramping up its sanctions against Iran in recent years, particularly targeting the country's oil industry in an effort to pressure the Iranian government. This has had a direct impact on Iran's ability to export its oil to key trading partners, such as Germany and the US.As a result, Iran is now facing a severe economic downturn, with the country struggling to maintain its trade relations with key partners. The future of Iran's oil industry remains uncertain, as the country continues to grapple with the effects of the ongoing sanctions imposed by the US.Overall, Iran's faltering oil exports and trade slump with Germany and the US highlight the significant economic challenges facing the country. As Iran continues to navigate the complex economic landscape created by the US sanctions, the future of the country's economy remains uncertain.

JOHANNESBURG - Under pressure over his track record on the economy, South African President Cyril Ramaphosa will convene a gathering of top officials and business leaders on Thursday to find solutions to the country's long-running unemployment crisis.More than two decades after the end of white minority rule, South Africa still has one of the highest jobless rates among major global economies, with more than a quarter of its work force unemployed.Ramaphosa, who took over from scandal-plagued Jacob Zuma in February, has made reviving the economy and boosting job creation a cornerstone of his reform drive.At a state of the nation address to parliament shortly after becoming president, he said the Jobs Summit would come up with a set of initiatives to drive inclusive growth."The summit is about unblocking economic constraints," said Tanya Cohen, chief executive of Business Unity South Africa, one of the organisers of the two-day summit outside Johannesburg."

Former Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke is calling on Congress to provide further support to the economy in the face of the ongoing pandemic. In a recent op-ed for the Washington Post, Bernanke emphasized the need for additional fiscal stimulus to help businesses and individuals struggling during this unprecedented crisis.Bernanke warned that without further assistance, the economy risks falling into a deeper recession and causing long-lasting damage. He stressed the importance of targeting support to those most in need, including unemployed workers, small businesses, and state and local governments facing budget shortfalls.While the Federal Reserve has taken aggressive actions to support the economy through monetary policy, Bernanke believes that fiscal policy measures from Congress are necessary to complement these efforts. He suggested that lawmakers should prioritize measures such as extending unemployment benefits, providing direct aid to small businesses, and investing in infrastructure projects to create jobs and stimulate economic growth.As the coronavirus pandemic continues to take a toll on the economy, Bernanke's call for Congressional support is a reminder of the urgent need for action to prevent further economic hardship. Time will tell if lawmakers heed his advice and pass additional stimulus measures to help steer the economy towards recovery.

Russia has announced that the air link between Russia and Egypt will be restored soon, according to Russian officials. The air travel between the two countries was suspended in 2015 after a Russian passenger plane was shot down over Egypt's Sinai Peninsula, killing all 224 people on board. Since then, the two countries have been working on implementing new security measures to ensure the safety of air travel between Russia and Egypt. Russian authorities have stated that they are confident that these measures will be effective in preventing any further incidents. The resumption of the air link is expected to boost tourism and strengthen the economic ties between Russia and Egypt.

United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres has issued a warning to North Korea following reports of a planned rocket launch by the country. Guterres stated that such actions would violate UN Security Council resolutions and escalate tensions in the region.The Secretary-General urged North Korea to refrain from any provocative actions that could further destabilize the situation on the Korean peninsula. He emphasized the need for dialogue and diplomacy to resolve conflicts peacefully.The international community has been closely monitoring North Korea's actions in recent months, particularly in light of the country's missile tests and nuclear activities. The United Nations has repeatedly called on North Korea to abide by its international obligations and work towards denuclearization.Guterres's warning comes as tensions between North Korea and the United States remain high, with both countries engaging in tough rhetoric and military drills. The Secretary-General called for all parties to exercise restraint and work towards a peaceful resolution to the ongoing crisis.It remains to be seen how North Korea will respond to the warning from the United Nations, but the international community will be watching closely for any further developments.

A senior Sudanese diplomat says the safety of United Nations' peacekeepers in his country will not be in jeopardy, if the International Criminal Court, or ICC, grants a request for an arrest warrant for Sudanese President Omar al-Bashir. A decision from the pre-trial judges at The Hague is expected later this month.Sudan's U.N. Ambassador, Abdalmahmood Abdalhaleem, said Thursday that Khartoum would abide by its international obligations."The safety of the peacekeepers is not at all an issue here. The issue is the safety and security of the entire people of Sudan," he said. "So the people should not look to the issue like isolated islands or separate islands. We know, as I said before, the obligations of Sudan towards the U.N. presence in Sudan. But equally, the U.N. should demonstrate its commitment to safeguard their own personnel as well as the lives of the Sudanese."Later this month, the International Criminal Court is expected to decide whether to grant its chief prosecutor's request for an arrest warrant for President Bashir on charges of genocide, war crimes and crimes against humanity for atrocities committed in Sudan's Darfur region during the past five years.Sudan has been pressing member states to call for a deferment of the case under what is known as Article 16. That would require at least nine of the 15 Security Council members voting in favor of the deferment, with none of the five veto-wielding members voting against it.

Ethiopia has strongly criticized the decision to freeze aid to the country by several western governments following the conflict in the Tigray region. The government labeled the move as unfair and claimed that it would only worsen the situation for millions of vulnerable people in need of assistance.The freezing of aid comes after reports of human rights abuses and atrocities committed by all sides involved in the conflict in Tigray. The Ethiopian government has denied the allegations and has called for an independent investigation to assess the situation.Ethiopian officials have stated that the country is already facing multiple challenges, including the COVID-19 pandemic and locust invasions, and the freeze on aid will only make matters worse for the population.

In a significant development, the United Nations has announced that Libyan factions have agreed to participate in peace talks scheduled for January. This agreement comes after months of negotiations aimed at finding a peaceful resolution to the conflict in Libya.The talks, which are set to take place in Geneva, Switzerland, will bring together representatives from the various factions involved in the conflict. The goal of the talks is to reach a ceasefire agreement and pave the way for a political solution to the crisis in the country.The UN has been working tirelessly to facilitate dialogue between the warring parties in Libya, with the hope of bringing an end to the violence and instability that has plagued the country for years. The agreement to participate in peace talks is seen as a positive step forward in the ongoing peace process.The international community has welcomed the news of the agreement, with many expressing hope that the talks will lead to a lasting peace in Libya.

MUSIC: "Our World" themeThis week on Our World: The World Health Organization declares a swine flu pandemic ... A potential new weapon in the fight against termites ... and coral reefs under threat ...PATTERSON: "We've got to work very hard to protect them because it shows us how the ecosystem should look and used to look around the planet before things started to go downhill."Those stories, horseback riding as therapy, our Website of the Week, and more.I'm Art Chimes. Welcome to VOA's science and technology magazine, "Our World."WHO declares swine flu pandemic, ups alert levelThe World Health Organization on Thursday declared its first pandemic in four decades and bumped the H1N1 swine flu threat level up to phase six, the highest alert level.WHO official Margaret Chan told reporters in Geneva that public health officials identified this pandemic earlier than any previous outbreak, and that it is - so far at least - not nearly as deadly as prior pandemics.CHAN: "Globally, we have good reason to believe that this pandemic, at least in its early days, will be of moderate severity. As we know from experience, severity can vary, depending on many factors, from one country to another."Dr.

NEW YORK - Tony voters on Tuesday celebrated everything from the goofy to the grotesque on Broadway, handing out nominations in double digits to such varied shows as Tina Fey's catty "Mean Girls," the sprawling AIDs drama "Angels in America," a grown-up Harry Potter play and a candy-colored slice of seafood in "SpongeBob SquarePants" musical."I feel like that's what you want — you want a diverse community coming to the theater," said Fey, who got a writing nomination for her Broadway debut. "It just feels like there's something for everyone, which is how it should be."Seven shows earned 10 or more nominations, led by "Mean Girls" and "SpongeBob SquarePants," with 12 each. "Angels in America," ''The Band's Visit" and "Carousel" tied with 11, and "Harry Potter and the Cursed Child" and "My Fair Lady" each got 10."It's really exciting to be part of this nice mix," said "Mean Girls" star Taylor Louderman, a first-time nominee who plays a high school queen bee laid low. "I'm really glad that we're not leaving out a genre or commercial theater."The best new musical category is filled by "The Band's Visit," ''Frozen," ''Mean Girls" and "SpongeBob SquarePants." Those musicals that failed to make the cut were the Hal Prince revue "Prince of Broadway," the Jimmy Buffett musical "Escape to Margaritaville" and "Summer," about disco diva Donna Summer.SpongeBob SquarePants animation character, SpongeBob SquarePants, is seen at Universal Studios, Disney Land, in Orlando, Florida. (Photo: Diaa Bekheet)J.K.

The former deputy leader of al-Qaida-linked militant group al-Shabab has been arrested in southern Somalia, sparking clashes between his supporters and pro-government forces that left eight people dead.Officials speaking on condition of anonymity say Mukhtar Robow, also known as Abu Mansoor, was invited to a meeting Thursday at the regional state presidential palace in Baidoa, 250 kilometers northwest of Mogadishu. They say upon arrival, he was immediately detained.Telecommunications in Biadoa were briefly shut down as disgruntled Robow supporters took to the streets.Robow's militia along with his supporters clashed with Ethiopian soldiers providing security to the palace, leaving eight people dead and more than 10 others injured, witnesses and officials told VOA's Somali service.Two Somali lawmakers who spoke to the VOA accused the Somali government of arresting Robow using Ethiopian military forces.A VOA stringer reports that sporadic gunfire could still be heard across the town Thursday after midday.Calls VOA made to several federal government officials to comment on the incident were not answered.In hiding Robow, who was trained by al-Qaida in Afghanistan, was one of the founders of al-Shabab in 2006 and served as its number-two leader for several years.

WASHINGTON - The United Nations human rights expert on North Korea is calling for Pyongyang to allow nationwide “unimpeded access” so aid organizations can help battle the coronavirus.“My call is [for] the international community to be prepared to respond [to help North Korea] and the [North Korean] government to allow for an unimpeded access for medical and humanitarian experts and actors,” Tomás Ojea Quintana, the U.N. Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights in North Korea, told VOA Korean Service.Although there are no confirmed cases of the coronavirus inside North Korea, the virus has been spreading fast in its neighboring countries of China and South Korea.“North Korea has a history of restricting access to international community agencies [to] all [parts of] the territory of North Korea. And that has continued to be an issue of concern of international agencies, including United Nations agencies working in North Korea,” Quintana said.The regime needs to let international aid groups work in rural areas to help diagnose cases of COVID-19 and contain its potential spread, he added.“The government needs to further lift this kind of restrictions for accessing the countryside,” Quintana said. “And in this case, with the outbreak of the coronavirus, how serious it is, that may be something that the government should reconsider.”Quintana said he believes North Korea needs help to contain the virus.“

TEL AVIV, ISRAEL - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu vowed on Wednesday to maintain a permanent Israeli military force in the West Bank, saying that if it weren't for Israeli troops stationed there Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas would be "overrun in two minutes" by Hamas militants. Netanyahu told a major Jewish conference that Israel doesn't have the liberty to repeat its mistake in Gaza, where Hamas violently seized control from Abbas after Israeli forces withdrew from the coastal strip. Asked about his vision for the West Bank, Netanyahu said he preferred to avoid labels such as "Palestinian state." But he did make clear his view that Abbas, known by his nickname Abu Mazen, and his Palestinian Authority owe their very existence to Israel's protection, and directly benefit from the presence of Israeli troops. "They'd be overrun in two minutes. A couple of years ago we uncovered a plot of 100 Hamas men to overthrow Abu Mazen. Overthrow? Kill him. Not kill him politically. Kill him. So, if we weren't there, they'd not be there, which is exactly what happened when we left Gaza," Netanyahu told an assembly of the Jewish Federation of North America. Israel withdrew from the Gaza Strip in 2005, evacuating its settlements there and pulling back all its troops.

OSLO, NORWAY - Norway's Lutheran Church voted on Monday in favor of allowing same-sex marriage, becoming the latest of a small but growing number of churches worldwide to do so.Last year the French Protestant Church allowed gay marriage blessings, while the U.S. Presbyterian Church approved a change in the wording of its constitution to include same-sex marriage.In a vote at the annual conference of the Norwegian Lutheran Church on Monday 88 delegates out of 115 in total backed same-sex marriage."Finally we can celebrate love independently of whom one falls in love with," said Gard Sandaker-Nilsen, leader of the Open Public Church, a religious movement within the church that had campaigned to change the rules.Under the new rules, priests who do not want to marry a same-sex couple will still have the right to object.The vote by Norway's Lutheran Church reflects increasingly liberal attitudes in wider Norwegian society to issues such as homosexuality.Norway became the second country in the world after Denmark to allow same-sex registered partnerships in 1993. The Nordic country of 5.2 million people has allowed civil same-sex marriage since 2009.

The rapid advancement of instant communication technology has been transforming customs and politics in developing countries around the world. With the rise of social media platforms, messaging apps, and other digital communication tools, people in these nations are now able to connect and share information more easily and quickly than ever before.One major impact of this shift is the way political movements and protests are organized and spread. In the past, it could take weeks or even months for news to travel across a country, making it difficult for citizens to mobilize and coordinate. However, with the advent of instant communication, individuals can now spread information about government actions or injustices in real-time, leading to more organized and widespread demonstrations.Additionally, instant communication has also been changing cultural customs and traditions in developing countries. People are now able to connect with others from different backgrounds and regions, leading to a blending of cultures and the sharing of customs and traditions. This has led to a more interconnected and globalized world, where ideas and practices are more easily exchanged and adopted.While instant communication has certainly brought about positive changes in the developing world, it has also raised concerns about privacy, misinformation, and the spread of hate speech. Governments and individuals must navigate these challenges carefully to ensure that the benefits of instant communication can be maximized while mitigating the risks.Overall, the impact of instant communication on customs and politics in the developing world is undeniable. As technology continues to advance, it will be crucial for societies to adapt and find ways to harness its power for the greater good.

ISTANBUL - With Turkey facing growing criticism from many of its Western allies, there is one nation it is developing closer ties with - France. Until recently, the two countries had endured a frosty relationship, but they are finding common ground over Syria. NATO member Turkey has been at odds with many of its Western allies over Syria and the battle against the Islamic State.But last month French President Francois Hollande hosted his Turkish counterpart, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, in Paris and made clear both had similar views on strategy in Syria.This meeting underscored a rapidly developing bilateral relationship, according to diplomatic columnist Kadri Gursel of the Turkish newspaper Milliyet and the Al Monitor website."After [French President Nicolas] Sarkozy has left office, relations between Turkey and France has become less complicated. We have to admit the bilateral between France and Turkey went quite well. On the economic perspective, on the political perspective and on the question related to Syrian question," said Gursel.Hollande's predecessor, Nicolas Sarkozy, enraged Ankara by blocking its EU membership bid, arguing Turkey is not a European country. Relations were further soured by Sarkozy backing Armenia’s claim Ottoman Turkey committed genocide against its Armenian minority, a charge Ankara denies.The Turkish government retaliated by shutting out French companies from the country’s large-scale construction projects. Carnegie Institute in Brussels visiting scholar Sinan Ulgen says French companies are benefiting from the improved relations."One certain benefit for France of this rapprochement, would be that the French companies who are interested in these big public contracts.

In the Republic of Congo, results from Sunday's election for 84 legislative seats are expected by the end of the week. Election monitors and oppositions parties complained the second round was not much better than the first, which was marred by accusations of fraud. Phuong Tran reports from VOA's West African bureau in Dakar that supporters of President Denis Sassou-Nguesso won most of the seats in that round.Forty small parties boycotted Sunday's election. Even though main opposition groups took part, analyst Paul Simon Handy says the outcome is evident."The election was far away from what can identify as free and fair," he said. "What the government is basically looking for is to ensure a majority and to push its lawmaking process. It is a slow and certain return to authoritarian rule."Election monitors complained of incomplete voting lists, as well as missing poll workers and identity cards. But officials from the National Electoral Agency said Sunday's election proceeded well.Even without electoral problems, Handy, of the South African Institute for Security Studies, says the long-alienated opposition is too fragmented to compete against the president."This opposition suffers from a weak organizational structure," he said. "The opposition is very divided, not really structured [and] with leaders who do not think about anything, just coming to power."Earlier this year, President Sassou-Nguesso named Pastor Ntumi, a representative of the major opposition group, the Ninjas, to head the country's peace and reconciliation efforts.Handy says to reach lasting peace, President Sassou-Nguesso needs to hold political dialogue with all of the armed military groups and exiled leaders. The analyst also says a new election overseen by international observers must be organized.The Republic of Congo is still recovering from civil wars that killed thousands and displaced about a quarter of the population. A peace accord signed earlier this year formally ended the revolt.

At least 20 people have been confirmed dead in the wildfires that are currently raging near Athens, Greece. The wildfires have been spreading rapidly due to strong winds and dry weather conditions in the area. Thousands of residents have been forced to evacuate their homes as the fires continue to burn out of control.Emergency services are working tirelessly to contain the wildfires and protect the affected communities. Several homes and buildings have already been destroyed, and the authorities fear that the death toll could rise as they continue to assess the damage.Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis has declared a state of emergency in the region and has requested assistance from other European countries to help combat the wildfires. Firefighting aircraft and helicopters have been deployed to assist in the efforts to extinguish the flames.The wildfires near Athens are a stark reminder of the devastating impact of climate change and the importance of taking action to prevent further destruction. Our thoughts are with the families of those who have lost their lives and with all those who have been affected by this tragic event.

MOSCOW - Russia has detained an American citizen in Moscow on accusations of spying, according to Russian state media.Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) officials issued a statement on Monday saying U.S. citizen Paul Whelan had been detained on December 28 “while carrying out an act of espionage,” and that they have opened a criminal probe.They provided no further details, but Russia’s state-run TASS news agency said that Whelan faces up to 20 years in prison if convicted.A State Department spokesperson said Monday the United States is aware of Russian authorities' detention of a U.S. citizen."We have been formally notified of the detention by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs," the official said in an emailed statement to VOA. "Russia’s obligations under the Vienna Convention require them to provide consular access. We have requested this access and expect Russian authorities to provide it."

The United States has imposed new sanctions targeting Iran's petrochemical industry in an effort to increase pressure on the country's economy. The sanctions specifically target Persian Gulf Petrochemical Industries Company, one of the largest petrochemical producers in the region. The US Treasury Department stated that the company has provided financial support to the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), a branch of Iran's military that the US has designated as a terrorist organization.This move comes as tensions between the US and Iran continue to escalate, with the US withdrawing from the Iran nuclear deal and imposing numerous sanctions on the country. The US has accused Iran of sponsoring terrorism and destabilizing the region, which Iran denies.The sanctions are aimed at cutting off Iran's sources of revenue and weakening its ability to fund its nuclear program and support militant groups in the region. The US has urged other countries to join in enforcing the sanctions, although some European countries have expressed their opposition to the US's unilateral approach.Iran has condemned the new sanctions, calling them "economic terrorism" and vowing to continue its petrochemical exports despite the US measures. The Iranian government has also accused the US of trying to dictate its foreign policy and interfere in its internal affairs.It remains to be seen how effective these new sanctions will be in achieving the US's goals of pressuring Iran to change its behavior. With tensions already running high between the two countries, the situation in the region is likely to remain volatile in the coming months.

The Sri Lankan government is urging European Union truce monitors to proceed with their mandate in the troubled island nation.Tamil Tiger rebels called for the replacement of the truce monitors after the EU labeled the rebel group a terrorist organization. The rebels told a Norwegian envoy earlier this week that the monitors from Denmark, Finland and Sweden must go. That would mean replacing 37 of the current 57 monitors now in the rebel-held territories.A government statement Saturday called the demand unreasonable. It said a unilateral decision is unacceptable and that the matter requires negotiations by all parties.Also Saturday, defense ministry officials said that gunmen shot and killed two soldiers. Authorities blamed Tamil Tiger fighters for the killings.Rebels have been fighting for more than two decades to establish a separate homeland for Sri Lanka's ethnic minority Tamils.

Dhibba Gaddisa, the father of Biyyansa Dhibba Gaddisa, told VOA that his son’s death was due to a beating he suffered when militia went to his son’s home in Mida-Qanyii district on 7 April.Biyyansa was an active supporter of the Oromo People’s Congress, which fielded opposition candidates for parliament in the 23 May election.Police Commander Demelash Gebremichael told media during a televised briefing on Biyyansa’s death that hospital records indicate Biyyansa died of intestinal cancer and pneumonia.The family alleges he was attacked and beaten in his home in western Shoa. Dhibba said local television reports by Oromiya police twisted his own account of the cause of his son’s death. Local officials deny that Biyannsa was attacked by local militia. VOA interviewed Dargi Marara, a cousin of Biyyansa, who said he has in his possession the X-rays from Black Lion Hospital, where Biyyansa was first taken, to prove his cousin was severely beaten. After he took the X-rays, Dargi said he escaped arrest by police agents who fired at him. He said he is in hiding and still has the X-rays.Police Commander Demelash has accused the Oromo Federalist Democratic Movement chairman, Bulcha Demeksa, of providing false information to the media when he was interviewed by Reuters.

In a bid to support the struggling automotive industry, the British government has announced a new stimulus package to help carmakers in the country. The measures include financial support, tax incentives, and infrastructure investments aimed at boosting the sector's recovery from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic.This lifeline comes as welcome news for car manufacturers who have been hit hard by the economic downturn caused by the pandemic. The automotive industry plays a significant role in the UK economy, providing thousands of jobs and contributing significantly to the country's GDP.Industry leaders have welcomed the government's intervention, stating that it will help sustain businesses and protect jobs in the sector. They are hopeful that these measures will provide the necessary support for carmakers to weather the current challenges and emerge stronger in the post-pandemic economy.The government's decision to throw carmakers a lifeline reflects its commitment to safeguarding key industries and supporting economic recovery. With the right support in place, the automotive sector in Britain can look forward to a more sustainable and prosperous future.

With less than 12 days before Ghana's presidential election, some citizens are expressing their frustration with several opinion polls which they say are heightening tension in the country. They said members of the two leading parties are using the polls to cause deep divisions among ordinary Ghanaians, which they said could undermine the country's democracy. Three of the latest polls showed the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP) and the main opposition National Democratic Congress (NDC) leading the other political parties ahead of the election. Vladimir Antwi-Danso is a senior lecturer at the Legon Centre for International Affairs of the University of Ghana. He tells reporter Peter Clottey that he blames the media and some politicians for misinformation that he says is driving up tensions in the country."The point is that I do not think that it's only the polls that are creating tensions or, which you could regard as divisive. For quite a long time the country has been polarized on a kind of dichotomous line between NPP and NDC and I strongly blame the media because every innocuous situation is put into and NPP-NDC dichotomy.
