He emphasized the need for a unified approach in tackling security challenges and called for the support of the international community in this endeavor.The Somali president-elect's commitment to reset security comes at a critical time for the country, which has been plagued by years of conflict and instability. His promise to prioritize security reflects the urgent need to address the ongoing security threats facing Somalia and to create a safe environment for its citizens.As President-elect Mohamud prepares to take office, the Somali people are hopeful that his leadership will bring about positive changes and lead to a more secure and stable future for their country.

Retail sales in the United States have recently shown a concerning trend, indicating potential risks to the country's economic outlook. The latest data released by the Commerce Department revealed that retail sales fell by 1.3% in May, the largest drop in more than a year.This unexpected decline has raised worries among economists and analysts about the strength of the US economy as it continues to recover from the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. Consumer spending, which accounts for a significant portion of economic activity, plays a crucial role in driving growth.The weak retail sales figures are attributed to various factors, including a decrease in demand for big-ticket items like automobiles and a shift in consumer preferences towards services as pandemic restrictions ease. Additionally, supply chain disruptions and ongoing labor shortages have also contributed to the slowdown in retail activity.While some experts believe that the decline in retail sales may be temporary and could bounce back in the coming months, others warn that it could signal broader challenges for the US economy. With inflation on the rise and concerns about the sustainability of economic growth lingering, policymakers will be closely monitoring the situation to gauge the need for any potential intervention.

The Pakistani Army has announced significant advances in its ongoing operations against pro-Taliban militants in the Swat Valley region. According to a statement released by the army, their forces have made major gains in key areas, pushing back the militants and capturing several strategic locations.The military offensive, which has been ongoing for several weeks, aims to root out the presence of Taliban fighters in the Swat Valley and restore peace and stability to the region. The militants have been responsible for a wave of violence and instability in the area, including targeted attacks on security forces and civilians.The Pakistani Army's success in pushing back the militants is seen as a significant victory in the fight against terrorism in the region. The army has been conducting targeted operations against the Taliban, using both ground forces and airstrikes to weaken their stronghold in the Swat Valley.The recent gains made by the Pakistani Army are a testament to their commitment to combating extremism and ensuring the safety of the local population. The army has vowed to continue their operations until the region is completely cleared of militants and peace is restored to the Swat Valley.

MOSCOW - Russia cautiously celebrated a move by the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) to reinstate its own laboratory for testing athletes for performance enhancing drugs, a decision that has divided the sports world by clearing a path for Russian athletes to return to international competition following a three-year suspension over allegations of state-sponsored doping.The decision by WADA marks the latest chapter in the long-running saga that has divided Russia and the West in recent years, including the Russian military intervention in Ukraine, meddling in the 2016 elections in the U.S., and intervention in Syria’s civil war.In Russia, the move was heralded as largely overdue recognition of its progress on an issue Russian sports officials say goes beyond Russia.FILE - Russia's Sports Minister Pavel Kolobkov addresses the media in Moscow, Feb. 1, 2018.“

The European Union has decided to back limited sanctions against Iran's nuclear program following months of inconclusive negotiations. From Paris, Lisa Bryant the EU's decision Tuesday is influenced by North Korea's nuclear bomb test last week.European Union foreign ministers meeting in Luxembourg have called for incremental sanctions, initially targeting people and materials linked to Iran's nuclear enrichment activities. Among the officials who spoke out strongly against Tehran's refusal to give up its nuclear program was French Foreign Minister Philippe Douste Blazy, who said the Iranian government left the EU no choice but to support limited sanctions.The Reuters news agency reports that the six nations involved in negotiations with Iran will discuss ways to go ahead with a sanctions resolution at the United Nations on Wednesday.The EU's decision follows weeks of inconclusive talks, principally involving the EU's foreign policy chief Javier Solana and Iran's top nuclear negotiator Ali Larijani. The West has offered Iran an incentive package to coax it to abandon it nuclear program, but Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad insists Tehran has the right to continue what he says are nuclear programs for peaceful purposes.Despite the impasse, Daniel Keohane, an analyst at the Center for European Reform in London, says the discussions were not a complete failure."It was good to keep the Iranians at the table, to keep them talking. The problem was perhaps expectations were a bit high."Keohane also says the Iranian leadership is divided over how hard a line to maintain in the nuclear negotiations.

Mozambique is gearing up for its upcoming elections, set to take place on October 15th. The country's citizens will have the opportunity to cast their votes and choose their leaders for the next term.Preparations for the elections are in full swing, with political parties campaigning vigorously across the nation. Candidates are outlining their visions for the country's future and engaging with voters to garner support for their respective parties.The elections are crucial for Mozambique, as the country continues to recover from the devastating impact of Cyclone Idai earlier this year. The new government will be tasked with addressing the challenges of rebuilding infrastructure, providing aid to affected communities, and ensuring the country's long-term stability and growth.As election day approaches, Mozambique's citizens are urged to exercise their democratic right to vote and participate in shaping the future of their country. The international community will be closely monitoring the electoral process to ensure transparency and fairness in the results.With just a few weeks left until the elections, Mozambique is bracing itself for a pivotal moment in its democratic history. The outcome of the elections will have far-reaching implications for the country and its people, making it crucial for all stakeholders to ensure a smooth and peaceful electoral process.

The European Union has raised concerns over human rights issues in Turkey, stating that the country's record on these fundamental rights is critical. This comes after a recent crackdown on dissent and freedom of expression in Turkey, including the arrest of journalists, academics, and activists.The EU has called on Turkish authorities to uphold their international human rights obligations and ensure that all individuals in the country are able to exercise their rights freely and without fear of reprisal. The EU also emphasized the importance of a free and independent media in a democratic society.Turkey has been a candidate for EU membership since 1999, but progress has been slow due to concerns over the country's human rights record and democratic practices. The EU's criticism of Turkey's human rights situation is likely to further strain relations between the two parties.The Turkish government has defended its actions, stating that it is necessary to maintain stability and security in the country. However, human rights organizations have raised alarms about the increasing crackdown on dissent and the erosion of basic freedoms in Turkey.The EU's criticism of Turkey's human rights situation highlights the ongoing challenges facing the country as it seeks to balance security concerns with democratic values. It remains to be seen how the Turkish government will respond to these concerns and whether it will take steps to address the issues raised by the EU.

MOGADISHU - Somalia's al-Shabab publicly cut off the right hands of two men on Thursday after a sharia court convicted them of breaking into a shop and stealing about $500, the al-Qaida-linked group said.The two men had confessed to the theft in Tiyeglow district of the southern Bakool region and were ordered to refund the money, Moalim Gedow, al-Shabab's Bakool governor told Reuters.An al-Shabab ambulance then took the men to hospital, Abdullahi Madey, a Tiyeglow resident told Reuters.Al-Shabab, which has been fighting for years to topple the central government and impose its own strict interpretation of Islamic law, carries out executions, floggings and amputations after summary trials in cases ranging from espionage to theft.

Lawyers for some of the victims of last year's shooting massacre in Norway have called for a new evaluation of the gunman, saying doctors monitoring him have seen no signs of paranoid schizophrenia and have not medicated him.A court in Oslo released a letter from the lawyers this week. The court is to decide by mid-January if Anders Behring Breivik should be given a new psychiatric evaluation.In November, two court-appointed psychiatrists declared the confessed gunman criminally insane.The 32-year-old Breivik set off a car bomb in Oslo on July 22 last year and then and went on a shooting rampage at a nearby youth summer camp, killing 77 people. Even though Breivik confessed, his lawyer says he denies criminal responsibility because he believes the massacre was needed to save Norway and Europe from an influx of Muslims and to punish politicians for embracing multiculturalism.Breivik's trial is set to begin April 16. If he is considered insane, he could end up in psychiatric care rather than getting a long prison sentence.Some information for this report was provided by AP and AFP.

Police removed a group of protesters at Paris' Republique Square on Friday afternoon. The protesters had gathered in the square to demonstrate against the government's recent policy decisions. The police arrived at the scene in riot gear and used water cannons to disperse the crowd.Eyewitnesses reported that some protesters were taken into custody as they resisted the police's orders to leave the square. The protest had initially been peaceful, with demonstrators waving banners and chanting slogans. However, tensions escalated when the police intervened to break up the gathering.The protest comes amidst growing public discontent over the government's handling of various issues, including the economy and the COVID-19 pandemic. President Macron's popularity has been waning in recent months, with many citizens expressing frustration over his administration's policies.Authorities have yet to comment on the incident at Republique Square. The police have since cordoned off the area, preventing any further gatherings from taking place. The situation remains tense, with both protesters and law enforcement on high alert.

United Nations delegates are demanding better coordination between UN rescue teams and non governmental organizations (NGOs) during natural disasters such as the recent earthquake in Indonesia. The appeal came at a regional conference in Budapest, Hungary of the UN's International Search And Rescue Advisory Group (INSARAG), a global network of more than 80 countries and disaster response organizations. A large number of natural disasters, centered in Southeast Asia the past few weeks, has helped to motivate United Nations delegates to agree to improve cooperation between U.N. rescue teams and non-governmental organizations.Among those expressing concerns about current inefficiencies is Hungarian official Attila Tatar, the outgoing chairman for Africa, Europe and the Middle East of the UN's International Search And Rescue Advisory Group. Tatar told VOA News that he and other delegates of the Advisory Group are concerned about what he calls "disaster tourism" where independent groups seem to compete with UN-led efforts. "I think it's a better way if we move together and everything will be synchronized. If a country for example wants special technical rescue teams and the non governmental services have not these teams but rather send water rescue teams, that's a big problem," he said.Tatar believes coordination is urgently needed and points to the size of the recent humanitarian disasters such as the earthquake and tsunami that killed more than 1,000 people in Indonesia and the Pacific islands of Samoa, American Samoa and Tonga this week.

Ethiopia has begun a nationwide campaign to immunize at least 16-million children over the next month.The Ministry of Health said 100,000 health workers and volunteers would go door-to-door to administer the polio vaccine to children under the age of five.The $15-million immunization drive is supported by the health ministry, the U.N. children’s agency (UNICEF), Rotary International and American and European aid agencies.UNICEF's senior programme officer in Ethiopia, Bruno Maes, praised the government’s effort to combat the renewed spread of wild polio virus. But he noted that past immunization efforts did not reach all of Ethiopia’s children.Health officials have confirmed 18 cases of wild polio virus have now been detected in Tigray, Amhara and Oromia regional states.

NOUAKCHOTT - Mauritania’s ruling party candidate Mohammed Ould Ghazouani has won an absolute majority of votes in the country’s presidential election, the electoral commission said Sunday.UPR candidate and former defense minister Ghazouani, 62, won 51.1 percent of the vote, according to data published on the National Electoral Commission (CENI)’s website.Ghazouani has promised to transform national industries to create more jobs around the country’s natural resources. He has also promised that he would regularly meet with members of opposition parties represented in parliament if elected president.President Mohamed Ould Abdel Aziz is stepping down, as is mandated by the constitution, after his two five-year terms. Saturday’s elections were seen as the country’s first democratic transition of power. The country’s last elections in 2014 were heavily criticized for being unfair and were boycotted by many opposition parties. Then-incumbent President Aziz won by 84%.

WASHINGTON - U.S. President-elect Joe Biden met Wednesday with his transition advisers as he continues to plan for taking control of the American government when he is inaugurated on Jan. 20.The projected winner of the Nov. 3 election, Biden has named an array of advisers to look at the operations of agencies throughout the government. He said Tuesday he could announce some key appointments before the annual Thanksgiving holiday on Nov. 26.What It Means to Become President-Elect in the USIt's the title borne by the winning candidate for the period between him or her prevailing in a presidential election and being sworn into officePresident Donald Trump has not conceded his apparent loss to Biden in last week’s national election and has filed numerous lawsuits contesting the outcome in key battleground states.With scant evidence so far, Trump has claimed that voting and vote-counting irregularities cost him the election. He is seeking to overturn Biden’s victory and claim a second four-year term in the White House.So far, however, judges have dismissed all the Trump lawsuits, with more yet to be considered.

The United States has launched two more satellites to peer at the sun and help scientists better predict when solar storms might disrupt Earth's electrical systems. The twin orbiters will provide the first three-dimensional view of these gigantic solar blasts.Scientists once thought the space between Earth and Sun was a vacuum. But we now know that the sun fills the void with gusts of hot, electrically charged atomic particles called the solar wind. Sometimes this wind blows hard.When the sun's outer layer, the corona, is very active, it hurls nearly one-third of its gaseous matter outward at supersonic speeds. Sometimes these so-called coronal mass ejections are aimed at Earth.Solar scientist Michael Kaiser at the U.S. space agency NASA says a heavy blast can produce a shock wave that compresses Earth's magnetic field. This can cause some lovely atmospheric colors, but otherwise wreaks havoc. "These particles can cause the northern lights, but they can also cause electrical damage to spacecraft that are in orbit or even to ground power systems and conceivably even astronauts. If they were exposed to radiation from one of these storms, they could be harmed," he said.Early detection of these solar charged-particle clouds is one of the goals of U.S., European, and Japanese solar observation satellites now in orbit.This detection allows government alerts to go out for operators to take protective measures with electrical grids, satellites, and astronauts.

In a historic move, the Episcopal Church has elected its first female Presiding Bishop. Bishop Susan Jones, a seasoned clergywoman known for her dedication to social justice issues, is poised to lead the church in a new direction that blends religion with activism.Bishop Jones, who previously served as the Bishop of a diocese in the Midwest, brings a wealth of experience and a fresh perspective to her new role. She is committed to promoting inclusivity within the church and working towards a more equitable society for all.Her election has been met with praise from both within and outside the church, with many hopeful that her leadership will bring about positive change. Bishop Jones has already outlined her vision for the church, which includes a focus on addressing climate change, supporting LGBTQ rights, and advocating for racial justice.As the first woman to hold this position, Bishop Jones hopes to inspire other women to pursue leadership roles within the church and beyond. She believes that by blending religion with activism, the Episcopal Church can play a meaningful role in creating a more just and compassionate world.With Bishop Jones at the helm, the Episcopal Church is poised to embark on a new chapter of progress and transformation. Her dedication to both her faith and her activism make her a powerful force for change, and many are eager to see the impact she will have on the church and the world at large.

South African President Cyril Ramaphosa is facing scrutiny and criticism over the country's struggling economy, with unemployment rates hitting record highs. In response to this pressure, Ramaphosa is now focusing on developing a new jobs plan to stimulate economic growth and create employment opportunities for South Africans.The country's economy has been hit hard by the COVID-19 pandemic, with lockdowns and restrictions leading to widespread job losses and economic downturn. South Africa's unemployment rate currently stands at 32.6%, the highest it has been in over a decade.In an effort to address this crisis, Ramaphosa has announced plans to invest in infrastructure projects, boost manufacturing, and support small businesses to create new job opportunities. The President has also highlighted the importance of investing in skills development and education to prepare the workforce for the changing economy.Critics argue that more needs to be done to address the root causes of unemployment in South Africa, including structural issues such as inequality, lack of access to quality education, and a reliance on extractive industries. However, Ramaphosa remains optimistic about the potential for his jobs plan to make a positive impact on the country's economy and workforce.As South Africa continues to grapple with the economic fallout of the pandemic, all eyes will be on Ramaphosa's efforts to turn the tide and create a more prosperous future for all South Africans.

Nearly 1,800 people were arrested Saturday across France in the latest round of “yellow vest” protests.Nationwide, the Interior Ministry says some 136,000 people rallied against France’s high-cost of living. Protesters also expressed their dismay with the presidency of Emmanuel Macron.Protests were mounted in a number of cities besides Paris, including Marseilles, Bordeaux, Lyon and Toulouse.The ministry said Sunday 1,723 people were arrested nationwide, with 1,220 of them ordered held in custody.Parisian police said they made 1,082 arrests Saturday, a sharp increase from last week’s 412 arrests.A picture taken in Paris, Dec. 9, 2018, shows a broken bus stop and (background) the Eiffel tower a day after a "yellow vest" protests as part of a fourth weekend of nationwide protests.Meanwhile, tourist destinations, including the Eiffel Tower and the Louvre Museum, reopened and workers cleaned up broken glass Sunday.The man who unleashed the anger, President Emmanuel Macron, broke his silence to tweet his appreciation for the police overnight, but pressure mounted on him to propose new solutions to calm the anger dividing France.On Saturday, French Interior Minister Christophe Castaner said violent outbreaks in Paris were “under control” despite ongoing disorderly acts he declared “totally unacceptable.”

Schools and the subway system remained closed Tuesday as Brussels, Belgium remained under tight security while authorities tried to find the key suspect in the November 13 Paris attacks.Belgium's Prime Minister Charles Michel said Monday that because serious threats still exist the capital will be on the highest state of alert until at least next week. But he said the country wants to return to normal and that on Wednesday kids would go back to school and the trains in Brussels will be running.Suspected Paris attacker Salah Abdeslam evaded a massive manhunt Sunday night involving raids in Brussels and its suburbs, but security forces Monday were also focusing on towns in eastern Belgium, suggesting police fear Europe’s most wanted man might be trying to flee to neighboring Germany.On the third day of an unprecedented lockdown of the Belgian capital, officials here remain tight-lipped about the biggest security operation mounted in the country since World War II.On Sunday night, 16 suspects were arrested in more than 19 raids in Brussels and outlying towns to the east and south of Belgium’s capital, which is also home to the main European Union institutions. One of the suspects, who was not named, has been charged in connection with the Paris attacks while 15 others were released.

During his visit to the Georgian capital of Tbilisi, U.S. President George W. Bush was denied a meeting with Abkhaz leader Sergei Bagapsh. This comes as a blow to the Bush administration, which had hoped to discuss the ongoing conflict in the region with Bagapsh.The Abkhaz leader cited scheduling conflicts as the reason for his refusal to meet with Bush. However, analysts believe that the real reason behind the decision may lie in the strained relations between Abkhazia and the United States.The Abkhaz-Georgian conflict has been ongoing for years, with both sides claiming sovereignty over the region.

The US Homeland Security Secretary, Kirstjen Nielsen, has suggested that Jared Kushner, senior advisor to President Trump and his son-in-law, may be part of the ongoing investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 US presidential election. Nielsen made the comment during a recent interview with ABC News, where she expressed concerns about potential security risks posed by individuals who have not been fully vetted.Kushner has been under scrutiny in connection with the Russia probe due to his involvement in meetings with Russian officials during the campaign and transition period. He has also faced criticism for failing to disclose his contacts with foreign officials on his security clearance forms.Nielsen's remarks have raised questions about Kushner's role in the administration and whether he may face legal consequences as a result of the investigation. The White House has not yet commented on the matter.The Russia probe, led by special counsel Robert Mueller, has been ongoing for over a year and has resulted in multiple indictments and guilty pleas from individuals associated with the Trump campaign. The investigation continues to be a source of controversy and speculation in Washington.

The United States and the European Union are launching a renewed push for an international climate deal. Prospects for such an agreement dominated their latest summit, which was hosted at the White House by President Barack Obama. President Obama says the U.S. and the EU are intensifying efforts to get a climate deal next month, when negotiators from around the world meet in Denmark."We discussed climate change extensively and all of us agreed that it was imperative for us to redouble our efforts in the weeks between now and the Copenhagen meeting to assure that we create a framework for progress in dealing with what is a potential ecologic disaster," said President Obama.In a brief statement to reporters after the summit, Mr. Obama did not spell out a specific strategy designed to secure agreement, but European Commission President Jose Manuel Barroso was upbeat that a deal could be struck."With the strong leadership of the United States we can indeed make an agreement," said Jose Manuel Barroso. "We are working on a framework agreement in Copenhagen that will be an important agreement for the world."Other issues discussed at the U.S - EU summit included Afghanistan, Iran, and ways to foster greater cooperation in combating terrorism.They also talked at some length about economic matters. President Obama indicated all sides want progress on trade."

Egyptian authorities have adjourned the second mass trial of members of the Muslim Brotherhood, a political group that has been banned and designated as a terrorist organization in the country.The trial, which began earlier this week, involves over 700 defendants accused of various charges including inciting violence, terrorism, and belonging to a banned organization. The Muslim Brotherhood was declared a terrorist group in Egypt following the ousting of former president Mohamed Morsi in 2013.The adjournment of the trial comes amid ongoing political tensions in Egypt, with the government cracking down on dissent and opposition groups. Human rights organizations have raised concerns about the fairness of the trials and the treatment of the defendants.Critics of the Egyptian government argue that the mass trials are politically motivated and are being used as a way to silence dissent and opposition to the ruling regime.The trial is set to resume at a later date, but the exact date has not yet been announced. The outcome of the trial will be closely watched both domestically and internationally, as it is seen as a test of the Egyptian government's commitment to upholding the rule of law and protecting the rights of its citizens.

The Algerian government and opposition groups came together in a rare show of unity to rally over concerns about security and the economy. Thousands of people gathered in the capital city of Algiers to demand action on these pressing issues.The protests were sparked by a recent increase in terrorist attacks and violence in the country, as well as a struggling economy that has left many Algerians struggling to make ends meet. Both the government and opposition leaders acknowledged the need for urgent reforms to address these challenges.The rally was peaceful, with participants from various political parties and backgrounds coming together to call for change. Many expressed frustration with the current state of affairs and called for greater transparency and accountability from the government.It remains to be seen whether this show of unity will lead to concrete actions to address the concerns of the Algerian people. However, the rally served as a powerful reminder of the importance of national unity in times of crisis.

The search is on for evidence of organic compounds on the planet Mars. According to scientists at the US Space Agency, NASA's Phoenix lander has confirmed the presence of frozen water near the planet's surface. They expect more discoveries as the lander's robotic arm acquires more samples of Martian soil and ice. VOA's Paul Sisco reports.On the surface of the planet Mars, NASA's Phoenix lander began digging with its robot arm last month. The first images of a small trench, a centimeter below the surface, suggested that scientists found what they were looking for: ice made of water. An image, taken June 15, shows small white cubes in the corner. Four days later, they are gone. The scientists say the ice vaporized after exposure to Martian air. "We have found the proof that we've been seeking to show that this hard bright material is really water ice and not some other substance," Peter Smith, a project scientist said.Roger Launius is a space historian at the National Air and Space Museum in Washington DC. "It is enormously significant because water, liquid water specifically, but water in any variety is the fundamental building block of carbon-based life as we understand it," Launius said. "So having it present there and perhaps having it present in great abundance might mean that there was life that evolved on Mars." The scientists hope to drop soil with ice into small ovens on the lander for analysis. That could provide information about the climate history of the red planet and possible evidence of organic compounds."

Bank queues have become a common sight in Zimbabwe, with many people having to wait for hours just to withdraw their own money. The country's ongoing economic crisis, marked by hyperinflation and a shortage of foreign currency, has led to severe cash shortages and limits on withdrawal amounts.The situation has become so dire that people often have to wake up in the early hours of the morning just to stand in line at the bank. Some even spend the entire day waiting, only to be told that the bank has run out of cash.Many Zimbabweans have expressed frustration and anger over the situation, with some blaming the government for mismanaging the economy. Others point to the lack of investment and job opportunities in the country.Despite the challenges, many Zimbabweans have no choice but to endure the long bank queues in order to access their own money. For them, it has sadly become a way of life in the struggling economy.

WASHINGTON - Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell says he has the votes to bust a planned Democratic filibuster of President Donald Trump's Supreme Court nominee as a showdown nears that could change the Senate, and the court, for generations.The Kentucky Republican says Democrats "seem determined to head into the abyss. They need to reconsider."Democrats are making clear they have no plans to do so, and are blaming Republicans for pushing them to attempt a nearly unheard-of filibuster of a qualified Supreme Court pick.

The resignation of former Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Scott Pruitt has left ongoing ethics probes within the agency inconclusive, according to sources within the EPA office.Pruitt, who faced numerous allegations of ethics violations during his tenure, stepped down from his position in July amidst mounting pressure from the public and lawmakers. However, the investigations into his conduct have continued, with some questioning whether his departure has hindered the ability to reach a clear conclusion.The EPA's Office of Inspector General has been leading the investigations into Pruitt's actions, including allegations of misuse of taxpayer funds, conflicts of interest, and retaliation against employees who raised concerns about his behavior. While some progress has been made in uncovering potential wrongdoing, the lack of cooperation from key individuals and the sudden resignation of Pruitt have created obstacles for investigators.Despite the challenges, the EPA office remains committed to completing the ethics probes and holding accountable any individuals found to have violated ethical standards. The resignation of Pruitt may have created a roadblock in the path to justice, but the agency is determined to see the investigations through to their conclusion.

A resumption of fighting in lands in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo previously claimed by rebel Hutu militias has added more than 100,000 newly displaced people to Congo's swollen itinerant rolls in the past two months. The vast expanse of territory, coupled with precarious security arrangements and highly complex logistics of reaching those in need, have accentuated a humanitarian collapse that threatens not only the refugees, but also the local families that have generously offered their facilities to house and assist the less fortunate. In the North Kivu regional capital, Goma, UN High Commissioner for Refugees public information officer for eastern DRC, David Nthengwe, says that attacks this month in eastern towns have resulted in several deaths and in the torching of hundreds of homes."The Hutu rebel group has stepped up its reprisal attacks against the civilians in eastern Congo, literally attacking and burning homes. And on the 17th and 18th of April, they burned over 300 houses, and at the same time, issuing threats that they would continue to attack," he said.Because of Congo's size and the dimensions of the displacement of more than 800,000 civilians since August of last year, camps have been filled to capacity, and local aid groups are unable to reach most of the fleeing victims."When we speak about 100,000 people, we are speaking about several villages that have been affected, where people have been displaced. It's impossible to have in each one of these places an NGO or a UN agency. Now, what's happening is that there is a limited presence of the NGO's. We in UNHCR are establishing a presence in Lubero.

In recent news, there has been growing speculation about the future of the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START). The treaty, which was originally signed in 1991 between the United States and the Soviet Union, has been crucial in limiting the number of nuclear weapons held by both countries.However, the treaty is set to expire in February 2021 and there are concerns that it may not be renewed. The Trump administration has been hesitant to extend the treaty, citing concerns about including China in future arms control agreements.This has raised fears among arms control advocates that without the treaty, there will be no limitations on the number of nuclear weapons possessed by the two countries. Many argue that this could lead to a dangerous arms race and increase the risk of nuclear conflict.There have been calls for both the United States and Russia to come to an agreement on extending the treaty before it expires. It remains to be seen whether the two countries will be able to negotiate a new agreement in time to prevent the treaty from lapsing.The future of the START treaty remains uncertain, but one thing is clear – its expiration would have significant implications for global security.

Zimbabwe has suspended exports of ostrich and other poultry after discovering a strain of avian flu that can be lethal to birds, but poses little risk to humans.Thursday, Zimbabwe's state-run newspaper The Herald reported that the H5N2 strain of avian flu was detected on two farms in Zimbabwe's Matabeleland province.It quotes the state veterinary services director, Dr. Stuart Hargreaves, as saying the virus was confirmed with samples sent to South Africa.The H5N2 strain of avian flu is not known to pass from birds to humans, unlike the H5N1 strain that has killed nearly 70 people in Southeast Asia since 2003.Dr.

Syrians of all ages are trying to flee their country to escape the violence there.Reyhanli Refugee Camp in Turkey, just across the Syrian border, is home to more than 3,000 refugees, many of them children.The Turkish government provides the basics - even a school, a playground and some modest toys.At first glance, the kids are playful, giving the V sign for victory every chance they get. But inside the small tents these children now call home, the reality of their lives is harder to hide.Mustafa Harmoush, 14, came to Turkey with his family eight months ago.HARMOUSH: "We came by car, and were picked up by three Turkish soldiers and brought to the camp."REPORTER: "What do you do here?"HARMOUSH: "Nothing."Mustafa is the nephew of one the first defectors from Bashar al-Assad's army who was kidnapped and brought back to Syria and forced to confess on national television. His older cousin was killed and found tied to a tree. Mustafa's eyes quickly filled with tears when I asked him about Syria. "They arrested my uncles and killed my uncles," Mustafa said.Sara, 13, recently arrived here from Homs, the focus of an intense military crackdown. She says shelling destroyed her home."There was blood all over the place," Sara recalled. "The government was killing people because they thought they were armed. But they were not armed. They were peaceful civilians."Sara says she quit school and leave Homs because the situation for the children, especially the young girls, was extremely dangerous."When the revolution started, the regime started abducting students, especially girls.

IRBIL, IRAQ - Thousands of refugees are pouring out of Fallujah through a safe exit corridor secured by the Iraqi army as the fight to retake the city from Islamic State extremists rages into its third week.According to Lise Grande, the UN humanitarian coordinator for Iraq, 3,300 people fled Monday, joining another 4,000 who escaped during the weekend.Thousands more are expected to make the journey in the coming days, overwhelming humanitarian efforts to help them.Most of the civilians fleeing the fighting are going into 27 camps housing displaced people from other parts of Iraq's Anbar province."The problem is, the camps are full," Grande said.There is a massive effort underway to catch up, with various U.N. agencies and non-governmental organizations working to build new camps, while providing food, safe drinking water, latrines, mobile clinics, and help for women who have suffered violence by IS extremists."We underestimated how difficult conditions were in Fallujah itself," Grande said, adding there were reports that "families have been brutalized, women have been brutalized, children have been brutalized."Watch video report from VOA's Zlatica Hoke:According to the United Nations, 43,000 people have run away from the city, despite the dangers of roadside bombs or being killed by Islamic State fighters.The sudden rush out came after Iraqi forces, working with local tribal fighters, opened one safe exit corridor for civilians.

Alcohol-related crashes killed nearly 18,000 people on American highways last year. Just a few years ago, New Mexico had one of the most serious drunk driving problems in the United States, with more alcohol-related fatalities per capita than any other state. Since then, legislators have addressed the problem with many new laws, including requiring ignition interlocks for repeat "DWI" (driving while intoxicated) offenders. It's basically a breath analyzer installed in the car that won't let the car be started if the driver has been drinking. The interlock requires the driver to pass a re-test at random intervals while driving, otherwise it will shut off the vehicle. It's meant to prevent people from taking a drink after they've started the car or from having a sober person start the car for someone who's been drinking.Other states have similar laws, but in 2005, New Mexico became the first to require interlocks for first-time offenders. Many officials say they're beginning to see positive results from mandatory interlock sentencing for all DWIs. Others disagree, and there have been problems with the law.Until a few years ago, Leslie Hines didn't really think twice about driving home after a few drinks or even after a few too many. But these days, that decision is out of her hands. Before she can start her car, she has to pass the interlock test. She takes a deep breath and blows into the device. A click, a beep, and a few moments later, the interlock message panel displays 'Drive Safely!'In 2004, Hines tried to drive home after a party. She got on the highway heading the wrong way without her headlights on. She says she still doesn't remember what happened. "Never had an accident, never been arrested for DWI, so I figured I was an okay driver after drinking," she explains. "This basically caught me off guard and by surprise.

It has been thirty years since former President George H.W. Bush first introduced his vision of a "thousand points of light" in his inaugural address. Today, his legacy continues to shine brightly through the Points of Light foundation, which has worked tirelessly to promote volunteerism and service in communities across the country.The organization, founded in 1990, has grown to become one of the largest volunteer networks in the world, with affiliates in over 250 cities and partnerships with corporations, nonprofits, and individuals. Its mission is to inspire, equip, and mobilize people to take action and create positive change in their communities.Through programs such as disaster response, youth service, and veterans support, Points of Light has made a significant impact on countless lives and communities. In the wake of natural disasters, volunteers have come together to provide relief and support to those in need. Through youth service programs, young people are empowered to make a difference and become leaders in their communities. And through partnerships with veterans organizations, Points of Light has helped to ensure that those who have served our country receive the support and recognition they deserve.As we reflect on the legacy of George H.W. Bush and the impact of his vision, it is clear that his points of light continue to shine bright, inspiring individuals and communities to come together and make a difference. Through the work of the Points of Light foundation, his spirit of service and commitment to making the world a better place lives on.

A rare opportunity has arisen for food enthusiasts in China, as ancient dishes once reserved for emperors are now available for purchase to the paying public. The delicacies, which have been a well-kept secret for centuries, are now being showcased and sold through a special exhibition in Beijing.The dishes on offer include a variety of exquisite and meticulously prepared meals that were once enjoyed by emperors of the past. From elaborate banquets to everyday meals, each dish provides a glimpse into the culinary traditions of China's imperial history.Visitors to the exhibition have the chance to not only admire the intricate preparation of the dishes but also sample them firsthand. The experience offers a unique opportunity to taste the flavors of the past and appreciate the artistry that went into creating these meals.According to the organizers of the exhibition, the goal is to share this important aspect of Chinese culture with a wider audience. By making these historic dishes available for public consumption, they hope to preserve and promote the culinary heritage of China for future generations to enjoy.For those interested in experiencing a taste of history, the exhibition will be open to the public for a limited time. Don't miss this chance to savor the food of the emperors and immerse yourself in the rich culinary traditions of China.

A center in Syria is making efforts to deradicalize teenagers who were once affiliated with the Islamic State (IS) militant group. The center, located in a region recently liberated from IS control, aims to provide counseling, education, and vocational training to help these teens reintegrate into society.Many of the teenagers at the center were recruited by IS at a young age and were forced to participate in violent activities. Now, they are seeking a way out of the extremist ideology that once consumed their lives.The center is working closely with psychologists, social workers, and religious scholars to provide a holistic approach to deradicalization. The teenagers are encouraged to engage in dialogue, reflect on their past actions, and learn critical thinking skills to challenge extremist beliefs.Despite the challenges ahead, the staff at the center are hopeful that with the right support and resources, these teenagers can successfully reintegrate into society and lead fulfilling lives free from extremism.

Stock markets across the Middle East experienced another day of declines, as ongoing economic uncertainty and political tensions continue to weigh on investor sentiment.In Saudi Arabia, the Tadawul All Share Index dropped by 2.5% as concerns over oil prices and the impact of the ongoing conflict in Yemen persisted. Similarly, the Dubai Financial Market saw a 3% decrease, while the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange fell by 2.8%.Analysts have pointed to a combination of factors contributing to the downturn, including the recent surge in COVID-19 cases, escalating geopolitical tensions in the region, and fears of a global economic slowdown.Investors are closely monitoring the situation and hoping for signs of stability in the coming days. However, uncertainty continues to prevail, and market volatility is expected to persist in the near term.

The United States is planning to unveil a peace plan for the Middle East later this year, according to officials. The plan, spearheaded by the Trump administration, aims to resolve the long-standing conflict between Israel and Palestine.The details of the plan have not been disclosed, but it is expected to address key issues such as borders, security, and the status of Jerusalem. The plan has been in the works for several months and is seen as a significant step towards achieving peace in the region.Both Israeli and Palestinian leaders have been briefed on the plan, and while there is skepticism on both sides, there is also cautious optimism that a breakthrough could be possible. The US hopes that the plan will provide a framework for negotiations and eventually lead to a lasting peace agreement.The Middle East has been marred by conflict for decades, with numerous attempts at peace failing to bring about a resolution. The US has been a key player in previous peace efforts, and hopes that their latest plan will be the one to finally bring an end to the conflict.With tensions high in the region and the situation becoming increasingly volatile, the timing of this peace plan is crucial. Many are watching closely to see if the US can succeed where others have failed, and if a lasting peace in the Middle East can finally be achieved.

April 7th marks World Health Day, a day dedicated to raising awareness about global health issues. This year, the focus is on the theme "Big Threats from Small Bites", highlighting the dangers posed by vector-borne diseases such as malaria, dengue fever, and Zika virus.These diseases are transmitted to humans through the bites of infected insects such as mosquitoes, ticks, and flies. They are responsible for millions of deaths each year, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that more than half of the world's population is at risk of contracting a vector-borne disease.Efforts to control these diseases include mosquito control measures, such as the use of insecticide-treated bed nets and indoor residual spraying. However, these efforts are often hindered by factors such as climate change, urbanization, and drug resistance.On World Health Day, health organizations around the world are calling for increased investment in vector control programs and research to combat these deadly diseases. By raising awareness and taking action to prevent vector-borne diseases, we can protect the health and well-being of millions of people worldwide.

BOGOTA - From the exploitation of workers in carnivals to forcing people into door-to-door sales, more under-reported forms of modern slavery are emerging in the United States which need to be tackled, according to a report released Wednesday.The anti-slavery group Polaris said it had analyzed data from 40,000 likely cases of human trafficking and labor exploitation based on calls to a national hotline since 2007 and divided these into 25 different types of modern slavery.While sexual exploitation in bars and forced labor in tobacco fields, nail salons and homes is well known, other forms of slavery are going under the radar with little action taken to tackle these crimes, said Polaris CEO Bradley Myles.Polaris, which runs the U.S. National Human Trafficking Hotline, said its study was based on the largest amount of data gathered on human trafficking in the United States."We found cases of personal sexual servitude where gang members hold someone basically in captivity in their house for purposes of their own sexual pleasure," Myles told Reuters, adding that traffickers had unique ways of recruiting and controlling victims.Last year, 7,572 trafficking cases were reported to the National Human Trafficking Hotline, a rise of 24 percent on 2015, according to Polaris, with at least 5,550 cases related to sex trafficking and 1,057 to trafficking for forced labor.The most common form of trafficking was sexual exploitation taking place in hotel rooms and private parties, described in the report as escort services.The report also uncovered more obscure types of trafficking, involving victims forced to work in acting, choir and dance troupes or made to operate rides and food stalls at carnivals and fairs. It also uncovered children being forced to beg."Our minds need to be as wide open as possible to see the totality of it than to narrow in on certain types," Myles said.Who is most at risk?

As Ghana's December election approaches, some Ghanaians are expressing their dissatisfaction with the increasing number of polls being conducted. The country has been inundated with various pre-election surveys, with several polling firms releasing their findings on a regular basis.Many citizens feel overwhelmed by the sheer volume of polls and question their accuracy and relevance. Some argue that the constant stream of conflicting poll results only serves to confuse voters and undermine the democratic process.Critics also point out that some polling firms may have biases or agendas that influence their results, leading to potential misinformation and manipulation of public opinion. Additionally, the cost of conducting multiple polls can be substantial and raises concerns about transparency and funding sources.While polling can provide valuable insights into public opinion and help shape political strategies, it is essential that the process is conducted ethically and responsibly. As the election draws nearer, Ghanaians will need to critically evaluate the validity and credibility of poll results to make informed decisions at the polls.

In a recent poll conducted in Palestine, President Mahmoud Abbas and his political party, Fatah, have emerged as the frontrunners in terms of popularity, outpacing their rival group, Hamas. The survey, which was carried out by a reputable research organization, found that a majority of Palestinians view Abbas and Fatah favorably, while support for Hamas has seen a decline.This news comes at a critical time for Palestinian politics, as tensions have been rising between Fatah and Hamas in recent months. The poll results indicate that Abbas and Fatah may have an edge in the upcoming elections, as they continue to garner support from the Palestinian population.It remains to be seen how this newfound popularity will impact the political landscape in Palestine, but one thing is clear - Abbas and Fatah are currently leading the way in terms of public opinion. Stay tuned for more updates on this developing story.

The only known survivor of the airliner that crashed off Comoros this week has been reunited with her family in Paris.Bahia Bakari, 14, returned to France on a government jet Thursday, and was met at the airport by her father and other relatives.Bakari's father, Kassim, told reporters he is happy to see his daughter, but sad for his wife, who is among the 152 people who are missing and presumed dead.Bakari suffered a broken collarbone in the crash. She clung to a piece of floating debris for some 12 hours until she was rescued. The Yemenia Air flight went down in the Indian Ocean early Tuesday.French and American ships are still searching for bodies and the plane's wreckage. The cause of the crash remains unclear. Witnesses reported high winds in Comoros at the time of the crash, while French officials have said inspectors found a number of faults with the airplane during a 2007 inspection.French and Yemeni officials said Wednesday that a signal from one of the so-called "black boxes" had been detected. But the French defense ministry now says that signal was from one of the plane's distress beacons.Earlier this week, Bakari's father said his daughter reported hearing people talking after the crash, indicating that some other people initially survived.Comorans in France angered by the crash tried to block passengers from boarding another Yemenia Air flight in Paris Wednesday.

Police in New Delhi are cracking down on the city?s Tibetan population, after a Tibetan exile set himself on fire this week to protest a visit by Chinese President Hu Jintao.Media reports say hundreds of Tibetan activists were detained or confined to their homes Tuesday as part of ?preventative measures? aimed at stopping another self-immolation bid.The Hindustan Times says police have ordered all Tibetans under house arrest until March 31, since many have disregarded a government ban on protests. As President Hu arrived Wednesday, police hauled groups of angry anti-China activists away from a banned protest near India?s parliament. President Hu is in India for a summit meeting of the so-called BRICS nations - Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa. Prominent Tibetan writer and activist Tenzin Tsundue was arrested while he was giving a speech at an academic seminar on India and Tibet. Hundreds of students at a youth hostel in New Delhi say they were prevented from leaving the facility to take their college exams.The crackdown comes after 27-year-old Tibetan exile Jamphel Yeshi set himself on fire Monday during an anti-Chinese protest. He was taken to a hospital with burns over 98 percent of his body. Activists said Wednesday that Yeshi died from his wounds.This is the second self-immolation by a Tibetan exile in New Delhi in recent months. Tibetan rights groups say 29 Tibetans have set themselves on fire in regions under Chinese control. Yeshi is thought to be the first Tibetan to die in India of self-immolation.China refers to the self-immolators as terrorists who are carefully coordinated by "trained separatists."Tibetans accuse China of pursuing a policy of deliberate cultural extinction in Tibetan-inhabited areas.

MOSCOW - Russia successfully test-launched its Tsirkon (Zircon) hypersonic cruise missile from a military vessel for the first time early last month, the TASS news agency said on Thursday, citing two military sources.President Vladimir Putin talked up hypersonic missiles in a March 2018 speech, saying they were part of a new generation of Russian weapons that could hit almost any point in the world and evade a U.S.-built missile shield. State television presenter Dmitry Kiselyov said in February 2019 that the Tsirkon missile could hit targets in the United States in less than five minutes if launched from submarines. "In accordance with the Tsirkon's state testing programme, in early January this year, the Admiral Gorshkov [ship] carried out the test launch of this missile from the Barents Sea to a ground target in one of the military ranges in the northern Urals," one source told TASS. The next stage in the Tsirkon's development after tests from the Admiral Gorshkov were complete would be a test launch from a nuclear submarine, the source said. The successful launch of the missile, whose January flight exceeded 500 kilometers (310 miles) according to another source, increased Russia's military capabilities.

The Syrian rebel enclave of Idlib is posing a threat to escalating the conflict between Turkey and Syria. The region, which is controlled by various rebel groups, is currently under attack by the Syrian government forces, backed by Russian military support. Turkey, which supports the rebel groups in Idlib, has warned that it will not stand by if the attacks continue and has sent reinforcements to the region.The situation in Idlib has raised concerns about a potential direct confrontation between Turkey and Syria, which could further destabilize the region. The United Nations has also warned of a humanitarian crisis in the area, as thousands of civilians have been displaced by the fighting.The escalating tensions in Idlib come at a time when efforts are being made to find a political solution to the Syrian conflict. The United States, Russia, Turkey, and other regional powers have been involved in diplomatic talks to try and bring an end to the long-standing civil war in Syria.However, the situation in Idlib highlights the challenges that remain in achieving a peaceful resolution to the conflict. With both Turkey and Syria showing willingness to use military force in the region, the international community is on high alert for any further escalation that could have far-reaching consequences for the region.

Turkey is currently facing a challenging diplomatic situation as it tries to balance its relationships with both Western nations and Iran. With tensions rising between the United States and Iran, Turkey finds itself in a delicate position as it tries to maintain strong ties with both sides.Turkey's strategic location between Europe and the Middle East has made it a key player in regional politics. However, the country's ties with Iran have raised concerns among its Western allies, particularly the United States.Despite its membership in NATO and close relationship with the US, Turkey has also been deepening its ties with Iran in recent years. The two countries have collaborated on various issues, including trade and energy.In an effort to navigate this complex geopolitical landscape, Turkey has been attempting to strike a balance between its Western allies and Iran. Turkish officials have emphasized the importance of maintaining good relations with both sides, while also expressing concerns about the escalating tensions in the region.As Turkey continues to navigate this delicate situation, it remains to be seen how it will manage to walk the line between the West and Iran. The country's ability to maintain strong relationships with both sides will be crucial in ensuring its own stability and security in the region.

Uganda's Minister of State for Defense says she is shocked by accusations that soldiers in the Ugandan army have been illegally logging valuable timber from southern Sudan and taking it back to Uganda. The allegations were made earlier this week in a detailed report by the independent Swiss-based research group, Small Arms Survey. VOA Correspondent Alisha Ryu has the story from our East Africa Bureau in Nairobi.Monday's report by the Small Arms Survey is largely based on interviews with eyewitnesses, who say that Ugandan soldiers have been clearing teak forests in southern Sudan since they arrived in the area in March 2003.The report says army officers, described as being business-minded, are supervising the illegal logging. In one forest near Uganda's border with Sudan, witnesses say as many as 200 trees were cut down before local authorities could be notified. The trees, they say, have been hauled away by trucks to Uganda.Teak is durable wood, mainly used in shipbuilding and for manufacturing outdoor furniture. It is resistant to warping and to insect and water damage, making the wood valuable and highly-prized around the world.The Ugandan soldiers are in southern Sudan taking part in Operation Iron Fist, a Sudan-approved military operation aimed at flushing out fighters from the Ugandan rebel Lord's Resistance Army, who moved their bases from northern Uganda to southern Sudan in 1994.

U.S. military judges in Guantanamo acted quickly Wednesday morning to suspend two of the 21 terrorism trials now in progress, based on a request from the Obama administration. Judges in the other trials are expected to do the same. One of the trials suspended Wednesday was the biggest of them all - the case against five men accused of involvement in the September 11th attacks on the United States in 2001. The moves came based on requests from military prosecutors, acting on orders from President Obama.The military trials are suspended for 120 days, and Pentagon Spokesman Bryan Whitman says he expects the Defense Department will soon receive further instructions from the new president. "The president has clearly made his intentions well known, running up to taking his oath of office yesterday in terms of what his intentions are," he said. "And he has taken the first steps with respect to his direction to the secretary of defense to order a pause to military commissions proceedings. I would expect that the department will get additional guidance and direction in the near future."Whitman says that additional direction could come within days, and he expects it to involve "a broad, comprehensive review." Some reports say President Obama will order the Defense Department to come up with a plan to close the Guantanamo detention center and either release its 250 detainees or move some of them to the United States for trial. Whitman could not confirm that, but Defense Secretary Robert Gates has already ordered a fresh study of the issue, in anticipation of such an order.

WASHINGTON - Former U.S. secretary of state Hillary Clinton is nearing the Democratic presidential nomination, but she faces one last contentious political fight in California, the country's most populous state, against her sole challenger, Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders.Clinton, looking to become the first female U.S. president, is very close to amassing a solid majority of delegates to the party's July national nominating convention. She has 2,310 of the 2,383 delegates she needs to be the first woman nominated for president by a major U.S. political party; Sanders has 1,542 pledged delegates.Clinton, the top U.S. diplomat from 2009 to 2013, is likely to clinch the nomination June 7. This could come while Californians are still voting next week, since she is a heavy favorite in the eastern state of New Jersey, where polls close three hours before California.Four other states also are holding nominating contests next Tuesday: Montana, New Mexico, North Dakota and South Dakota, but California is drawing the largest share of Clinton's attention. She has canceled scheduled campaign rallies elsewhere this week to return to the West Coast for a five-day swing through California.She is hoping to avert an embarrassing popular-vote defeat in the biggest state, which would sting even though she would earn a sizable share of the state's 548 delegates to the Democratic national convention, allocated on a proportional basis.Some political surveys show Clinton with a wide lead in California.

A veteran American journalist and his Afghan translator were reported killed in Afghanistan Sunday, after coming under attack while traveling with an Afghan army unit in southern Helmand province.The U.S.-based National Public Radio identified the dead as photojournalist David Gilkey and translator Zabihullah Tammana. Two other journalists in their convoy were reportedly unharmed.No other details were immediately available, and it was not clear late Sunday the size of the military unit or whether it suffered other casualties.U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry, who is in Beijing, said in a statement, "This attack is a grim reminder of the danger that continues to face the Afghan people... and the courage of intrepid journalists."An NPR website described the 50-year-old Gilkey as an award-winning photographer and video editor, and one of the first unescorted journalists to enter Afghanistan after the September 11, 2001, terror attacks on the United States.He also covered the 2008-2009 conflict in the Gaza Strip between Israel and Hamas, known widely as the Gaza War, as well as the aftermath of the devastating earthquake that struck Haiti in early 2010.

LOS ANGELES - Oscar-winning actor Jeff Bridges will receive a lifetime achievement award at January's Golden Globes ceremony for his wide range of work, from Western "True Grit" to comedy "The Big Lebowski," the Hollywood Foreign Press Association said on Monday.Bridges will be honored by the HFPA with the Cecil B. DeMille Award, an annual accolade given to a person who has made a lasting impact on the world of film.The 69-year-old actor has been nominated for five Golden Globes and won once for his role as a faded country music star in 2009 drama "Crazy Heart." That performance also earned Bridges an Oscar. He also famously played the slacker known as "The Dude" in 1998's "Big Lebowski.""

In a statement released earlier today, the White House announced that the CIA Acting Director has been deemed the best woman for the job. The Acting Director, who has not been named in the statement, has been praised for her dedication, experience, and leadership skills.The decision comes after a thorough selection process, which included interviews with several highly qualified candidates. The White House spokesperson emphasized that the Acting Director's appointment is a testament to her capabilities and the trust she has earned within the intelligence community.The Acting Director will now lead the CIA in its crucial mission to protect national security and gather intelligence to support US foreign policy. Her appointment has been met with positive reactions from both sides of the political spectrum, with many praising her expertise and commitment to public service.As the first woman to hold this prestigious position, the Acting Director's appointment is seen as a significant milestone in the history of the CIA. Her leadership is expected to bring a fresh perspective and new ideas to the agency as it faces complex challenges in an ever-changing global landscape.The White House has expressed confidence in the Acting Director's ability to lead the CIA with integrity and excellence, and looks forward to working closely with her to ensure the safety and security of the United States.

Many are expressing skepticism over the recent cease-fire agreement in Syria, with concerns about whether all parties involved will adhere to the terms of the truce. The agreement, brokered by Russia and Turkey, aims to halt the violence in the war-torn country and allow for humanitarian aid to reach those in need.However, previous cease-fire attempts in Syria have been short-lived and often violated by various factions fighting in the conflict. With multiple armed groups and foreign powers involved in the war, the complexity of the situation makes it difficult to guarantee a lasting peace.Some analysts are hopeful that this latest cease-fire could be a step towards a more permanent resolution to the conflict, while others remain cautious given the track record of previous agreements falling apart. The United Nations has urged all parties to respect the cease-fire and work towards a political solution to end the years of bloodshed in Syria.As the world watches closely to see if the cease-fire will hold, the fate of millions of Syrians caught in the crossfire hangs in the balance. Only time will tell if this agreement will bring much-needed relief to the war-torn country.

The Netherlands is gearing up for its annual tulip season, with experts predicting a brilliant display of colorful blooms across the country. The Dutch Tulip Forecast for this year is looking exceptionally bright, with fields expected to be bursting with vibrant hues of red, yellow, pink, and orange.As the tulip season approaches, tourists from all around the world are expected to flock to the Netherlands to witness the beauty of the iconic Dutch flower fields. The vibrant tulip fields make for a popular tourist attraction, drawing visitors who come to admire the stunning sights and take photos amongst the blossoms.Local farmers and growers have been diligently preparing their fields for the upcoming season, ensuring that the tulips are in prime condition for visitors to enjoy. With careful cultivation and attention to detail, the Dutch tulip growers are anticipating a spectacular show of blooms that will captivate visitors and locals alike.The Dutch Tulip Forecast is calling for a brilliant display this season, with a chance of tourists adding to the excitement. As the tulip season approaches, all eyes will be on the Netherlands as the country bursts into color with the arrival of spring.

TUNIS - Tunisia's former Prime Minister Mehdi Jomaa launched a new political party on Wednesday which he said would be non-ideological and could "restore hope for Tunisians" frustrated by the country's transition.Jomaa led a technocratic government in 2014, a year that ended with free elections and a new constitution seen as key steps following the 2011 overthrow of Zine El Abidine Ben Ali.For the past two years, Tunisia has been governed by a coalition led by the secularist Nidaa Tounes party and the moderate Islamist party Ennahda.National politics can still be highly polarized, and some of the problems that helped fuel the 2011 protests remain unresolved, including high unemployment and the continued marginalization of rural areas."After two years since 2014, we passed from hope to frustration, a difficult situation, a lack of strategy, favoritism and corruption," Jomaa said. "We want to restore hope for Tunisians through our alternative party."

Health officials in Haiti say the death toll from the nation's cholera epidemic has risen to more than 1,100, as the government and the United Nations appeal for an end to unrest linked to the outbreak. Haiti's health ministry reported Wednesday that nearly 18,400 people have been hospitalized since the outbreak was first reported late last month. The cholera epidemic in Haiti is beginning to show up elsewhere in the Americas. Health officials in neighboring Dominican Republic, and in the United States, have reported their first cases, raising fears of a repeat of the 1991 cholera epidemic in Peru.Dr. Jon Andrus is the deputy director of the Pan American Health Organization, part of the World Health Organization."The experience of 1991 resulted in some six years of transmission throughout the Americas," said Dr. Andrus. "So we had this epidemic of cases that continued at lower levels, but eventually countries were able to interrupt transmissions, but it took several years." The Pan American Health Organization and other health organizations have issued a joint strategy for controlling Haiti's cholera epidemic and reducing its impact."Haiti has been successful in eradicating polio, measles and ruebella," he said. "With those successes we know they can do the job. This will be more challenging require much longer term commitment and sustainability of partnership and govt ownership but it can be done." Health organizations are already working with the Dominican Republic to prepare for outbreaks there. And they are bracing for outbreaks in other countries in the Americas."

CAPITOL HILL - U.S. senators of both political parties said Thursday that the public's window into federal probes involving Russia and related matters could be constrained now that a special counsel has been appointed to lead the probe.Senators spoke after meeting Thursday behind closed doors with Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who appointed former FBI Director Robert Mueller to head the Justice Department's investigation of Russian meddling in last year's presidential election and any collusion by the Trump campaign.WATCH: Graham: Rosenstein to Proceed as Criminal InvestigationSorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline."Congress' ability to conduct investigations of all things Russia has been severely limited," said Republican Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina. "If I were Mr. Mueller, I would jealously guard the witness pool. So, one of the biggest losers in this decision is the public."

Eritrea has accused Ethiopia of using military aggression as a diversionary tactic to shift attention away from internal issues. The two neighboring countries have a long history of conflict and tensions, with ongoing border disputes and allegations of support for rebel groups on both sides.The latest escalation in tensions began when Ethiopia launched a military offensive in the Tigray region, targeting the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF), which the government accuses of attacking federal military bases. Eritrea has denied involvement in the conflict, despite reports of Eritrean troops crossing the border to support Ethiopian forces.Eritrean officials have criticized Ethiopia's actions, calling them a diversionary ploy to distract from the country's domestic problems. They have also accused Ethiopia of trying to drag Eritrea into the conflict in order to justify its actions.The international community has called for a peaceful resolution to the conflict, urging both countries to engage in dialogue and work towards a peaceful resolution. The United Nations has also called for the protection of civilians caught in the crossfire and for humanitarian access to be granted to those in need.As the situation continues to escalate, the world watches closely to see how the conflict between Ethiopia and Eritrea will unfold.

The South African President is facing mounting pressure and calls for his resignation after allegations of personal misconduct have surfaced. The scandal involves accusations of corruption, abuse of power, and unethical behavior.The President's actions have sparked outrage among the public and opposition parties, with many calling for a thorough investigation into the allegations. Some members of the ruling party have also expressed their concerns and have urged the President to step down in order to preserve the integrity of the office.The President has denied any wrongdoing and has vowed to cooperate with any investigations into the matter. However, the pressure continues to mount as more evidence of his misconduct is brought to light.The scandal has caused a rift within the government and has raised questions about the President's ability to lead the country effectively. As the pressure mounts, it remains to be seen how the President will respond to the calls for his resignation and whether he will be able to weather the storm of controversy surrounding him.

A team of researchers from the University of Cambridge has developed a new technique to detect water on planets in other solar systems. This breakthrough could potentially lead to the discovery of habitable exoplanets outside of our own solar system.The new technique involves analyzing the light that passes through the atmospheres of exoplanets. By studying how different molecules in the atmosphere absorb and scatter light, the researchers are able to determine the presence of water vapor.Dr. Sarah Johnson, lead researcher on the project, explained that this new technique could revolutionize the way we search for water on exoplanets. "Water is essential for life as we know it, so finding water on other planets is a key step in the search for habitable worlds," she said.The team tested their technique on a known exoplanet with a water-rich atmosphere and were able to successfully detect the presence of water vapor. This promising result has excited the scientific community and raised hopes for finding potentially habitable exoplanets in the near future.The researchers plan to continue refining their technique and applying it to a wider range of exoplanets in the coming months. With advancements like these, the search for extraterrestrial life beyond our own solar system is becoming more promising than ever before.

MEXICO CITY - Mexico's President-elect Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador vowed Monday his country would not be threatened by a wall, in a thinly veiled rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump's jabs across the border."Mexico is going to become a power — and will change the balance of power. Nobody will threaten us that our borders will be closed or militarized," warned Lopez Obrador, who takes office December 1."This will be possible because the country will grow and there will be jobs."

A recent survey conducted by a major polling organization revealed that 52% of Americans would be "very comfortable" with a woman president. This marks a significant shift in public opinion compared to previous years, indicating a growing acceptance of female leadership in the highest office of the country.The survey also found that 28% of Americans would be "somewhat comfortable" with a woman president, bringing the total percentage of those who are at least somewhat comfortable with the idea to 80%. This shows a strong majority of the population is open to the idea of a female president.While progress has been made in terms of public acceptance of female leadership, there is still work to be done to achieve gender equality in politics. Women continue to be underrepresented in political leadership roles, both in the United States and globally.Despite these challenges, the survey results are a positive sign that attitudes towards female leadership are evolving. As the United States continues to strive for equality and inclusivity in all areas of society, it is encouraging to see a growing number of Americans open to the idea of a woman president.

China blames extremists intent on creating an independent Xinjiang for the bloody riots that killed nearly 200 people earlier this month in the region's capital, Urumqi. Wu Shimin, the vice minister of China's State Ethnic Affairs Commission, says the protesters who caused the violence in Urumqi were not motivated by economic factors.Wu says the rioters were solely seeking independence for the far northwestern region of Xinjiang. But he says China will never allow them to realize their goal.Authorities say unrest in Xinjiang began July 5, when a peaceful protest by ethnic Uighurs in Urumqi turned violent after police intervened. The Uighurs went on a rampage, smashing windows, burning cars and beating people from the country's dominant Han Chinese ethnic group.The government says 197 people died and more than 1,600 were wounded. Chinese officials have even made a rare admission that police killed 12 people during the rioting.
