This statement comes after several activists were reportedly arrested during protests in Havana.The US State Department condemned the actions of the Cuban government, stating that the arrests were a clear violation of human rights and freedom of expression. The department urged Cuban authorities to release all political prisoners immediately and respect the rights of its citizens.The Cuban government, however, has defended the arrests, claiming that the dissidents were inciting violence and destabilizing the country. Cuban officials have also accused the US of meddling in their internal affairs and attempting to undermine the government.Human rights organizations have also expressed concern over the situation in Cuba, with Amnesty International calling for the immediate release of all political prisoners. The organization has called on the international community to put pressure on the Cuban government to respect the rights of its citizens.The US has indicated that it will continue to monitor the situation in Cuba closely and will take further action if necessary.

The Commonwealth has selected Indian diplomat, Aparna Rao, as its new leader. Rao, who previously served as India's Ambassador to the United Kingdom, has extensive experience in international relations and diplomacy. She is the first Indian to hold the position of leader within the Commonwealth and is expected to bring a fresh perspective to the organization. The decision to appoint Rao as the new leader was met with widespread support from member countries, who believe that her leadership will strengthen the Commonwealth's role on the global stage. Rao has already outlined her priorities, including promoting economic growth, addressing climate change, and fostering closer ties between Commonwealth nations. As she takes on this new role, all eyes will be on Aparna Rao to see how she will guide the organization in the coming years.

Indian-American Congressman, Raj Patel, who was recently elected to represent the diverse constituency in his district, sat down for an exclusive interview to discuss his hopes and plans for his time in office.Patel, who ran on a platform of unity and progress, expressed his excitement at the opportunity to serve his community and make a difference. "I am honored and humbled to have been chosen to represent the voices of my fellow Americans in Congress," he said.When asked about his priorities, Patel highlighted the importance of healthcare reform, economic growth, and immigration reform. He stressed the need for bipartisan cooperation to address these critical issues facing the nation."I believe that by working together, we can find common ground and make real progress for all Americans," Patel added.As the first Indian-American elected to Congress from his district, Patel hopes to be a voice for diversity and inclusivity in government. He emphasized the importance of representation and ensuring that all voices are heard in the decision-making process."I am committed to working tirelessly to bring about positive change for our community and our country," Patel concluded.With a fresh perspective and a determination to make a difference, Raj Patel is poised to bring about meaningful change during his tenure in Congress. The constituents in his district can look forward to a promising future under his leadership.

National Security Advisor John Bolton announced today that plans are in motion for a meeting between President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin next month in Paris. The meeting is expected to take place during the commemoration of the 100th anniversary of the armistice that ended World War I.This potential meeting comes amid strained relations between the United States and Russia, with ongoing tensions over issues such as Syria, Ukraine, and alleged Russian interference in the 2016 US presidential election. However, both leaders have expressed a willingness to engage in dialogue to address these issues and improve relations between the two countries.The White House has not yet confirmed the details of the meeting, but Bolton stated that the two leaders have agreed in principle to meet in Paris. It remains to be seen what topics will be discussed and what outcomes may result from the meeting.Critics of the potential meeting have expressed concern that President Trump may be too accommodating towards President Putin, while supporters argue that diplomatic engagement is crucial for resolving conflicts and advancing US interests on the global stage.As preparations for the meeting continue, all eyes will be on Paris next month as the world watches to see what unfolds between the two leaders.

The Obama Foundation has recently announced a significant influx of high-dollar donations from international sources. This news comes as the foundation continues its efforts to promote civic engagement and leadership development around the world.Among the notable donors are wealthy individuals and organizations from various countries, demonstrating the global reach and impact of the foundation's work.

The Chinese government has dismissed a report by the Department of State that says China retains "some elements" of a biological weapons program.The report was mandated by Congress and published by the Associated Press Tuesday. It says evidence indicates that Russia, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and possibly Cuba maintain biological weapons programs.The report also said China retains "some elements" of a 50-year-old biological warfare program, a statement that drew anger from Chinese officials. Zhang Yan of the Chinese Foreign Ministry's arms control department dismissed the report."These statements are far from the truth, and are irresponsible," he said. "We hope that the U.S. side will stop such erroneous practices, and we also express our strong dissatisfaction."The denial came as Chinese officials unveiled a white paper on China's arms control policy.

Protests in Mexico over the increasing number of disappearances have escalated in recent days, with thousands of people taking to the streets to demand justice and action from the government.The protests were sparked by the disappearance of a group of students who were reportedly abducted by a criminal gang. Despite pleas from their families and the public, the authorities have so far been unable to locate the missing students.The government has been criticized for its slow response to the disappearances, with many accusing officials of turning a blind eye to the issue. President Lopez Obrador has promised to prioritize the investigation into the disappearances and bring those responsible to justice.The protests have also highlighted the larger issue of violence and insecurity in Mexico, with many questioning the government's ability to protect its citizens. The United Nations has called for a thorough investigation into the disappearances and for measures to be taken to address the root causes of the problem.As the protests continue to grow in size and intensity, it remains to be seen whether the government will be able to quell the public anger and address the underlying issues that have led to the current crisis.

Syrian rebels have posted an Internet video that appears to show the bodies of 20 soldiers whom they executed in the northern city of Aleppo. In the video published Monday, the bodies of men dressed in military fatigues can be seen lined up on a pavement with their heads bloodied. The men were blindfolded and handcuffed. Rebels also can be heard calling the dead men "dogs." The British-based Syrian Observatory for Human Rights said the incident happened within the past few days. U.N. officials have accused both Syrian government and rebel forces of committing atrocities in the country's civil war. Speaking in Geneva Monday, U.N. High Commissioner for Human Rights Navi Pillay urged the U.N. Security Council to refer Syria to the International Criminal Court, to ensure that all perpetrators of rights violations do not escape justice. Also in Geneva, U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon rebuked the Security Council for failing to take a unified approach to ending the Syrian crisis. He said he is "deeply troubled by the aerial bombardments of civilians by government forces" in Syria, and he expressed concern at increasing sectarian tensions and the deteriorating humanitarian situation.Ban urged nations and Syrians to support the diplomatic efforts of the new U.N.-Arab League envoy for the Syrian crisis, Lakhdar Brahimi.

The  is a new global news program that provides viewers with the latest updates on world events and current affairs. This program covers a wide range of topics, including politics, economics, culture, and more.

TORONTO - Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday that he plans to be respectful and engage the United States with a fact-based approach to solving problems a day after Donald Trump called Canada a "disgrace'' for policies that hurt American farmers. Trudeau said during a news conference alongside visiting Italian Prime Minister Paolo Gentiloni on Parliament Hill that he will stand up for Canada's interests and people. "The way to do that is to make arguments in a respectful fashion, based on facts, and work constructively and collaboratively with our neighbors,'' said the Liberal leader. The U.S. president took aim at Canada's dairy industry this week for creating a new lower-priced classification of milk product that he argues hurts U.S. producers. Trump said it has put farmers in Wisconsin and New York state out of business. Canada changed its policy on pricing domestic milk to cover more dairy ingredients, leading to lower prices for Canadian products including ultra-filtered milk that compete with U.S. milk. Canada's dairy sector is protected by high tariffs on imported products and controls on domestic production as a means of supporting prices that farmers receive. Trump said on Thursday "what they've done to our dairy farm workers is a disgrace''. The U.S. president criticized Canadian policies related to a few industries including lumber, timber and energy, adding that officials will have to get to the negotiating table with Canada very quickly.

UNITED NATIONS - The U.N. special rapporteur on the situation of human rights in North Korea said Wednesday that government is sending tens of thousands of its citizens abroad to work under harsh conditions and is making more than $1 billion a year from their confiscated salaries.In his latest report to the U.N. General Assembly to be formally presented on Thursday, Special Rapporteur Marzuki Darusman says as many as 50,000 workers are laboring under conditions that “amount to forced labor”. The vast majority are working in China and Russia, but others are in Africa, the Middle East and Asia.“I think it reflects the really tight financial and economic situation in the North,” Darusman told reporters.North Korea has been under targeted international sanctions since 2006 for its launch of ballistic missiles and nuclear weapons tests.The special rapporteur said North Koreans are mainly employed in the construction, mining, logging and textile sectors and it is believed the government is earning between $1.2 billion and $2.3 billion a year from this system.In his report, Darusman says laborers often work up to 20 hours each day in unhealthy and unsafe environments with inadequate food rations and rarely a day off. They are threatened with repatriation if they do not perform well or violate rules.

Thousands of people from around the world packed Israel’s streets of Tel Aviv for the city’s annual Gay Pride march, one of many festivals for gay rights taking place this weekend.The festival is billed as the largest event of its kind in the deeply conservative Middle East.Israeli police estimated that more than 100,000 people participated Friday with many coming from other countries.The annual parade featured floats and dancers with this year’s theme being “Bisexuality Visibility.”Israelis and tourists march during the Gay Pride Parade in Tel Aviv Israel, June 9, 2017. About 200,000 people from the LGBT community in Israel and abroad attended in Tel Aviv's annual gay pride parade.The festival is sponsored by the city of Tel Aviv, which has promoted gay tourism in recent years, becoming one of the world’s most gay-friendly travel destinations.While Tel Aviv is seen as liberal and welcoming of gays, Jerusalem is seen as more conservative with the population’s views varying on gay rights.

The turnout at the Mekele Municipal Hall for Arena Tigrai for Democracy and Sovereignty's first public meeting surprised even the organizers, who estimated more than 2,500 attended the February 6 meeting. Most appeared to be young.The party chair, Gebru Asrat, is a former president of Tigrai region and former member of the region's ruling Tigrai People's Liberation Front. However, his party has organized around principles opposed to those of the TPLF. "TPLF is now in a campaign to register the whole Tigrai people under revolutionary democracy without the people's consent," said Gebru.Local police surrounded the hall with tight security and the organizers hosted a lively discussion. In spite of some apparent efforts to disrupt the meeting, Gebru and the party's officers maintained a free and open political discussion. The people need to see free and fair political discussion among political parties, one speaker said.Lawyer and party vice-chair Berhanu Berhe raised the issue of the loss of the port of Assab to Eritrea. "We can claim and argue to get right on the port of Assab legally, historically and politically."Arena Tigrai opposes the revolutionary democracy of the nation's ruling Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front with a political system they identify as liberal democracy.

In a recent press conference, President Trump addressed the controversy surrounding former FBI Director James Comey's firing. Trump downplayed the controversy, stating that the search for a new FBI Director is moving rapidly. Despite the ongoing scrutiny and criticism, Trump remains confident in the progress being made to fill the important position within the bureau. The search for a new FBI Director comes after Comey's unexpected dismissal and has been met with intense speculation and public interest. Trump's remarks signal his determination to swiftly appoint a new leader for the FBI, as the administration faces continued challenges and scrutiny over its handling of the bureau.

ISTANBUL, TURKEY - EU and Turkish officials say they have reached an agreement in principle to resolve the migrant crisis. Turkey, which is hosting over two million Syrian refugees and is the migrants' main transit route into Europe, is seen as the key to stemming the exodus.Pro-government newspapers in Turkey described the emergency summit on the migrant crisis held in Brussels as a historic victory for the country, with the EU agreeing to resettle one Syrian refugee from Turkey in return for every Syrian refugee Turkey takes back from Greece.But the United Nations and international human rights groups are questioning the legality of immediate deportations. Andrew Stroehlein of the U.S.-based group Human Rights Watch said there were too many unanswered questions."Neither side is saying how they are going to assess people individually, how they are going to evaluate their needs for international protection. Two sides are promising these rapid fire mass expulsions not based on anyone’s particular situation," he said. This year alone, more than 120,000 migrants made the perilous journey across the Aegean Sea from Turkey to Greece. More than 200 have died.A man looks at a graveyard in Mytilene, Greece, Feb. 17, 2016, for refugees and migrants who drowned in their attempt to cross the Aegean sea from Turkey to the island of Lesbos.Questions are also being raised over whether the EU will be able to honor the commitment to accept an estimated tens of thousands of Syrian refugees. There is also concern about EU leaders' support of Ankara’s proposal to send Syrians back to safe-haven areas in Syria.Stroehlein said the proposal was alarming."The suggestion that they might send back Syrians back to Syria is just appalling. The idea there is going to be safe zone in anything but in name is just unrealistic in the extreme. This would mean to send people to deadly danger.

French President Emmanuel Macron is set to address the nation following a weekend of violent protests across the country. The protests, sparked by rising fuel prices and the high cost of living, have led to clashes between demonstrators and police, resulting in injuries and arrests.Macron is expected to acknowledge the frustrations of the protesters and announce measures to address their concerns. The government has already announced plans to increase minimum wage and reduce taxes on low-income earners in an effort to alleviate the economic burden on the middle and working class.The protests, which have been ongoing for several weeks, have shaken Macron's government and raised questions about his leadership. Many demonstrators are calling for his resignation, accusing him of being out of touch with the struggles of ordinary citizens.In his upcoming address, Macron is likely to try to strike a balance between acknowledging the legitimate grievances of the protesters and defending his administration's policies. The outcome of his speech could have significant implications for the future of his presidency and the direction of French politics.

Colombian authorities have announced that they have made the largest drug bust in the country's history. The operation took place in the city of Medellin, where police seized over 10 tons of cocaine hidden in a warehouse.According to police officials, the drugs were destined for international markets, including the United States and Europe. The bust is a major blow to drug trafficking organizations operating in Colombia, which is one of the world's largest producers of cocaine.The successful operation was the result of months of intelligence gathering and cooperation between Colombian law enforcement agencies. Several individuals were arrested in connection to the drug bust, and authorities are continuing their investigation to dismantle the criminal network behind the operation.The Colombian government has been working tirelessly to combat drug trafficking in the country, and this latest bust is a significant victory in the ongoing fight against the illegal drug trade.

After generations of trawling the same waters, the fishermen on the coast of Tamil Nadu in southeastern India know where to cast a net or park a boat without resorting to signs or GPS maps.But their customary rights over this common space - a right won by families who have fished it for centuries - are under threat as the demands of modern life threaten age-old livelihoods and their once fertile habitat.First, families' land and precious sea access was usurped by factories and ports. Now, their rights are under fresh attack by a newly amended Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) law."Governments have treated the coastline as an empty space that economic actors can take over, forgetting that it is common property of coastal villages, towns and cities," said Kanchi Kohli, a researcher at think tank Center for Policy Research."The changes to the law negate the socio-ecological uniqueness of this space and opens it up to mindless real estate development, mass-scale tourism and industry," she said.R.L.

Ethiopia has launched a nationwide polio campaign in an effort to protect children from the crippling disease. The campaign will aim to vaccinate over 13 million children under the age of five, with health workers going door-to-door to ensure that every child is reached. Polio, a highly infectious viral disease, can cause paralysis and even death in severe cases. This initiative comes as part of Ethiopia's ongoing efforts to eradicate polio and ensure the health and well-being of its population. The government is urging all parents to ensure their children receive the vaccine to prevent the spread of the disease and protect the future health of the country.

Russian prosecutors launched a search at the Moscow office of Amnesty International, a human rights organization known for its criticism of the Russian government. The search, which took place on Wednesday, comes amidst a crackdown on civil society groups in the country.According to a statement from Amnesty International, the search was conducted as part of an investigation into alleged violations of Russia's laws on "undesirable organizations". The organization has denied any wrongdoing and has called the search a "politically motivated attack".This is not the first time that Amnesty International has faced scrutiny in Russia. In 2016, the organization's office in Moscow was raided by authorities, leading to the organization being listed as a "foreign agent". The label has been used by the Russian government to target NGOs and human rights groups that receive funding from abroad.Critics of the Russian government see the latest search as part of a broader effort to silence dissenting voices and limit freedom of expression in the country.

Sweden's Magdalena Forsberg has continued her dominance of the World Cup biathlon circuit this season by winning a women's 12.5 kilometer mass start event in Brezno-Osrblie, Slovakia.Forsberg finished with one miss in the shooting portion but took her sixth win of the season with a time of 35 minutes, 45.4 seconds. Russian Olga Pyleva was second 32.7 seconds behind the Swede. Germany's Uschi Disl was third with four missed targets.Meanwhile, Finland's Vesa Hietalahti won the men's 15-kilometer mass sprint event, his first World Cup win in six years. Hietalahti miss one target in the shooting and finished the race in 42:50.6.Germany's Alexander Wolf finished second (41.5 seconds back) with two misses in the shooting. Vyacheslav Derkach of Ukraine was third more than one minute, 10 seconds behind the winner.

Despite efforts to implement reforms and improve conditions for Native American women, the latest statistics show that they continue to face alarmingly high rates of crime. According to a recent report by the Department of Justice, Native American women are more likely to be victims of violence, sexual assault, and homicide compared to women of other races in the United States.One of the key factors contributing to this issue is the lack of resources and support for Native American communities. Many reservations lack adequate law enforcement agencies, which leads to delays in responding to crimes and providing assistance to victims. Additionally, jurisdictional issues between tribal, state, and federal authorities often complicate the investigation and prosecution of crimes committed against Native American women.Advocates and tribal leaders have been calling for greater awareness and action to address this crisis. They are pushing for increased funding for law enforcement agencies on reservations, improved coordination between different authorities, and culturally sensitive support services for victims of violence.Despite the challenges, there have been some positive developments in recent years. The reauthorization of the Violence Against Women Act in 2013 included provisions to strengthen protections for Native American women, and some tribes have implemented their own programs to address violence against women within their communities.However, much more needs to be done to ensure the safety and well-being of Native American women. It is crucial for policymakers, law enforcement agencies, and community leaders to work together to address the root causes of violence and create a safer environment for all members of Native American communities.

Delegations from South and North Korea are meeting to discuss the resumption of family reunions. Thousands of families remain separated by the political division of the Korean peninsula. South Korea dispatched a delegation Wednesday morning to the Mount Kumgang resort in North Korea. There they will meet with representatives from Pyongyang to discuss the resumption of family reunions, overseen by the Korean Red Cross. Kim Young-chol, the head of Seoul's representatives, says Kim says his team will try to ensure that during the next round of reunions as many elderly South Koreans as possible will have the opportunity to meet with their separated relatives. Earlier this month, North Korea proposed resuming the reunions. The last time families from the divided Koreas met was in 2007. The communist North called off reunions in response to what it considers South Korean President Lee Myung Bak's hard-line policy. Mr. Lee has pushed North Korea to fulfill its pledges to end its nuclear program and had scaled back aid to the impoverished country.But in recent weeks, the Kim Jong Il government in the North has offered a series of good will gestures to both South Korea and the United States. Last Friday, it sent representatives to honor former South Korean President Kim Dae-Jung, who died a few days earlier.

Archaeologists in the Philippines have uncovered a new species of human cousin, according to a recent study published in the scientific journal Nature. The newly discovered species, named Homo luzonensis after the island of Luzon where the bones were found, is believed to have lived on the island as recently as 50,000 years ago.The bones were discovered in Callao Cave in northern Luzon, where previous excavations have also revealed evidence of early human activity. The team of researchers found several foot and hand bones, as well as teeth, belonging to at least three individuals of Homo luzonensis.What sets Homo luzonensis apart from other human species is its combination of ancient and modern features. The researchers believe that this new species may have descended from an earlier human species that migrated to the Philippines and adapted to its unique environment.The discovery of Homo luzonensis adds to the growing understanding of human evolution in Southeast Asia. It also raises new questions about the diversity of early human species in the region and their interactions with each other.Further research is needed to fully understand the implications of this groundbreaking discovery and its significance in the broader context of human evolution.

A suicide bomber targeted a group of policemen in Southern Afghanistan, resulting in the tragic deaths of 25 officers. The attack took place in the city of Kandahar, a region known for ongoing violence and conflict.The bomber approached the group of policemen and detonated the explosive device, causing chaos and devastation in the area. The casualties included both experienced officers and new recruits who were on duty at the time of the attack.This incident highlights the dangerous and volatile situation in Afghanistan, where security forces are constantly targeted by militant groups. The Afghan government has condemned the attack and vowed to bring the perpetrators to justice.The families of the victims are left to mourn the loss of their loved ones, while the community grapples with yet another senseless act of violence. The international community has also expressed its condolences and support for the people of Afghanistan during this difficult time.

International donors are meeting in Saudi Arabia Saturday to discuss how to help Yemen use billions of aid dollars to strengthen the economy and the government. Nearly four years ago, international donors pledged around $5 billion to help Yemen. Only about 10 percent of that money has been spent so far. Analysts say Yemen's government is plagued by inefficiency and corruption, making it hard to put the money to good use. The attendees, which include the six members of the Gulf Cooperation Council and other international donors, are expected to formulate strategies to overcome these hurdles.Stability in Yemen has become a growing concern for neighboring Saudi Arabia, the United States and others in the international community as al-Qaida increasingly uses Yemen as a base for planning and launching terrorist attacks. Al-Qaida fighters in Yemen claimed responsibility for a failed suicide attack on a U.S.-bound airliner in late December.

The United States has announced plans to offer Russia cooperation on a new missile defense system. This proposal comes as tensions between the two countries have escalated in recent years over various issues, including arms control agreements and alleged interference in each other's domestic affairs.The US hopes that by working together on a missile defense system, it can help alleviate some of the mistrust and build a more stable relationship with Russia. This new initiative is part of President Biden's broader efforts to improve relations with Moscow and reduce the risk of conflict between the two nuclear-armed countries.The details of the proposed cooperation have not been disclosed yet, but it is expected to involve sharing technology and information to enhance the capabilities of both countries' missile defense systems. This move could potentially benefit both countries by increasing their ability to defend against missile threats and reducing the risk of accidental conflict.It remains to be seen how Russia will respond to this proposal, as the country has historically been wary of US missile defense systems deployed near its borders.

President Bush and Kazakh President Nursultan Nazarbayev say they are united in fighting terrorism and spreading democracy in Central Asia. Some human rights groups are criticizing President Nazarbayev's own commitment to democracy inside Kazakhstan.Welcoming President Nazarbayev to the Oval Office, President Bush said the oil-rich nation has been a key ally in the fight against terrorism.Mr. Bush says the United States and Kazakhstan are together in the effort to defeat extremism and support forces of moderation throughout the world. He thanked President Nazarbayev for contributing to the fight against terrorists in Iraq and Afghanistan.Some human rights groups, including Freedom House and Human Rights Watch, had called on President Bush to focus on civil liberties in Kazakhstan during his talks with President Nazarbayev.Since independence in 1991, Mr.

The White House says the detention of protestors in Cuba ahead of a papal visit underscores Havana's "disdain" for the universal rights of its people.In a statement Monday, the White House called for Cuban authorities to "abandon their tactics of intimidation and harassment to stifle peaceful dissent." Cuban authorities detained more than 50 members of the Ladies in White protest group late Saturday and Sunday, including many who intended to participate in the group's weekly protest march Sunday through Havana. An activist with the Ladies in White said more than 30 of the women were detained Sunday as they prepared to attend Mass at a Roman Catholic Church. Others were arrested late Saturday. They have all since been released.Pope Benedict, who is visiting the island next week, is expected to encourage the government to grant increased rights to political activists.

Experts have confirmed that North Korea’s recent missile launches were conducted to test missiles targeting South Korea. The launches, which took place over the past week, were seen as a direct provocation towards the South Korean government. According to military analysts, the missiles tested by North Korea have the capability to reach key strategic locations in South Korea, posing a significant threat to the region. The launches have raised tensions in the area, with South Korea closely monitoring the situation.The United Nations has condemned the missile launches, calling them a violation of international agreements. The South Korean government has expressed concern over the escalating situation and has urged North Korea to cease its provocative actions.It remains to be seen how the international community will respond to these recent developments, as tensions continue to rise on the Korean peninsula. North Korea’s aggressive actions are likely to further strain diplomatic relations in the region.

WHITE HOUSE - Two days before acting director Gina Haspel's confirmation hearing to run the Central Intelligence Agency, the White House is defending her as the best woman for the job."She is 100 percent committed to going through this confirmation process and being confirmed as the next leader of the CIA," White House Press Secretary Sarah Sanders told reporters Monday.The 61-year-old veteran agency operative, who is currently the acting CIA director, offered to withdraw from consideration for the permanent position amid concerns about her involvement in previous harsh interrogation programs, but Trump — according to administration officials — has encouraged her to hold firm."She wants to do everything she can to make sure the integrity of the CIA remains intact, isn't unnecessarily attacked. And if she felt that her nomination would have been a problem for that and for the agency, then she wanted to do what she could to protect the agency," Sanders said.White House press secretary Sarah Sanders speaks during the press briefing at the White House, May 7, 2018. Sanders said the White House has compete confidence in Gina Haspel, President Donald Trump's nominee to head the Central Intelligence Agency.The CIA on Monday delivered "a set of classified documents to the Senate today so that every senator could review acting director Haspel's actual and outstanding record," according to an agency spokesperson. "These documents cover the entirety of her career, including her time in the CIA's Counterterrorism Center in the years after 9/11.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is set to address the nation on the ongoing political standoff with Russia. The tensions between the two countries have been escalating in recent weeks, with Russia amassing troops along the border and Ukraine expressing concerns over a potential invasion.The address comes as Zelensky has called for international support and assistance in deterring any aggressive actions from Russia. The Ukrainian President is expected to outline his government's strategy and response to the current situation, as well as call for unity and resilience among the Ukrainian people.The political standoff between Ukraine and Russia has raised fears of a potential military conflict in the region, with the international community closely monitoring the developments. Zelensky's address is expected to shed light on the current status of the situation and provide reassurance to the Ukrainian population.As tensions continue to rise, the world is watching closely to see how the situation will unfold and whether diplomatic efforts can prevent further escalation. Zelensky's address to the nation will be a critical moment in determining the future direction of the political standoff between Ukraine and Russia.

The Obama administration has recently announced a new strategy for addressing the ongoing crisis in Sudan, drawing cautious approval from anti-genocide groups. The new strategy aims to increase diplomatic pressure on the Sudanese government to end the violence and human rights abuses in the region.Anti-genocide groups have long been calling for a more robust and proactive approach to the situation in Sudan, where conflicts have resulted in widespread displacement and loss of life. The Obama administration's new strategy includes the possibility of imposing targeted sanctions on individuals responsible for the violence, as well as increasing support for peacebuilding efforts in the region.While many anti-genocide groups welcome the new strategy as a step in the right direction, some remain skeptical of the administration's commitment to truly addressing the root causes of the crisis in Sudan. They argue that more concrete actions, such as increased humanitarian aid and diplomatic engagement, are needed to make a meaningful impact on the situation.Overall, the new Obama strategy on Sudan has sparked a cautious sense of optimism among anti-genocide groups, who hope that it will lead to a more effective and sustainable solution to the crisis in the region. However, they also emphasize the need for continued vigilance and pressure on the administration to ensure that it follows through on its commitments to address the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Sudan.

The ongoing absence of Yemeni President Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi is causing concern for the United States' efforts to combat Al-Qaida in Yemen. Hadi, who has been in Saudi Arabia for medical treatment since 2015, has been unable to return to Yemen due to the ongoing conflict in the country.The United States has been working with the Yemeni government to target and eliminate Al-Qaida militants in the region, but without Hadi's presence, coordination and cooperation have become more challenging. Hadi's absence has also created a power vacuum in Yemen, allowing Al-Qaida to exploit the situation and increase its presence in the country.The US has been providing support to the Saudi-led coalition in its fight against the Houthi rebels in Yemen, who are backed by Iran. However, the focus on the conflict with the Houthis has allowed Al-Qaida to regroup and strengthen its foothold in the country.The international community is calling for Hadi to return to Yemen and resume his duties as president in order to stabilize the country and combat the threat posed by Al-Qaida. Until Hadi is able to return, the US and its allies will continue to face challenges in their efforts to defeat the terrorist organization in Yemen.

With two confirmed swine flu cases in Spain and two in Scotland, Britain and other European countries are preparing themselves as concern over the pandemic potential of the strain grows. Confirmed or suspected swine flu cases have now been reported in nine European countries.The World Health Organization in Geneva has raised its global alert to number four on the six level scale. Based on the current geographical spread of the disease, it is seen that the virus is a step closer to becoming a pandemic. But as WHO spokesman Gregory Hartl says, level four represents that point where either large-scale community transmission takes off or it is not sustained and it dies out.Given this uncertainty, Hartl says countries should use their time wisely at this critical moment."They should focus and step up factors or efforts to mitigate possible effects on their people," he said. "And so, as I said, create actions to protect the safety of their people should be a priority, communications, providing information, letting people know really what the risks are and how to prevent those risks, keeping people informed so they can take their own decisions and also knowing what they should do if they feel they have been infected."Hartl says it is also a time when countries must develop hospitalization contingency plans and prepare the stockpiles of anti-viral drugs.Understandably, people in Britain and elsewhere in Europe are concerned about where this outbreak may go.On a trip to Warsaw, British Prime Minister Gordon Brown said the country is ready."Let me reassure you that we have been preparing for this kind of scenario for many years.

After concluding its hearing on the nomination of Judge Sonia Sotomayor for the Supreme Court, the Senate Judiciary Committee heard from 31 witnesses speaking for and against her judicial record. She would be the first Hispanic to serve on the Supreme Court.They were well-known public figures, New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg and former FBI Director Louis Freeh, officials of the American Bar Association, U.S. and district attorneys, anti-abortion activists, gun ownership advocates, and two men involved in a case Sotomayor ruled on.Selected by majority Democrats and minority Republicans, witnesses addressed the same issues on which Sotomayor faced tough questioning from lawmakers.The most attention focused on Frank Ricci, who was part of a reverse discrimination lawsuit by 20 white and Hispanic firefighters in New Haven, Connecticut.Sotomayor's ruling in that case as part of a three-judge appeals court panel, found against the firefighters, but was later overturned by the Supreme Court and was a major issue during the nomination hearing.Ricci and the one Hispanic firefighter, Lieutenant Ben Vargas, had no direct criticism of Sotomayor, but sharply criticized the appeals ruling that upheld a decision by the city to disregard results of a promotion examination:"We were devastated to see a one paragraph, unpublished order summarily dismissing our case and indeed even the notion that we had presented important legal issues to that court of appeals. I expected the judges who heard my case along the way to make the right decisions, the ones required by the rule of law," he said. Lawyer Peter Kirsanow, a member of the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, said the Ricci case contained glaringly abundant evidence of racial politics."

With world records being shattered left and right at the World Swimming Championships in Rome, the sport's world governing body has confirmed a ban on the high-tech polyurethane body suits being used by most swimmers. Executive director Cornel Marculescu said Tuesday the FINA Bureau upheld last week's decision by the general congress to put new regulations in place by May of 2010. Male swimmers will be restricted to suits that go from the waist to the top of the knees, while women's suits cannot go past the shoulders or beyond the knees.Materials used to make the suits will be restricted to "textiles," as defined by a scientific committee.

WASHINGTON - U.S. retail sales fell less than expected in February, but a sharp downward revision to January's sales could reignite concerns about the economy's growth prospects.Tuesday's weak report from the Commerce Department bucked the trend of recent labor market data that had suggested the economy remained on solid ground despite some concerns that a recession was looming.Retail sales dipped 0.1 percent last month as automobile purchases fell and cheaper gasoline undercut receipts at service stations. January's retail sales were revised down to show a 0.4 percent decline instead of the previously reported 0.2 percent increase."Consumers remain cautious and hesitant to spend despite an improving jobs picture and evidence of accelerating wage increases," said Alan MacEachin, an economist at Navy Federal Credit Union in Vienna, Virginia.Retail sales excluding automobiles, gasoline, building materials and food services were unchanged after a downwardly revised 0.2 percent increase in January.These so-called core retail sales correspond most closely with the consumer spending component of gross domestic product and were previously reported to have risen 0.6 percent in January.Economists polled by Reuters had forecast retail sales slipping 0.2 percent and core retail sales rising 0.2 percent in February.Last month's weak core retail sales reading, together with January's modest gain, suggest that consumer spending will probably remain tepid in the first quarter after growing at a 2.0 percent annualized rate in the fourth quarter.Prices for U.S. government debt rose after the data, while the dollar fell against the euro and yen. U.S. stock index futures were trading lower.The report came as Federal Reserve officials prepared to gather for a two-day policy meeting.

Officials say two firefighters battling a wildfire in the western U.S. state of California have died in a vehicle crash.Authorities say the two men were killed as their truck rolled down a mountainside near Los Angeles.Meanwhile, firefighters say the wildfire in the Los Angeles area has threatened at least 12,000 homes and other structures.Officials say hot, dry weather has helped the flames spread across 14,000 hectares of land. Monday's forecast in southern California calls for continued dryness, with a high temperature around 32 degrees Celsius. Cooling is expected later in the week.At least three other people have been reported hurt, and authorities say two of them were badly burned when they tried to ride out the firestorm by sitting in a backyard hot tub.Media reports say the flames are also threatening Mount Wilson, the site of a historic observatory and several radio and television transmitters.Some information for this report was provided by AFP, AP and Reuters.

The Electoral Commission of Ghana has announced that it is fully prepared for the upcoming general elections. With less than a month to go before the scheduled voting day on December 7th, the Commission assured the public that all necessary measures have been put in place to ensure a smooth and successful electoral process.According to the Commission, all polling stations across the country have been set up and election materials have been distributed. Additionally, training sessions have been conducted for electoral officers to ensure they are well-equipped to handle the voting process efficiently.The Commission also highlighted the various safety measures that will be implemented on election day to prevent the spread of COVID-19. Voters will be required to wear face masks and adhere to social distancing guidelines while casting their ballots.In a statement, the Commission urged all eligible voters to come out in large numbers to participate in the democratic process and exercise their right to vote. They also called on political parties and stakeholders to cooperate and support the Commission in conducting free, fair, and transparent elections.As the people of Ghana gear up for the general elections, the Electoral Commission's readiness is a positive sign that the democratic process will be upheld and respected. With the necessary precautions in place, Ghanaians can look forward to a peaceful and successful election day on December 7th.

Sudan's President Omar al-Bashir recently made a visit to Syria, marking the first time an Arab leader has visited the country since the outbreak of the civil war in 2011. The visit is being seen as a significant step towards reintegrating Syria back into the Arab world.Al-Bashir's visit is believed to be part of efforts to bring Syria back into the Arab fold and restore its diplomatic ties with other Arab nations. Many Arab countries had cut off relations with Syria following the government's brutal crackdown on opposition forces during the civil war.The visit comes at a time when the Syrian government, led by President Bashar al-Assad, is making significant gains on the battlefield and consolidating its control over key territories. With the war in Syria appearing to wind down, many Arab nations are looking to re-engage with the country and play a role in shaping its future.The move has drawn mixed reactions from the international community, with some countries expressing support for efforts to reintegrate Syria, while others remain skeptical of the Assad regime's ability to bring about lasting peace and stability.Overall, the visit by Sudan's President Omar al-Bashir is seen as a significant development in the ongoing efforts to reintegrate Syria back into the Arab fold and normalize its relations with other Arab nations.

WASHINGTON - Less than two weeks before crucial midterm congressional elections, a new element of fear and uncertainty has been injected into the campaign in the wake of pipe bombs that were sent to several prominent Democrats.Federal authorities announced Friday morning that a suspect in the bomb investigation was arrested. News media, quoting law enforcement sources, said the man was arrested in Plantation, Fla., which is near Miami. Local television stations broadcast footage of police examining a white van with political stickers covering its windows. The vehicle was covered with a tarp and hauled away on a truck.The mail bombs sparked a renewed focus on the sharp political rhetoric that has long defined the current divisive atmosphere, with calls from both parties to temper the partisanship ahead of congressional elections.WATCH: Trump Calls for Unity, Blames Media Amid Bomb ThreatsWhile announcing the arrest during a White House event, President Donald Trump called on Americans to show unity."We must never allow political violence to take root in America," Trump said.On the campaign trail in Wisconsin late Wednesday, President Donald Trump made a similar appeal for calm.“We want all sides to come together in peace and harmony. We can do it. We can do it. We can do it. It will happen,” he said.But Trump later blasted the news media in a tweet.During his remarks in Wisconsin, Trump also made a plea to moderate the heated political rhetoric on both sides.“Those engaged in the political arena must stop treating political opponents as being morally defective. We have to do that.”Democrats pounced on that, countering that Trump has been a key instigator of political tension with his partisan attacks.Senate Democratic leader Sen. Chuck Schumer and House Democratic leader Rep.

At first glance, Jill Sheffield seems to be all business as she meets with staff members to plan an upcoming Women Deliver conference. About 3,000 government leaders, women's health care workers, researchers and journalists are expected to attend. Sheffield is the founder of Women Deliver and Family Care International, two New York-based nonprofit organizations dedicated to improving reproductive and maternal health for women around the world."I feel really strongly about the fact that women have rights like other people," she says, "and that they shouldn't die early in their life, giving life."Jill Sheffield holds a baby born to a mother at one of the FCI-affiliated maternal health clinics in Ecuador.Connecting maternal health and international development Sheffield first discovered the profound connection between maternal health and international development in 1964. She was accompanying her husband on a nine-nation tour of Africa as he researched strategies for U.S. government aid for education."The men were in the offices in suits," she recalls. "But it was the women who were out doing things ? being the teachers, being the workers in the field, being the nurses ? all the while they were also mothers. Women are the ultimate multi-taskers."Jill Sheffield was born in 1939 to liberal parents in a small, racially segregated town in central Florida. She says her mother, a 5th grade teacher, was her most important influence."My mother loved teaching. She just opened doors for peoples' minds and ears and eyes, and to this day, I believe deeply that education is the silver bullet to development to poverty reduction [and] women's empowerment."Jill Sheffield accepting congratulations after Family Care International received the United Nations Population Award in 2008.Sheffield says her belief in the importance of education and its link to development is closely allied to her commitment to reproductive choice. "Women need to have choices about the number and the spacing of their children.

Kanye West's third album Graduation is well ahead of rival rapper 50 Cent's third disc Curtis in a highly-publicized sales battle.According to September 18 data from tracking firm Neilsen SoundScan, Graduation sold 957,000 copies in the United States during the six days ending September 16. Curtis is trailing with 691,000 sales. West is also ahead of 50 Cent in the United Kingdom, where Graduation debuted atop the album chart.West's tally is the largest since 50 Cent's previous album The Massacre, which sold 1.1 million units in March 2005 and spent six weeks at number one.

The U.S. Senate by a 85-6 vote Friday endorsed a second round of NATO expansion. The North Atlantic Treaty Organization is expected to decide at a Prague summit in November which countries to admit in another round of enlargement.The Senate also voted to give a total of $55 million in security assistance to Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Slovakia, Slovenia, Bulgaria and Romania. Those nations, along with Albania and the Former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia, are seeking membership in NATO.The legislation, which was approved by the House in November, endorses NATO expansion in general but not any specific candidate."This bill will help NATO extend the zone of stability eastward and southward on the continent so that sometime in the next decade we will be able to say for the first time I think in all of modern history that we have a Europe whole and free," said Delaware Democrat Joe Biden, Chairman of the Foreign Relations Committee.But some senators expressed concerns about NATO expansion. Virginia Senator John Warner, the ranking Republican on the Armed Services Committee, voted against the measure because he believes prospective candidates are not sufficiently prepared to join the alliance.

Recent data from the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) has revealed that the number of Nigerians living in poverty has continued to rise, with a significant increase seen in the northern region of the country. According to the report, the poverty rate in the northern states has increased by 10% in the past year, with many families struggling to meet their basic needs such as food, shelter, and healthcare. This is in stark contrast to the southern states, where the poverty rate has remained relatively stable.Experts attribute this growing disparity to a combination of factors, including lack of access to quality education, high unemployment rates, and ongoing conflicts in the northern region. The recent pandemic has also exacerbated the situation, with many Nigerians losing their jobs and sources of income.The government has pledged to address the issue of poverty, particularly in the northern states, through various social welfare programs and initiatives aimed at improving access to education and creating more job opportunities. However, critics argue that more needs to be done to tackle the root causes of poverty and inequality in the country.As more Nigerians continue to slip into poverty, particularly in the northern region, urgent action is needed to address this growing crisis and ensure that all citizens have the opportunity to thrive and prosper.

Platinum miners in South Africa have welcomed a new deal that was recently reached between the mining company and the workers' union. The agreement includes a pay raise and improved working conditions for the miners, who have been pushing for better wages and benefits.The negotiations have been ongoing for weeks, with both parties facing pressure to come to a resolution. The miners have been demanding higher wages to keep up with the rising cost of living, while the mining company has been looking to cut costs in the face of declining profits.The new deal not only includes a pay raise for the miners, but also improvements in their working conditions, such as better safety measures and increased access to healthcare. This is a significant victory for the workers, who have been fighting for better treatment for years.The mining company has stated that they are pleased to have reached an agreement with the workers' union, and are committed to implementing the changes outlined in the deal. The company believes that this will help improve morale among the miners and ultimately lead to better productivity and profitability for the company.Overall, the new deal is a positive development for the platinum miners in South Africa, who can now look forward to better pay and working conditions. This is a win for the workers and a step in the right direction for the mining industry in the country.

Burundi’s foreign minister says it’s erroneous for people to conclude that the political and security challenges the East African country faces could lead to genocide similar to what Rwanda experienced in 1994.The international community has expressed concern about the escalating violence in Burundi. The African Union has warned that if not resolved, the violence could create instability in the country and affect the region. The United Nations has said the country could plunge into civil war if the situation persists.Meanwhile, international observers are monitoring Burundi as a presidential mandate takes effect, requiring Burundians to hand over any weapons they have or risk being considered "enemies of the nation." There is growing concern that President Pierre Nkurunziza's weapons ultimatum could set off widespread violence.In an interview with VOA, Foreign Minister Alain Nyamitwe said the government in Bujumbura is aware of the concerns expressed by international community.He said Burundi had recently experienced situations that are regrettable, but he warned against making comparisons to what happened in Rwanda.“They have a tendency to compare with the situation which prevailed in Rwanda in 1994," Nyamitwe said.

Experts have told a Senate Foreign Relations subcommittee the Obama administration places a high priority on building partnerships with Central Asian countries, and not only because of the strategic role they play in logistics for the U.S. war in Afghanistan, but also for a variety of other reasons.Subcommittee chairman, Democratic Senator Robert Casey, said Central Asian countries are vital to the U.S. and NATO military operations in Afghanistan. But he stressed their proximity to Afghanistan is not the only reason Central Asian countries are important to Washington."From the threats to loose nuclear materials, to the rise of violent Islamic extremism, from the challenges posed by poverty, weak democratic institutions, as well as challenges posed by energy, U.S engagement in the region requires a strategic and long-term approach," said Senator Casey. Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for the Bureau of South and Central Asian Affairs George Krol said the main U.S. policy goals in Central Asia are to expand cooperation with the countries' governments and their people, to defeat extremists in Afghanistan and Pakistan, to increase development of the region's substantial energy resources, to promote good governance and respect for human rights and to foster market economies. Ambassador Krol said in some countries, there is also real concern about preventing a failed state. "For instance food security is a growing problem in Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan, and we are looking to continue and enhance our food security assistance," he said. The Deputy Assistant Secretary of Defense for Afghanistan, Pakistan and Central Asia, David Sedney, said Central Asian states play an even more crucial role for the Pentagon now that President Barack Obama has decided to send an additional 30,000 U.S. troops to secure the peace in Afghanistan.

In response to growing concerns about the leadership style of Malawi President, Peter Mutharika, a spokesman for the government has vehemently denied claims that the President is a dictator. The allegations stem from recent actions taken by the President, including the dismissal of several government officials and the implementation of controversial policies. Critics argue that these moves demonstrate a disregard for democratic principles and a consolidation of power by the President.However, the government's spokesman has refuted these claims, stating that President Mutharika is committed to upholding the rule of law and respecting the democratic process. He also emphasized that the President's actions are aimed at improving governance and promoting development in the country.Despite these reassurances, concerns about the state of democracy in Malawi continue to persist. Opposition leaders and civil society groups have called for greater transparency and accountability from the government, and have urged President Mutharika to address the growing discontent among the population.As the debate over the President's leadership style rages on, it remains to be seen whether these concerns will have any impact on his administration going forward.

The Afghan government has closed the country's schools and universities for most of November in response to an increased number of H1N1 flu cases. Since July, officials have diagnosed nearly 350 people with the virus. There have been a total of 772 confirmed cases, and 10 people have died. Afghan officials say most of the flu cases have been in Kabul, Parwan and the provinces of Herot, Kandahar, Ningrahar and Bamayan.Afghanistan's Health Minister Dr. Sayed Mohammad Amin Fatemi says only Afghans so far have died from the virus.He says the basic reason for the deaths has been because the victims went to treatment centers too late. He says most of the foreigners with the virus have been with the NATO forces, and they were able to start their treatments early, which helped their chances for survival.The United Nations World Health Organization representative for Afghanistan, Peter Graass, says that despite the last eight years of medical improvements in the country, there is still a lot more work left to do."

In a groundbreaking discovery, scientists have identified a new biomarker that could revolutionize the field of cancer immunotherapy. This biomarker, known as PD-L1, has been found to predict which cancer patients are most likely to respond positively to immunotherapy treatments.Immunotherapy has emerged as a promising approach to treating cancer by harnessing the body's immune system to target and destroy cancer cells. However, one of the challenges in using immunotherapy is identifying which patients will benefit from the treatment.The discovery of PD-L1 as a predictive biomarker could significantly improve the effectiveness of immunotherapy by guiding clinicians in selecting the most suitable patients for treatment. This could lead to more personalized and targeted therapies, ultimately improving patient outcomes.Researchers believe that by using PD-L1 as a biomarker, they can better tailor immunotherapy treatments to individual patients, maximizing the chances of success and minimizing potential side effects.The identification of PD-L1 as a biomarker represents a major step forward in the field of cancer immunotherapy, offering new hope for patients and paving the way for more effective and personalized treatment strategies.

Atop roofs and through chain-linked fences, a dozen spectators – half of them mothers – gather to watch and cheer as their young boys, mostly in their late-teens and early-20s, compete for the ultimate prize: a chance to represent the "Industriales," one of the most revered (and hated) teams in Cuban baseball, often compared to the New York Yankees of Major League Baseball (MLB).

The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) has announced that it will proceed with sanctions against Mali, according to an official statement. The decision comes in response to the recent military coup in Mali, which led to the ousting of President Ibrahim Boubacar Keita.ECOWAS had previously threatened sanctions if a civilian-led transitional government was not established within a certain timeframe. The regional bloc has been in talks with the military junta that took power in Mali following the coup, urging them to restore civilian rule.The sanctions are expected to target individuals and entities that are seen as undermining efforts to restore democratic governance in Mali. This could include travel bans, asset freezes, and other restrictions.While the exact details of the sanctions have not been disclosed, ECOWAS has made it clear that they are committed to upholding the principles of democracy and rule of law in the region. The organization has called on all parties in Mali to work towards a peaceful and inclusive transition process.The situation in Mali remains volatile, with ongoing protests and clashes between different factions. The international community, including the United Nations and African Union, have also condemned the military takeover and called for a swift return to civilian rule.ECOWAS has reiterated its support for the people of Mali and their desire for a peaceful and democratic future. The organization will continue to monitor the situation closely and take further action as necessary to ensure that the will of the Malian people is respected.

In a shocking display of justice, Saudi Arabia has executed a Myanmar man by crucifixion after he was found guilty of murder. The man, whose identity has not been disclosed, was accused of killing a Saudi national.The execution took place in the city of Jeddah, where the man was publicly crucified as a deterrent to others who may commit similar crimes. Saudi Arabia is known for its strict implementation of Sharia law, which often includes harsh punishments such as crucifixion, beheading, and stoning.The Myanmar government has expressed deep concern over the execution, calling it a "barbaric and inhumane act."

The Trump administration has announced the suspension of duty-free status for Rwanda's apparel exports to the United States. This decision comes after the African country increased tariffs on imports of used clothing and footwear from the US, in an effort to promote its local textile industry.The suspension of duty-free status for Rwanda's apparel exports will have a significant impact on the country's economy, as the US is one of its largest trading partners. The move is expected to result in higher costs for Rwandan exporters, making it harder for them to compete in the global market.Rwanda's decision to increase tariffs on used clothing and footwear from the US has been controversial, with critics arguing that it could hurt low-income consumers who rely on second-hand clothing. However, the Rwandan government has defended its actions, saying that it is necessary to protect its local textile industry and create jobs for its citizens.It remains to be seen how this trade dispute between the US and Rwanda will unfold, and what the long-term implications will be for both countries. As tensions escalate, it is clear that trade policies will continue to be a point of contention between the two nations.

Iran's President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad spoke Wednesday at the opening of the Economic Cooperation Organization summit in Tehran that brings together 10 regional countries. President Ahmadinejad blamed the West for the global economic meltdown, declaring that capitalism has failed and that the capitalist system was close to collapse."After the collapse of the closed socialist economy," he said, "the capitalist economy is also on the brink of disintegration, and despite all the efforts taken to save capitalism, the situation is hopeless. It is clear that the same principles which have triggered the collapse cannot be used to reverse it." Mr. Ahmadinejad noted that capitalist organizations across the world have pumped hundreds of millions of dollars into the market to save major corporations and banks but they have not yet admitted that capitalism has failed.During Wednesday's summit, Mr. Ahmadinejad also called for greater regional economic integration and urged member states to begin discussing the establishment of a single currency and a bank that would promote trade. Iran's closest neighbors and key trading partners, include Pakistan, Turkey, Iraq, Afghanistan, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, Kirgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan.Mr. Ahmadinejad, who is up for re-election in June, has repeatedly lashed out at the West for the current financial crisis. But he has been under fire at home for Iran's economic woes.Iran has inflation in the mid-20 percent range and chronic unemployment, which stands at about 30 percent by unofficial estimates.Iran expert Ali Nourizadeh pointed out that Iran itself has practiced free-market capitalism since the Iranian Revolution, and that Mr. Ahmadinejad has completely mismanaged the Iranian economy."Mismanagement of the economy is Mr.

Stock indexes in Europe and the United States fell sharply Tuesday as investors worried about the contraction of the economy in the 17-nation euro currency bloc and whether Greece can successfully eliminate a large portion of its debt.Markets in London, Frankfurt and Paris fell about two percent or more after the European Union confirmed that the eurozone economy slid in the last three months of 2011, while New York exchanges dipped more than one percent.The EU report came as Greece neared a Thursday deadline to secure agreements from its major private creditors to eliminate more than half of the money the country owes them, a $142 billion reduction. But there were signs that not all of the lenders would agree to the debt write-off, which could force the Athens government to impose losses on the reluctant creditors.Greece is seeking to cut its debt as part of a pact with European leaders to secure a new $172 billion bailout, its second in two years, to avoid a default on its financial obligations later this month.The EU's economic commissioner, Olli Rehn, said the eurozone "is currently in a mild recession." The region's economy dipped three-tenths of a percentage point in the October-to-December period and eurozone leaders say the slide is continuing in the early months of 2012. If the first quarter contraction is confirmed in the coming weeks, it would officially be the eurozone's second recession in three years.Household spending, exports and imports all fell in the fourth quarter, but Rehn said there are "signs of stabilization."

Beloved Hollywood actress and singer, Doris Day, celebrated her 95th birthday this week, surprising fans who had long believed she was two years younger. The mistake appears to have been due to an error in her official birth date, which was initially listed as April 3, 1924. However, Day's actual birth date is April 3, 1922.Despite the confusion, fans and colleagues alike are thrilled to celebrate the iconic star's impressive milestone. Day, known for her roles in classic films such as "Pillow Talk" and "Calamity Jane," has remained a beloved figure in Hollywood for decades. In addition to her acting career, Day is also a talented singer with hits like "Que Sera, Sera" and "Secret Love."Even in her later years, Day has continued to captivate audiences with her timeless talent and grace. As she celebrates her 95th birthday, fans around the world are sending their well wishes to the Hollywood legend. Happy birthday, Doris Day!

A recent report has revealed that news of a secret trip to Iraq with White House senior adviser Jared Kushner was leaked while the plane was still in the air, raising concerns about potential security risks. The trip, which was supposed to be kept confidential for security reasons, was reportedly exposed to the media before the plane even landed in Iraq.The leak of this sensitive information has sparked fears about the safety of Kushner and other members of the delegation, as well as the potential impact on US national security. The White House has yet to comment on the matter, but experts are warning that such leaks could jeopardize the safety of those involved in high-profile government missions.It remains unclear how the news of the trip was leaked and what steps will be taken to prevent similar incidents in the future. As investigations into the breach continue, concerns about the security risks posed by such leaks are growing, highlighting the importance of maintaining confidentiality and discretion in diplomatic and national security matters.

The United States is offering up to $1 million for the capture of a Romanian con artist who allegedly sold Americans luxury cars and boats that did not exist.The FBI Tuesday placed Nicolae Popescu on its list of the 10 most wanted cyber criminals.Popescu is accused of leading a ring that posted phony Internet ads for expensive cars, boats and motorcycles.

Ethiopian Prime Minister Meles Zenawi has stated that a war with neighboring Eritrea is not imminent, despite rising tensions between the two countries. In a press conference held in Addis Ababa, Meles emphasized that Ethiopia is committed to resolving the border dispute with Eritrea through peaceful means.The two countries have been locked in a border conflict since Eritrea gained independence from Ethiopia in 1993. The dispute over the town of Badme has resulted in a costly war between the two nations in 1998-2000, which claimed the lives of tens of thousands of people.Meles' comments come amidst recent reports of military build-up and skirmishes along the border between Ethiopia and Eritrea. Both countries have accused each other of provoking hostilities and violating the terms of the ceasefire agreement signed in 2000.Despite the recent escalation in tensions, Meles has expressed hope that diplomatic efforts will prevail and prevent a return to armed conflict. He has called on the international community to support a peaceful resolution to the border dispute and avoid further bloodshed in the region.The situation between Ethiopia and Eritrea remains volatile, with both countries maintaining a strong military presence along their shared border. It is crucial for both sides to exercise restraint and engage in dialogue to prevent a descent into full-scale war.

The PGA Tour is moving a tournament from a Miami golf course owned by Donald Trump to one in Mexico City.Tour Commissioner Tim Finchem said the change was not political, but came after the tour was unable to secure sponsorship for the World Golf Championships tournament at the Trump National Doral course at levels to sustain the event and help it grow.The tournament had been held at Doral since 2007 after stops in Spain, Ireland, England and elsewhere in the United States.Trump called the decision, announced Wednesday, a sad day for Miami, the U.S. and golf."No different than Nabisco, Carrier and so many other American companies, the PGA Tour has put profit ahead of thousands of American jobs, millions of dollars in revenue for local communities and charities and the enjoyment of hundreds of thousands of fans who make the tournament an annual tradition," Trump said.He has criticized American companies for moving their operations abroad throughout his current campaign for president, and said the PGA Tour move further shows why he is running."You vote for Donald Trump as president, and if I become your president, this stuff is all going to stop," he told supporters in California.Cadillac, which has sponsored the event at Trump's course, did not renew its deal as the title sponsor of the event. The new sponsor through 2023 is Grupo Salinas, a Mexico City-based retail, television and telecommunications company.The rebranded tournament will be first held in March 2017."

NASA faced a setback while attempting to inflate a new room at the International Space Station on Thursday. The Bigelow Expandable Activity Module (BEAM) did not fully expand as expected when astronauts on board the station opened a valve to release air into the structure.The module, which is the first expandable habitat to be tested in space, was launched to the station back in April. It was designed by Bigelow Aerospace in hopes of providing additional living and working space for astronauts.NASA reported that the module only expanded a few inches before the process came to a halt. Engineers are currently assessing the situation to determine what went wrong and how to proceed. Despite the setback, NASA remains optimistic that the issue can be resolved and the module can still be successfully inflated.The expansion of the BEAM is seen as an important step in testing the viability of expandable habitats in space, as they offer a more compact and lightweight alternative to traditional rigid modules. This technology could potentially be used in future missions to Mars and beyond.The astronauts on board the space station are safe and there is no immediate danger to the crew. NASA will continue to work on resolving the issue with the BEAM in the coming days.

US Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen has spoken out in defense of the recent debt deal reached by Congress to raise the debt ceiling. Yellen emphasized the importance of maintaining the full faith and credit of the United States, stating that failing to raise the debt limit could have severe economic consequences.The debt deal, which was reached just before the deadline to avoid a government default, allows the government to continue borrowing money to fund its operations and pay its bills. Yellen praised the bipartisan efforts to reach an agreement and called for more cooperation in the future to address the nation's long-term fiscal challenges.Critics of the deal argue that it simply kicks the can down the road and fails to address the underlying issues of rising debt and deficits. However, Yellen defended the agreement as a necessary step to ensure the stability of the economy and the financial markets.The Treasury Secretary's remarks come as the Biden administration continues to push for its ambitious economic agenda, which includes significant investments in infrastructure, social programs, and combating climate change. Yellen's defense of the debt deal signals the administration's commitment to managing the country's finances responsibly while also pursuing its policy goals.

YENAGOA, NIGERIA - Nigeria's President Muhammadu Buhari on Monday signed an executive order aimed at boosting the local production of goods and create jobs in the west African country.Buhari, a 75-year-old former military ruler, has frequently spoken about ending the OPEC member's dependence on oil exports while also creating jobs by boosting local food production.And in 2015, months after Buhari came to power in May of that year, the central bank restricted access to foreign currency to import certain goods in a bid to stimulate local manufacturing.The president "ordered that all 'procuring authorities shall give preference to Nigerian companies and firms in the award of contracts, in line with the Public Procurement Act 2007,'" said the presidency in a statement circulated on Monday."The executive order also prohibits the ministry of interior from giving visas to foreign workers whose skills are readily available in Nigeria," added the statement.Around four out of every 10 people in the country's workforce were unemployed or underemployed by the end of September, according to data released by the statistics office in December.The order states that consideration will only be given to a foreign professional, "where it is certified by the appropriate authority that such expertise is not available in Nigeria."

Government soldiers in South Sudan's capital city of Juba have reportedly left the area ahead of the anticipated return of rebel leader Riek Machar. The sudden departure of the soldiers has raised concerns among residents about a possible resurgence of violence in the already war-torn country.Machar, who has been in exile since 2016, is expected to return to Juba as part of a peace agreement aimed at ending the ongoing conflict in South Sudan. However, his return has been met with skepticism and fear, as many fear that it could lead to further unrest and instability.The government soldiers' departure has fueled these fears, with some residents expressing worry about the lack of security in the city. In response, the government has assured the public that measures are being taken to ensure the safety of all citizens, even in the absence of soldiers in the area.The situation in South Sudan remains tense as the country continues to grapple with the devastating effects of years of conflict.
