The Trump administration is demanding answers from Cuba about eight detainees it says are political prisoners held by the communist government.In an open letter to Cuba's foreign minister released on Tuesday, Secretary of State Mike Pompeo said Cuba had refused to respond to multiple U.S. queries about the eight, who he said were illustrative of more than 100 political prisoners in Cuba. The list includes members of dissident groups and a journalist.Pompeo accused Cuba of reneging on promises to release them and other prisoners of conscience that date to the Obama administration and ignoring requests to even discuss them. He recalled that in October, Cuban delegates disrupted an event at the United Nations when U.S. representatives sought to raise the issue of political prisoners, an incident for which Pompeo has sought a U.N. investigation into alleged property damage."

A recent study has suggested that playing video games may actually benefit individuals with dyslexia. The research, conducted by a team of scientists at the University of Oxford, found that certain types of video games can help improve reading and writing skills in people with dyslexia.Dyslexia is a learning disorder that affects a person's ability to read, write, and spell. It is estimated that around 10% of the population has some form of dyslexia. Traditionally, individuals with dyslexia have struggled with reading and writing tasks, which can impact their academic performance and overall quality of life.However, the findings of this study suggest that video games could be a valuable tool in helping individuals with dyslexia overcome some of their challenges. The researchers believe that certain video games, particularly those that require quick decision-making and visual attention, can help improve the cognitive abilities of people with dyslexia.Dr. James Smith, lead author of the study, emphasized the importance of further research in this area. "While more studies are needed to fully understand the potential benefits of video games for individuals with dyslexia, our findings are promising and suggest that video games could be a valuable addition to existing interventions for dyslexia," he said.This research highlights the potential of video games as a therapeutic tool for individuals with dyslexia, offering new hope for those struggling with the disorder. As the study gains attention in the scientific community, more research is likely to follow to explore the full extent of the benefits that video games may offer for individuals with dyslexia.

The United States has revoked the citizenship of a former Nazi concentration camp guard who has been living in Tennessee. Friedrich Karl Berger, 95, served as a guard at a Neuengamme subcamp in 1945, where prisoners were held in "atrocious" conditions and forced to work to the point of exhaustion.Berger immigrated to the United States in 1959 and became a naturalized citizen in 1973. However, evidence recently emerged showing his participation in the persecution of prisoners during World War II. An investigation by the Department of Justice's Human Rights and Special Prosecutions Unit found that Berger had guarded prisoners during their forced evacuation and transfer to the main camp, where many died.US Assistant Attorney General for the Justice Department's Criminal Division, Leslie R.

A group of economists have warned that the stimulus package proposed by the US government might not create as many jobs as intended. The package, which aims to boost the economy and create employment opportunities, could fall short of its goals due to various factors such as lack of demand, inefficiencies in implementation, and unexpected economic challenges.Economists have expressed concerns that the stimulus might not be enough to offset the damage caused by the ongoing pandemic and its economic fallout. They argue that more targeted and effective measures are needed to address the root causes of unemployment and stimulate sustainable economic growth.While the stimulus package is expected to provide much-needed relief to struggling businesses and individuals, it remains to be seen whether it will be able to generate the desired impact on job creation. The economists urge policymakers to closely monitor the effectiveness of the stimulus and make necessary adjustments to ensure that it achieves its intended goals.As the debate over the stimulus continues, experts emphasize the importance of implementing a comprehensive and well-thought-out strategy to address the current economic challenges facing the country. Only time will tell whether the stimulus will be successful in creating the jobs needed to jumpstart the US economy.

A new report says there is enough food in northern Mali to meet local demand, despite recent conflict in the region.The report from the U.S.-fundedsays traders from southern Mali, Algeria, and Niger continue to supply markets in northern Mali's main towns and cities.It says the situation is worse in more isolated rural areas, where it says emergency needs remain high.The report notes household incomes are down and food prices are up in northern Mali but predicts the overall food situation will improve because of a promising harvest and expanding humanitarian aid programs.Northern Mali was plunged into conflict early this year when Tuareg separatists renewed their rebellion.

HANOI, VIETNAM - Vietnam's rubber stamp National Assembly elected Communist Party General-Secretary Nguyen Phu Trong as the country's president on Tuesday, consolidating his influence as the most powerful man in the Southeast Asian nation.The 74-year-old Trong is the first Vietnamese leader to hold the two positions since founding President Ho Chi Minh in the 1960s. He succeeds President Tran Dai Quang, who died last month after battling a viral illness for more than a year. Raising one hand and placing the other on the constitution, Trong vowed during the swearing-in ceremony to be "absolutely loyal to the nation, people and the constitution." He acknowledged in his acceptance speech that despite impressive achievements in recent years, Vietnam faces many challenges. "Many heavy tasks and duties are waiting ahead of us," he said. Earlier this month, the party's Central Committee endorsed Trong as the sole candidate for the presidency. Nguyen Khac Giang, a researcher at the Vietnam Institute for Economic and Policy Research in Hanoi, said Trong's serving as general secretary and president could weaken the collective leadership, which was seen as being more democratic than China's single-party rule. "When power is concentrated in an individual, there's a tendency which could be negative in a way that could lessen the collective leadership inside the party," Giang said. Vietnam does not have a single paramount leader, with the country run through the collective leadership of the general secretary, president, prime minister and National Assembly chair.

KAYES, MALI - A motorbike rider issuing warnings to fellow townspeople is emblematic of the social structures already in place that have proved helpful in the fight against Ebola in West Africa.A toddler traveling with her grandmother from Guinea died of the disease two weeks ago in the Malian town of Kayes. But since then, griots, or praise singers — important in their role as advisers and communicators — as well as local healers and religious leaders have been spreading awareness of the disease and how to guard against it.As a result, almost two weeks into the incubation period for 79 hospital workers and family members in Kayes, no new cases of the disease have been reported.Ibrahim Sacko, a traveling storyteller, tours around Kayes on his motorbike to spread awareness on Ebola. His most important message is for people to wash their hands, Kayes, Mali, Nov. 6, 2014. (Katarina Höije)One griot, Ibrahim Sacko, has done his part by crisscrossing Kayes on his motorbike, using a sound system mounted on top to give instructions to people and tell them to wash their hands.“I saw what happened in Mali’s neighboring countries, in Guinea, Sierra Leone and Liberia, and it scared me," Sacko said. "When I heard that the disease had arrived in Kayes, I acted immediately."

Former Malaysian Prime Minister Najib Razak has been hit with another charge in connection to the 1MDB scandal. This latest charge comes as a result of alleged corruption and money laundering linked to the state investment fund. The charge was filed by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) and adds to the growing list of legal troubles facing Najib. He is already facing multiple charges related to the misappropriation of funds from 1MDB, which has been at the center of a major corruption scandal that has rocked the country.Najib has consistently denied any wrongdoing and claims that the charges against him are politically motivated. However, his critics argue that the evidence against him is overwhelming and that he must be held accountable for his alleged actions.The 1MDB scandal has had far-reaching implications for Malaysia, with billions of dollars allegedly siphoned off from the fund and used to finance lavish lifestyles and political campaigns. The case has also sparked investigations in multiple countries, including the United States and Switzerland.As Najib faces this new charge, the people of Malaysia are watching closely to see if justice will be served and if those responsible for the corruption and mismanagement of funds will be held accountable.

In a recent development, the ongoing debt talks in the United States have been described as 'constructive' by officials involved in the negotiations. However, despite the progress made, significant differences still remain between the two parties.The talks, which have been taking place for several weeks now, aim to address the country's growing debt and find a solution that is agreeable to both sides. While there has been some movement towards a potential agreement, key issues such as spending cuts and revenue increases continue to be points of contention.Both Democrats and Republicans have been working tirelessly to reach a compromise that will prevent a potential default on the country's debt obligations. The outcome of these negotiations is crucial not only for the US economy but also for the global financial markets.As the talks continue, it is clear that both sides are committed to finding a resolution. However, with significant differences still on the table, it remains to be seen whether a deal can be reached before the looming deadline. Stay tuned for more updates on this developing story.

The White House responded Tuesday to a major U.S. newspaper report about President Barack Obama's role in the U.S. war against al-Qaida, including decisions to place suspected terrorists on a list of people to be killed. In a report published Tuesday, The New York Times newspaper said that since he came to office, Mr. Obama has assumed an unprecedented role in personally overseeing a "shadow war" against al-Qaida, including making final decisions whether to place individuals on a "kill list." The newspaper cited dozens of current and former U.S. officials and Obama administration advisers who were interviewed for the report, who confirmed the existence of the list of al-Qaida suspects who can be targeted for attack by unmanned aircraft. President Obama significantly increased the use of drones to strike terrorist targets in Pakistan. Use of the aircraft also has increased in Yemen, where the United States has directed military support to help the government fight al-Qaida-backed forces.The New York Times report said Mr. Obama personally approves drone strikes in Yemen, Somalia and Pakistan, and decides whether to attack a terror suspect when the suspect's family is present. White House Press Secretary Jay Carney said President Obama has made clear the United States will take whatever steps are necessary to protect Americans, but act in a manner that is "lawful and consistent" with U.S. values. Carney was asked about a specific aspect of the report, involving what The New York Times said was a method that counts all men of military age in a strike zone as militants, unless there is intelligence to the contrary.

Up to 70,000 South African construction workers are expected to go on strike Wednesday in a move that will threaten timely completion of stadiums being readied for the FIFA 2010 World Cup. The major sticking point between the National Union of Mineworkers, which represents South African construction workers is a demand for a 13 percent increase in wages. Union spokesperson Lesiba Seshoka told VOA the employers' offer of 10 percent is simply not enough because it barely covers inflation."We would like to see a situation in which workers get real increases. From the 13 percent that we are asking for, the real increase is therefore [4.6 percent] which we believe will actually make a difference, particularly for workers who are earning as little as little as R2,600 [$325] a month," said Seshoka.Many of the workers who are expected to lay down tools from Wednesday are working on stadium construction for the FIFA 2010 World Cup and also related projects such as Gautrain, the rapid-rail project that will service Johannesburg, Pretoria and the main international airport, O.R. Tambo.Some South Africans have accused the unions of using the World Cup to pressure South Africa. There is also concern that over the long term, international investors will be reluctant to invest their money in South Africa if the country fails to deliver its World Cup project on time. But Seshoka argued that primary responsibility for timely delivery of these projects rests on the shoulders of the employers."

KABUL, Afghanistan - In a surprising turn of events, Afghan Minister of Finance, Ahmad Jan Naeem, has stated that his recent appointment may not be final. Naeem, who was appointed to the position just last week, made the announcement during a press conference earlier today."I want to clarify that while I am honored to have been chosen for this important role, there are still some discussions taking place regarding my appointment," Naeem said.The Minister did not provide further details on the nature of the discussions or when a final decision might be made. However, sources close to the government suggest that there may be concerns raised regarding Naeem's qualifications and experience for the position.Naeem's potential withdrawal from the Minister of Finance position comes at a crucial time for Afghanistan, as the country continues to face economic challenges and works towards rebuilding after decades of conflict. The uncertainty surrounding his appointment has caused some instability in the financial markets, with investors closely monitoring the situation.The Afghan government has yet to make an official statement regarding Naeem's appointment, but many are eagerly awaiting further updates on the situation. In the meantime, Naeem remains in his position, carrying out his duties while the discussions continue.

Incoming Somali President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud, the target of an assassination attempt earlier this week, says restoring security will be his first order of business once he officially assumes office Sunday. Mohamud spoke Saturday to VOA from the country's heavily-fortified presidential palace in Mogadishu. He arrived there Thursday, three days early, under heavy security hours after three suicide bombers attacked a Mogadishu hotel where he was residing. At least four people, including all three bombers, were killed. Mohamud, an academic and political moderate, said his first presidential priority will be to determine why so many young Somali men and women have sought to join the outlawed al-Shabab militant group. He said his administration will then try to create what he called "opportunities for them to defect from [the] al-Shabab group." He also called on militants to leave Somalia voluntarily as soon as possible. Speaking of the assassination attempt, Mr. Mohamud said he knew he would be facing serious risks when he decided to seek the presidency. But he said the attack will not deter him from upcoming duties. Mohamud, elected September 10, takes over a country that has not had a stable central government since 1991. However, hopes for peace and stability are rising.

Farmers in northern Serbia blocked several roads Monday to protest government plans to cut the agriculture budget. Members of a farmers' association had said they would drive their tractors to the Ministry of Agriculture and other government buildings in Belgrade. But Serbian authorities dispatched police and security forces to prevent them from reaching the capital. News reports from the region say several thousand tractors clogged main roads in the Vojvodina region, with the biggest blockade near the town of Pan?evo, northeast of Belgrade. The farmers' association, which calls itself "100 P Plus," accuses the government of reneging on its promise last year to increase budget funds to help farmers. It says the government is instead planning to cut the already meager funds. The government adopted a budget Monday that entitles registered farm households to subsidies of about $200 per hectare, the same amount as last year. But the government has limited the size of subsidized land to 10 hectares. The measure is acceptable to small farmers, but not to the owners of large farms.

Hundreds of trucks have been blocked on the border between Cameroon and the Central African Republic (CAR) due to ongoing security concerns in the region. The trucks, which are primarily carrying goods and supplies, have been stranded for days as a result of increased violence and unrest in the area.The border between Cameroon and CAR has been a hotbed of conflict in recent months, with armed groups and militia carrying out attacks on both sides. This has led to a significant increase in security measures, including the deployment of additional troops and checkpoints along the border.Truck drivers have expressed frustration over the delays, as they are unable to continue with their deliveries and are losing money as a result. Many are calling for increased security measures and a more streamlined process for crossing the border.The governments of both Cameroon and CAR are currently working to address the situation and ensure the safe passage of goods and supplies across the border. However, until a resolution is reached, it is likely that the blockade will continue to affect hundreds of trucks and their drivers.

WASHINGTON - The Trump administration asked Congress on Monday to increase NASA spending next year by an extra $1.6 billion to accommodate the accelerated goal of returning Americans to the surface of the moon by 2024.The increased funding request, announced by President Donald Trump on Twitter, comes nearly two months after Vice President Mike Pence declared the objective of shortening by four years NASA's timeline for putting astronauts back on the moon for the first time since 1972.The proposed increase would bring NASA's total spending level for the 2020 fiscal year to $22.6 billion. The bulk of the increase is earmarked for research and development for a human lunar landing system, according to a summary provided by NASA."Under my Administration, we are restoring @NASA to greatness and we are going back to the Moon, then Mars," Trump tweeted late on Monday. "I am updating my budget to include an additional $1.6 billion so that we can return to Space in a BIG WAY!"NASA had previously aimed to return crewed spacecraft to the lunar surface by the year 2028, after first putting a "Gateway" station into orbit around the moon by 2024.The newly accelerated goal - an endeavor likely to cost tens of billions of dollars - comes as NASA has struggled with the help of private partners to resume human space missions from U.S. soil for the first time since the shuttle program ended in 2011.The U.S. Apollo program, NASA's forerunner to the effort at returning humans to Earth's natural satellite, tallied six manned missions to the moon from 1969 to 1972.So far, only two other nations have conducted controlled "soft" landings on the moon - the former Soviet Union and China.

In a controversial move, Russian police have conducted raids on the homes of several opposition leaders just days before a planned protest in Moscow. The raids targeted prominent figures in the opposition movement, including Alexei Navalny and Lyubov Sobol.The police claimed they were searching for evidence of extremism and incitement to violence, but many critics believe the raids are politically motivated in an attempt to silence dissent. Navalny, a fierce critic of President Vladimir Putin, has been the target of numerous legal challenges and arrests in the past.The upcoming protest, scheduled for this weekend, is in support of independent candidates who have been barred from running in Moscow's upcoming local elections. The candidates were disqualified on dubious grounds, sparking widespread anger and protests in the city.Opposition leaders have condemned the raids as an attack on their democratic rights and a clear attempt to intimidate them ahead of the protest. The raids come amidst growing political tension in Russia, as public dissatisfaction with the government continues to rise.Despite the crackdown, opposition leaders have vowed to proceed with the protest as planned, calling on supporters to join them in standing up for their rights and demanding fair elections. The Russian authorities, however, have warned that the protest is illegal and have threatened to take action against anyone who participates.The situation remains tense as both sides dig in their heels, with the outcome of the protest likely to have significant implications for the future of political dissent in Russia.

Despite facing significant setbacks in recent years, analysts warn that the ideology of the Islamic State (IS) still poses a threat to global security.IS, also known as ISIS, lost control of its self-declared caliphate in Iraq and Syria in 2019, following military defeats by the international coalition fighting against the group. The death of its leader, Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, in a US raid in 2019 was seen as a major blow to the group's operations.However, experts caution that IS continues to inspire and radicalize individuals around the world through its online propaganda and recruitment efforts. The group's ability to attract followers from various countries remains a concerning issue for security officials.Recent attacks claimed by IS in regions such as Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia are a stark reminder of the group's persistence. The deadly suicide bombing at a wedding in Kabul, Afghanistan, earlier this year, which killed dozens of people, is just one example of the ongoing threat posed by IS.Analysts emphasize the importance of countering IS's ideology to prevent further violence and destabilization. Efforts to address the root causes of extremism, such as social and economic marginalization, are crucial in the fight against groups like IS.While the physical caliphate may have crumbled, the danger of IS's ideology spreading and inspiring violence continues to be a significant concern for global security. Vigilance and cooperation among countries will be essential in countering the threat posed by IS and similar extremist groups.

A man in France has been arrested after allegedly killing his mother and sister in their home in Paris. The Islamic State (IS) has claimed responsibility for the attack, although authorities are still investigating the motive behind the killings.The man, whose identity has not been released, reportedly stabbed his mother and sister before fleeing the scene. He was apprehended by police a short time later and taken into custody.This incident has raised concerns about the prevalence of extremist violence in France, as the country has experienced several terror attacks in recent years. The IS group has claimed responsibility for a number of these attacks, which have left many people dead or injured.Authorities are continuing to investigate the circumstances surrounding this latest incident and are working to determine if the suspect had any connections to terrorist organizations. The French government has condemned the attack and expressed its condolences to the family of the victims.The suspect is currently being held in police custody as the investigation unfolds. More information is expected to be released in the coming days as authorities piece together the events leading up to the attack.

In a strong statement following a bombing incident in a busy market in Turkey, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan labeled the attack as a "crime against humanity." The bombing, which occurred in the southeastern part of the country, resulted in numerous casualties and injuries.President Erdogan condemned the act of violence and emphasized the need for solidarity and unity in the face of such atrocities. He vowed to take action to bring the perpetrators to justice and ensure the safety and security of the Turkish people.The bombing has sparked fear and outrage among the Turkish population, with many calling for increased security measures and vigilance. The government has deployed additional security forces to the region to prevent further attacks and maintain peace and order.International leaders have also expressed their support and solidarity with Turkey during this challenging time.

Pamplonans took to the streets for the third consecutive day to protest the controversial gang rape verdict that acquitted five men of rape charges and convicted them of a lesser crime. The case stems from the 2016 San Fermin festival in Pamplona, where the men sexually assaulted an 18-year-old woman. The decision by the court has sparked outrage and renewed calls for justice for the victim. Protesters are demanding a change in the legal system to ensure that victims of sexual violence are properly protected and that perpetrators are held accountable. The ongoing demonstrations have attracted a large crowd and show no signs of slowing down as the community continues to demand justice and support for the victim.

The city of Lagos, one of the most populous cities in Nigeria, has announced plans to address its ongoing issues with traffic congestion and overpopulation through a new development initiative.The Lagos state government revealed that it will be investing in infrastructure projects aimed at improving transportation and creating more residential spaces to accommodate the growing population. The goal is to ease the burden on the city's already strained infrastructure and improve the quality of life for residents.Some of the proposed projects include the construction of new roads and bridges, the expansion of public transportation systems, and the development of affordable housing options. These initiatives are expected to not only reduce traffic congestion but also provide more housing opportunities for the city's residents.Lagos has been facing significant challenges due to its rapid population growth and inadequate infrastructure. The city's streets are often congested with traffic, leading to long commutes and reduced productivity. Additionally, the lack of affordable housing has forced many residents to live in overcrowded conditions.By investing in development projects that prioritize transportation and housing, Lagos aims to create a more sustainable and livable city for its residents. The government is optimistic that these initiatives will help alleviate some of the city's most pressing issues and improve the overall quality of life for its residents.

In a surprising turn of events, Britain is once again facing the challenge of minority rule as no single party secured a majority in the recent general election. With the Conservative party winning the most seats but falling short of a majority, talks of forming a coalition government or relying on smaller parties for support have begun.This is not the first time Britain has experienced minority rule, with the most recent example being the 2017 general election. The instability and uncertainty that come with minority rule can create challenges for governance and decision-making, as parties must work together to pass legislation and address pressing issues facing the country.As negotiations begin to form a government, all eyes are on the various parties to see if they can come to an agreement that will provide stable leadership for the country. The outcome of these negotiations will have far-reaching implications for Britain and its future direction.The comparative novelty of minority rule presents both opportunities and challenges for the UK, and it remains to be seen how this latest political development will shape the country's governance in the years to come.

This year's search for Zimbabwe's own singing idol has kicked off with auditions in Mutare, Bulawayo and Harare. The winner of the competition (the CBZ A Academy) will win a recording deal. Last year's teenager idol, 18-year-old Jonah Sithole, has just released his debut album 'Diversity'. But critics say it fails to live up to the hype his winning the contest created. Voice of America English to Africa Service reporter Derek Moyo in Harare, Zimbabwe, says Sithole's 10-track CD has failed to rock local music charts, despite its executive producer Gary Thompson pleading with the media to give the idol "a chance".The CD cover features Thompson praising Sithole, who he describes as a sensational, original, and talented individual who has a "great attitude." The producer concludes by writing "You gotta believe in him, I do".Unfortunately, Sithole's CD fails to impress. Local critics have slated Sithole's performance as "lacking identity". They also argue there's nothing Zimbabwean about his compositions.The opening track on Sithole's album is titled Diversity. It also features Tapiwa Mugadza and an artist called "Young Doe". The lyrics urge people to unite, while celebrating their diversity. This is not a new sentiment; in fact critics say it sounds more like he's copying African leaders appeals for an African renaissance.The tracks on the CD are heavily influenced by American R'n'B and contains up-tempo beats.Like most other young Zimbabwean musicians, Sithole sings about love. Tracks dealing with matters of the heart include 'Equation of me and you', 'Ease Up', 'Puerto-Rican Girl', 'Need to know' and 'Right Now'.

NIKINCI, SERBIA - Russian soldiers parachuted into open fields in western Serbia on Friday as part of an unprecedented joint military drill that has stirred controversy in the European Union candidate-country given the West-Russia standoff over Ukraine.The one-day anti-terrorist exercise, around 50 km (30 miles) from Serbia's border with NATO-member Croatia, illustrated the balancing-act Belgrade faces; safeguarding relations with its big-power ally while pursuing closer integration with Europe at a time of deep West-Russia tension.Troops of the U.S. Ohio National Guard are expected to take part in exercises in southern Serbia later this month.Friday's exercise, a mock assault on a terrorist base planned since early 2014, involved around 200 Russian troops with armored vehicles, deployed by Ilyushin IL-76 transport aircraft.It was Serbia's first such joint drill with Russia since the end of its state union with Montenegro in 2006.Explosions reverberated and thick smoke billowed across the fields as Russian APCs opened fire with 30 mm cannon and guided missiles - possibly an uncomfortable sight for the Ukrainian military attache who was present.“No secrets about this drill,” Serbian Defense Minister Bratislav Gasic said, responding to suggestions that the government had tried to keep the exercise low-profile to avoid upsetting Western allies.“People in Serbia are concerned about how we'll preserve neutrality, but no one abroad is asking us that,” he said.Serbia is not a member of NATO but does cooperate with the Western alliance. It says it has no plans to join NATO, reflecting deep resentment among Serbs since the alliance waged an 11-week air war to drive Serbian forces from the then southern province of Kosovo in 1999.

In recent years, the Islamic community in the United States has faced increased scrutiny and discrimination due to the political climate of fear and rhetoric surrounding terrorism.Politicians and media outlets have often portrayed Islam as a violent and extremist religion, leading to negative stereotypes and prejudices against Muslim Americans. This has created a hostile environment for Muslims living in the US, with many experiencing discrimination in various aspects of their daily lives, from employment to education to social interactions.The fear-mongering rhetoric used by some politicians has not only stigmatized the Islamic community but has also contributed to a rise in hate crimes against Muslims in the country. According to a report by the Council on American-Islamic Relations, there has been a significant increase in anti-Muslim incidents in the US in recent years, with many cases going unreported.The impact of this rhetoric on the Islamic community cannot be understated. Many Muslims in the US live in constant fear of being targeted or discriminated against, simply because of their religion. This has led to a sense of isolation and alienation among Muslim Americans, who feel marginalized and misunderstood by society at large.It is crucial for politicians and media outlets to be mindful of the language they use when discussing Islam and the Islamic community. By promoting understanding and tolerance, rather than fear and division, we can create a more inclusive and welcoming society for all Americans, regardless of their religious beliefs.

The polls in Angola have officially closed after a day of voting in the country's general elections. However, the process has been marred by claims of irregularities and discrepancies. Opposition parties have accused the ruling party of tampering with the voting process in order to secure a victory. Reports have surfaced of long lines, delays in voting, and malfunctioning voting machines in certain polling stations.The National Electoral Commission has denied any allegations of misconduct, stating that the voting process was fair and transparent. They have assured the public that all votes will be counted accurately and that the results will reflect the will of the people.As the country awaits the official results, tensions remain high as opposition parties threaten legal action and protests if they suspect foul play. The international community is closely monitoring the situation in Angola and calling for a peaceful resolution to any disputes that may arise.Overall, the outcome of Angola's general elections remains uncertain as the country navigates through these challenging times of political uncertainty and unrest.

Moody's Investor Services says it may downgrade the U.S. credit rating unless Congress reaches a deal to cut U.S. debt next year. Rival Standard & Poor's cut Washington's credit rating a year ago following hyper-partisan wrangling that pushed the United States to the brink of default on its loans. Moody's and other agencies are watching closely as Democrats and Republicans in Congress face deadlines on $1.2 trillion in tax and spending issues. The two sides are bickering over whether or not to continue tax cuts for the wealthy.

Fans around the world are coming together to remember the King of Rock and Roll, Elvis Presley, on the 30th anniversary of his death. The legendary singer passed away on August 16, 1977, but his legacy continues to live on through his music and impact on popular culture.From his early hits like "Hound Dog" and "Jailhouse Rock" to his iconic performances in films such as "Blue Hawaii" and "Viva Las Vegas," Elvis captivated audiences with his charisma and unique sound. His influence can still be seen today in artists across various genres.Events are being held in Memphis, Tennessee, where Elvis lived at his famous Graceland mansion, as well as around the world to honor his memory. Fans are sharing stories of how Elvis touched their lives and celebrating the music that made him a global superstar.Thirty years after his untimely passing, Elvis Presley remains a cultural icon and a beloved figure in music history. His music continues to inspire new generations of fans and his memory will forever live on in the hearts of those who appreciate his enduring legacy.

DUBAI - Iran's top diplomat on Saturday dismissed the possibility of war erupting in the region, saying Tehran did not want a conflict and that no country had the "illusion it can confront Iran," the state news agency IRNA reported.Tensions between Washington and Tehran have increased in recent days, raising concerns about a potential U.S.-Iran conflict. Earlier this week the United States pulled some diplomatic staff from its Baghdad embassy following attacks on oil tankers in the Gulf."There will be no war because neither do we want a war, nor has anyone the idea or illusion it can confront Iran in the region," Foreign Minister Mohammad Javad Zarif told IRNA before ending a visit to Beijing.'I Hope Not,' Trump Says on Possibility of War With IranMichael Bowman and Shahla Arasteh contributed to this report.President Donald Trump says he hopes the U.S. is not going to war with Iran amid rising tensions in the Middle East, as an Iran diplomat downplayed such prospects."I hope not," Trump said when asked about the possibility of a conflict with Tehran as he began talks with Swiss President Ueli Maurer. The U.S. and Iran do not have diplomatic ties but Switzerland represents U.S.President Donald Trump has bolstered economic sanctions and built up U.S. military presence in the region, accusing Iran of threats to U.S. troops and interests.

A landmark ruling by the High Court in Kenya has opened the door for Kenyans to sue Britain for abuses committed during the colonial era. The court ruled that a group of Kenyans who were detained and tortured during the Mau Mau rebellion in the 1950s can seek damages from the British government.The Mau Mau rebellion, also known as the Kenya Emergency, was a violent struggle against British colonial rule in Kenya. Thousands of Kenyans were detained in prison camps and subjected to torture and abuse. The ruling is significant as it acknowledges the atrocities committed by the British authorities during this period.The case was brought to the court by four elderly Kenyans who were part of the Mau Mau rebellion. They argued that they were subjected to horrific treatment, including beatings, sexual abuse, and forced labor. The British government had previously argued that the case should be dismissed due to the passage of time, but the court ruled in favor of the claimants.This ruling could have far-reaching implications for other former colonies seeking justice for colonial-era abuses. It sets a precedent for holding colonial powers accountable for their actions, even decades after the fact.The British government has expressed regret for the abuses committed during the colonial era, but has so far stopped short of issuing a formal apology or offering compensation. It remains to be seen how the government will respond to the court's ruling and whether it will open the door for further claims from other former colonies.

The conflict in South Sudan has now entered its second year with no sign of resolution in sight. The ongoing violence between government forces and rebel groups has resulted in a humanitarian crisis, with millions of people displaced and in need of assistance.The conflict originally began in December 2013, when a political dispute between President Salva Kiir and former Vice President Riek Machar escalated into a full-scale war. Since then, multiple attempts at peace talks and ceasefires have failed to bring an end to the fighting.The United Nations and various human rights organizations have raised concerns about the widespread human rights abuses and atrocities being committed by both sides in the conflict. Civilians have been targeted, with reports of killings, rape, and looting becoming increasingly common.The international community has called for an immediate end to the violence and for both sides to engage in meaningful dialogue to reach a peaceful resolution. However, with deep-rooted ethnic tensions and power struggles at play, finding a lasting solution to the conflict remains a challenging task.As the conflict in South Sudan continues to escalate, the hope for a peaceful resolution grows dimmer. The impact on the country's population, economy, and infrastructure has been devastating, with no end in sight to the suffering and hardship being endured by the people of South Sudan.

Former Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad made headlines once again at the Tehran Summit on Economic Development, where he placed blame on the West for Iran's current economic struggles. Ahmadinejad, known for his controversial statements during his time in office, criticized Western powers for imposing sanctions on Iran and causing the country's economy to suffer. He argued that Iran's economic woes are a direct result of Western interference and unfair policies.The former president's remarks were met with mixed reactions from attendees at the summit. Some agreed with his assessment, pointing to the impact of sanctions on Iran's ability to trade and access international markets. Others criticized Ahmadinejad for deflecting blame away from the Iranian government's own economic mismanagement.Despite the differing opinions, Ahmadinejad's speech highlighted the ongoing tensions between Iran and the West, particularly in the economic sphere. As Iran continues to grapple with inflation, unemployment, and other economic challenges, the debate over the root causes of these issues is likely to persist.

Ethiopian Information Minister Berhan Hailu says the government is not responsible for the deaths of dozens of people during three days of clashes between the police and protesters.In an interview with VOA, Mr. Berhan blamed the violence on what he called politically motivated people who have attacked police and damaged property in Addis Ababa. He also called the protests illegal and described the gatherings as "violent."Mr. Berhan also told VOA that police are currently fully in control of the country. He dismissed charges by the New York-based Committee to Protect Journalists which says the Ethiopian government has threatened journalists. Ethiopia has accused the Voice of America and Deutsche Welle of bias.Mr. Berhan said journalists are not reporting the reality of the situation and are working with unlawful, politically motivated people who are intent on destabilizing Ethiopia.

President Bush says he is confident that religious moderates in the Middle East will ultimately overcome the extremism fueling terrorist violence. VOA White House Correspondent Scott Stearns reports, Mr. Bush also announced a special envoy to the Organization of the Islamic Conference.President Bush says the concept of religious freedom and individual rights is expanding in every region of the world except one."In the Middle East, we have seen instead the rise of a group of extremists who seek to use religion as a path to power and a means of domination," said President Bush. "This self-appointed vanguard presumes to speak for Muslims. They do not."The president says extremists falsely claim that America is at war with Muslims, when he says it is radicals who are Islam's true enemies by attacking religious shrines in hopes of dividing Shi'ite from Sunni.Mr. Bush spoke at a ceremony marking the 50th anniversary of the Islamic Center of Washington. The president first visited that mosque six days after the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001, to denounce reprisals against Muslim Americans.On Wednesday, Mr. Bush said he is working to encourage democracy in the Middle East, not as a plan imposed from outside but as something that he says is being seized by the people of the region themselves."Millions seek a path to the future where they can say what they think, travel where they wish, and worship as they choose," he said. "They plead in silence for their liberty. And they hope someone somewhere will answer."The president says elections in Afghanistan and Iraq are important milestones on the path to democracy, but he says the struggle does not end there."

United Nations Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon is urging North Korea to heed the call of the international community by canceling an upcoming rocket launch.Ban spoke Thursday in Geneva, as North Korean engineers prepared to launch a rocket that Pyongyang says will carry a weather satellite into space. Western nations and regional neighbors accuse the North of using the launch as a ploy to test a ballistic missile that could later be fitted with a nuclear warhead.The U.N. chief repeated warnings that the launch, which is set to take place sometime between Thursday and Monday, will raise tensions in the region. He also said it "clearly" violates a U.N. Security Council resolution aimed at reining in North Korea's nuclear weapons program.Meanwhile, military forces in South Korea and Japan were on high alert Thursday on what the North says is the first day of a five-day window for the launch.Any plans for a Thursday launch dimmed by mid-day with no sign of new activity at the launch pad outside a cloud-covered Pyongyang. In Tokyo, Prime Minister Yoshihiko Noda repeated his nation's appeal for Pyongyang to cancel the launch but said Japanese forces are ready to shoot the rocket down if it strays over Japanese territory."In case it happens, we are on full alert. Up to the last minute, we urge North Korea to refrain from launching," said Noda.South Korea has also put its forces on heightened alert and threatened to shoot down the rocket if it appears likely to crash into South Korean territory.

A group of displaced and adopted Native Americans are seeking to reconnect with their cultural roots and find their way back to their homeland. Known as the Lost Birds, these individuals were separated from their families and communities as children and placed into non-Native homes or boarding schools.The Lost Birds are now on a mission to reclaim their heritage and identity by tracing their ancestry and locating their relatives. Many of them have experienced feelings of isolation and disconnection from their Native culture and are determined to rediscover their roots.Through the use of genealogy research, social media, and community outreach, the Lost Birds are making strides in their journey to reunite with their families and communities. Some have already been able to locate their relatives and establish connections with their tribal nations.The process of reuniting with their families and reclaiming their heritage is not without challenges, but the Lost Birds remain resilient and determined. They are supported by various organizations and individuals who are dedicated to helping them navigate the complexities of their search for identity and belonging.As the Lost Birds continue their quest to find their way home, they are shedding light on the long-lasting effects of forced assimilation and displacement experienced by Native American communities. Their stories serve as a powerful reminder of the importance of preserving and honoring Indigenous cultures and traditions.

Two Republican U.S. senators have assailed President Donald Trump for his constant attacks on critical stories about his presidency, saying they undermine a basic tenet of democratic societies, a free and open news media.Senator Jeff Flake, a frequent Trump critic from the western state of Arizona, said in a Senate speech Wednesday that Trump's disparagement of the media is similar to that of former Soviet dictator Joseph Stalin. He said Trump's "assault" on the media is "unprecedented" and "unwarranted."Sen. Jeff Flake Cites President Kennedy on VOA to Argue for Free PressSorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline."It is a testament to the condition of our democracy that our own president uses words infamously spoken by Josef Stalin to describe his enemies," Flake said in a reference to a tweet last February in which Trump declared major U.S. news outlets to be the "enemy of the American People.""It bears noting that so fraught with malice was that phrase 'enemy of the people,' that even [Stalin's successor] Nikita Khruschev forbade its use," Flake said."Despotism is the enemy of the people. The free press is the despot’s enemy, which makes the free press the guardian of democracy. When a figure in power reflectively calls any press that doesn’t suit him ‘fake news’ it is that person who should be the figure of suspicion, not the press," Flake added.Sen.

Voters in Indonesia are to elect a president by direct vote for the first time on Monday. The ballot is being viewed as a major milestone in the country's six-year old transition to democracy. Although campaigning by the five presidential and vice-presidential candidates has been intense, election-related violence has been low.About 150 million voters are eligible to cast ballots at a half-million polling stations across Indonesia's 13,000 islands.Despite the size of the task, the National Election Commission says the campaign and preparation have overall been peaceful and orderly.The Center for Electoral Reform is one of half-a-dozen organizations that are fielding tens of thousands of observers to monitor the elections. Deputy-Director Hadar Gumay says there have been logistical problems in a few remote areas, but voting is expected to go smoothly."This election is much more simple than the previous, legislative elections, in terms of ballot papers, in terms of how to vote, it is just much much simple[r]," he said.Mr. Gumay says election officials are more experienced now and have learned from problems with the legislative vote three months ago, when hundreds of thousands of candidates competed and ballot papers were often larger than the polling booths.Public-opinion surveys released days before the election show former security minister Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono has more than 40 percent of the vote and a wide lead over his rival candidates, President Megawati Sukarnoputri, former armed forces chief General Wiranto, legislative assembly speaker Amien Rais and vice-president Hamzah Haz.

A Somali rights group has reported that at least 81 people have been killed in fierce fighting in the capital city of Mogadishu. The clashes are said to have erupted between government forces and militants belonging to the al-Shabab extremist group.According to the Somali Human Rights Defenders (SHRD) organization, the fighting has resulted in the deaths of 57 civilians, 16 government soldiers, and 8 al-Shabab militants. The group also stated that dozens of others have been injured in the violence.The situation in Mogadishu remains tense as both sides continue to engage in heavy fighting, with reports of airstrikes and artillery shelling being used by government forces to push back the militants. The SHRD has called for an immediate ceasefire to prevent further loss of life and to allow for the safe evacuation of civilians caught in the crossfire.The Somali government has yet to comment on the latest casualties, but the ongoing violence in the capital highlights the challenges faced by authorities in maintaining stability and security in the country.

DETROIT - Travis Kalanick, the combative and troubled CEO of ride-hailing giant Uber, resigned Tuesday under pressure from investors.The company's board confirmed the move early Tuesday, saying in a statement that Kalanick is taking time to heal from the death of his mother in a boating accident -while giving the company room to fully embrace this new chapter in Uber's history.” He will remain on the Uber Technologies Inc. board.In a statement, Kalanick said his resignation would help Uber go back to building -rather than be distracted with another fight.”The resignation came after a series of costly missteps by Kalanick and the fast-growing company that he helped found eight years ago. Uber on Monday embarked on a 180-day program to change its image by allowing riders to give drivers tips through the Uber app, something the company had resisted under Kalanick.The San Francisco-based company is trying to reverse damage done to its reputation by revelations of sexual harassment in its offices, allegations of trade secrets theft and an investigation into efforts to mislead government regulators.Uber's board said in a statement that Kalanick had -always put Uber first.”While building the world's biggest ride-hailing service, Uber developed a reputation for ruthless tactics that have occasionally outraged government regulators, drivers, riders and its employees.The company's hard-charging style has led to legal trouble. The U.S.

WASHINGTON - For the second time in as many votes, the “Islamic factor” is expected to play a key role in the outcome of Jakarta’s gubernatorial election. The latest local poll suggests that a run-off vote for the city's de-facto mayor on Wednesday is too close to call.The run-off quickly became a continuation of a struggle that emerged in the initial vote, when moderates favoring a pluralistic society faced off against conservatives eager for Islam to dominate politics and society in Indonesia's huge capital city.FILE - Jakarta's Governor Basuki "Ahok" Tjahaja Purnama gestures inside the courtroom during his blasphemy trial in Jakarta, Indonesia, Jan. 3, 2017.Violence and protests marked campaigning before a three-way vote in February that left the current governor, Basuki "Ahok" Tjahaja Purnama, who is ethnically Chinese and a Christian, pitted against Anies Baswedan, who is Muslim. With only 164,255 votes separating the two, and third-place candidate Agus Harimurti Yudhoyono holding a 17-percent share of the vote, neither Purnama nor Baswedan won a majority, and a run-off was required.FILE - An election official explains to an elderly woman how to give her vote at a polling station in Jakarta, Feb. 15, 2017.This stage of the campaign has been punctuated on social media by the frequent use of a word once heard only rarely in public discourse in Jakarta — “kafir,” or heathen, to describe Purnama and the voters supporting him, even those who are Muslim. Banners commanding “Don't vote for a heathen” hang throughout Jakarta, the capital of a Muslim-majority nation that recognizes all citizens' right to freedom of religion.The change in the tone of the campaign reflects Baswedan's changing views.

The 2022 World Cup in Egypt is set to showcase the incredible diversity of American soccer talent on the world stage. With a team composed of players from various cultural backgrounds, including Latino, African American, and European heritage, the United States national team is a true reflection of the melting pot that is American society.Led by a dynamic roster of players such as Christian Pulisic, Weston McKennie, and Giovanni Reyna, the American squad is expected to make a strong showing in the tournament. These young stars have already made a name for themselves in top European leagues and are now ready to represent their country on the global stage.The diversity of the American team is not only evident in the players' backgrounds but also in their playing styles. With a mix of technical skill, physicality, and speed, the United States team is sure to bring an exciting and dynamic brand of soccer to the World Cup.As the tournament kicks off in Egypt, fans from around the world will have the opportunity to see the true diversity and talent that American soccer has to offer. With a team that represents the best of American society, the United States national team is poised to make a lasting impression on the world stage.

In a groundbreaking discovery, scientists have confirmed the presence of ice on Mars. Using data collected from the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, researchers have been able to identify large deposits of ice just below the surface of the planet. This finding not only sheds light on the geological history of Mars but also brings us one step closer to understanding the possibility of human colonization on the red planet.The presence of ice on Mars has long been suspected, but this is the first time that it has been definitively confirmed. The discovery of these ice deposits opens up new possibilities for future exploration and research on Mars. Scientists believe that this ice could potentially be used as a valuable resource for future missions, providing water for human consumption as well as fuel for spacecraft.The confirmation of ice on Mars is a significant milestone in our exploration of the solar system. It raises new questions about the potential for life on Mars and the feasibility of establishing a human presence on the planet. As we continue to unravel the mysteries of Mars, this discovery will undoubtedly shape the future of space exploration for generations to come.

Former President Barack Obama delivered a powerful speech on Tuesday, emphasizing the importance of the United States taking the lead in reducing the spread of nuclear weapons around the world. Speaking at a nuclear disarmament conference in Washington, D.C., Obama highlighted the dangers of nuclear proliferation and stressed the need for global cooperation in order to prevent a potential catastrophic event.Obama's remarks come at a critical time as tensions between nuclear-armed states continue to rise and the threat of nuclear warfare remains a significant concern. He called on world leaders to prioritize nuclear disarmament and outlined specific steps that can be taken to reduce the risk of nuclear conflict.The former president also underscored the importance of maintaining diplomatic channels and engaging in dialogue with countries that possess nuclear weapons. He warned against the dangerous consequences of escalating nuclear tensions and emphasized the need for a renewed commitment to nuclear non-proliferation efforts.As the only country to have used nuclear weapons in warfare, the United States has a unique responsibility to lead the global effort in reducing the spread of nuclear weapons. Obama's impassioned plea for action serves as a reminder of the grave consequences of nuclear proliferation and the urgent need for international cooperation to address this critical issue.

The Missouri Health Department has rejected the license renewal application from Planned Parenthood, a move that could potentially jeopardize the organization's ability to provide essential health services in the state.This decision comes after months of legal battles between Planned Parenthood and the state of Missouri. The health department cited various concerns, including allegations of patient safety violations and failure to comply with state regulations.Planned Parenthood has vowed to fight the decision, arguing that it is politically motivated and will harm the thousands of patients who rely on their services for reproductive health care.If the rejection stands, Planned Parenthood's clinic in St. Louis would be the only remaining abortion provider in the state of Missouri. This could have serious implications for women's access to reproductive health care in the region.The organization has been a frequent target of anti-abortion activists and lawmakers, who have pushed for stricter regulations and funding cuts for the organization.Planned Parenthood has stated that they will continue to provide care for their patients while they challenge the decision in court.

Teachers in Arizona and Colorado have continued their strikes for the third day in a row, resulting in schools remaining closed in both states. The strikes are part of a national movement to demand higher pay and increased education funding.In Arizona, thousands of teachers marched to the state Capitol in Phoenix, carrying signs and chanting slogans calling for better wages and classroom resources. The teachers in Arizona are among the lowest paid in the country and have been pushing for a 20% salary increase.Meanwhile, in Colorado, teachers have been rallying at the state Capitol in Denver, advocating for increased education funding. Colorado ranks near the bottom in terms of per-pupil spending, and teachers are demanding more resources for their classrooms.The strikes in both states have caused widespread disruptions for students and parents, who have had to find alternative childcare arrangements. However, many parents have expressed support for the teachers' demands, recognizing the importance of investing in education.Negotiations between teachers' unions and state officials are ongoing, but a resolution has not yet been reached. As the strikes continue, it remains uncertain when schools will reopen and when students will be able to return to their classrooms.

The ongoing conflict in Syria has brought Turkey and France closer together, strengthening their diplomatic ties. Despite their differences on various issues, such as Turkey's military actions in Syria and France's support for Kurdish groups, the two countries have found common ground in their efforts to stabilize the region.French President Emmanuel Macron has called for a political solution to the conflict in Syria, while Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan has emphasized the need to address security concerns in the region. Both leaders have also expressed a commitment to fighting terrorism and promoting stability in the war-torn country.This renewed cooperation between Turkey and France comes at a time when the situation in Syria remains volatile, with ongoing violence and humanitarian crises affecting millions of people. By working together, these two countries hope to find a way forward that will bring peace and security to the region.As the conflict in Syria continues to evolve, the partnership between Turkey and France will be crucial in addressing the complex challenges that lie ahead. With their shared goals and mutual interests, these two countries are poised to play a key role in shaping the future of the Middle East.

Former CIA director John Brennan has issued a strong warning to President Donald Trump, stating that he will take legal action if the president continues to revoke security clearances of former intelligence officials. Brennan, who had his security clearance revoked by Trump earlier this week, has been a vocal critic of the president and has accused him of abusing his power.In an interview with CNN, Brennan stated that he believes Trump is trying to silence his critics and is using the revocation of security clearances as a means of retaliation. Brennan also mentioned that he has consulted with his lawyers and is prepared to take legal action if necessary to protect his rights.The revocation of Brennan's security clearance has sparked outrage among many former intelligence officials, who see it as an attempt by the president to punish those who speak out against him. Critics argue that revoking security clearances for political reasons sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the integrity of the intelligence community.Trump has defended his decision to revoke Brennan's security clearance, citing concerns over Brennan's erratic behavior and questionable credibility. The president has also hinted that he may revoke the security clearances of other former officials who have criticized him, including former FBI director James Comey and former national security advisor Susan Rice.As the standoff between Brennan and Trump escalates, many are waiting to see if Brennan will follow through on his threat to sue the president. The outcome of this legal battle could have far-reaching implications for the relationship between the executive branch and the intelligence community.

French President Emmanuel Macron is hopeful that his recent concessions will help calm the ongoing protests by the "Yellow Vest" movement. Macron announced measures such as a minimum wage increase and tax cuts for pensioners in an attempt to address the grievances of the protesters.The Yellow Vest movement, named after the high-visibility jackets worn by protesters, began as a response to a planned fuel tax increase but has since evolved into a broader protest against economic inequality and Macron's leadership. The protests have often turned violent, with clashes between demonstrators and police resulting in injuries and destruction of property.Macron's recent announcement of economic reforms is seen as a significant shift in his policies and a recognition of the concerns raised by the protesters. However, it remains to be seen whether these measures will be enough to appease the Yellow Vests and bring an end to the protests.The Yellow Vest movement has garnered widespread support in France, with polls showing that a majority of the French public sympathize with the protesters' demands. The movement has also sparked similar protests in other countries, highlighting the global issue of economic inequality.As Macron navigates this turbulent period in his presidency, he will need to continue engaging with the concerns of the Yellow Vests and working towards solutions that address the underlying issues driving the protests. Only time will tell whether Macron has succeeded in drawing the sting of the Yellow Vests or if the protests will continue to escalate.

South Sudan’s information minister says a section of the international community is undermining the government in Juba, by turning civil society groups and public sector workers against the administration. He says it is an effort seeking regime change in the country.In an interview with VOA on Sunday, Information Minister Michael Makuei said, “There are some members of the international community who are now agitating here, agitating the civil society, agitating the public servants to go on strike. This is something orchestrated in order to declare the government a failed government. This is something to do with the policy of a regime change, [and] this is what we know.""There are certain elements within the international community that are doing this and we know them,” Makuei added. He did not identify who the "elements" may be.Public sector workers have begun a strike to pressure the government for unpaid salaries. Some say they have not been paid for the past three to four months and the strike is the last resort to ensure the administration pays them.Makuei denied the workers have not been paid for several months.“We have heard that in some institutions there are some public sector workers who are already on strike and others will follow. All these things are being done on the pretext that their salaries have not been paid. Others have been exaggerating it, saying people have not been paid for four months, others for five months and so forth, which is not true,” said Makuei.“The government of South Sudan is doing its level best, and within this coming week they would be paid and everybody would have his salary, if it is the question of salary.

China has categorically denied a recent report from the United States that claimed the country has biological weapons capabilities. The Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson dismissed the allegations as baseless and urged the US to focus on international cooperation to combat the COVID-19 pandemic instead.The US State Department released a report earlier this week, accusing China of maintaining an offensive biological weapons program in violation of international agreements. The report also raised concerns about China's lack of transparency in its biological research activities.In response, the Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson emphasized that China is a committed party to the Biological Weapons Convention and strictly adheres to its obligations under the treaty. The spokesperson also highlighted China's active participation in global efforts to fight the COVID-19 pandemic, including sharing information and resources with other countries.The tensions between the US and China over the issue of biological weapons capabilities come at a time when the two countries are already engaged in a bitter dispute over trade, technology, and human rights issues. The latest report is likely to further strain relations between the world's two largest economies.Both China and the US have a history of accusing each other of weaponizing diseases, with the COVID-19 pandemic exacerbating existing suspicions and mistrust between the two countries. The denial by China and the strong rebuttal from the US highlight the deepening rift in their already strained relationship.

VATICAN CITY - Pope Francis marked the 50th anniversary of the turning point in the Catholic Church's relations with other religions by calling Wednesday for greater interfaith collaboration in the face of religious extremism.Francis devoted his usual Wednesday general audience catechism lesson to the importance of the “Nostra Aetate” declaration, which revolutionized the church's relations in particular with Jews. The statement “In Our Time” was one of the most important documents to emerge from the Second Vatican Council, the 1962-65 meetings that brought the church into the modern world. It said Christ's death could not be attributed to Jews as a whole, recognized the shared spiritual patrimony between Christians and Jews and decried all forms of anti-Semitism. Jews, Muslims, Hindus and Buddhists were in the VIP seats in St. Peter's Square for the special audience and were treated to a meet-and-greet session with the pope after it ended. In his remarks, Francis said the declaration had transformed Catholic-Jewish relations from “indifference and opposition to collaboration and good will. From enemies and strangers we became friends and brothers.” He decried that the rise of terrorism had fomented suspicion and condemnation about religion in general. He said that while no religion is immune from fundamentalists, the world must look instead at the “positive values” that religions promote, especially in caring for the neediest. “We can walk together, taking care of one another and of creation,” he said.

The Democratic National Convention is gearing up for the highly anticipated roll call vote to officially nominate John Kerry as the party's candidate for the 2004 presidential election. The vote is scheduled to take place on July 29th, with delegates from all 50 states participating in the historic event.Kerry, a senator from Massachusetts and decorated Vietnam War veteran, has been campaigning tirelessly for the nomination and is expected to easily secure the majority of votes from the delegates. His selection as the Democratic nominee will set the stage for a fierce battle against incumbent President George W. Bush in the upcoming election.The roll call vote is a crucial moment for the Democratic Party as they seek to unite behind a single candidate and rally support for their platform. The convention, held in Boston, has seen a mix of excitement and anticipation as delegates prepare to cast their votes for Kerry.Stay tuned for updates on the roll call vote and the Democratic Party's official nomination of John Kerry for the presidency.

Former United Nations chief Kofi Annan met with Kenya's president and prime minister Monday to urge their coalition government to hasten the pace of reforms in the country. Annan served as chief mediator in the emergency talks that produced the government's power-sharing deal at the beginning of 2008.The former U.N. chief arrived in Kenya on Sunday and began Monday what he said would be a packed three-day push to pressure the government to fully implement the reform agenda he helped negotiate to curtail the nation's ethnic hostilities.Addressing reporters outside his hotel in Nairobi late Sunday, Annan said that Kenyan people are losing patience with the lack of tangible action from their leaders, a full 18 months after the reform agenda was first agreed upon. "Of course I've been following events very closely, and clearly the Kenyan people are expecting more from the coalition government - more unity of purpose, more progress on the reform agenda, and more concrete action to end impunity and combat corruption," he said. The United States and other Western nations have begun upping their own pressure on Kenya's leaders to follow through on its commitments to key reforms.Less than two weeks ago the U.S. ambassador to Kenya announced that 15 senior Kenyan officials have received private letters threatening action if the reform process continues to lag behind schedule.A few days ago the British envoy to Kenya announced that the U.K. also has its own list of 20 individuals who it has hit with travel bans for involvement in corruption, and the European Union joined in by saying it too was greatly concerned with the pace of reforms.

Santiago, Chile - Cardinal Ricardo Ezzati, the highest-ranking Catholic official in Chile, appeared before a prosecutor on Thursday as part of a sex abuse investigation.Ezzati, who serves as the Archbishop of Santiago, was questioned for over three hours regarding allegations of cover-up and abuse by priests in the Chilean Catholic Church. The investigation is part of a broader probe into widespread abuse within the Church that has led to a crisis of credibility in one of Latin America's most Catholic countries.The Cardinal has denied any wrongdoing and maintains his innocence in the face of the accusations. "I have cooperated fully with the authorities and will continue to do so to clarify any doubts and demonstrate my innocence," Ezzati stated after the meeting.The Catholic Church in Chile has been rocked by numerous abuse scandals in recent years, leading to a series of investigations and resignations of bishops and other officials. Pope Francis has acknowledged the gravity of the situation and has called for a thorough investigation into the allegations.The appearance of Cardinal Ezzati before the prosecutor marks a significant moment in the ongoing efforts to address the issue of sex abuse within the Catholic Church in Chile. The outcome of the investigation will likely have far-reaching implications for the Church and its leadership in the country.

A team of rescuers in India is facing challenges in retrieving the bodies of five climbers who went missing on their way to the summit of Nanda Devi, India's second highest mountain. The climbers, four Britons and one Indian, are believed to have been hit by an avalanche.Due to high winds and difficult terrain, the rescue operation has been delayed, leaving the bodies stranded at an altitude of over 5,000 meters. The team is currently unable to reach the location where the bodies are believed to be located.Authorities are working to find a way to safely retrieve the bodies, but the extreme weather conditions are making the operation extremely challenging. The families of the missing climbers are anxiously waiting for any updates on the situation.The climbers were part of a larger expedition attempting to summit Nanda Devi East, but bad weather forced them to turn back. It is believed that they were caught in an avalanche during their descent.The rescue team is determined to continue their efforts to retrieve the bodies, despite the difficulties they are facing. The harsh conditions on the mountain have made the operation a race against time, with hopes of bringing closure to the families of the missing climbers.

In a move towards combating corruption within its government, Liberia has agreed to international oversight of its anti-corruption efforts. The West African nation, known for its history of corruption and economic challenges, has taken this step in hopes of improving transparency and accountability within its institutions.The agreement was reached with the support of the United Nations and other international organizations, which will provide technical assistance and monitoring to ensure that Liberia's anti-corruption measures are effectively implemented.President George Weah, who has made tackling corruption a key priority of his administration, expressed his commitment to working with the international community to root out corrupt practices and promote good governance in Liberia.This decision has been welcomed by the public and civil society organizations, who have long been advocating for stronger anti-corruption measures in the country. Many are hopeful that this international oversight will lead to meaningful change and help rebuild public trust in Liberia's government.With this new agreement in place, Liberia is taking a significant step towards fighting corruption and improving governance for the benefit of all its citizens.

PARIS - Voting started in France on Sunday to elect thousands of local councilors in a test of the far-right National Front's growing hold on grassroots politics.Marine Le Pen's anti-immigrant, anti-euro FN party is banking on building a base of locally elected officials to become more mainstream and target national ballots.While it came second in the election run-up last week, in a setback to its claims that it has become the country's first party, the FN did win one in four votes and is all but certain to see a big jump from its current two councilors.“

The US Justice Department is ramping up its efforts to crack down on sanctuary cities, which are municipalities that limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement. Attorney General William Barr announced on Tuesday that the department will be increasing resources and personnel dedicated to targeting these cities.Sanctuary cities have long been a point of contention between local governments and the federal government. Supporters argue that they promote trust between law enforcement and immigrant communities, while opponents say they undermine national security and immigration enforcement.The Justice Department's latest move comes as part of the Trump administration's broader crackdown on illegal immigration. The department has already taken legal action against several sanctuary cities, including filing lawsuits and withholding federal funding.Critics of the administration's approach argue that it is driven by political motivations rather than genuine concerns for public safety. They point to studies showing that sanctuary cities are not associated with higher crime rates and argue that the crackdown is only serving to create fear and division within immigrant communities.As the battle between sanctuary cities and the federal government continues to escalate, it remains to be seen what impact these intensified efforts will have on the enforcement of immigration laws and the relationship between local and federal authorities.

LUCKNOW, INDIA - Retrieving the bodies of five climbers of an international team from a notoriously dangerous Himalayan mountain isn't immediately possible because helicopters are having difficulty operating in strong turbulence, an Indian official said Saturday.Uttarakhand state Magistrate Vijay Jogdande also said the Indian Mountaineering Foundation could launch a ground expedition to help evacuate the bodies, which have been spotted at an altitude of 5,000 meters (16,404 feet).He was responding to a statement by the foundation Saturday that it was ready to launch an expedition.
