He helped develop the hand in a lab backed by the Italian Institute of Technology and the INAIL state workers' compensation prosthetic center.The Hennes has only one motor that controls all five fingers, making it lighter, cheaper and more able to adapt to the shape of objects."This can be considered low-cost because we reduce to the minimum the mechanical complexity to achieve, at the same time, a very effective grasp, and a very effective behavior of the prosthesis," De Michieli said. "We maximized the effectiveness of the prosthetics and we minimized the mechanical complexity."

A recent study has revealed that the tactics used by pro-Iran social media accounts to spread propaganda and misinformation are becoming increasingly sophisticated. The study, conducted by researchers at a leading university, analyzed the behavior of these accounts and found that they are now using more advanced techniques to deceive and manipulate online audiences.According to the researchers, the accounts in question are using a combination of fake news, doctored images, and misleading headlines to push their agenda. They are also exploiting social media algorithms to amplify their reach and influence. This sophisticated approach has made it harder for platforms to detect and remove these deceptive accounts.Experts warn that the rise of these more sophisticated tactics poses a serious threat to the integrity of online discourse and public opinion. They emphasize the need for social media companies to step up their efforts to combat misinformation and propaganda, particularly when it comes to foreign actors with malicious intentions.The study's findings serve as a stark reminder of the growing challenge of disinformation in the digital age. As technology continues to evolve, so too must our strategies for identifying and countering deceptive practices on social media. Only by remaining vigilant and informed can we hope to mitigate the impact of these harmful tactics on our society.

The US trade deficit hit a 10-year high in October as Americans used a stronger dollar to snap up record imports, the government reported Thursday.The result showed the trade gap has continued to swell despite the punitive tariffs imposed this year on allies and adversaries alike by US President Donald Trump, who has focused intently on the subject with the goal of reducing the deficit.Amid Trump's high-stakes trade war with Beijing, the total trade gap rose 1.7 percent to $55.5 billion, driven by all-time high imports, according to the Commerce Department.The gap in goods trade with China likewise continued to expand, rising two percent to $38 billion, seasonally adjusted, as key exports like soybeans fell.The October figure handily overshot analyst expectations, and could confirm weaker economic growth in the final quarter of 2018.

A group of Syrian children has found refuge in a camp in Turkey as they flee the ongoing conflict in their home country. The children, who have been displaced from their homes due to the violence, are now living in a temporary shelter provided by the Turkish government.The camp, located near the Syrian border, offers the children a safe place to stay, as well as access to basic necessities such as food, water, and medical care. Many of these children have lost their families or have been separated from them during the chaos of the conflict.Despite the difficult circumstances they face, the children are grateful for the shelter and support they have received in the camp. They are hopeful for a better future and dream of returning to their homes in Syria someday.The Turkish government has been praised for its efforts to provide aid and assistance to the displaced children, showing compassion and solidarity in the face of a humanitarian crisis. As the conflict in Syria continues to devastate the lives of many, the international community must come together to support and protect those most vulnerable, especially the children who are caught in the crossfire.

Officials in Afghanistan say two NATO soldiers have been wounded in a bomb attack in the north of the country.The roadside blast hit a convoy of the NATO-led International Security Assistance Force Thursday in Baghlan province, 160 kilometers north of Kabul. Local officials say two Dutch soldiers were wounded.Taleban fighters have been involved in clashes with U.S. and Afghan government troops in the south and east of the country.

GENEVA - A United Nations expert says North Korea’s abusive treatment of its population could amount to crimes against humanity. The U.N. special investigator on the human rights situation in North Korea is calling for an international probe into what he says are grave, systematic and widespread violations. In his report, the U.N. special investigator, Marzuki Darusman, identifies nine patterns of human rights violations, ranging from torture and enslavement to enforced disappearances and murder. He submitted a massive amount of documentation to the U.N. Human Rights Council, and says it shows a pattern of abuse that may amount to crimes against humanity. For example, he says up to 200,000 people are detained under slave-like conditions in a number of what he calls political concentration camps, collection centers and labor training camps. He says whole families are punished and deported to these centers for the transgression of one individual. “These are camps that have the purpose of driving the people being detained there toward a slow death by having them forcibly labor … and the dire conditions of the camps give a picture of the horrid conditions that prevail for the sole purpose of separating these people from the population at large,” Darusman said. The investigator cites torture and detention without due process of law as some of the most flagrant human rights violations. He says the government violates peoples’ right to food by manipulating the amount they receive. He says food is used as a means to control the population and make it dependent on the government.

Tensions are escalating in East Cameroon as local communities clash with Chinese gold miners over alleged environmental damage and unfair treatment.The conflict has been brewing for months, with residents accusing the Chinese miners of polluting their water sources and destroying farmland. In response, the miners have denied the allegations and argued that they are operating legally and in accordance with local regulations.Protests and demonstrations have broken out in several villages, leading to clashes with police and the deployment of additional security forces. The situation remains tense, with both sides refusing to back down.Local authorities have called for calm and dialogue to resolve the conflict peacefully. However, the deep-rooted grievances and mistrust between the communities and the miners make finding a resolution challenging.The Chinese government has expressed concern over the situation and called for a peaceful resolution. Meanwhile, human rights groups have criticized both the Chinese miners and the Cameroonian government for their handling of the situation.The tensions in East Cameroon highlight the growing concerns over foreign investment in the country's natural resources and the need for more transparency and accountability in the mining sector. As the standoff continues, the fate of the affected communities and the future of the Chinese gold mining operations remain uncertain.

In preparation for the upcoming Tour de France, a total of 23,000 police officers have been mobilized to ensure the safety and security of the event. The annual cycling race, which is set to begin on June 26th, attracts thousands of spectators and participants from around the world.The increased police presence is in response to recent security concerns, as large-scale sporting events are often targets for potential threats. Authorities are taking extra precautions to prevent any incidents that could disrupt the race or put participants at risk.In addition to the massive police force, organizers of the Tour de France have also implemented strict COVID-19 protocols to protect the health and well-being of all involved.

A young Florida woman who traveled to Colorado and bought a shotgun for what authorities feared would be a Columbine-inspired attack just days ahead of the 20th anniversary was found dead Wednesday in an apparent suicide after a nearly 24-hour manhunt. Jefferson County Sheriff Jeff Shrader said 18-year-old Sol Pais was discovered by the FBI with an apparent self-inflicted gunshot wound. The manhunt had led to the closing of Denver-area schools as a precaution. During the manhunt, the FBI said Pais was "infatuated" with Columbine and made threats ahead of Saturday's anniversary of the attack that killed 13 people at Columbine High School in 1999. The FBI described her "extremely dangerous." The Miami Beach high school student flew to Colorado on Monday night and bought a pump-action shotgun and ammunition, authorities said. Agents had focused the search around the base of Mount Evans, a popular recreational area about 60 miles southwest of Denver. All classes and extracurricular activities for about a half-million students were canceled as a precaution, though sheriff's spokesman Mike Taplin said the young woman's threats were general and not specific to any school. "This has become a massive manhunt ... and every law enforcement agency is participating and helping in this effort," said Dean Phillips, agent in charge of the FBI in Denver. Authorities said Pais was last seen not far from Columbine - in the Jefferson County foothills outside Denver - in a black T-shirt, camouflage pants and black boots. The alert also said police who come into contact with her should detain her and evaluate her mental health. Colorado Gov. Jared Polis said federal, state and local law enforcement were "dedicating all of their resources to locate this dangerous individual." "We know that there is a lot of anxiety right now in Colorado," Polis said in a statement.

South Korea has dismissed a North Korean proposal to hold military talks in late May or early June.Seoul dismissed the proposal Monday, calling the offer a "bogus peace offensive for bogus peace that lacks sincerity."A South Korea Defense Ministry spokesman said the North's proposal issued Saturday did not include a denuclearization plan, but was instead aimed at disrupting international cooperation on sanctions and splitting domestic public opinion.The Defense Ministry said North Korea is responsible for raising tensions on the Korean Peninsula with its nuclear tests and missile launches.The two Koreas share the world's most heavily fortified border since their war in the early 1950s ended with an armistice, not a peace treaty. About 28,500 U.S. troops are deployed in South Korea as deterrence against North Korea.

China has assured the Philippines that they will maintain friendly relations despite recent reports of a potential war threat. The Chinese government has stated that they value the strong bilateral ties between the two countries and will continue to work towards maintaining peace and stability in the region.The reported war threat came after a Filipino official warned that China could resort to military action if the Philippines continues to push back against their territorial claims in the South China Sea. However, Chinese officials have downplayed these claims, stating that they are committed to resolving disputes through peaceful means.The Philippines and China have had a long history of tensions over territorial disputes in the South China Sea, with both countries claiming ownership over certain islands and reefs in the area. Despite these disagreements, both countries have also sought to strengthen economic and diplomatic ties in recent years.It remains to be seen how the situation will develop, but for now, it appears that China is intent on maintaining friendly relations with the Philippines. This news will surely come as a relief to many who were concerned about the potential for conflict between the two countries.

President Donald Trump has requested an additional $1.6 billion in funding for NASA as part of his administration's goal to return American astronauts to the moon by 2024. The proposed budget increase would bring NASA's total funding for the next fiscal year to $22.6 billion.The announcement came as a surprise to many in the space community, as Trump had previously criticized NASA's moon mission plans as too costly and behind schedule. However, the president now appears to be fully on board with the initiative, calling for a "bold and exciting" return to the lunar surface.NASA Administrator Jim Bridenstine praised the funding boost, stating that it would enable the agency to accelerate its timeline for the Artemis program, which aims to send the first woman and the next man to the moon. The additional funds would also support the development of new technologies and systems needed for future missions to Mars.Critics have raised concerns about the feasibility of Trump's ambitious moon mission timeline, pointing to the significant technical and logistical challenges that lie ahead. Some also question the administration's priorities, arguing that NASA's resources could be better spent on other scientific research and exploration efforts.The proposal will now be considered by Congress as part of the overall federal budget negotiations. Lawmakers will have the final say on whether the additional funding for NASA is approved and allocated as requested by the president.

A young protester tragically lost his life during the ongoing unrest in Venezuela, bringing the death toll to 37. The 23-year-old man was reportedly shot during a clash with security forces in the city of Caracas. The protests, which have been fueled by economic hardship and political turmoil, have been ongoing for several weeks. International human rights organizations have condemned the use of excessive force by security forces and called for a peaceful resolution to the crisis. The government has been accused of cracking down on dissent and restricting freedom of speech. The death of the young protester serves as a stark reminder of the high stakes involved in the current situation in Venezuela.

In a recent report by a US magazine, it has been revealed that Congress has approved a major expansion of covert operations in Iran. The details of the operations have not been disclosed, but sources suggest that they could involve intelligence gathering, cyberattacks, and other clandestine activities.This approval comes at a time of heightened tensions between the United States and Iran, with both countries engaging in a war of words and threats. The expansion of covert operations is seen as a response to Iran's increasing aggression in the region, including attacks on oil tankers and the downing of a US drone.Critics of the decision argue that increasing covert operations could escalate the conflict and lead to further instability in the region. However, supporters believe that such actions are necessary to protect US interests and ensure the safety of American personnel in the region.As of now, the exact details of the covert operations remain classified, but it is clear that the US government is taking a more aggressive stance towards Iran. The implications of this decision are yet to be seen, but one thing is certain - tensions between the two countries show no signs of easing anytime soon.

After decades of speculation and debate, scientists have finally unraveled the mystery of the Tully Monster. The bizarre creature, which lived over 300 million years ago during the Carboniferous period, has puzzled researchers since its discovery in Illinois in 1958.New research published in the journal Nature reveals that the Tully Monster is actually a vertebrate, specifically a type of lamprey. By studying the creature's eye structure and muscle arrangement, scientists were able to confirm its classification and shed light on its evolutionary history.This breakthrough marks a significant advancement in our understanding of ancient marine life. The Tully Monster, with its long tubular body and unique claw-like appendages, provides valuable insights into the diversity of life forms that existed in prehistoric oceans."We are thrilled to finally solve the Tully Monster mystery and place it within the tree of life," said Dr. Emily Johnson, lead researcher on the study. "This discovery opens up new possibilities for studying the evolution of vertebrates and the ecosystems they inhabited."The identification of the Tully Monster as a lamprey reinforces the importance of continued research and exploration in paleontology. By unraveling the mysteries of the past, scientists can better understand the world we live in today.

LOS ANGELES - To Doris Day's many admirers, the pert and fresh-faced charmer who starred in “Pillow Talk” and “Move Over Darling” is ageless.But Day turns 95 on Monday - which is a birthday surprise to even the star herself, who has long pegged her age to a 1924 birthdate that would make her 93. Media outlets have variously reported her as between 93 and 95. A copy of Day's birth certificate, obtained by The Associated Press from Ohio's Office of Vital Statistics, settles the issue: Doris Mary Kappelhoff, her pre-fame name, was born on April 3, 1922, making her 95. Her parents were Alma and William Kappelhoff of Cincinnati. “I've always said that age is just a number and I have never paid much attention to birthdays, but it's great to finally know how old I really am!'' Day said in a statement Sunday. She's in excellent company with other vibrant Hollywood standouts lucky enough to reach that milestone year, including Betty White, a close friend, and Carl Reiner. “There has long been speculation and rumors about Doris' age and we get this question a lot, looks like we finally have the answer,” said Day's spokesman, Charley Cullen Walters. “The story I have heard the most is that at one point Doris was up for a role when quite young and her age may have been miswritten on the audition form.

Myanmar's parliament is creating a new role for the head of the newly elected ruling party, Aung San Suu Kyi.Lawmakers voted Tuesday to establish the position of State Counselor, drawing opposition from military representatives in parliament. The move to make Suu Kyi the country's de facto top leader, initiated last week, was the new parliament's first legislative act.Suu Kyi, who stated during the election campaign that she would hold a position “above the president,” is also Foreign Minister and Minister of the President's Office. Because her children are foreign nationals, Suu Kyi is constitutionally barred from being president of Myanmar, which is also known as Burma.The military representatives, who hold 25 percent of the seats under the constitution drafted by the former military government, say the new position is unconstitutional.

Special counsel Robert Mueller may have submitted his final report on the investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election, but the legal battles involving President Donald Trump are far from over. In recent weeks, there has been an increase in "spinoff" cases related to Trump, ranging from investigations into his inaugural committee to his business practices. These cases are being handled by various federal prosecutors and state attorneys general, ensuring that Trump will continue to face legal scrutiny long after the Mueller report.One such case involves the Southern District of New York's investigation into the Trump Organization's finances, including potential violations of campaign finance laws. Another case being closely watched is the ongoing investigation into the hush money payments made to adult film star Stormy Daniels by Trump's former lawyer Michael Cohen.With Democrats in control of the House of Representatives, there is also the possibility of further investigations and hearings into Trump's conduct. This could lead to additional legal challenges and potentially even impeachment proceedings.While the Mueller report may have provided some closure to the Russia investigation, it is clear that Trump's legal troubles are far from over. As the various spinoff cases continue to unfold, it remains to be seen what impact they will have on the Trump presidency.

Cameroon has received praise from international organizations and human rights groups for restoring internet access in English-speaking regions after a blackout that lasted more than three months.The blackout, imposed by the government in January, was part of a crackdown on protests and unrest in the Anglophone regions of the country. The move was widely criticized by activists and the international community, who saw it as a violation of freedom of expression and information.The restoration of internet access comes after pressure from various groups, including the United Nations and the African Union. The government has stated that the decision to lift the blackout was made in order to promote dialogue and reconciliation in the country.Many residents in the affected regions have welcomed the news, as they rely on the internet for communication, business, and access to information. The blackout had severely impacted daily life and the economy in these areas.While the restoration of internet access is a positive step, concerns remain about the ongoing political crisis in Cameroon and the treatment of English-speaking minority populations. Human rights organizations continue to call for a peaceful resolution to the conflict and respect for the rights of all citizens.

Chinese culture centers around food, and imperial cuisine elevates Chinese food to a rarified art form. In the past, only the emperor and his family could eat imperial food. But now in communist China, anyone can eat imperial food - that is, anyone who has enough money. It took Meiweizhen Restaurant's chef Bai Wei two days to put together a concoction of. "This dish was made everyday for the Empress Dowager, but it wasn't made to eat, just to look at," he said.China's last dynasty fell nearly a century ago. The People's Republic of China was founded in 1949 with a strongly anti-imperialist vision.Meiweizhen did not allow VOA to film while people were dining because it said its customers prefer to remain discreet. But the pictures on the wall indicate that at least some of the patrons are from the new elite, people like Chinese Premier Wen Jiabao and basketball star Yao Ming.Meiweizhen's location also suggests its clients have ambitions of commercial success. It is at the China World Trade Center, with one entrance on the street level and one entrance in the shopping mall.Chef Bai comes from a family of imperial chefs. His grandfather prepared food for Puyi, the last emperor of China. The food he prepares now is a meticulous recreation of imperial recipes he researched in the archives of the Forbidden City, where the emperor lived."This dish, when I've finished making it, it's very satisfying.

Russian President Vladimir Putin said Thursday he would be willing to meet with U.S. President Donald Trump.Speaking at an Arctic forum, Putin and Finland's president, Sauli Niinisto, said they were open to the idea of such a meeting, and Niinisto said he would be honored to host the summit in his country.Putin said Russia has many friends in Washington and relations between the two countries would get better at some point.He denied involvement in any efforts to influence the U.S. presidential election last year and called the allegations of Russian meddling “lies” and “provocations.”Putin said any contacts Russian diplomats had in the U.S. were part of routine work. He said Russia proposed a joint cyber security effort with the U.S. long ago, but it was refused by Washington.

The Islamic State (IS) has claimed responsibility for the recent attacks in Sri Lanka, stating that it was carried out as revenge against the 'Crusader Coalition'. The attacks, which targeted churches and hotels on Easter Sunday, resulted in the deaths of over 250 people and injured hundreds more.The 'Crusader Coalition' refers to the group of countries that have been involved in military operations against the Islamic State in the Middle East. The IS statement did not provide any evidence to support their claim, but the Sri Lankan government has confirmed that the attacks were carried out by a local extremist group with possible international support.The IS claim has raised concerns about the group's continued ability to inspire and coordinate attacks across the globe, even as its territorial presence in Iraq and Syria has been significantly weakened. The Sri Lankan government has vowed to take strong action against those responsible for the attacks and has declared a state of emergency to ensure the safety and security of its citizens.The international community has condemned the attacks and offered support to Sri Lanka during this difficult time. Investigations into the attacks are ongoing, with authorities working to uncover the full extent of the network behind the coordinated bombings. The IS claim serves as a reminder of the ongoing threat posed by extremist groups and the need for continued vigilance and cooperation in combating terrorism.

Christmas 2018 will be a day of sadness in the tiny Guatemalan village of San Antonio Secortez.It is the day when the family of seven year-old Jakelin Caal will hold her funeral.Jakelin died earlier this month while in the custody of the U.S. Customs and Border Patrol after she crossed into the United States with her father, Nery. They were part of one of the caravans of Central American migrants.Jakelin's small white coffin arrived at the airport in Guatemala City Sunday and was brought 354 kilometers north to the dirt-poor village.Among the while balloons and flowers surrounding the casket was a hand-written message to Guatemalan President Jimmy Morales saying "We ask you for jobs, electricity, potable water, roads...so we don't have to emigrate."Nery Caal entered the U.S. in the hopes of finding work, which does not exist across much of Guatemala.It is still unclear exactly how Jakelin became ill.She was apparently well when agents arrested her and her father along with other migrants when they crossed the U.S. border into New Mexico on December 6. She became sick on the bus ride to a border patrol station and arrived with 41 degree Celsius fever.Emergency medical teams flew her to a hospital in El Paso, Texas, where she died two days later.

When Serena Williams told the umpire at the U.S. Open final that he owed her an apology, that he had stolen something from her, and then she got penalized for her words, Breea Willingham could relate to her frustration and anger.Willingham isn’t a tennis star, but she is a black woman. She and others like her say Williams’ experience resonates with them because they are often forced to watch their tone and words in the workplace in ways that men and other women are not.And if they’re not careful, they say, they risk being branded “Angry Black Woman.”“So much of what she experiences we experience in the workplace, too,” said Willingham, a professor of criminal justice at the State University of New York at Plattsburgh. “As black women ... we’re expected to stay in our lane, that lane that has been created for us. Any time we step out of that lane, then we become a problem.”The stereotype of the “Angry Black Woman” is alive and well, said Felicia Martin, 36, a federal employee who lives in Brooklyn. She recalls once seeing a white female co-worker cursing and throwing things and not facing repercussions, while she’s been told to calm down for expressing her own upset in a normal tone of voice.“If I’m upset about something, I should get to express that to you,” Martin said.During Saturday’s championship loss to Naomi Osaka, Williams got a warning from the chair umpire for violating a rarely enforced rule against receiving coaching from the sidelines. An indignant Williams emphatically defended herself, denying she had cheated. A short time later, she smashed her racket in frustration and was docked a point.

WASHINGTON - The top Republican and Democratic leaders in the U.S. House of Representatives, often at sharp odds over contentious government policies, joined Wednesday in a common plea for humanity in the immediate aftermath of a gunman's attack on a congressman and four others at a baseball field.“We are united, we are united in our shock, we are united in our anguish. An attack on one of us is an attack on all of us,” House Speaker Paul Ryan, the Republican leader, told a hushed and packed chamber hours after the shooting in nearby Alexandria, Virginia. Representative Steve Scalise, the third-ranking Republican, was wounded while practicing for a charity congressional baseball game.In this image from House Television video, House Speaker Paul Ryan of Wis., speaks June 14, 2017, on the House floor at the Capitol in Washington, as he talks about the shooting in nearby Alexandria, Virginia.“I ask each of you to join me to resolve, to come together, to lift each other up and to show the country, to show the world, that we are one House, the people's House, united in our humanity,” Ryan said. “It is that humanity which will win the day, and it always will.”In this image from House Television video, House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi of Calif., speaks June 14, 2017, on the House floor at the Capitol in Washington, as she talks about the shooting at the Republican congressional baseball practice.The leader of the minority Democrats, Congresswoman Nancy Pelosi, said, “To my colleagues, you're going to hear me say something you've never heard me say before. I identify myself with the remarks of the speaker. They were beautiful remarks, Mister Speaker. Thank you so much for the sentiments that they represent. Thank you so much.“Again, we are not one caucus or the other in this House today,” Pelosi said.

The Bush administration says it is not contemplating curtailing the current surge of U.S. forces in Iraq, as it prepares its first report on the surge due this week to an increasingly skeptical Congress. VOA's Al Pessin reports from the Pentagon.Both here and at the White House, officials steered reporters away from a New York Times story that says senior officials are discussing changing the Iraq strategy President Bush announced in January. White House spokesman Tony Snow put it this way."There is no intensifying discussion about reducing troops," said Tony Snow.At the Pentagon, spokesman Bryan Whitman acknowledged that Defense Secretary Robert Gates postponed a trip to Latin America in order to participate in policy meetings on Iraq this week. He said discussions are going on about what he called "the way ahead" in Iraq. And he reported that Secretary Gates participated in a national security team meeting early Monday morning, after meeting with the commander of U.S. forces in the Middle East, Admiral William Fallon.But Whitman also noted that the latest security operation in Iraq is just three weeks old, and began as soon as all the extra forces President Bush ordered to Iraq had arrived."What this department is focused on is implementing the full capabilities that the surge forces are giving the commanders on the ground," said Bryan Whitman.Whitman dismissed comments by a growing number of Republican members of Congress, who have said the new Iraq strategy is not working and needs to be changed."

In a historic move, Pakistan and India have signed a deal with Turkmenistan for the construction of the long-awaited Turkmenistan-Afghanistan-Pakistan-India (TAPI) natural gas pipeline. The agreement was finalized during a meeting between the leaders of the three countries in Turkmenistan's capital, Ashgabat.The TAPI pipeline is expected to transport natural gas from Turkmenistan's Galkynysh gas field to Afghanistan, Pakistan, and India, spanning over 1,800 kilometers. The project aims to enhance regional energy cooperation and provide a reliable source of gas to meet the growing energy demands of the participating countries.The signing of the deal marks a significant milestone in the TAPI project, which has faced numerous challenges and delays since it was first proposed over two decades ago. The pipeline is seen as a crucial step towards promoting economic development and stability in the region, as well as reducing reliance on other sources of energy.Officials from all three countries expressed optimism about the potential benefits of the TAPI pipeline, including improved energy security, job creation, and increased revenue for the participating nations. Construction of the pipeline is expected to begin soon, with completion targeted for the next few years.The signing of the TAPI pipeline deal reflects the commitment of Pakistan, India, and Turkmenistan to strengthening cooperation and promoting sustainable development in the region. As work on the project progresses, it is hoped that the pipeline will bring about positive economic and social impacts for all involved parties.

A strike by pilots at Spanish airline Iberia has led to the cancellation of 150 flights, affecting thousands of passengers. The pilots are protesting against proposed job cuts and changes to their working conditions.The strike, which began on Monday, has caused chaos at airports across Spain, with many travelers left stranded and frustrated. Iberia has apologized for the inconvenience and is working to rebook affected passengers on alternative flights.The airline is advising passengers to check the status of their flight before heading to the airport and is offering refunds or the option to reschedule for those affected by the strike. Negotiations between the pilots' union and Iberia management are ongoing in an effort to reach a resolution and minimize further disruptions to travel plans.

Ethiopia's Federal police on Wednsday moring arrested a suspect in the death of 25-year-old Brian Adkins, a U.S. diplomat.Adkins' body was found in his home in Addis on February 2 by embassy colleagues when he failed to report to work that day.Ethiopian federal police spokesman Demsash Hailu told VOA that the suspect was found in possession of Adkin's camera, cell phone and laptop computer. Police and U.S. embassy sources declined to give details as the case is still under investigation. The death is also being investigated by the U.S. Bureau of Diplomatic Security.Adkins was on his first overseas assignment as a Pickering Fellow and worked in the consul's office in Addis. He was found dead in his home last Monday.

As voters in Britain head to the polls today, there is a sense of uncertainty surrounding the outcome of the election and the extent of the Conservative Party's lead.The latest polls show the Conservatives with a lead over the Labour Party, but the margin is narrow and there are concerns about a potential hung parliament. Prime Minister Boris Johnson has been campaigning heavily on his Brexit deal and promises of economic growth, while Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn has focused on issues such as healthcare and social welfare.The outcome of this election will have significant implications for the future of Britain, particularly in relation to Brexit. If the Conservatives secure a majority, it is likely that Johnson will push ahead with his Brexit deal and aim to have the UK leave the EU by the end of January. However, if there is a hung parliament, it could lead to further uncertainty and potentially even a second referendum.Voters are urged to make their voices heard today and cast their ballots in what is expected to be a closely contested election. Stay tuned for updates as the results come in throughout the night.

According to officials, two-thirds of Iraqi expatriates have already voted in the country's parliamentary election on the first two days of balloting. The voting took place in 30 countries around the world, with a total of 1.2 million expatriates eligible to participate. The election is being closely watched as Iraq continues to face political instability and security challenges. The high voter turnout among expatriates is seen as a positive sign for the country's democratic process. The remaining expatriates still have the opportunity to cast their votes in the coming days before the election results are announced.

Berhanu Nega and some former members of the Ethiopia’s main opposition, the Coalition for Unity and Democracy, today launched a new party that will not only pursue peaceful struggle in the bid to “bring democracy to Ethiopia”.The alternatives of the new party include sit-ins, strikes, demonstrations and not working effectively in government offices, Berhanu said. He said Ginbot 7 could, if necessary, resort to the “use of force”. He added that the new party will work with armed fronts, the CUD had avoided in the past, as long as their interest is to bring democracy to Ethiopia.Berhanu, who was elected mayor of Addis Ababa in 2005 but was never able to serve in that office, said his patience over what he calls a flawed political process has worn “thin”.He said movements to bring justice to Ethiopia have not be welcomed by Meles and Bereket. Meles Zenawi is Ethiopia’s prime minister and Bereket Simon is his special advisor. “They cannot give us a dose of democracy in a measurement of their choice,” Berhanu said.The newly formed party, Ginbot 7: Movement for Justice Freedom and Democracy, vowed to pursue peaceful means of struggle if the rules of the game are fair.Berhanu called for mutual respect and real commitment for a smooth and democratic transfer of power.

A lawmaker from Taiwan's ruling coalition paid respects Monday at a Tokyo war shrine viewed by critics as a symbol of Japan's past militarism.Su Chin-chiang, chairman of the Taiwan Solidarity Union, a partner in the ruling coalition, visited Yasukuni Shrine, where 14 convicted Japanese war criminals are honored along with other war dead - including thousands from Taiwan.Annual visits to the shrine by Japanese Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi have been a major cause of friction with China and South Korea.Taiwan was colonized by Japan from 1895 to 1945.Mr.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visited Juno Beach on Sunday to pay tribute to the soldiers who landed there on D-Day. The visit was part of a series of events commemorating the 77th anniversary of the Allied invasion of Normandy during World War II.Trudeau laid a wreath at the Juno Beach Centre and spoke with veterans and their families who had gathered for the occasion. He highlighted the bravery and sacrifice of the soldiers who fought on the beaches of Normandy, including the Canadian troops who played a crucial role in the success of the operation.The Prime Minister also emphasized the importance of remembering the lessons of D-Day and the sacrifices made by those who fought for freedom and democracy during the war.

U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton Friday urged global action to curb conflict-related violence and sexual exploitation against young women and girls. Clinton is to chair a special session of the U.N. Security Council on the issue next Wednesday. Clinton says preventing the exploitation and marginalization of girls is no longer an afterthought but a core foreign policy objective of the United States, which is co-sponsoring a Security Council resolution on the issue next week.The secretary of state joined her counterpart from the Netherlands, Maxime Verhagen, at a meeting at U.N. headquarters Friday previewing next week's council meeting.On an Africa trip last month, Clinton met with women and girls raped and otherwise abused by soldiers and irregular forces in the conflict in the eastern part of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the DRC.She told U.N. delegates and women activists here that the harrowing testimony she heard only underscored for her the point that societies cannot reach their full potential if half the population, women and girls, are oppressed and subjugated."When women are accorded their rights and afforded equal opportunities and education and health care and employment and political participation, they invest in their families, they lift them up, they contribute to their communities and their nations," Clinton said. "When they are marginalized, when they are mistreated, when they are ignored, when they are demeaned, the progress is not possible, no matter how rich and well-educated the elite may appear."

SINGAPORE - Questions about the U.S. commitment to the Asian region will be a key focus as Secretary of Defense Jim Mattis attends the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore this weekend.In recent years, the annual meeting has typically been an opportunity for countries in the region to voice frustrations and concerns about China — be it Beijing’s artificial island building in the South China Sea or territorial claims. This year, however, Washington may be getting most of the attention, analysts said.Watch: US Security Policy in Spotlight at Annual Asia Defense SummitSorry, but your browser cannot support embedded video of this type, you can download this video to view it offline.President Donald Trump’s announcement this week that the U.S. would exit the Paris climate change agreement, along with other campaign trail and policy follow-throughs, are not only raising concerns about the environment and free trade agreements, but also spilling over into the security arena as well.Mattis seems well-prepared to assure allies and others in the region that Washington will not turn its back on Asia. He is leading a robust delegation to the regional security forum in Singapore and spending nearly three days in the island nation.U.S. Secretary of Defense James Mattis speaks at the 16th IISS Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, June 3, 2017.His attendance at the meeting is a first for the Trump administration, and delegates at the meeting will be looking for clarity and assurances about U.S. commitment to the Asian Pacific.“In the Asia Pacific — probably one of the fastest growing regions in terms of military spending and development of military capability — we’re not seeing very definitive statements from the new administration in terms of what their approach is on defense and security in this part of the world,” said William Choong, a Shangri-La Dialogue senior fellow.En route to the meetings, Mattis said he would emphasize the need for “international order” in the region.“

MONTCLAIR, N.J. - Lynn Fedele sits at a booth at a natural-food deli, next to a wall painted light jungle green with bright red accents. She sips a steaming cup of peppermint chamomile tea and talks politics."I think it's very important to take the House out of the hands of the Republicans," she sighs, referring to the battle for control of the U.S. House of Representatives.Fedele describes herself as a left-leaning progressive. She didn't vote for Republican Donald Trump for president in 2016. Nor did she vote for Democrat Hillary Clinton, knowing Clinton would win New Jersey. (Clinton won 55 percent to Trump's 41 percent.)She instead voted for Jill Stein, hoping that her Green Party would get 5 percent of the vote and the federal matching funds that go with it. Stein finished with just over 1 percent of the U.S. vote.FILE - Rookie Democratic candidate Lauren Underwood greets supporters at the opening of her campaign office in St. Charles, Ill., July 29, 2018. If elected to the U.S. House in the midterms, Underwood would be the first woman and first minority to represent the predominantly white district once represented by GOP House Speaker Dennis Hastert.The U.S. midterm congressional elections, held halfway through a president's term, typically signal the public's judgment of the performance of the incumbent president. If voters turn against Trump, they could change the control of Congress. Democrats need a net gain of 23 seats in the House to take control of that chamber and two seats in the Senate to achieve a majority there.Many analysts agree the key voting bloc that could flip Congress consists of women like Fedele.Suburban? Check. She lives in Montclair, N.J.Educated? Check.

In recent years, there has been a growing spotlight on large families and the unique challenges they face. From financial struggles to social stigma, these families often navigate a complex landscape in order to thrive and support one another.One of the key issues facing large families is the financial burden of providing for multiple children. With the rising cost of living and expenses such as education and healthcare, parents with many children can find themselves stretched thin. This can lead to increased stress and pressure on the family unit, as well as potential disparities in opportunities for each child.Additionally, large families can sometimes face judgment or criticism from society. There may be stereotypes or misconceptions about their ability to properly care for and raise so many children. This can create feelings of isolation and alienation for these families, who may feel misunderstood or judged by others.Despite these challenges, many large families find strength and resilience in their numbers. They often develop strong bonds and support networks within their own family, relying on each other for emotional and practical assistance. This sense of unity can help them navigate the difficulties they face and come out stronger on the other side.As the spotlight continues to shine on large families, it is important for society to recognize and appreciate the unique experiences and contributions of these families. By offering support and understanding, we can help create a more inclusive and compassionate community for all families, regardless of size.

Police in Athens have fired tear gas on angry protesters in Athens. The street clashes on Tuesday came on the first day of a 48-hour general strike. These are just the latest episodes in an ongoing campaign by Greek citizens against their government?s austerity measures. Gut anger is hurled into the Greek night air as protesters rally outside parliament to tell their elected politicians that they?ve had enough.Both night and day, a community of protesters has also formed in the main square outside parliament. They?ve been here for over a month now. Foula Farmakidi is one of them. ?I?m here actually because I don?t think I have any other choice than to be here. We don?t have any future so we have to do something about it,? Farmakidi explained.They?re disputing cuts in public spending and tax hikes, which over the past year have weakened the welfare state and made many jobless. They eat here, some sleep here, and most debate politics and economics. It?s a form of peaceful and contained action. But the outlook on the streets has at times been more militant. Police have fired tear gas at activists, many of them young men throwing stones and fire bombs. And trade unions play their own major part. They?

Residents in the neighborhood of Oak Street have been dealing with a smelly situation for the past week. A large skunk, nicknamed 'Mr. Stinky' by locals, has been roaming the area and leaving behind a strong odor wherever he goes.According to witnesses, 'Mr. Stinky' has been spotted digging through trash cans and causing a disturbance in the quiet residential area. Some residents have even reported encounters with the skunk, resulting in them having to endure the pungent smell for hours.Animal control has been notified of the situation and is working to safely remove the skunk from the neighborhood. In the meantime, residents are advised to stay away from the skunk and report any sightings to authorities.Despite the inconvenience caused by 'Mr. Stinky', some residents have expressed sympathy for the skunk, noting that it is simply trying to survive in its natural habitat. Others, however, are eager for the skunk to be removed so that they can enjoy their neighborhood without the constant odor.For now, residents of Oak Street will have to continue to deal with the presence of 'Mr. Stinky' until he is safely relocated.

As the deadline for nominations for the next leader of the Conservative Party approaches, the question on everyone's mind is: who is hoping to be Britain's next Prime Minister?Several prominent Conservative politicians have thrown their hats into the ring, including current Foreign Secretary Jeremy Hunt, former Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson, Environment Secretary Michael Gove, and Home Secretary Sajid Javid. All four have stated their intentions to succeed Theresa May as the leader of the party and the country.Each candidate brings their own unique set of policies and vision for the future of the United Kingdom. With Brexit looming large on the horizon, the next Prime Minister will face the daunting task of navigating the country through these uncertain times.While the field of candidates may still grow in the coming days, one thing is clear: the race for Britain's next Prime Minister is well underway. Stay tuned for updates as the Conservative Party begins the process of selecting their new leader.

After facing months of disruption due to the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), children in the affected regions are finally able to return to school.The Ebola outbreak, which began in August 2018, had forced the closure of schools in the North Kivu and Ituri provinces in an effort to prevent the spread of the deadly virus. However, with the recent decline in Ebola cases and the efforts of health workers to contain the outbreak, schools have been deemed safe to reopen.This is a significant milestone for the children in these regions, as education is vital for their future development and well-being. Many children had been out of school for months, missing out on valuable learning opportunities.Authorities are working to ensure that schools are equipped with the necessary resources to prevent the spread of Ebola, such as hand washing stations and information on proper hygiene practices. Health screenings are also being conducted to monitor the health of students and prevent any potential outbreaks.While the reopening of schools is a positive step forward, it is important to continue monitoring the situation closely and ensure that children are able to receive a quality education in a safe environment. The return to school for these children is a sign of hope and progress in the fight against Ebola in the DRC.

The U.S. government has allocated $350 million to help the country prepare for the H1N1 flu virus as well as the seasonal flu. U.S. officials say a vaccine for the H1N1 flu could be available by October before the flu season starts in the northern hemisphere.The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that more than 400 people have died from the H1N1 flu virus out of some 90,000 confirmed cases. In June the WHO declared this outbreak to be the first pandemic in more than 40 years. Recently, WHO Director General Margaret Chan predicted the worldwide spread of H1N1 or the swine flu is certain."Once a fully fit pandemic virus emerges, its further international spread is unstoppable," she said.Currently, countries in the southern hemisphere are beginning their flu season. Chile, Argentina and Australia are seeing large numbers of cases. The U.S. government held a summit July 9 to prepare for the start of the flu season in the northern hemisphere.Secretary of Health and Human Services Kathleen Sebelius said government agencies at every level are preparing a defense against the H1N1 flu as well as the seasonal flu.

A recent poll has shown that support for former President Barack Obama's efforts to improve relations with Cuba is increasing among Americans. The poll, conducted by a reputable research organization, revealed that a majority of respondents now support Obama's push to boost ties with the island nation.Obama made significant strides in improving relations with Cuba during his time in office, including easing travel restrictions and reopening diplomatic channels. However, these efforts have faced setbacks in recent years, as the Trump administration rolled back some of the policies put in place by the Obama administration.Despite this, the poll indicates that public opinion on the issue is shifting. Many Americans now see the benefits of improving relations with Cuba, including increased trade and cultural exchanges. The poll also found that a growing number of respondents believe that engaging with Cuba is the best way to promote democracy and human rights on the island.The results of the poll come at a time when the Biden administration is considering how to approach relations with Cuba. President Biden has expressed his desire to reverse some of the Trump administration's policies towards Cuba, but has also emphasized the need to address human rights concerns on the island.Overall, the poll suggests that there is growing support among Americans for a more constructive approach to relations with Cuba. It remains to be seen how the Biden administration will navigate these challenges in the coming months.

In a recent development in the East China Sea, both the United States and Japan have expressed strong objections to China's actions in the region. This comes after China expanded its presence in disputed waters, raising tensions among the three countries.The US and Japan have both criticized China for its aggressive actions in the East China Sea, calling them a violation of international norms and threatening regional stability. The two countries have urged China to act in accordance with international law and to refrain from further provocations.The US has stated that it stands with Japan in defending its territorial integrity and sovereignty in the East China Sea. Japan has also reiterated its commitment to protecting its territorial waters and airspace from any encroachment.China, on the other hand, has defended its actions in the region, stating that it has a right to defend its sovereignty and territorial integrity. The Chinese government has called on the US and Japan to respect its rights and to refrain from interfering in its internal affairs.The situation in the East China Sea remains tense, with all three countries standing their ground. It is unclear how this dispute will be resolved, but it is clear that tensions in the region are on the rise.

An old railroad line in New York City is back in use. It doesn't deliver livestock anymore, it delivers pleasure to thousands of people every day and stimulates economic development in a few New York neighborhoods. An elevated railroad line has been turned into an "elevated park" right in the middle of New York City. It's called the "Highline." It's a newly renovated and elevated promenade that was once a railway line for delivering cattle and other foodstock. In 1980, the train made its last delivery, bringing frozen turkeys to lower Manhattan. In a densely populated city, the Highline now provides open space for relaxation as it winds through neighborhoods once noted for slaughterhouses. New Yorkers and tourists are enjoying it. This once drab neighborhood is being changed, seemingly overnight, thanks to the Highline.It's an oasis in a sea of concrete. The walkway includes more than 100 species of plants inspired by the wild landscape left after the trains stopped running. New construction is everywhere. Apartments, office towers, restaurants and even a museum have sprouted alongside the promendade. Those who live closest to it are proud, but at the same time wary."There's real pressure being put on working class people around here to still get a place," said a visitor. "So, you know, the Highline both honors the past and the industrial grittiness but it adds to the gentrification. It is a little tricky, but we are glad to have it."The first section of the Highline was inaugurated in May, after 15 years of planning and political battles.

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) has announced that it will be launching an investigation in Alaska following a deadly midair collision. The incident occurred on Tuesday afternoon near the town of Soldotna, resulting in the loss of multiple lives.According to initial reports, a small plane carrying tourists on a sightseeing trip collided with another aircraft in midair. The exact circumstances leading up to the collision are still under investigation, and the NTSB is working to determine the cause of the tragic incident.Alaska has a high rate of aviation accidents due to its rugged terrain and challenging weather conditions. The NTSB has previously conducted investigations in the state and is experienced in handling such cases.Authorities are urging the public to avoid the area as the investigation continues. Our thoughts are with the families and loved ones of those affected by this tragedy.

Vice President Mike Pence recently made a promise to the people of Venezuela, pledging continued support until democracy is restored in the country. Speaking at a rally in Miami, Pence emphasized the United States' unwavering commitment to the Venezuelan people and their struggle for freedom.Pence reiterated the US government's support for interim president Juan Guaidó and the National Assembly in their efforts to bring about democratic reforms in Venezuela. He condemned the oppressive regime of Nicolás Maduro and called on other countries to join the US in supporting the Venezuelan people.The Vice President also announced new sanctions targeting individuals and entities who continue to support Maduro's regime. Pence made it clear that the US will not stand idly by while the Venezuelan people suffer under a repressive government.The support from the United States comes at a crucial time for Venezuela, as the country continues to face a humanitarian crisis marked by widespread food and medicine shortages. The promise of continued support from the US gives hope to many Venezuelans who are fighting for a brighter future for their country.As Pence's pledge of support reverberates throughout Venezuela, the people are left with a renewed sense of determination to push for democratic reforms and bring an end to the Maduro regime. The Vice President's words serve as a reminder that the international community stands in solidarity with the Venezuelan people in their quest for freedom and democracy.

Warning of a dangerous wave of incoming smog, Chinese authorities on Friday issued their second ever red alert for pollution in the capital, Beijing.Forecasters expect the cloud of smog to hover over much of northern China from Saturday until Tuesday, and say pollution levels reach more than 20 times the safe limit.Under a red alert, schools and some factories are closed, and half of all private cars will be pulled from the street. All outdoor construction will also be halted."I'm actually very concerned," said 34-year-old Beijing resident Cheng Xianke. "I'm concerned about the rather large impact it will have on elderly people and children, because children want to go out to play… For us who commute to work it's not so bad, but still I'm very concerned about the pollution. I think the government needs to put more effort into solving this."

The Trump administration is reported to be considering a new executive order aimed at blocking asylum seekers and immigrants from crossing the U.S.-Mexico border, as a caravan of migrants continues to make its way north through Mexico.News reports quoting unnamed administration officials say authorities are weighing a range of administrative and legal actions on grounds of national security to restrict the ability of migrants to seek asylum.Although no decision has reportedly been made yet, immigration attorneys told VOA the move would be quickly challenged in court.Migrants travel on a cattle truck, as a thousands-strong caravan of Central American migrants slowly makes its way toward the U.S. border, between Pijijiapan and Arriaga, Mexico, Oct. 26, 2018.Some reports have suggested a complete border closure is under consideration, but analysts consider such a move politically impossible because of the billions of dollars in commerce between the U.S. and Mexico.Instead, the administration is likely aiming for a narrower action particularly targeting asylum seekers who arrive in the United States without traveling through regular border crossings.Border Patrol agents and others stand next to a new stretch of border wall in Calexico, Calif., Oct. 26, 2018.Homeland Security Secretary Kirstjen Nielsen told reporters in California Friday that “everything is on the table’’ as the administration considers new measures to stop illegal immigration.The development comes Friday as Mexico increased its efforts to stop the caravan from reaching the United States, offering temporary identification papers and jobs to migrants who register for asylum in Mexico. Mexican President Enrique Pena Nieto said migrants must remain in the southern states of Chiapas and Oaxaca to qualify for the plan.Leon Fresco, a former U.S.

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has announced that they are halting the approval process for a proposed gold and copper mine in order to further assess potential environmental impacts.The mine, known as the Pebble Mine, has faced significant opposition from environmental groups and local communities due to concerns about its potential to harm the surrounding ecosystem. The EPA's decision to pause the approval process comes after a recent court ruling that found the agency had not adequately considered the potential impacts of the mine on the region's salmon populations.In a statement, the EPA explained that they will be conducting a thorough review of the project to ensure that all potential environmental risks are properly addressed. The agency emphasized that they are committed to protecting the environment and will not move forward with the approval process until they are confident that the mine will not cause significant harm.The Pebble Mine project has been a contentious issue for years, with supporters arguing that it will bring much-needed economic development to the region, while opponents believe that the environmental risks far outweigh any potential benefits. The EPA's decision to pause the approval process is a significant development in the ongoing debate over the future of the mine.

CHARLOTTE, North Carolina — As Democrats arrived in Charlotte, North Carolina for their party’s national convention - where they will formally nominate President Barack Obama for a second term - they were greeted by hundreds of protesters affiliated with the Occupy Wall Street movement. Many of the protesters are concerned about the influence of corporate money in politics.In Charlotte North Carolina, the protests are bigger and livelier than similar events at the Republican National Convention in Tampa, because protesters say this is where President Obama and the Democratic Party are meeting to chart a path forward for the next four years.“The Democratic National Convention is a big tent for everything that America is," said Sheri Bailey, who traveled from Washington, DC to Charlotte, North Carolina not as a Democrat supporting President Obama, but as a protester hoping to have her voice heard.“This is how this country was forged, through protest, of speaking your voice clearly, and we’ve lost a lot of that over the last few decades," she said.Bailey was among hundreds gathered in Charlotte for a week of events targeted at “Wall Street South,” organized by activists like Sara Flounders. “The city of Charlotte is the second largest financial center in the country after Wall Street, and is considered Wall Street South. That’s actually why the DNC chose it. It is a major corporate banking center," she said.Organizers say the protests here in Charlotte are an extension of the Occupy Wall Street protests across the country, targeted at companies like Bank of America, and the largest power company in the United States, Duke Energy. Both have corporate headquarters in downtown Charlotte.Duke Energy is a target of GreenPeace USA’s Robert Gardner, who is critical of the company’s environmental record. He says those protesting represent a diverse group of disaffected voters.“

In a bold and unprecedented move, the infamous hacker group 'Anonymous' has declared 'total war' against President Donald Trump. The announcement was made in a video posted on social media, where a figure wearing a Guy Fawkes mask claimed that the group will be targeting the Trump administration in various ways.The video message from 'Anonymous' comes amidst growing tensions in the United States over the handling of the COVID-19 pandemic and the ongoing protests against police brutality and racism. The group has a history of targeting governments and organizations they perceive as oppressive or corrupt.While the specifics of their plans remain unclear, the message from 'Anonymous' warns that they will be launching cyber attacks and releasing sensitive information about President Trump and his associates. The group has also urged people to join them in their efforts to resist the current administration.It is yet to be seen how the Trump administration will respond to this declaration of 'total war' from 'Anonymous'. However, this latest development is a stark reminder of the power and influence that hacker groups can have in the digital age.

WASHINGTON - After announcing two major global initiatives on climate change last week, the Obama administration pivoted on Monday to American towns and cities to help them adapt to the impacts of global warming.The move came after a task force of U.S. governors, mayors and tribal leaders sent Vice President Joe Biden and senior White House officials recommendations on how they can help local communities deal with extreme weather.White House officials also unveiled measures, including a Web-based climate resilience toolkit, to help local leaders adopt measures to prepare municipalities for rising sea levels, droughts, diseases and other climate impacts.The recommendations come as the new Republican leadership eyes reining in executive actions in the administration's Climate Action Plan, which includes the efforts to support cities and towns.The task force, appointed last November by Obama, said recommendations focus on how Washington can modernize programs and policies to incorporate climate change and provide tools to help local communities better design their own adaptation measures.One measure calls for tools to track health problems linked to climate change, such as asthma.Another asks Washington to integrate climate resilience planning criteria in all federal programs, such as those that provide transportation funding, “to ensure these projects will last as long as intended.”The recommendations would “reorient” existing funds at a time when seeking new funding in Congress would be problematic since the new Republican leadership has said it will use federal budgets to weaken the president's climate plan.“At the local level, we just shake our heads at Washington.

Exit Polls: Two Pro-Kremlin Parties Will Win Local Russian ElectionsAccording to exit polls conducted during the local elections in Russia, it has been predicted that two pro-Kremlin parties will emerge victorious. The United Russia party, which supports President Vladimir Putin, is expected to secure a significant number of seats in various regions across the country.Another pro-Kremlin party, the Liberal Democratic Party of Russia, is also projected to perform well in the elections. These results suggest that the current political landscape in Russia continues to favor parties aligned with the ruling government.While the final results are yet to be announced, these exit polls provide a glimpse into the potential outcome of the elections. Stay tuned for more updates on the local Russian elections.

Turkey's ruling party, the Justice and Development Party (AKP), has announced former Prime Minister Binali Yildirim as its candidate for the upcoming elections. Yildirim, a close ally of President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, is expected to lead the party in the race for the premiership. The decision comes as the AKP looks to maintain its hold on power amidst growing challenges and opposition in the country. Yildirim's candidacy is seen as a strategic move by the party to secure a strong position in the upcoming elections. The announcement has already sparked debates and discussions among political analysts and the public about the future of Turkey under his leadership.

Australia has come under intense scrutiny and criticism for its practice of separating refugee families who arrive in the country seeking asylum. This controversial policy has sparked outrage from human rights organizations and activists worldwide.According to reports, the Australian government has been detaining asylum-seekers who arrive by boat and separating children from their parents. These families are often placed in immigration detention centers, where conditions have been described as inhumane and overcrowded.The United Nations has called on Australia to end this practice immediately, stating that it violates the rights of children and goes against international human rights laws. Many have also raised concerns about the mental and emotional toll that this separation takes on both the children and their parents.Australian officials have defended their actions, stating that they are necessary to deter people from attempting dangerous journeys by sea. However, critics argue that this policy is cruel and unjust, and that there are more humane ways to handle the asylum process.As pressure mounts on Australia to change its policies, it remains to be seen how the government will respond to the growing backlash. In the meantime, advocates continue to call for an end to the separation of refugee families and for more compassionate treatment of those seeking refuge in Australia.

Defense Secretary Chuck Hagel recently spoke about the ongoing challenges that US troops are facing in Afghanistan. Despite progress being made, Hagel emphasized that there are still obstacles that need to be overcome.One of the main challenges highlighted by Hagel is the continued threat posed by the Taliban and other insurgent groups in the region. These groups have been able to adapt to the changing security situation and continue to pose a threat to both Afghan forces and coalition troops.Additionally, the Afghan government still faces issues of corruption and instability, which have hindered efforts to establish lasting peace and security in the country. Hagel stressed the importance of addressing these issues in order to ensure the long-term success of the mission in Afghanistan.While the US has been able to significantly reduce its troop presence in Afghanistan in recent years, Hagel emphasized that a complete withdrawal of troops is not yet possible. He stated that a residual force will be necessary to continue training and supporting Afghan security forces, as well as conducting counterterrorism operations.Overall, Hagel's comments highlight the complex and challenging nature of the situation in Afghanistan, and the continued efforts that will be required to achieve lasting peace and stability in the region.

The U.S. plane maker Boeing said Tuesday it agreed to sell 30 of its 737 MAX jets to Iran's Aseman Airlines, a deal worth $3 billion.The sale marks the second such Boeing deal made possible by the 2015 nuclear agreement signed by former U.S. president Barack Obama to relieve sanctions on the Middle Eastern country. IranAir struck a $16.6 billion deal with the company in December for 80 planes."Boeing confirms the signing of a Memorandum of Agreement with Iran Aseman Airlines, expressing the airline's intent to purchase 30 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes with a list price value of $3 billion,” the plane maker said in a statement. “The agreement also provides the airline with purchase rights for 30 additional 737 MAXs.”Aseman Airlines is scheduled to start receiving the aircraft in 2022, though the deal is still contingent on approval from the U.S. government.The nuclear agreement limited Iran’s nuclear capabilities in exchange for the lifting of international economic sanctions. U.S. President Donald Trump heavily criticized the deal during his election campaign and has said he would like to see in renegotiated.Aseman Airlines flies both domestic and international flights from Iran.

Russians have been quick to criticize Thursday's vote in Estonia's parliament approving the removal of a controversial Soviet war statue from the center of the capital, Tallinn. Lisa McAdams report from Moscow.Estonian lawmakers approved the statue's removal, by a vote of 46 to 44, on the bill's third and final reading. It calls for the two-meter bronze statue of a Red Army soldier to be moved from the middle of a downtown public transportation hub to a more remote location, within one month.The measure still needs presidential approval. But that has not stopped the chorus of indignation from Estonia's minority-Russian population, which makes up one-third of the tiny Baltic nation's populace.Andrei Zarenko, an ethnic Russian living in Tallinn, says he and other like-minded protesters will urge Estonian President Thomas Hendrik-Ilves to veto the move.Zarenko says the plan to remove the soldier is as egregious as those who deny the Holocaust. He says it is an ill-advised attempt to re-write history.The speaker of Russia's parliament, Boris Gryzlov, echoed that sentiment in comments broadcast on Russian television. Gryzlov warns the plan could "seriously complicate relations between Estonia and Russia."Estonia, and its two Baltic neighbors - Latvia and Lithuania - were annexed by Moscow after Red Army forces drove out occupying German forces, at the end of World War II.

The recent terrorist attacks in Paris have put Republican outsiders in a difficult position as they navigate the implications of the tragic events. With the rise of anti-immigrant sentiments and calls for stricter border controls, these candidates are faced with the challenge of addressing national security concerns while also promoting their outsider status.Many Republican outsiders have capitalized on the fear and anger following the attacks, using them as a platform to push their own agendas. Donald Trump, in particular, has been vocal in his calls for increased surveillance and tougher immigration policies. Meanwhile, other candidates like Ted Cruz and Ben Carson have also weighed in on the situation, offering their own solutions to the perceived threats.However, the Paris attacks have also highlighted the limitations of the outsider candidates. While their tough rhetoric may appeal to some voters, questions have been raised about their ability to handle complex foreign policy issues and work effectively with other countries to combat terrorism.As the Republican primary race heats up, the Paris attacks have forced outsider candidates to walk a fine line between capitalizing on national security concerns and demonstrating their readiness for the presidency. With the eyes of the nation on them, these candidates will need to carefully navigate this delicate balancing act in the coming months.

A rights group has reported that the Islamic State militant group carried out mass executions in Syria. The group, known for its brutal tactics and human rights abuses, allegedly targeted civilians in the city of Raqqa.The report, released by the rights group on Thursday, detailed the execution of at least 45 individuals in the past week. The victims were reportedly accused of spying for the Syrian government or collaborating with rival militant groups.Witnesses described the executions as "horrific" and "barbaric", with victims being shot in public squares or beheaded in front of crowds.

The White House said on Friday that President Donald Trump spoke by phone on Monday to Libyan military commander Khalifa Haftar and discussed "ongoing counterterrorism efforts and the need to achieve peace and stability in Libya."The statement said Trump "recognized Field Marshal Haftar's significant role in fighting terrorism and securing Libya's oil resources, and the two discussed a shared vision for Libya's transition to a stable, democratic political system."It was unclear why the White House waited several days to announce the phone call.On Thursday, both the United States and Russia said they could not support a U.N. Security Council resolution calling for a ceasefire in Libya at this time.Also on Thursday, mortar bombs crashed down on a suburb of Tripoli, almost hitting a clinic, after two weeks of an offensive by Haftar's eastern troops on the Libyan capital, which is held by an internationally recognized government.Trump arrived on Thursday at his Mar-a-Lago club in Palm Beach, Florida, for the Easter weekend.Russia objects to the British-drafted resolution blaming Haftar for the latest flare-up in violence when his Libyan National Army (LNA) advanced to the outskirts of Tripoli earlier this month, diplomats said.The United States did not give a reason for its decision not to support the draft resolution, which would also call on countries with influence over the warring parties to ensure compliance and for unconditional humanitarian aid access in Libya.

Terrified Muslims hid in their homes in northwest Burma on Monday after armed police dispersed a Buddhist mob that torched houses and surrounded a mosque in the latest outbreak of sectarian violence. Clashes between Buddhists and Muslims have killed at least 237 people and left more than 150,000 homeless since June of last year. The violence threatens to undermine political and economic reforms launched in the two years since a quasi-civilian government replaced a military junta. The situation in the town of Thandwe was precarious after police restored order by firing shots in the air to break up the mob late on Sunday, said two security sources, who sought anonymity as they were not authorized to speak to the media. Thandwe, 162 miles from Yangon, the capital, is in Rakhine state, the region worst-hit by the religiously-motivated violence. As in several previous bouts of communal unrest, a minor disagreement triggered an outpouring of anger, a local Muslim politician said. “We're now scared and hiding inside our homes, like the previous times,” Kyaw Zan Hla, chairman of the Kaman Muslim Party, told Reuters over the telephone, adding that about 200 people had joined the mob, some wearing masks and carrying flaming torches. He said he had himself become embroiled in a row after he objected to a Buddhist man parking a motorcycle in front of his home late on Saturday and rumors spread that he had insulted Buddhism. Police reported no deaths or injuries from the incident in Thandwe, home to an airport used by tourists who visit resorts on the popular Ngapali beach nearby.

The World Food Program is gearing up for a potential onslaught in Idlib, Syria. As tensions rise in the region, the organization is taking proactive measures to ensure that aid can reach those in need in the event of a crisis.Idlib, one of the last remaining rebel-held strongholds in Syria, has been the target of numerous attacks and airstrikes in recent years.
