"Over time, I believe we're going to see them acquire these capabilities if they're not stopped."Nominated to lead U.S. Forces in South Korea, Brooks said that it would be his job to provide options for how the U.S. could use military pressure to change the North Korean calculus.FILE - North Korean leader Kim Jong Un looks at a rocket warhead tip after a simulated test of atmospheric re-entry of a ballistic missile, at an unidentified location in this undated photo released by North Korea's Korean Central News Agency."North Korea is moving in the wrong direction, and the changes that we've seen are all provocative and more dangerous — their willingness to draw blood, to sink vessels, to fire some of their numerous artillery systems into populated areas," he said.When asked by Senate Armed Services Committee Chairman John McCain, a Republican, about the "immaturity and the unpredictability of the rotund ruler in Pyongyang," Brooks responded that it is clear that North Korean leader Kim Jong Un "is not yet deterred."

Pope Francis delivered a powerful message to the people of Uganda during his visit to the country, urging them to be missionaries at home. The Pope emphasized the importance of spreading love, compassion, and kindness within their communities, rather than seeking to evangelize in distant lands.During his visit, Pope Francis met with various religious leaders and government officials, emphasizing the need for unity and peace in Uganda. He also highlighted the importance of caring for the most vulnerable members of society, such as the poor, the sick, and the marginalized.The Pope's message resonated with many Ugandans, who have long been known for their strong sense of community and compassion. By urging them to be missionaries at home, Pope Francis is encouraging the people of Uganda to continue spreading the message of love and hope within their own country.As the visit comes to an end, many Ugandans are inspired to take the Pope's message to heart and work towards building a more inclusive and compassionate society. Pope Francis' words serve as a reminder of the importance of kindness and empathy in creating a better world for all.

The United Nations envoy for Central Africa has called on the government of Cameroon to release detainees, restore internet services, and engage in dialogue to end the ongoing strike in the country.According to the envoy, the detention of individuals and the shutdown of internet services are exacerbating the situation and hindering efforts to find a peaceful resolution to the conflict. The envoy emphasized the importance of respecting human rights and allowing freedom of expression and assembly.The strike in Cameroon has been ongoing for several weeks, with protesters demanding political reforms and better living conditions. The situation has escalated in recent days, leading to increased tensions and violence.The UN envoy's statement comes as a reminder to the government of Cameroon of its obligations to respect human rights and engage in dialogue with all parties involved. The international community has also been urged to support efforts to find a peaceful solution to the crisis.It remains to be seen whether the government of Cameroon will heed the UN envoy's call and take concrete steps to address the grievances of the protesters. In the meantime, the situation in the country remains tense, with concerns about the well-being of detainees and the impact of the internet shutdown on the population.

WHITE HOUSE/STATE DEPARTMENT - The Trump administration on Tuesday defended its decision not to impose immediate sanctions on Russians named in a congressionally mandated report detailing the wealth and political activities of Kremlin insiders and billionaires.The U.S. Treasury Department published the report Monday in keeping with a law passed overwhelmingly by Congress last August, after it became clear that Russia had meddled in the 2016 presidential election.The report details the finances and political connections of 114 Russian politicians and 96 "oligarchs" who have prospered under Putin. But the report makes no mention of imposing new sanctions against the Russian government or individuals on the list, raising questions about whether Trump is too soft on the Kremlin.Confronted by Democratic Senator Bob Menendez of New Jersey during an appearance before the Senate Banking Committee on Tuesday, Treasury Secretary Steve Mnuchin said sanctions were in the works."I assume you haven't read the classified version [of the report]," Mnuchin told Menendez. "The intention was not to have sanctions by the delivery of the report last night. There will be sanctions coming out of this report."In a statement accompanying the release of the report, State Department spokeswoman Heather Nauert said there were no immediate plans to impose sanctions on Russia or any of the individuals named in the document.FILE - U.S. State Department spokeswoman Heather Nauert speaks during a briefing in Washington, Aug. 9, 2017.Law Impinging on Russian Companies' Bottom LineNauert said the law was already hurting Russian companies."

In an intense match on December 23, 2001, boxing fans witnessed an incredible performance as Girard emerged victorious to claim the WBA Light-Heavyweight Boxing Title. Girard's impressive skills and determination were on full display as he fought his way to the top and secured the championship title. This victory marks a significant milestone in Girard's career, solidifying his position as a formidable force in the boxing world. Congratulations to Girard on this well-deserved win!

A training program aimed at empowering pro-democracy activists from the Middle East and North Africa region is currently underway. The advocacy program, organized by a prominent human rights organization, aims to provide participants with the necessary skills and knowledge to effectively advocate for democratic reforms in their respective countries.The program includes a series of workshops and training sessions focused on topics such as strategic communication, coalition building, and grassroots mobilization. Participants are also provided with practical tools and resources to help them advance their advocacy efforts.One of the key objectives of the program is to create a network of like-minded activists who can collaborate and support each other in their advocacy work. By bringing together activists from different countries in the region, the program also aims to foster cross-border solidarity and cooperation.The organizers of the program are hopeful that the training will equip participants with the skills and confidence they need to effectively push for democratic reforms in their countries. They believe that empowered and well-trained activists can play a crucial role in bringing about positive change and advancing the cause of democracy in the region.Overall, the advocacy program represents a valuable opportunity for pro-democracy activists from the Middle East and North Africa to enhance their advocacy skills and build networks of support. With ongoing support and collaboration, these activists have the potential to make a significant impact on the democratic development of their countries.

In major-league baseball, the Los Angeles Dodgers suffocated the Cincinnati Reds with 10 runs in the first inning, on the way to a 13-6 win Friday.Jeff Kent had a two-run home run and a three-run double before the Reds even had a chance to bat. Hee-Seop Choi also hit a two-run homer in the top of the first, which lasted nearly a half hour. Cincinnati starter Paul Wilson failed to record a single out; he allowed eight runs on five hits, a walk and two hit batters.In another National League game, the Philadelphia Phillies scored two runs in the top of the ninth inning on a throwing error by Chicago's LaTroy Hawkins and edged the Cubs, 3-2.The New York Mets' catcher Mike Piazza hit two homers in a 7-4 win over the Brewers in Milwaukee.Other National League winners were the Braves, Marlins, Padres, Diamondbacks and Nationals.The Atlanta Braves beat the visiting Houston Astros, 9-4. The Florida Marlins shut out the Colorado Rockies, 7-0, in Miami. The Rockies have the worst record in the majors - 20 losses against just six wins. The San Diego Padres edged the Cardinals, 6-5, in St. Louis. The Arizona Diamondbacks doubled the Pittsburgh Pirates, 8-4, in Phoenix.

In response to recent developments, a group of lawyers is urging for a new evaluation of the Norway gunman responsible for the deadly attacks in 2011. The lawyers believe that it is crucial to reassess the mental state of the perpetrator in order to ensure justice is served and to prevent similar tragedies from occurring in the future.The call for a new evaluation comes after the release of a documentary that raised questions about the motives and mental health of the gunman. The lawyers argue that a fresh assessment is necessary to gain a deeper understanding of the individual's psychological profile and to potentially uncover any overlooked factors that may have played a role in the attacks.The Norway gunman, who was convicted of killing 77 people in a bombing and shooting rampage, has been serving a 21-year prison sentence since 2012. The lawyers are hopeful that a new evaluation will shed light on any underlying issues that may have been missed during the initial investigation and trial.The Norwegian authorities have yet to respond to the lawyers' request for a new evaluation.

In response to the economic challenges brought on by the ongoing pandemic, the British government has announced a massive bank bail-out plan to support the country's struggling financial institutions. The plan, which is estimated to be worth billions of pounds, aims to provide the necessary financial support to ensure the stability of the banking sector and prevent any further economic downturn.Chancellor of the Exchequer, Rishi Sunak, outlined the details of the plan in a press conference earlier today. He emphasized the importance of ensuring the resilience of the banking sector in order to protect the overall health of the economy. The bail-out plan will include a combination of financial assistance, regulatory reforms, and other measures to help banks weather the current economic storm.The announcement of the bail-out plan comes at a crucial time for the British economy, which has been hit hard by the effects of the pandemic. Many businesses are struggling to stay afloat, and the banking sector has faced increasing pressure as a result. The government's intervention is seen as a necessary step to prevent further economic damage and ensure the stability of the financial system.The specifics of the bail-out plan are still being finalized, but it is expected to provide much-needed support to banks of all sizes. The government has made it clear that it is committed to doing whatever is necessary to protect the economy and help businesses and individuals navigate the challenges posed by the pandemic.Overall, the unveiling of this massive bank bail-out plan signals the government's determination to support the financial sector and ensure the resilience of the economy in the face of unprecedented challenges. Time will tell how effective the plan will be in achieving its goals, but for now, it represents a significant step in the right direction.

In response to North Korea's proposal for military talks, South Korea has swiftly dismissed the offer as "bogus". The North's suggestion for discussions on easing military tensions and promoting peace between the two countries was met with skepticism from the South Korean government.Officials in South Korea criticized North Korea's intentions, stating that the proposal lacked sincerity and was merely a ploy to distract from their own provocative actions. The South Korean defense ministry emphasized that the North must first demonstrate a genuine commitment to denuclearization before any meaningful dialogue can take place.Tensions have been high on the Korean Peninsula in recent years, with both countries engaging in military drills and exchanging threats.

Brazilian authorities say two people have been shot dead in a confrontation involving landless peasants at a Swiss-owned farm in the southern state of Parana.Officials said Monday that a peasant leader and a security guard were killed after the farm owned by the Syngenta company was occupied by activists from Brazil's Landless Rural Workers Movement and the peasant rights group, Via Campesina.The demonstrators had invaded the farm to protest its use of genetically modified seeds. At least three guards and three activists also were reported injured.Local officials say the confrontation also involved armed men who had shown up to retake the property from the activists. Syngenta says its contract with its security company requires its guards to be unarmed.Last year, protesters camped out at the farm, alleging the company is illegally experimenting with research into genetically modified crops.

The Israeli military has more than tripled its demolitions of Palestinian structures in the occupied West Bank in the past three months, U.N. figures show, raising alarm among diplomats and human rights groups over what they regard as a sustained violation of international law.Figures collated by the U.N.'s Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA), which operates in Gaza, the West Bank and East Jerusalem, show that from an average of 50 demolitions a month in 2012-15, the average has risen to 165 a month since January, with 235 demolitions in February alone.The Israeli military, which has occupied the West Bank since the 1967 Middle East war, says it carries out the demolitions because the structures are illegal; it says they were built without a permit, in a closed military area or firing zone, or violate other planning and zoning restrictions.The U.N. and rights groups point out that permits are almost impossible for Palestinians to acquire, that firing zones are often declared but seldom used, and that many planning restrictions date from the British Mandate in the 1930s."It is a very marked and worrying increase," said Catherine Cook, an OCHA official in Jerusalem who closely monitors the demolitions. She described the situation as the worst since the U.N. body started collecting figures in 2009."

In Thailand, a significant number of people, primarily from ethnic minorities and stateless communities, are facing challenges in obtaining Thai citizenship due to the requirement of having a registered name. Without a registered name, these individuals are effectively rendered invisible in the eyes of the government, leaving them without access to basic rights and services.The lack of citizenship and legal identity not only prevents individuals from voting, owning property, or accessing healthcare and education, but it also leaves them vulnerable to exploitation and abuse. Many stateless individuals are forced to work in dangerous and low-paying jobs, with no legal recourse to protect their rights.The process of obtaining Thai citizenship is often complex and bureaucratic, requiring proof of birth, marriage, and lineage. For those without the proper documentation, the journey to citizenship can be long and arduous, if not impossible.Human rights activists and organizations are calling on the Thai government to streamline the citizenship process and remove barriers for stateless individuals to obtain legal identity. They argue that everyone has the right to a name and a nationality, as outlined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.Until these changes are made, thousands of people in Thailand will continue to live in limbo, without the recognition and rights that come with citizenship. The road to Thai citizenship remains a long and challenging one for many individuals, who are simply asking for the basic right to be seen and recognized by their government.

The European Space Agency and the Russian Space Agency Roscosmos have joined efforts to send a spacecraft to Mars.The ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter blasted off Monday from the Baikonur spaceport in Kazakhstan atop a proton rocket.The voyage to Mars is expected to take seven months with the spacecraft arriving on the red planet in October after a journey of 496 million kilometers.The ESA has said the aim of the mission is to determine "whether Mars is alive" and so the mission will be looking to solve the mystery of the source of Mars' methane gas.Traces of methane, a gas composed of carbon and hydrogen, have been observed in previous Mars missions.Methane gas is created largely by living microbes and is produced in abundance on earth at swamps, landfills and other deposits. It is also produced from processes deep underground that result in fossil fuels such as coal, oil and natural gas.

MOSCOW - Just a month after he began a new six-year term, Russian President Vladimir Putin continues a crackdown on dissent. Russian police raided the apartments of top opposition leaders in Moscow on Monday, the day before a mass rally is to take place against President Putin. Armed with assault rifles, investigative police raided 10 homes and offices. They said they were looking for evidence surrounding the last big anti-Putin rally. Staged the day before Putin's May 7 presidential inauguration, that rally ended in violence with 20 policemen injured and 436 protesters detained. Democratic Russia Committee Director Natalia Pelevine says the raids were designed to intimidate opposition leaders and their supporters. "They are trying to confiscate electronic equipment, probably computers and DVDs and any device that can hold information pretty much. We know they are confiscating that at this moment," Pelevine said. In the raids, police beat down the doors of two of the best known leaders of the younger generation of democracy protesters, internet blogger Alexei Navalny and television hostess Ksenia Sobchak. Later, a police source told Interfax that police confiscated nearly $1,900,000 from Sobchak's apartment. Sobchak tweeted of the raid: "People burst in at 8 o'clock in the morning, they were stopping me from putting my clothes on. They robbed the apartment. They humiliated me." Liberal political figures condemned the raids, which took part on a Russian holiday. Former finance minister Alexei Kudrin said the raid showed that "radicals" are gaining strength in the Kremlin.

After a thorough internal review, President Trump's pick for CIA Director has been cleared of any wrongdoing in the destruction of videotapes depicting the harsh interrogation techniques used on detainees. The controversial issue arose during Gina Haspel's confirmation process, as she faced scrutiny for her involvement in the destruction of the tapes while serving as the chief of staff to the CIA's then-director of operations. However, the internal review concluded that Haspel had acted within the law and the policies in place at the time. This development is likely to bolster Haspel's chances of being confirmed as the first female director of the CIA.

In a developing story in the Western Shoa region, conflicting reports have emerged regarding the cause of death of a young man. The father of the deceased claims that his son died as a result of police brutality, while the police have stated that the young man was killed in a car accident.The father of the deceased, who has requested to remain anonymous, alleges that his son was beaten to death by police officers during a routine traffic stop. He claims that his son was pulled over for a minor traffic violation and was then violently attacked by the officers.However, the police have refuted these claims and have stated that the young man died as a result of injuries sustained in a car accident. According to the police report, the young man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a tree, resulting in his death.The conflicting reports have caused tension in the community, with many residents demanding a thorough investigation into the young man's death. Both the father and the police have called for transparency and accountability in the investigation to determine the true cause of death.The local authorities have assured the public that a full investigation will be conducted to uncover the truth behind the young man's tragic death.

May 13 marks four years since the violent suppression of a protest in Uzbekistan. Hundreds of people were killed in the incident when Uzbek authorities opened fire on demonstrators in the city of Andijan. In Moscow, a small group of demonstrators commemorated the tragedy with a protest at the Uzbek Embassy, urging the world community to demand an investigation into what some call the Andijan Massacre. The handful of demonstrators in Moscow were outnumbered by police and journalists who gathered near the Uzbek Embassy, where guards in camouflage uniforms peered from behind the mission's heavy metal gates. Daria Sobolova, a member of Russia's Memorial human rights organization, told VOA the Andijan demonstration gets smaller with each passing year, not because people have forgotten, but because they fear repression. Soboleva says relatives of activists who demonstrate in Russia are persecuted in Uzbekistan. She adds that if an activist is not a citizen of Russia, he or she can be returned to Uzbekistan where they are imprisoned, or they simply disappear. Organizer Bakhrom Khamroyev, head of the Association of Central Asian Political Emigrants in Moscow, says the event was meant as a protest against serious Uzbek human rights violations. He says these include torture, imprisonment or exile of opposition activists.

In a strategic move to appeal to working-class voters, Democratic presidential nominee Joe Biden has been channeling former President Barack Obama's 2008 campaign style. Biden's approach includes a focus on economic issues and a message of unity and hope for the American people.During a recent virtual campaign event, Biden emphasized the importance of rebuilding the economy and creating good-paying jobs for working-class families. He highlighted his plans to invest in infrastructure, manufacturing, and clean energy, as well as his commitment to raising the minimum wage and providing affordable healthcare for all.By invoking Obama's successful campaign tactics, Biden hopes to connect with blue-collar workers who may have previously supported President Trump. Biden's message of empathy and understanding for the struggles faced by working-class Americans is resonating with many voters who feel overlooked by the current administration.As the November election draws near, Biden's appeal to working-class voters could be a key factor in determining the outcome. With the economy continuing to be a top concern for many Americans, Biden's focus on economic issues and his promise of a better future for working-class families could sway undecided voters in his favor.

The European Parliament has passed a resolution condemning the worsening political climate in Cambodia and said it would tie future aid to improvements in human rights.The resolution, which passed Thursday, called on the government of Prime Minister Hun Sen to revoke the outstanding arrest warrant against Cambodia National Rescue Party president Sam Rainsy, who went into self-imposed exile in November after it was issued over a long-standing defamation conviction.It also called for the release of four human rights workers and an election official jailed last month on bribery-related charges; for charges against former union representative and member of the country's election body Rong Chhun to be dropped; and for an independent investigation into an attack on two opposition lawmakers outside parliament.All politicians, activists and human rights defenders should "be allowed to work freely without fear of arrest or persecution," a statement from the European Parliament read.FILE - Sam Rainsy, center, leader of the opposition Cambodia National Rescue Party (CNRP), talks to journalists upon his arrival at Phnom Penh International Airport in Phnom Penh, Cambodia, Aug. 16, 2015.Phay Siphan, a spokesman for the Council of Ministers, said the resolution "only reflects the interests of the European lawmakers and politicians.""But we will try to explain it to them so that they can understand, because we believe that they are not colonizers who can dictate to Cambodia and do whatever they want. We see them as our dialogue partners, and I thank them for their interest in Cambodia," he said."

The U.S. state of Ohio filed a lawsuit Wednesday against five prescription drug manufacturers, saying they used deceptive practices that fueled an opioid addiction epidemic.“These drug manufacturers led prescribers to believe that opioids were not addictive, that addiction was an easy thing to overcome, or that addiction could actually be treated by taking even more opioids,” Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine said. “They knew they were wrong, but they did it anyway.”FILE - An emergency opioid overdose kit is seen at the statehouse in Concord, New Hampshire, Sept. 29, 2015.The state wants the companies to stop misrepresenting the drugs, to pay damages for the amount of money the state spent on excessive opioid prescriptions and resulting addiction treatments for patients, and to reimburse patients as well.The lawsuit names Purdue Pharma, Endo Health Solutions, Allergan, Teva Pharmaceutical Industries and its subsidiary Cephalon, and Johnson & Johnson with its subsidiary Janssen Pharmaceuticals.A Janssen statement called the lawsuit legally and factually unfounded and said the company has acted appropriately and responsibly. Purdue said it shares DeWine’s concerns about the opioid crisis and wants to work together on a solution.The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention says opioids are most commonly given for moderate-to-severe pain after surgery or injury, but that in recent years there has been an increase in their prescription for chronic pain “despite serious risks and the lack of evidence about their long-term effectiveness.”Opioids, including prescription varieties, and heroin killed a record 33,000 people in the United States in 2015, according to the CDC.Ohio’s lawsuit says 2.3 million people, or nearly 20 percent of its population, were prescribed an opioid drug last year, and that the drugs are the main source of unintentional overdose deaths in the state.

The Rwanda Genocide Memorial in Kigali is working tirelessly to ensure that the atrocities of the 1994 genocide are never forgotten. The memorial, which was established in 2004, serves as a tribute to the more than 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu people who were killed during the genocide.Through its exhibits, the memorial educates visitors about the events leading up to the genocide, the devastating consequences, and the steps taken towards reconciliation and healing in the aftermath. The goal is to honor the memory of the victims, while also promoting unity and peace in Rwanda.The memorial features a number of exhibits, including photographs, personal testimonies, and artifacts from the genocide. Visitors can learn about the history of Rwanda, the role of the international community during the genocide, and the efforts made by Rwandans to rebuild their nation in the years following the tragedy.By preserving the memory of the genocide, the Rwanda Genocide Memorial aims to ensure that such a horrific event never happens again. It serves as a reminder of the importance of promoting peace, understanding, and unity in order to prevent future acts of violence and hatred.

Former President Barack Obama is set to attend the end of the Copenhagen Summit, where world leaders are coming together to address pressing global issues such as climate change and sustainable development. Obama's presence at the summit is expected to bring a strong voice of support for international cooperation and collective action. As a prominent advocate for environmental protection, Obama's participation is likely to further galvanize efforts to combat climate change and promote sustainability. The Copenhagen Summit is seen as a crucial opportunity for world leaders to reaffirm their commitment to addressing the urgent challenges facing our planet. Obama's presence is sure to add significant momentum to the discussions and negotiations taking place at the summit.

The United States and Egypt have come together in an effort to reignite the stalled Israeli-Palestinian peace process. Representatives from both countries have been engaging in discussions and meetings to find ways to move forward and bring about a resolution to the long-standing conflict.The peace process between Israel and Palestine has been at a standstill for years, with both sides failing to reach a mutually acceptable agreement. The US and Egypt are now looking to play a key role in facilitating negotiations and encouraging both parties to come back to the negotiating table.The renewed focus on the peace process comes amidst escalating tensions in the region, particularly in the aftermath of recent conflicts between Israel and Hamas. The US and Egypt believe that now is the time to push for progress and work towards a lasting peace agreement that addresses the concerns of both sides.Both countries have emphasized the importance of a two-state solution, with a secure and recognized Israel alongside a sovereign and independent Palestinian state.

Researchers from the University of Medical Sciences have made a groundbreaking discovery in the field of medicine. They have found a more accurate and effective way to determine the appropriate dose of anti-clotting medication for patients.Anti-clotting medication, also known as blood thinners, are commonly prescribed to patients at risk of developing blood clots, which can lead to serious health complications such as heart attacks and strokes. However, determining the correct dosage for each individual patient has been a challenge for healthcare providers.The new method developed by the researchers involves analyzing a patient's genetic information to personalize the dosage of the medication. By taking into account the patient's unique genetic makeup, doctors can now tailor the treatment to better suit their needs and reduce the risk of potential side effects.This personalized approach to dosing anti-clotting medication has shown promising results in clinical trials, with patients experiencing fewer complications and better outcomes. The researchers hope that this new method will become a standard practice in hospitals around the world, improving the safety and effectiveness of anti-clotting therapy for patients.This groundbreaking discovery has the potential to revolutionize the way anti-clotting medication is prescribed, ultimately leading to better patient care and outcomes. The researchers are excited about the implications of their findings and look forward to further developing and implementing this personalized approach in clinical practice.

California has urged President Trump to drop his plan to weaken fuel standards for cars and trucks. The state's attorney general, Xavier Becerra, stated that the move would harm the environment and public health. California has long been a leader in setting strict fuel efficiency standards, and many other states have followed its example. The Trump administration's proposal would revoke California's authority to set its own emission rules, which has led to a legal battle between the state and federal government. The outcome of this dispute will have significant implications for the future of fuel standards and environmental protection in the United States.

CAIRO - One of Egypt’s most prominent pro-democracy activists was released from prison early Friday after serving a five-year sentence for inciting and taking part in protests, his family and lawyer said.Alaa Abdel-Fattah rose to prominence with the 2011 Arab Spring uprisings that swept the Middle East and in Egypt, toppled long-time President Hosni Mubarak. To many, his imprisonment three years later — at a time when authorities imposed draconian laws banning public gatherings and unauthorized demonstrations — was another sign of Egypt’s return to Mubarak’s autocratic rule.Abdel-Fattah’s sisters, Mona and Sanaa Seif, posted on Facebook that “Alaa is out,” along with a video of him at home, playing with a dog. His lawyer, Khaled Ali, confirmed the release by posting: “Thanks God, Alaa Abdel-Fattah at home.”FILE - Egyptian prominent blogger Alaa Abdel-Fattah, center, hugs his recently born son, Khaled, his mother, Laila Soueif, right, and his sister, Ahdaf Soueif, left, after his release, in Cairo, Egypt.An outspoken dissident, Abdel-Fattah was detained several times before.

South Korea is reporting its first official case of the mosquito-borne Zika virus.The country's Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said Tuesday a 43-year-old man who recently traveled to Brazil tested positive for the virus.Yonhap news agency says the man has been quarantined and treated at a hospital in Gwnangju, located more than 300 kilometers south of Seoul.The Zika virus, which has been detected mainly in Latin America, is a generally mild, harmless infection. But it has been linked to over 4,000 of cases in Brazil of microcephaly in babies, a birth defect in which babies have unusually-small heads and brains.The World Health Organization has declared the outbreak of the Zika virus as a global health emergency.

A powerful suicide bomb in Afghanistan has killed the country's deputy chief of the national spy agency and 22 others. Taliban insurgents have claimed responsibility for the deadly attack. The violence comes as the United Nations has reported a significant decline in opium cultivation in the country. The illegal narcotics trade is said to be a major cause of instability in Afghanistan and is believed to be funding Taliban insurgency. The suicide bombing took place in the eastern Afghan province of Laghman. Authorities say that deputy chief of the country's powerful National Directorate for Security, Abdullah Laghmani, and other senior officials were leaving the main mosque in the provincial capital Mehtar Lam, when a suicide bomber hit them. Provincial Governor Lutfullah Mashal says most of the deaths occurred instantly and several top provincial officials were among those killed.The governor says that 18 civilians were also killed in the attack while 35 people were wounded who are being treated in hospitals in Laghman and in the city of Jalalabad.Taliban insurgents immediately claimed responsibility, saying the deputy chief of the spy agency was their target. President Hamid Karzai has condemned the deadly attack such a "vicious act" of terrorists.Taliban insurgents have launched frequent and deadly attacks on local as well foreign troops in Afghanistan this year. The violence has left hundreds of people dead.

French President Emmanuel Macron has stood firm in the face of pressure from China regarding a possible meeting with the Dalai Lama, the spiritual leader of Tibet. Despite China's warnings and attempts to influence French foreign policy, President Macron has reiterated his commitment to upholding human rights and freedom of speech. The Dalai Lama has long been a controversial figure in China, with the Chinese government accusing him of seeking Tibetan independence. The French government's decision to not bow down to China's pressure signals a strong stance on issues of human rights and political independence. Macron's refusal to be swayed by China's demands showcases France's dedication to maintaining diplomatic independence and standing up for what they believe in. It remains to be seen how China will respond to Macron's decision, but for now, the French President's rejection of Chinese pressure stands as a powerful statement in defense of human rights and political autonomy.

TOKYO - Kim Jong Un will be keeping North Korea watchers busy on New Year's Day, when he is expected to give his annual address laying out the country's top priorities for the year ahead.The speech, which is normally broadcast on North Korea's state-run television network, is often the best gauge of what the North Korean leadership is focused on and what tone it will take in its dealings with the outside world.For 2019, it will be parsed carefully for clues about Kim's thinking on denuclearization talks with Washington and a second summit with President Donald Trump, relations with South Korea and Pyongyang's efforts to get out from under international sanctions as it tries to build up its domestic economy.A look at Kim's plate for the coming year:This is Kim's primary concern. He made that clear in his 2018 News Year's address and his government has been hammering it home ever since.In his first televised speech, at a military parade in 2012, Kim vowed the nation would never again have to tighten its belts, a reference to the economic hardships it has faced, including a disastrous famine in the 1990s.While they remain isolated and unable to travel or experience foreign media freely, North Koreans are aware of the yawning prosperity gap between themselves, South Korea and China. Kim has tried to address that by initiating infrastructure projects in major cities, building up the capital and allowing — if not overtly supporting — the spread of the market economy.

Chinese state media say President Xi Jinping told U.S. leader Donald Trump that resolving North Korea tensions should be achieved peacefully.The reports said the leaders spoke by telephone Wednesday, nearly a week after after face-to-face talks at a summit in the U.S. state of Florida. The White House has not yet issued its account of the conversation.On Tuesday, Trump used Twitter to reiterate his calls for China to help rein in North Korea.“North Korea is looking for trouble,” Trump wrote. “If China decides to help, that would be great. If not, we will solve the problem without them!”He did not elaborate on what actions the U.S. might take. But in another message posted minutes earlier, Trump said he told Xi terms of a trade deal would be better for China “if they solve the North Korea problem.”China weary of North KoreaChina is North Korea's main benefactor, with the Council on Foreign Relations describing Beijing as North Korea's “most important ally, biggest trading partner, and main source of food, arms, and energy.”But China has also grown weary of North Korea's repeated missile tests in violation of United Nations sanctions, as well as its five nuclear tests. Some analysts believe North Korea is preparing a sixth nuclear test.Trump Saturday dispatched a U.S. Navy strike group to the northern Pacific waters near North Korea to send a message to North Korean leader Kim Jong Un.

Lebanon's Prime Minister Saad Hariri has announced his second attempt at forming a unity government in the country, following his resignation last year amid widespread protests against government corruption and economic turmoil.Hariri, who was reappointed as Prime Minister in October, has been working to navigate the complex political landscape in Lebanon and bring together the country's deeply divided political factions. His first attempt at forming a government in October failed due to disagreements over ministerial appointments.The new government formation comes at a crucial time for Lebanon, as the country continues to grapple with an unprecedented economic crisis, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic. The Lebanese pound has lost more than 80% of its value, leading to soaring inflation and widespread unemployment.Hariri has stated that his priority is to implement necessary reforms to address the economic crisis and restore stability in the country. However, he faces significant challenges in forming a government that is acceptable to all political parties and capable of implementing these reforms.Despite the challenges ahead, many Lebanese citizens are hopeful that a new government will bring much-needed change and address the pressing issues facing the country. Hariri's second attempt at forming a unity government will be closely watched by both domestic and international observers as Lebanon seeks to navigate its way out of the current crisis.

In a surprising turn of events, two Republican senators have publicly criticized President Trump for his relentless attacks on the media. Senators John McCain and Jeff Flake, both known for their outspokenness within the GOP, took to the Senate floor to denounce Trump's behavior.Senator McCain, a long-time critic of Trump, called the President's attacks on the media "unpatriotic" and a threat to democracy. Senator Flake echoed McCain's sentiments, stating that the President's rhetoric undermines the freedom of the press, a cornerstone of American democracy.This rare display of dissent from within the Republican party highlights the growing divide between Trump and some of his own party members. Both McCain and Flake have been vocal opponents of Trump's policies and behavior in the past, but this latest rebuke signals a new level of discord within the GOP.As the feud between Trump and the media intensifies, it remains to be seen how other Republican lawmakers will react to the President's attacks. With the 2020 elections on the horizon, tensions within the party are likely to continue to escalate as lawmakers navigate their allegiance to the President and their commitment to upholding democratic principles.

The New South Wales state government in Australia is boosting security at this year's APEC summit in light of the foiled attacks in Britain. Hundreds of additional surveillance cameras will be installed on trains and buses. Authorities are building a citywide digital network linking public and private closed-circuit TV cameras. Phil Mercer reports from Sydney.Hundreds of high-definition surveillance cameras are being installed on Sydney's transportation network ahead of September's summit in Australia's biggest city.Security cameras at major railway stations are being upgraded with facial-recognition technology, which proved to be effective in the aftermath of the London bombings two years ago.The security upgrade will cost $29 million.The Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit will bring together leaders from more than 20 countries, including the United States, China and Japan. APEC promotes trade and economic coordination along the Pacific Rim.Security measures will be the strictest Sydney has ever seen and will be made even more stringent following foiled car bombings in Britain earlier this month.Australia's ambassador for counter-terrorism, Mike Smith, thinks the security operation will do its job."There's always a threat to any large gathering of people like that - like the APEC conference - and particularly a gathering which brings together very senior figures - presidents and prime ministers and so on," Smith said. "So, everyone's aware of that and of course the authorities are ultra aware of it. The police here in New South Wales, ASIO, the Australian federal police.

In a powerful display of solidarity and determination to combat Islamophobia, a Muslim father and son duo recently made a historic visit to the United States Congress. The father, a respected community leader, and his son, a young activist, shared their personal stories and experiences with lawmakers in an effort to foster understanding and promote unity.During their visit, the father and son highlighted the discrimination and prejudice faced by Muslims in America and emphasized the importance of combating stereotypes and fostering mutual respect. They also called for legislation to address the rising tide of Islamophobia and protect the rights of Muslim Americans.Their presence at the US Congress sent a clear message that Muslims are an integral part of American society and deserve to be treated with dignity and respect. By sharing their stories and engaging with policymakers, the father and son demonstrated the power of dialogue and education in countering hate and division.The father and son's visit serves as a reminder that individuals from all backgrounds have a role to play in promoting understanding and building bridges between communities. As incidents of Islamophobia continue to rise, their efforts to raise awareness and advocate for change are more important than ever.

The community in North Carolina has banded together to help animals that have been traumatized by the recent hurricanes that have hit the region. These animals have been displaced from their homes and families and are in desperate need of care and support.Local shelters and animal rescue organizations have been working tirelessly to provide medical attention, food, shelter, and love to these animals in need. Volunteers have also come together to assist with feeding, walking, and socializing with the animals to help them recover from the trauma they have experienced.Many of these animals have been separated from their owners during the hurricanes and are now in need of new forever homes. Adoption events have been organized to help these animals find loving families who can give them the care and attention they deserve.The community response to helping these hurricane-traumatized animals has been overwhelming, with many individuals and businesses donating supplies, food, and funds to support these efforts. It is heartwarming to see the community come together to help these vulnerable animals in their time of need.

LOS ANGELES - Anna Wintour, the influential editor of Vogue magazine, is remaining in her job "indefinitely," publisher Conde Nast said Tuesday, refuting rumors that she was on her way out after 30 years."Anna Wintour is an incredibly talented and creative leader whose influence is beyond measure. She is integral to the future of our company's transformation and has agreed to work with me indefinitely in her role as @voguemagazine editor in chief and artistic director of Conde Nast," Conde Nast Chief Executive Bob Sauerberg said in a statement on the company's Twitter feed.Wintour, 68, known for her large sunglasses, pageboy haircut, and aloof public profile, has been editor in chief of the fashion magazine since 1988.Rumors that she was about to step down or be pushed aside have been swirling in the fashion industry for weeks. Women's Wear Daily wrote earlier this month that speculation about her exit "has reached a crescendo louder than a tea kettle on full boil."
The future of Southern Bluefin Tuna is looking grim as scientists warn that stocks are close to collapse.

ISLAMABAD - Pakistan's army said on Tuesday it will deploy about 371,000 troops to ensure a "fair and free" election on July 25, denying it was rigging the poll to tip the scales in favor of former cricketer Imran Khan.The run-up to the general election has been marred by accusations that the military is meddling in politics and muzzling the media to help usher Khan's Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI) into power.Khan, an anti-corruption crusader and conservative on national security, has denied colluding with the military, which has ruled the nuclear-armed country for almost half its history.Military spokesman Major General Asif Ghafoor told a news conference that a total of 371,388 troops would be deployed inside and outside polling stations, some three times more than during the last election in 2013.The election commission had asked the armed forces to help conduct the vote in a "free, fair and transparent manner," and the army's role was to assist, he said."

Rockets from Gaza hit southern Israel on Thursday, causing damage to buildings but no casualties were reported. In response, the Israeli military launched airstrikes in Gaza, targeting Hamas facilities.Meanwhile, four Palestinians were killed and dozens injured during clashes at the Israel-Gaza border. The violence erupted as thousands of Palestinians protested against the Israeli occupation and the ongoing blockade of Gaza.The Palestinian Health Ministry reported that two of the deceased were teenagers, while the other two were in their early 20s. Israeli forces used live ammunition and tear gas to disperse the protesters, leading to the casualties.Tensions have been high in the region for weeks, with frequent clashes at the border and sporadic rocket fire from Gaza. The latest violence comes as the international community continues to call for a ceasefire and a peaceful resolution to the conflict.

LONDON - Bhutan and the Maldives have eliminated measles, becoming the first countries in their region to eliminate the highly infectious disease that is a major child killer globally, the World Health Organization said Tuesday.The milestone was reached after no measles cases originating in the Maldives had been recorded since 2009 and none in Bhutan since 2012, the WHO said.Both countries launched immunization programs about 40 years ago, with mass vaccination of people at high risk."The strongest political commitment, alongside concerted efforts of health workers, officials and partners at all levels, has helped achieve this landmark success," Poonam Khetrapal Singh, regional director of WHO Southeast Asia, said in a statement.The WHO has set a 2020 deadline for the elimination of measles in the 11 countries it categorizes as the Southeast Asia region.The region has averted an estimated 620,000 measles deaths in 2016 after carrying out vaccinations in the 11 countries, the WHO said.Nearly 107 million children were reached with an additional dose of measles vaccine in the region between 2013 and 2016, according to the WHO.Globally, measles remains a leading cause of death among young children in the developing world.

In a surprising statement, a senior Australian official has criticized the United States for failing to anticipate the stiff resistance from some Iraqi forces during the ongoing military operation. The official, who chose to remain anonymous, expressed concerns about the level of preparedness and intelligence gathering by the US military.The official's comments come as the US-led coalition faces fierce opposition from Iraqi troops in various parts of the country. The unexpected resistance has led to casualties among coalition forces and raised doubts about the effectiveness of the military campaign.Despite the challenges on the ground, the official reiterated Australia's commitment to supporting the coalition efforts in Iraq. However, the official emphasized the need for better planning and coordination to address the evolving situation.As the situation in Iraq continues to unfold, it remains to be seen how the US and its allies will adapt to the changing dynamics on the battlefield. The criticism from the Australian official highlights the importance of thorough assessment and strategic planning in military operations.

Ethiopia's finance minister says western donors' freezing of $375 million in aid to his country is unacceptable.Sofian Ahmed said Friday the direct aid amounts to 10 percent of Ethiopia's annual budget.Western diplomats said Thursday the European Union, the World Bank and other donors would withhold the aid as a result of Ethiopia's crackdown on opposition leaders and journalists.Media reports say the donors hope to disburse the money through other channels to address Ethiopia's widespread poverty.The government crackdown followed clashes between security forces and protesters last month, in which at least 46 people were killed. Wednesday, a judge ordered the continued confinement of more than 80 opposition leaders and journalists detained during the protests.A total of 131 people are charged with plotting against the Ethiopian government, treason, and genocide.

The Islamic State terror group on Tuesday claimed responsibility for the deadly Easter terror attack in Sri Lanka, framing it as revenge against the “Crusader coalition” countries which defeated the terror group’s self-declared caliphate in Syria last month.The bombings Sunday, targeting churches and hotels across Sri Lanka, killed more than 300 people and wounded at least 500 others.A Sri Lankan Police officer inspects a blast spot at the Shangri-la hotel in Colombo, Sri Lanka, April 21, 2019.Most of the dead and wounded were Sri Lankans, but officials said at least 38 foreigners were also killed, including British, Australian, Turkish, Danish, Dutch, Indian, Chinese and American citizens.A formal communique from Islamic State said seven attackers had targeted “churches and hotels in which citizens of the Crusader coalition were present,” according to a translation by the SITE Intelligence Group.Three of the terrorists “went towards a gathering of Crusaders in the Shangri-La, Cinnamon, and Kingsbury hotels in downtown Colombo, and detonated a number of devices, then detonated their explosive vests on the Crusaders,” the message said.Separately, IS’ Amaq news agency issued a video showing the attackers pledging allegiance to IS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi.U.S. officials have yet to confirm IS’ claim, but said Monday it appeared likely that the terror group had at least inspired the Sri Lankan attackers.If confirmed, the attacks would be the first major terror attacks perpetrated by IS since the collapse of its self-declared caliphate almost a month ago in the northeastern Syrian town of Baghuz.At the time, U.S. and coalition officials were quick to point out that while IS had lost control over all the territory it had once held, the group remained potent, both as an active insurgency in Iraq and Syria, and as a terror network with affiliates across the globe.“

The United States government has announced a $1 million reward for information leading to the capture of a Romanian fugitive wanted on charges of human trafficking and money laundering. The fugitive, identified as Ionut Moldovan, is believed to have fled to Romania after being indicted in the US for his alleged involvement in a criminal network that exploited vulnerable individuals for profit.According to authorities, Moldovan is considered to be a flight risk and is believed to have connections to organized crime groups in Eastern Europe.

A German newspaper says local police have arrested an Iranian diplomat on a highway in southeastern Germany on suspicion of involvement in a plot to bomb an Iranian opposition event in France.In a report published Monday, the Bild newspaper named the arrested diplomat as Assadollah A., a 47-year-old working at the Iranian embassy in the Austrian capital, Vienna.In a phone call with VOA Persian, a Berlin-based journalist who worked on the story said German security officials told Bild that police stopped the diplomat as he was driving a rental car on a highway in the state of Bavaria on Sunday night local time.The journalist said there were several other people in the car, and their identities were not confirmed. He said German police suspected that the diplomat had explosives in the car, but none were found.Belgian authorities said Monday the Iranian diplomat, whose name they confirmed, was detained in Germany as a contact of an Iranian-origin married couple arrested earlier by Belgian police in Brussels. They said police officers caught the couple on Saturday in possession of 500 grams of homemade explosive TATP and a detonation device in the couple’s car.Belgian prosecutors named the husband and wife as 38-year-old Amir S. and 33-year-old Nasimeh N. and said they were suspected of plotting to bomb a Saturday meeting of the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI) in the French town of Villepinte, near Paris. The prosecutors charged the couple with attempted terrorist murder and preparing a terrorist act.“We are not sure what the Iranian diplomat was doing in Bavaria,” the Bild journalist said.

A political analyst has predicted that the recent election in the Democratic Republic of Congo, which has been marred by allegations of fraud and irregularities, will only serve to solidify the current president's grip on power.President Joseph Kabila, who has been in office since 2001, did not run for re-election in the latest poll. However, his preferred candidate, Emmanuel Ramazani Shadary, is widely expected to emerge as the winner despite widespread reports of voter suppression and manipulation.According to the analyst, the flawed election process will likely lead to increased dissatisfaction among the Congolese people, many of whom were already skeptical of the country's democratic institutions. This, in turn, will make it easier for President Kabila and his allies to maintain control and influence over the government.International observers have criticized the election for its lack of transparency and fairness, with some calling for an independent investigation into the allegations of fraud. However, it remains to be seen whether any concrete action will be taken to address these concerns.As the country awaits the final results of the election, many are bracing themselves for further unrest and political uncertainty in the days and weeks to come. Critics fear that the outcome will only serve to deepen the political crisis in the DRC and further entrench President Kabila's rule.

BANGKOK - Seventeen Maldivian journalists arrested during a protest the previous day were summoned to give statements on Monday evening.“We don’t know if we will be formally charged yet,” said Zaheena Rasheed, editor of the Maldives Independent.The journalists, however, are bracing for the worst, considering how democratic gains in the Maldives have eroded in recent years, including a crackdown on the press.Police in Male on Sunday unleashed pepper spray on dozens of demonstrators, which included journalists and activists, protesting the government’s move to criminalize defamation and other actions recently taken against the media.A statement issued by police says officers moved to break up the demonstration with “minimum force” after it moved into a protected zone near the presidential office and some people scaled barricades.Rasheed denies the police version of events, saying the protestors had already been pushed back by authorities from the protected zone before they were arrested."By this time many journalists had been targeted and pepper-sprayed at close range. I got up to leave because I saw many members of my team getting arrested. And then the police official said I would not be able to go back, I'd have to get in the police van,” she told VOA.Maldivian media outlets and their journalists have been facing a variety of threats.“Media offices have been attacked and there's been numerous death threats against journalists, both over text messages and also in person, as well as assaults,” said Rasheed. “There's been no justice for any attack on journalists and media offices."FILE - Maldivian policemen patrol the area where demonstrators gathered for a mass rally in Male', Maldives on Nov. 27, 2015.

Former President Barack Obama made a statement recently highlighting the importance of women in society. During an event in Pennsylvania, Obama expressed his belief that women are "not an interest group" but rather essential contributors to the fabric of our society.He emphasized the need for women to be recognized and valued for their unique perspectives and contributions to various aspects of society. Obama's remarks come at a time when discussions around gender equality and women's rights continue to be prominent in the public discourse.The former president's words serve as a reminder of the importance of gender equality and the need for society to continue working towards creating a more inclusive and equitable world for all individuals, regardless of gender. Obama's message resonates with many who believe in the power and importance of women in shaping our world.

A group of scientists is set to march on Washington to protest the use of 'alternative facts' in public discourse. The march, organized by the Union of Concerned Scientists, aims to draw attention to the importance of evidence-based research and decision-making.The term 'alternative facts' gained popularity in recent years, particularly during the Trump administration, when officials would present inaccurate or misleading information as if it were true. Scientists argue that this phenomenon undermines the credibility of research and hinders efforts to address pressing issues such as climate change, public health, and national security.The march is scheduled to take place on May 22nd, with participants marching from the National Mall to the Capitol building. Organizers hope to raise awareness about the importance of respecting scientific integrity and promoting accurate information in public policy.Many prominent scientists and researchers are expected to participate in the march, with speakers addressing the crowd about the dangers of promoting 'alternative facts' over scientifically verified data. The event is also seen as a way to show solidarity among the scientific community and to demonstrate the importance of evidence-based decision-making in shaping policies that affect us all.Overall, the march on Washington is intended to send a clear message to policymakers and the public alike: facts matter, and it is crucial to base decisions on sound scientific evidence rather than on misleading or false information.

Seoul, South Korea - On June 25th, South Korea marked the 61st anniversary of the start of the Korean War, a conflict that lasted from 1950 to 1953. The war began when North Korean forces invaded the South, leading to a bloody and devastating conflict that left millions dead and families divided.During a ceremony in Seoul, President Moon Jae-in paid tribute to the sacrifices made by those who fought in the war, as well as the civilians who endured immense hardship. He emphasized the importance of remembering the war and its legacy, in order to work towards peace and reconciliation on the Korean Peninsula.The Korean War ended in an armistice rather than a formal peace treaty, leaving the two Koreas technically still at war. Efforts to denuclearize and establish lasting peace on the peninsula have been ongoing, with both North and South Korea engaging in diplomatic talks and summits in recent years.As South Korea commemorates the anniversary of the start of the Korean War, the country continues to strive for peace and stability in the region, while also remembering the sacrifices and suffering endured during one of the most devastating conflicts in modern history.

Chilean President Michelle Bachelet's social reform plans are facing a major test as the country's economy continues to weaken. The South American nation has been grappling with sluggish growth and high levels of inequality, prompting Bachelet to push for significant reforms during her second term in office.One of the key pillars of Bachelet's social agenda is education reform, which aims to provide free higher education to all Chilean students. However, the economic downturn has put pressure on the government's budget, making it increasingly difficult to fund such ambitious programs.In addition to education reform, Bachelet has also introduced measures to increase the minimum wage and improve healthcare services for low-income families. These initiatives have been met with both support and criticism, with some arguing that they are not sustainable in the current economic climate.As the economy continues to struggle, Bachelet's ability to implement her social reform plans will be put to the test. With growing public discontent and upcoming elections in 2017, the president faces an uphill battle in delivering on her promises to improve the lives of Chile's most vulnerable populations. Only time will tell if Bachelet can navigate the challenging economic environment and successfully implement her ambitious social agenda.

Police in the United Arab Emirates have made an arrest in connection with the killing of an American woman. The victim, identified as 35-year-old Sarah Johnson, was found dead in her apartment in Dubai last week. Authorities have arrested a 28-year-old man who was seen leaving the victim's building on the night of the murder. The suspect, whose identity has not been released, is currently in custody and is being questioned by police. The motive behind the killing is still under investigation. Friends and family of Johnson have expressed shock and sadness over her tragic death. The American embassy in the UAE is providing assistance to Johnson's loved ones during this difficult time. More updates on this case are expected as the investigation continues.

Dozens of short-finned pilot whales have been found stranded on a beach in Indonesia's Java Island. Local authorities and volunteers have been working tirelessly to save the stranded whales, but sadly, 10 of them have already died.The whales were first spotted by local fishermen early in the morning, and by the time rescuers arrived, the animals were already struggling to survive. Efforts are being made to refloat the remaining whales and guide them back into deeper waters.It is still unclear why the whales became stranded, but experts believe that they may have been disoriented by strong underwater currents or been following a sick or injured whale. Short-finned pilot whales are known to be highly social animals and often travel in large pods.Authorities are urging the public to stay away from the area to allow rescuers to do their work. The incident is a stark reminder of the challenges faced by marine wildlife in the face of human activities and environmental changes.

New antibacterial films made from fruits and vegetables may protect produce in the future. Eric Libby reports.Take a bite into a crispy apple and taste the? cinnamon? That might be the future if Tara McHugh of the U.S. Agricultural Research Service has her way.She and her colleagues use pureed fruits and vegetables mixed with oils from herbs and spices to create antibacterial films. These sheets ? less than a millimeter thick ? are wrapped around produce and other foods, to keep them from spoiling and to avoid contamination.McHugh says the films "are made from at least 85% fruit or vegetable." They have the same color as the fruit or vegetable from which they are made. "So the carrot is bright orange, the tomato is red [and] broccoli is green." McHugh also adds essential oils from herbs and spices that flavor the films. The main reason the oils are added, though, is because of their antibacterial properties. Oregano and cinnamon are especially potent against bacteria. McHugh says that although she is aware that bacteria may develop resistance she does not expect any problems since these herbs and spices have been around for centuries. Besides protection, McHugh says there are other benefits to the fruity films.

LOS ANGELES - Football players from the Darfur region of Sudan plan to compete in the VIVA Cup, an international competition that will begin in Erbil, in the Kurdish region of Iraq, on June 4. Supporters of the all-refugee team, Darfur United, have been working from California to make it happen. "Darfur United draws its players from 12 refugee camps in Chad -- where it was created and is still based." The players have had only weeks to get into shape for the "VIVA Cup," a competition for nations and groups not affiliated with the International Football Federation, FIFA. But their British-born coach Mark Hodson, who lives in California, says he has a high opinion of their talent. “Technically, pretty strong. They can do a lot of things with the ball. Physically, I was really impressed. I was surprised how big and strong they were and really athletic,” Hodson said. As the team gets into shape in Chad, supporters in suburban Los Angeles are raising funds to pay for the trip to the Kurdish region of Iraq. The LA Galaxy professional soccer team and its star British player David Beckham donated jerseys for a charity auction, and the players in Chad signed a commemorative ball. The refugees fled violence in their country after civil war broke out in 2003. Gabriel Stauring, founder the charity i-ACT, for “interactive activism,” spearheaded the drive to create the soccer team. “Every single one of our players has the story of having their village attacked, of seeing family and friends being killed, mothers, sisters being raped in front of them, and then having to walk across the desert to survive and make it to one of these camps,” Stauring said. Stauring says soccer has been an important diversion for the refugees. “They just make balls out of anything.

GENEVA - The U.N. children's fund reports the vast majority of children living in Ebola-affected areas of eastern Democratic Republic of Congo have returned to school where they are taught ways to avoid infection.School began one month ago in Democratic Republic of Congo. The U.N. children's fund says efforts to get children to return to school in Ebola-affected areas in conflict-ridden eastern DRC have been hugely successful.It says 80 percent are attending schools in Beni and Mabalako health zones. They are the epicenters of the current Ebola outbreak, which was declared August 1 in North Kivu and Ituri Provinces. The latest World Health Organization report finds 207 cases of Ebola, including 130 deaths.UNICEF spokesman, Christophe Boulierac, said the return of so many children to the classroom is encouraging. He said school provides the children who are living in an area of epidemic and conflict with a sense of normalcy. He said school offers them a protective environment.Boulierac said children in school learn how to prevent getting Ebola and when they go home, they promote regular hand washing with their families. He says this helps avoid further spread of the disease in the community.UNICEF has identified more than 1,500 schools in the areas affected by the Ebola epidemic. Among them are 365 schools located in the high-risk epicenters of the outbreak.

Zimbabwean civic leaders are urging South African President Thabo Mbeki to include civil society representatives in the country's halting process of conflict resolution.Non-governmental organizations are proposing to hold what they describe as a national stakeholders conference to air views on the way out of the deep crisis that has developed since the country's March 29 round of presidential and general elections.Those elections gave the opposition Movement for Democratic Change a majority in the lower house of parliament, unseating President Robert Mugabe's ZANU-PF party after three decades in power. Mr. Mugabe trailed opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai in the presidential poll.

The Tokyo Olympic Games, originally scheduled for the summer of 2020 but postponed to 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, are facing yet another setback. Organizers have announced that the training for volunteers, which was set to begin in February, will be delayed as a result of the ongoing virus situation in Japan.The decision comes as the country struggles to contain a recent surge in COVID-19 cases, with Tokyo being one of the most affected regions. The safety and well-being of volunteers, staff, and athletes are top priorities for the organizers, prompting the delay in training.It is unclear at this time when the training will resume, as the situation with the virus remains fluid. However, officials are hopeful that they will be able to proceed with the Games as scheduled in July. The Olympic Games have already been postponed once, and any further delays or cancellations would have significant implications for athletes and fans around the world.The International Olympic Committee and the Japanese government are closely monitoring the situation and working to ensure that the Games can proceed safely. As the world continues to grapple with the challenges posed by the pandemic, the fate of the Tokyo Olympics remains uncertain.

In Somalia, a country plagued by decades of conflict and instability, street children are left to fend for themselves in the harsh urban environment. These vulnerable young individuals, many of whom have been orphaned or abandoned, face immense challenges as they struggle to survive on the streets.Without a stable family or support system, these children are often forced to resort to begging, scavenging for food, and engaging in risky activities to make ends meet. They are deprived of their basic rights to education, healthcare, and protection, leaving them extremely vulnerable to exploitation and abuse.Despite efforts by local organizations and government agencies to address the issue of street children in Somalia, the problem persists due to the lack of resources and systemic issues such as poverty and insecurity. More needs to be done to provide these children with the necessary support and care they need to thrive and succeed in life.As the international community continues to focus on humanitarian crises in other parts of the world, the plight of Somalia's street children often goes unnoticed. It is crucial that attention is drawn to their struggles and that concrete actions are taken to improve their living conditions and ensure their rights are protected. Until then, these vulnerable children will continue to fend for themselves in a world that has failed to provide them with the care and support they deserve.

A Florida man accused of killing an unarmed teenager made a court appearance today in front of a judge. The defendant, identified as John Smith, is facing charges of first-degree murder in connection with the shooting death of 17-year-old Mark Johnson.According to authorities, the incident took place last week in a quiet residential neighborhood. Witnesses reported hearing gunshots and seeing Smith flee the scene before police arrived. Johnson was found lying on the ground with a gunshot wound to his chest and was pronounced dead at the scene.Smith was arrested a few hours after the shooting and has been held in custody since then. During his court appearance, he remained silent and did not enter a plea. The judge set his bail at $1 million and scheduled a preliminary hearing for next week.The tragic incident has shocked the community, with many residents expressing their outrage and calling for justice for the slain teenager. Johnson's family and friends were present in the courtroom, visibly distraught by the loss of their loved one.The case is still under investigation, and authorities are urging anyone with information to come forward. The next court date for Smith has been set for next Tuesday.

Two Ethiopians running for parliament on the ticket of the ruling Ethiopian People’s Revolutionary Democratic Front in weredas 1 and 2 opened their campaigns on Saturday.Dr. Abraham Tekeste and Feleke Yimer told gatherings in Addis they listed schools, housing and other infrastructure as projects they want to pursue if elected."EPRDF continues to thrive to sustain the fast-growing economy that it achieved for the last several consecutive years," said Abraham. He added that his party, which has been in power since 1991, is committed and is capable of ensuring active participation in the election by all Ethiopians.In other Ethiopian election news, Girma Negassa, a candidate from the Oromo People's Congress, alleges that he was threatened and attacked by government militias in his western Shoa community and by members of a local administration."I was beaten by these named individuals and repeatedly warned against running as OPC candidate," Girma said.Girma also listed the names of other opposition candidates who he says remain in jail and unable to pursue their campaigns.

In a brutal display of punishment, Somalia's al-Shabab militant group has amputated the hands of two men who were convicted by a Sharia court in the town of Bardere. The men were accused of theft, and their hands were cut off in a public square as a crowd looked on.Al-Shabab, which is affiliated with al-Qaeda, has been enforcing its strict interpretation of Islamic law in areas under its control in Somalia. This includes punishments such as amputations, stonings, and executions for various crimes.Human rights organizations have condemned the actions of al-Shabab, calling them barbaric and inhumane.

In 2018, several celebrities have been using their star power for good causes, making a positive impact on society. From Meghan Markle to Michelle Obama, here are 10 celebrities who have been making a difference this year.1. Meghan Markle - The Duchess of Sussex has been a vocal advocate for women's rights and has used her platform to speak out against gender inequality and promote female empowerment.2. Michelle Obama - The former First Lady has continued her work on promoting education and healthy living through her foundation, Reach Higher and Let's Move.3. Leonardo DiCaprio - The actor and environmental activist has been raising awareness about climate change and working to protect our planet through his foundation, Earth Alliance.4. Angelina Jolie - The actress and humanitarian has been actively involved in advocating for refugees and displaced persons through her work with the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees.5. Beyoncé - The singer has used her influence to promote body positivity, feminism, and racial equality through her music and public appearances.6. Emma Watson - The actress and UN Women Goodwill Ambassador has been a leading voice in the #MeToo movement and has been fighting for gender equality in the film industry and beyond.7. Justin Trudeau - The Canadian Prime Minister has been a strong advocate for LGBTQ rights, gender equality, and environmental protection.8. Ellen DeGeneres - The talk show host has been actively involved in several charitable causes, including LGBTQ rights, animal welfare, and disaster relief efforts.9. Emma Stone - The actress has been using her platform to raise awareness about mental health issues and support organizations that provide resources and support to those in need.10. Chance the Rapper - The musician has been a philanthropist, donating millions of dollars to support public schools in Chicago and advocating for criminal justice reform.These celebrities are just a few examples of how influential figures can use their fame for good causes and make a positive impact on society. Their dedication to making a difference is inspiring and serves as a reminder that we all have the power to create change in the world.

WASHINGTON - A growing number of immigrants in the United States are being incarcerated in detention centers for increasingly extended periods of time, and without being afforded the legal protections given to criminals, according to a recent report issued by the American Immigration Council.The report, entitled “The Landscape of Immigration Detention in the United States,” says the number of people imprisoned for violating U.S. immigration laws has increased “more than fivefold in the past two decades,” from an average daily detention population of less than 7,000 in 1994 to more than 44,000 in 2017.The rise in immigration detention temporarily dipped in 2014, even though the administration of President Barack Obama increased efforts to deport immigrant criminal offenders and recent border crossers.
