South Korea, as a neighboring country and ally of the US, has a vested interest in finding a solution to the North Korean nuclear issue.The talks between the US and South Korea are crucial in coordinating a united front against North Korea's nuclear ambitions. Both countries will likely discuss diplomatic strategies, sanctions, and other measures to pressure North Korea to abandon its nuclear program.The international community is closely watching these talks, as the North Korean nuclear threat has the potential to destabilize the region and pose a global security risk. It remains to be seen whether the US and South Korea can find a way to address this pressing issue and prevent a further escalation of tensions.

MOMBASA, KENYA - A washed-out road in Kenya has blocked the flow of goods from Kenya's Indian Ocean port of Mombasa for a second straight day, officials said on Wednesday, choking the main trade route into East Africa.Mombasa handles imports such as fuel and other vital goods for Uganda, Burundi, Rwanda, South Sudan and eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo.Queues began forming at about 10 a.m. local time (0700GMT) on Wednesday when heavy rains swept away a temporary road about 50km from Mombasa. The road was being used as a diversion while the main highway leading to the capital, Nairobi, was repaired.Police and truckers stuck on the road say the queues are now almost 50km long, with many trucks, busses and cars stranded far from towns and villages where they can purchase water and food.“I have not eaten since yesterday morning, not showered and not even changed clothes,” Nathaniel Chweya, a truck driver hauling 10 cars to the Ugandan capital of Kampala, told Reuters.Police urged patience as some motorists threatened to stage protests. “We are working on it. This is not an easy task and we are all affected. Give us time,” Martin Kariuki, Coast regional traffic police commandant, said.Willingtone Kiberenge, acting chief executive of the truck owners' umbrella body, Kenya Transporters Association, said the delays were piling on losses for trucking companies.“We have more than 1,500 trucks stuck in the traffic since yesterday. Nothing much has changed and the problem is escalating.

WASHINGTON - New Jersey Sen. Cory Booker voiced support Monday for former U.S. Vice President Joe Biden's bid for the Democratic presidential nomination, his latest former challenger to endorse him just ahead of six state primary elections he faces Tuesday against Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders.Booker, who dropped out of the party's presidential contest in January before voting began a couple weeks later, said in a Twitter comment, “The answer to hatred & division is to reignite our spirit of common purpose. @JoeBiden won’t only win - he’ll show there’s more that unites us than divides us. He’ll restore honor to the Oval Office and tackle our most pressing challenges. That’s why I’m proud to endorse Joe.”Booker's endorsement came a day after California Sen. Kamala Harris, who dropped out of the contest in December, endorsed Biden, saying that she felt that he was best prepared to "steer America through these turbulent times."Harris, often mentioned by U.S. political analysts as one of several possible vice-presidential running mates with Biden, said the U.S. needs a president "who reflects the decency and dignity of the American people, a president who speaks the truth; and a president who fights for those whose voices are too often overlooked or ignored."Former Foe, Sen. Kamala Harris, Endorses Biden Presidential BidVanquished Democratic opponents are supporting the former vice president's campaign against TrumpSeveral former rivals have now endorsed Biden, a list that also includes former South Bend, Indiana Mayor Pete Buttigieg and Minnesota Senator Amy Klobuchar.

The United States has recently increased pressure on China regarding two key issues in the Asia-Pacific region: North Korea and the South China Sea.In relation to North Korea, the US is seeking Chinese cooperation in implementing sanctions and diplomatic efforts to denuclearize the Korean Peninsula. This comes as tensions between the US and North Korea have escalated in recent months, with the North Korean regime conducting multiple missile tests.Furthermore, the US is also pushing China to adhere to international law and respect the sovereignty of neighboring countries in the South China Sea. China's construction of artificial islands and military buildup in the region have raised concerns among its neighbors and the US about freedom of navigation and regional stability.The US has been engaging in high-level discussions with Chinese officials to address these issues, emphasizing the importance of cooperation in order to achieve peace and security in the region. The outcome of these talks will be crucial in determining the future dynamics of US-China relations and the stability of the Asia-Pacific region.

Guatemala is home to a large and diverse indigenous population, with more than half of Guatemalans of Mayan descent. The Mayan Indians in Guatemala, who largely live in the western highlands, suffered horrific atrocities during the country's 36-year civil war that ended in 1996. Despite some progress since the war, the Mayan people continue to face discrimination and live in grinding poverty.Isabel Rosario Lopez rises at dawn to prepare breakfast for a bustling household of six children. This day, she has a guest helping her prepare a meal. She spends her day in her mountain village tending to her home and weaving beautifully intricate textiles, a tradition passed down by Mayan women for centuries. Her husband toils in the surrounding cornfields or weaves baskets and sells firewood for income.Children play and laugh in this village, which is located high up in the clouds and offers spectacular views of the surrounding lush countryside far below. But life is not easy. Sunny days give way to cold nights where families struggle to stay warm.Girls fetch water and do laundry at the bottom of a steep hill. The village is wired for electricity, although the cost of the utility is far out of reach for these villagers.

PANAMA CITY - A Panamanian court has granted bail to the two founders of Mossack Fonseca, the law firm at the center of the Panama Papers scandal, in a case allegedly tied to a sprawling corruption scandal in Brazil.Following their February arrest on charges of money laundering, Jurgen Mossack and Ramon Fonseca each paid $500,000 Friday and will be set free soon, the attorney for the two lawyers, Guillermina McDonald, told Reuters.McDonald said the court ruled the two were not a flight risk because they had been cooperating with the investigation.Another lawyer from the firm, Edison Teano, was still being held, she said.FILE - Partner of the Panama-based law firm Mossack Fonseca, Ramon Fonseca speaks during an interview at his office in Panama City,, April 7, 2016.Mossack Fonseca emerged from obscurity in April 2016 with the Panama Papers, millions of documents stolen from the firm and leaked to the media that illustrated how the wealthy use offshore corporations to avoid taxes.

The U.S. Senate on Tuesday plans to release a long-awaited report detailing the CIA's interrogation methods since the September 11, 2001 attacks.It will be the first public documentation of the CIA's alleged use of torture on al-Qaida suspects during the so-called War on Terror. And administration officials fear the report could incite violence against U.S. interests around the world.White House spokesman Josh Earnest said Monday that the U.S. government has been working for months to prepare for the possible impact of the release of a report on the CIA's interrogation methods in the war on terror.It would be the first public documentation of the CIA's alleged use of torture on al-Qaida suspects since the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks on the United States.Earnest said President Barack Obama felt it was important for the American people to have as clear a look as possible into exactly what happened.The spokesman said that despite possible risks, the White House believes it is critically important that the declassified summary of the report is released.The 6,000-page report is said to detail such extreme interrogation techniques as sleep deprivation and waterboarding, which simulates drowning.

HARARE - The United Nations says it needs $21 million to ease food insecurity in Zimbabwe as the country deals with measures imposed to stop the spread of the coronavirus. The government says it is going to release funds to help people most affected by the COVID-19 lockdown, such as the old and the unemployed.In a recent analysis, United Nations experts said 45 percent of Zimbabwe’s rural population is either in “crisis” or “emergency” mode in terms of food security.The analysis said the situation is likely to worsen with the coronavirus pandemic.The World Food Program is stepping in to try to ease the impact.Via Whatsapp, Claire Nevill, the WFP spokeswoman in Zimbabwe, said the U.N. agency has increased the number of food distribution points to limit overcrowding and distributed protective clothing, among other hygienic measures, to curb the spread of the disease.But Nevill said the WFP needs more funding.“In order to provide food assistance to almost four million vulnerable Zimbabweans, in the context of these new health and safety measures, WPF requires an additional $21 million for the next six months. This funding shortfall is on top of an existing gap of $130 million faced by WFP Zimbabwe for the next six months. Our assessments show that the harvest will be much later this year.

The United States said Tuesday it hopes to unveil a Middle East peace plan later this year, but Vice President Mike Pence says that depends on the Palestinians' willingness to return to negotiations with Israel.As Pence ended a three-day Middle East trip, he told the Reuters news agency, "The White House has been working with our partners in the region to see if we can develop a framework for peace. It all just depends now on when the Palestinians are going to come back to the table."A senior White House official told reporters in Jerusalem that U.S. negotiators are reluctant to insist on a timeline because past deadlines for negotiations have often been missed.WATCH: Pence ends Middle East trip?"It'll come out both when it's ready and when both sides are actually willing to engage on it," the White House official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.Pence said in the Reuters interview that he and President Donald Trump believe the decision to move the U.S. embassy in Israel from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem would boost peace-making possibilities. Palestinian leader Mahmoud Abbas denounced Trump's December announcement about moving the embassy and refused to meet with Pence on the vice president's trip. Pence said in a speech Monday before the Knesset, Israel's lawmaking body, that the United States would move the embassy by the end of 2019.FILE - U.S. Vice President Mike Pence listens as U.S. President Donald Trump announces that the United States recognizes Jerusalem as the capital of Israel and will move its embassy there, during an address at the White House, Dec. 6, 2017.

More than 200 organization representatives and individuals have visited the U.S. Embassy in Hanoi this week to pay respects to the late U.S. Senator John McCain, according to reports from the official Vietnam News Agency (VNA) and Tin Tuc News.Among them were top Vietnamese leaders, representatives from the Communist Party Central Committee’s Commission for External Relations, several ministries, agencies, organizations and the Vietnam-U.S. Society, the local media reported.McCain, who was tortured and held captive for five and a half years as a prisoner of war in Hanoi during the Vietnam War, died of brain cancer on Saturday at age 81 in Arizona. Memorial events began there on Wednesday, when McCain’s body was taken to the Capitol and will continue Saturday in Washington at the National Cathedral before his burial Sunday at the United States Naval Academy Cemetery, in Annapolis, Maryland.Jay Smith plays the bagpipe during ? memorial service for U.S. Senator John McCain at North Phoenix Baptist Church in Phoenix, Arizona, Aug. 30, 2018.“Senator John McCain has always been a symbol of the lawmaker– veteran generation of the Vietnam War. “He took a leading role in … healing the wounds of war, and normalizing and promoting a comprehensive Vietnam-U.S. partnership,” Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Pham Binh Minh wrote on the condolence book at the U.S.

In southern Lebanon, a force of 12,000 United Nations peacekeepers are working diligently to maintain peace and stability in the region. The peacekeepers, from various countries around the world, are part of the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL).Since its establishment in 1978, UNIFIL has played a crucial role in monitoring the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, as well as ensuring the security and humanitarian assistance in the area. The peacekeepers patrol the region, conduct patrols, and provide support to the local communities.The presence of UN peacekeepers in southern Lebanon has helped prevent escalations of conflict and has provided a sense of security for the residents in the region. Despite the challenges they face, the peacekeepers remain committed to their mission of maintaining peace and stability in the area.UNIFIL's efforts have not gone unnoticed, as they continue to receive support and recognition from the international community for their dedication and service. As conflicts persist in other parts of the world, the work of these peacekeepers in southern Lebanon serves as a reminder of the importance of international cooperation and peacekeeping efforts in resolving conflicts and maintaining peace.

The tragic shooting at a gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida that left 49 people dead and dozens more injured has quickly become a focal point in the US Presidential campaign. Both Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton and Republican nominee Donald Trump have responded to the tragedy, with Clinton emphasizing the need for stricter gun control measures and Trump emphasizing the need for stronger national security measures.The shooting, which took place in the early hours of Sunday morning, has reignited the debate over gun control and terrorism in the United States. President Obama has called the shooting an act of terror and hate, and has urged Americans to come together in the face of this tragedy.Clinton has reiterated her support for stricter gun control laws, calling for universal background checks and a ban on assault weapons. She has also emphasized the need to combat terrorism both at home and abroad.Trump, on the other hand, has focused on the issue of national security, calling for a temporary ban on Muslim immigrants and criticizing Obama for not using the term "radical Islamic terrorism" to describe the attack in Orlando.The Orlando tragedy has brought the issue of national security and gun control to the forefront of the Presidential campaign, with both candidates offering different solutions to prevent future tragedies. As the country mourns the loss of innocent lives in Orlando, the debate over how to prevent such senseless acts of violence continues.

Thousands of pilgrims from around the world have gathered in Jerusalem to mark Good Friday, one of the holiest days on the Christian calendar. The city's streets are filled with a mix of tourists and worshippers, all coming together to retrace the steps of Jesus Christ on his final journey to the cross.The atmosphere is one of reverence and reflection as the faithful walk the Via Dolorosa, the path believed to be taken by Jesus on the way to his crucifixion. Many carry crosses, sing hymns, and offer prayers as they walk through the Old City towards the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, believed to be the site of Jesus' crucifixion, burial, and resurrection.For Christians, Good Friday is a solemn day of remembrance and mourning, but also one of hope and redemption. It is a time to reflect on the sacrifice of Jesus and the promise of salvation for all who believe.In addition to the traditional religious observances, many pilgrims also take the opportunity to explore the rich history and culture of Jerusalem, visiting sites such as the Western Wall, the Dome of the Rock, and the Mount of Olives.Despite the challenges posed by the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, the spirit of unity and faith remains strong among the pilgrims in Jerusalem on this Good Friday. As they come together to commemorate the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus, they also find comfort and solace in the shared experience of their faith.

Mozambicans are in the second day of reflection Tuesday, a day ahead of the October 28 general election.Twenty-nine political parties are participating in the parliamentary elections.In the presidential vote, main opposition RENAMO candidate Afonso Dlhakama and Daviz Simago of the new Mozambique Democratic Movement are challenging incumbent President Armando Guebuza of the ruling FRELIMO party.Political observers say there was a heavy use of the internet in the campaigns with the leading political parties setting up online sites to propagate their message.VOA's Scott Bobb, who is in the Mozambican capital, Maputo to cover the election said that Mozambicans are ready to vote despite reported isolated cases of intimidation during the campaigns ahead of Wednesday's vote."I spoke to all three of the major parties on Saturday and Sunday and that is exactly what they said that there were some isolated incidents, intimidation, harassment?there was a case where some policemen were alleged to have done that; they were actually tried and convicted and fined and sentenced," Bobb said.He said Mozambicans are determined to uphold the rules governing the election."There seems to be a desire to observe the law. Yes there were complaints in particular over the fact that some of the opposition party candidates were prevented from becoming candidates because their papers were not in order, which the opposition alleges were due to technicalities," he said.Bobb said the electoral body seems determined to hold a credible vote."One senses that the electoral commission which is composed of members of the parties themselves is trying to hold a just election," Bobb said.He said there is excitement ahead of Wednesday's vote."One senses that there is a group of Mozambicans that are interested and are excited.

In a recent turn of events, former actress and socialite Lindsay Lohan has reportedly checked herself back into rehab. This is not the first time Lohan has sought treatment for substance abuse issues, as she has been in and out of rehab facilities over the past decade.Sources close to Lohan have expressed concern for her well-being, citing her recent erratic behavior and public appearances. It is unclear at this time which rehab facility Lohan has entered, or how long she plans to stay.Lohan's struggles with addiction have been well-documented in the media, with the actress herself admitting to her battles with drugs and alcohol in the past.

MORONI - The Indian Ocean state of Comoros will hold a constitutional referendum in July on presidential term limits that could result in the next vote behind held two years ahead of schedule, its president has said.The move could allow the President Azali Assoumani, who won the last election in 2016, to run the country for two fresh five year-terms instead of the one five-year term allowed in the constitution. The next vote is scheduled for 2021.The referendum if passed would do away with a constitution meant to rotate power every five years between the archipelago's islands.That system was intended to promote stability and power-sharing in a coup-prone country. Comoros has had more than 20 coups or coup attempts since it declared independence from France in 1975."In a case where the referendum will win the support of the population, the presidential election will be held in 2019, not in 2021," Assoumani said on Sunday during a visit to the island nation of Anjouan.Under the constitution, the presidency of the Union of Comoros rotates between the three islands of Grande Comore, Anjouan and Moheli every five years.Assoumani hails from Grande Comore.Although details of the referendum are yet to be released, a recently completed national dialogue recommended that a president could be re-elected. This would open the door for a leader to run the country for two five-year terms.Assoumani, a former military officer, first seized power in a coup in 1999 and ruled until 2006 after winning the country's first multi-party poll in 2002.

Lawmakers in the United States are pushing for a new initiative to promote tourism in Native American communities beyond popular destinations like Yellowstone National Park. The goal is to showcase the rich culture and history of Native American tribes across the country.Currently, many tourists only visit a handful of well-known Native American attractions, missing out on the opportunity to learn about the diverse traditions and customs of other tribes. By expanding tourism to include more Native American communities, lawmakers hope to not only boost economic development in these areas but also to educate the public about the unique heritage of indigenous peoples.Advocates for the initiative argue that by promoting tourism in Native American communities, visitors will have the chance to experience authentic cultural exchanges and support local businesses. Additionally, by highlighting the contributions of Native Americans to American history, lawmakers hope to foster a greater appreciation for the indigenous peoples of the United States.Efforts to promote tourism in Native American communities have already begun in some states, with local officials partnering with tribal governments to develop new tourist attractions and cultural experiences. By working together, lawmakers and tribal leaders aim to create a more inclusive and diverse tourism industry that celebrates the rich heritage of Native American peoples.

The ongoing COVID-19 pandemic has significantly impacted Holy Week celebrations around the world this year. With many countries implementing strict lockdown measures and social distancing guidelines, traditional gatherings and ceremonies have been either canceled or moved online.Despite the challenges posed by the virus, the spirit of Holy Week remains strong as people find new ways to connect and celebrate. Churches have adapted by live-streaming services, organizing virtual prayer groups, and offering drive-in ceremonies to ensure that the faithful can still come together in some capacity.In countries such as Italy and Spain, where Holy Week processions are a significant cultural tradition, communities have found innovative ways to keep the spirit alive while adhering to safety measures. In some areas, residents are displaying religious icons in windows or on balconies for others to see, creating a sense of unity despite physical separation.As the world continues to navigate the challenges of the pandemic, the message of hope and resilience that is central to Holy Week is more important than ever. While the way we celebrate may look different this year, the underlying spirit of faith and community remains unchanged.

The recent mine disaster in Brazil has left a trail of destruction and raised serious health risks for those living in the affected areas. The collapse of a dam at a mining facility in the state of Minas Gerais has caused a massive amount of mud to spill into nearby communities, contaminating water sources and leaving many residents exposed to toxic substances.Rescue efforts are currently underway, with 25 people still missing and feared to be trapped under the mud. The search and rescue teams are facing challenges due to the hazardous conditions and the vast amount of debris covering the area.Local authorities are urging residents to avoid contact with the contaminated water and to seek medical attention if they experience any symptoms of illness. The environmental impact of the disaster is also a major concern, with experts warning of long-term damage to the ecosystem and public health.The mining company responsible for the disaster has come under scrutiny for its safety practices and environmental management. Investigations are ongoing to determine the causes of the dam collapse and to hold those responsible accountable for the tragic event.As the search for the missing individuals continues, the focus remains on ensuring the safety and well-being of the affected communities and preventing further harm from the toxic mud.

In a country known for its strong ruling party, challengers in Ethiopia are facing an uphill battle as they seek to unseat the current government. The ruling party, the Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF), has been in power for over 27 years and has maintained a firm grip on the country's political landscape.Despite this, a number of opposition parties have emerged in recent years, hoping to bring about change and challenge the dominance of the ruling party. However, these challengers face long odds as they navigate through a political system that is heavily skewed in favor of the ruling party.One of the main obstacles facing opposition parties is the lack of resources and access to the media. The ruling party has a strong presence in the country's state-owned media outlets, giving them a significant advantage in terms of reaching voters and shaping the narrative around the upcoming elections.Additionally, there have been concerns about the fairness and transparency of the electoral process in Ethiopia. Opposition parties have accused the ruling party of using state resources to their advantage and engaging in voter intimidation tactics to maintain their grip on power.Despite these challenges, opposition parties remain hopeful and determined to bring about change in Ethiopia. They are mobilizing supporters, reaching out to voters, and advocating for a more inclusive and democratic political system.As the country prepares for the upcoming elections, all eyes will be on the challengers as they face long odds in their bid to unseat Ethiopia's ruling party and bring about a new era of governance in the country.

Asian currencies have taken a hit as trade fears continue to grip the region. Authorities are working tirelessly to prevent a potential economic crisis.The ongoing trade tensions between the United States and China have caused uncertainty and volatility in the markets. This has resulted in a decline in the value of many Asian currencies, including the Chinese yuan, South Korean won, and Japanese yen.In response, central banks and governments in the region are implementing various measures to stabilize their currencies and economies. China, for example, has taken steps to support its yuan by selling more foreign exchange reserves and easing monetary policy.South Korea and Japan have also intervened in the foreign exchange market to prevent further depreciation of their currencies. The Bank of Korea has stated that it will closely monitor the situation and take necessary actions to ensure stability.Overall, Asian countries are facing a challenging economic environment as they navigate the trade tensions and try to protect their currencies from further decline. It remains to be seen how effective these efforts will be in averting a potential crisis.

Israel has formally begun the process of forming a new government, following last week's elections. A center-left coalition is emerging with a sweeping plan to relinquish large parts of the West Bank.Israeli President Moshe Katzav opened talks with political parties presenting their choices for the country's next prime minister.Mr. Katzav is expected to appoint acting Prime Minister Ehud Olmert to form the next government. Mr. Olmert's Kadima party won the most parliamentary seats, with 28. Since that is far short of a majority in the 120-member Knesset, or parliament, he will have to form a coalition with other parties.Mr. Olmert should be able to cobble together a coalition with dovish, left-wing parties that support his plan for unilateral withdrawals from large parts of the West Bank during the next four years.Mr. Olmert says that, with the recent election of the Islamic militant group Hamas, Israel does not have a Palestinian peace partner. So, Israel would draw its own borders unilaterally.Prime Minister Ariel Sharon began the process when he pulled Israel out of the Gaza Strip last August and dismantled all 21 settlements there. Mr. Sharon is in a coma, after suffering a stroke in January, and now Mr. Olmert is picking up where he left off."We are going in the area of conflict management," said Israeli spokesman Dore Gold. "This conflict, at present, with Hamas in power, cannot be resolved.

Activists have reported that Greek authorities have intercepted a ship that was attempting to head to Gaza, in a bid to break the Israeli blockade of the Palestinian territory. The ship, named "Freedom Flotilla", was carrying humanitarian aid and activists from various countries who were hoping to deliver much-needed supplies to the people of Gaza.The activists have criticized the Greek government for stopping the ship, arguing that the blockade imposed by Israel is illegal under international law and that the people of Gaza are in desperate need of assistance. They have called on the international community to put pressure on both Greece and Israel to allow the ship to continue its journey to Gaza.The Greek government has not yet commented on the incident. Israel maintains that the blockade is necessary for security reasons, as it seeks to prevent weapons and other materials that could be used for military purposes from entering Gaza.The interception of the ship has sparked outrage among supporters of the Palestinian cause, who see it as yet another example of the ongoing oppression faced by the people of Gaza. The activists on board the ship have vowed to continue their efforts to break the blockade and deliver aid to Gaza, despite the obstacles they face.

The U.S. Attorney's office has announced that there will be no prosecution in the case of the over-budget Colorado VA hospital. The project, which was initially estimated to cost $328 million, ended up costing over $1.7 billion. Despite calls for accountability and possible criminal charges, prosecutors have determined that there is not enough evidence to move forward with a case.The hospital, located in Aurora, Colorado, has been plagued with issues since construction began in 2011. Delays and cost overruns have been attributed to a number of factors, including mismanagement and design changes. The project was finally completed in 2018, but the financial repercussions have continued to be felt by taxpayers.While many are disappointed by the lack of prosecution in this case, some believe that the focus should now be on ensuring that similar mistakes are not made in the future. The Department of Veterans Affairs has already taken steps to improve its project management processes and oversight to prevent similar situations from occurring.Despite the lack of criminal charges, the fallout from the over-budget Colorado VA hospital will likely continue to be felt for years to come. It serves as a stark reminder of the importance of transparency and accountability in government projects, especially those that are meant to serve our nation's veterans.

The Justice Department is stripping U.S. citizenship from an Iowa man, who confessed to being a guard at two Nazi concentration camps in World War II.A federal appeals court Tuesday upheld a lower court decision revoking 81-year-old John Hansl's citizenship.Hansl was a guard at the Sachsenhausen concentration camp near Berlin and the Natzweiler camp in France.The court ruled that Hansl had strict orders to shoot prisoners who tried to escape, marched them at gunpoint, and watched over them during slave labor.The court said Hansl and other SS guards played an essential role in the Nazi genocide.Hansl was born in present-day Croatia and came to the United States in 1955.

Sri Lanka's police have arrested the brother of the country's former leader, accusing him of being involved in a controversial land deal. Gotabaya Rajapaksa, the brother of ex-president Mahinda Rajapaksa, was taken into custody on Thursday following allegations of financial misconduct related to a land purchase in Colombo.The case centers around accusations that Gotabaya Rajapaksa used his political connections to obtain prime land in the capital city at a significantly lower price than market value. The land deal has sparked public outrage and calls for accountability from government officials.The arrest of Gotabaya Rajapaksa comes as part of a broader crackdown on corruption in Sri Lanka. The current government, led by President Maithripala Sirisena, has made combating corruption a key priority since coming into power in 2015.Critics of the Rajapaksa family allege that they used their positions of power to amass wealth and properties illegally during their time in office. The arrest of Gotabaya Rajapaksa is seen as a significant step towards holding those in power accountable for their actions.The former leader's brother is expected to face charges related to financial misconduct and corruption in connection to the land deal. The case has once again brought attention to the issue of corruption in Sri Lanka and the need for transparency and accountability in government dealings.

The spokesman for Malawi?s President, Bingu Wa Mutharika, has dismissed as without merit accusations that the leader is becoming autocratic and intolerant of dissent.Mutharika has come under increasing pressure to resolve what his opponents call the country?s economic crisis. Critics blame him for failing to resolve disputes with international donors, who are withholding funds to the southern African nation.But, Dr. Heatherwick Ntaba said Mutharika is not to blame for the country?s current fiscal and economic challenges. His comments came after former President Bakili Muluzi said he will not be intimidated by the government?s tactics following the arrest and subsequent release of his son, Atupele Muluzi, who attended a recent anti-government rally.?Perhaps he is not too happy that his son had been arrested for disobeying the law. Perhaps he did not advise his son quite clearly,? said Ntaba. ?We are not surprised that he is blaming the government and the dictatorial tendencies he is talking about?the country?s successes and achievements cannot be achieved in a dictatorial setting. So, what he is saying does not hold water on the ground.?Ntaba said Vice President Joyce Banda postponed a scheduled rally near the capital, Lilongwe, following a police directive. He maintains that the young Muluzi should have obeyed the police directive.?She did postpone that meeting because it was the police asking her to do that and no problems occurred there. It is the norm and she obeyed the law,?

In the wake of the tragic events that unfolded in Paris, France, on November 13th, the world has come together to show solidarity with the city and its people. From New York to Tokyo, cities around the globe have lit up iconic landmarks in the colors of the French flag - blue, white, and red - to show support for those affected by the terrorist attacks.Social media has also been flooded with messages of solidarity and support, with hashtags such as #PrayforParis and #SolidariteavecParis trending worldwide. People from all walks of life are expressing their grief and solidarity with the victims and their families.In a time of uncertainty and fear, the global outpouring of support for Paris serves as a reminder of the power of unity and compassion. As the city begins to heal and rebuild, the world stands with them, sending thoughts and prayers for peace and strength during this difficult time.

LONDON - Britain's Queen Elizabeth II will make an extremely rare nationwide address to the nation on Sunday as it grapples with an increasingly deadly coronavirus outbreak. "Her Majesty The Queen has recorded a special broadcast to the United Kingdom and the Commonwealth in relation to the coronavirus outbreak," Buckingham Palace said. The queen rarely addresses the nation directly apart from her traditional televised Christmas Day message.

The United States and Egypt sought ways Wednesday to revive the Israeli-Palestinian peace process after Egyptian President Abdel-Fattah el-Sissi said this week he would "make every effort" toward a solution.U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry met with the Egyptian leader in Cairo, with a State Department spokesman later saying the top American diplomat "expressed his appreciation" for Sissi's "strong support for advancing Arab-Israeli peace."But spokesman Mark Toner did not elaborate on any specific new effort to renew the moribund Israeli-Palestinian peace process that broke down in 2014 with no agreement.The Kerry-Sissi meeting came a day after the Egyptian leader said an Israeli-Palestinian accord would make Cairo's relations with Tel Aviv "warmer."Sissi said there was a "real opportunity" for peace in the region. He said an Israeli-Palestinian pact would "give safety and stability to both sides. If this is achieved, we will enter a new phase that perhaps no one can imagine now."Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu praised Sissi's "willingness to invest every effort to advance a future of peace and security between us and the Palestinians."Egypt and Israel reached a peace agreement in 1979, the first accord between an Arab country and the Jewish state.

The hacker collective known as Anonymous says it has declared “total war” on Republican presidential hopeful Donald Trump.In a video released March 4, the shadowy group said his campaign for the U.S. presidency was “deeply disturbing.”“Your inconsistent and hateful campaign has not only shocked the United States of America, you have shocked the entire planet with your appalling actions and ideas,” said a masked man with a robotic voice. “You don’t stand for anything except for your personal greed and power. This is a call to arms.”The group is calling the campaign against the real estate mogul #OpTrump, saying it will begin on April 1, which is April Fool's Day in the United States.In another post, the group said the operation would include denial of service attacks on several Trump-related websites, including Trump’s main site, DonaldJTrump.com. The post also included what Anonymous says is some of Trump’s personal information.Anonymous has hacked Trump before.Last December, in the wake of Trump's call for a ban on Muslims entering the U.S., the group crashed the Trump Tower New York website and leaked voicemails, reported the Telegraph newspaper.The April 1 effort, the group said, will be much bigger."

Nigeria's Kaduna state police command says two Americans and two Canadians kidnapped by gunmen on Tuesday have been released.Police say the four were kidnapped along Kwoi-Jere road in the Kagarko local government area, Kaduna state.The gunmen are reported by Nigeria's Vanguard newspaper to have killed two Nigerian policemen who were escorting the foreigners. Kidnappings for ransom are common on the road between Kaduna and Abuja. Two Germans were abducted in the region last February but were later freed.A Sierra Leone diplomat and former head of the army, Nelson Williams, was kidnapped along the road in 2016 and held for five days before his release.State Commissioner of Police Agyole Abeh said one suspect has been arrested in connection with the abduction.

Warfarin is a medication used by millions of people around the world. It is also used as rat poison. The medication prevents blood clots in those who form clots easily. These clumps of cells can cause a stroke if they reach the brain, or they can form clots in the lung that can be fatal. But dosing for the drug is tricky: Give too little and it's not effective; give too much and the patient's blood will be too "thin," and a simple cut can cause excessive bleeding. It's the same property that makes warfarin a good rat poison. Doctors usually have to try different doses to find the correct dose. It's a process that can take weeks and requires many blood samples from patients. Now an international team of doctors has found a better way to determine the right dose for patients, other than the usual method. Michael Wagner from the University of North Carolina was part of that team. "With many drugs, people can have a fairly wide range of dosing and still get good effects, and not get the bad effects," Wagner says. "That's not true for warfarin; the range that you have to maintain in your dosing is very narrow, and it differs substantially between different people. People may vary as much as 10-fold in how much warfarin that they need in order to achieve the same level of anti-clotting effect."

Former President Barack Obama has once again called for increased resources to combat drug abuse in the United States. In a recent speech, Obama emphasized the need for a comprehensive approach to address the ongoing drug crisis in the country.Obama highlighted the importance of prevention, treatment, and enforcement efforts in tackling drug abuse. He emphasized the need for more funding for addiction treatment programs, as well as increased support for law enforcement agencies to crack down on drug trafficking.The former president also stressed the importance of addressing the root causes of drug abuse, including economic instability, mental health issues, and lack of access to quality healthcare. He called for a holistic approach that focuses on both prevention and treatment, rather than solely relying on punitive measures.Obama's comments come at a time when drug overdose deaths continue to rise in the United States, with opioids being a major contributing factor. He urged lawmakers to prioritize funding for drug abuse prevention and treatment programs, and to work towards creating a more comprehensive and effective strategy to combat the drug crisis.As the country continues to grapple with the devastating effects of drug abuse, Obama's call for increased resources and a comprehensive approach to tackling the issue is a timely reminder of the urgent need for action.

Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen made a historic visit to the small African nation of Swaziland, strengthening diplomatic ties with one of the few countries that still recognizes Taiwan as a sovereign state.During her visit, President Tsai met with Swazi King Mswati III and discussed various bilateral cooperation projects, including agriculture, healthcare, and infrastructure development. The two leaders also signed agreements aimed at further enhancing the relationship between their two countries.The visit to Swaziland comes amid growing pressure from China, which considers Taiwan to be a renegade province and has been actively working to isolate it diplomatically.

Ethiopian journalist, Sara Ababa, was recently honored for her outstanding work in amplifying the voices of marginalized communities in her country. Ababa, who works for a prominent news outlet in Ethiopia, has dedicated her career to shedding light on important social issues and giving a platform to those who are often overlooked.Through her powerful storytelling and investigative reporting, Ababa has brought attention to issues such as human rights abuses, environmental concerns, and social injustices. She has fearlessly tackled taboo topics and given a voice to those who have been silenced or oppressed.Ababa's commitment to journalism and advocacy for the voiceless has not gone unnoticed. She was recently recognized with an award for her outstanding contributions to the field of journalism and her dedication to promoting social change through her work.In a statement following the award ceremony, Ababa expressed her gratitude for the recognition and reaffirmed her commitment to using her platform to speak out on behalf of those who are often left unheard. She emphasized the importance of journalism in holding those in power accountable and giving a voice to the voiceless.As Ethiopia continues to grapple with social and political challenges, journalists like Sara Ababa play a crucial role in advocating for change and bringing attention to important issues. Ababa's dedication to her craft and her commitment to speaking truth to power make her a true beacon of hope for the marginalized communities she represents.

GENEVA - The U.N. refugee agency is appealing for $184 million to support its life-saving operations for nearly a half-million Cameroonians displaced inside their country and as refugees in Nigeria.Violent clashes between the Cameroonian military and armed separatists in the English-speaking parts of the country have intensified during the past year.The U.N. refugee agency estimates more than 437,000 Cameroonians are internally displaced and more than 35,000 others have fled to Nigeria in search of refuge.The UNHCR said most of the victims are women and children who have fled their homes with very little, and they often arrive in impoverished host communities where food, health, education, water and sanitation are scarce.UNHCR spokesman Babar Baloch tells VOA that violence is forcing civilians caught in the crossfire to flee for their lives.“Civilians have suffered immensely and also burning of houses, torture, rape, kidnapping of school children in this region is a great concern for us," Balock said. "And, in terms of burning houses, we have reports that in over 172 villages in these regions, houses have been burned or destroyed.”Baloch said the UNHCR, which he said is severely underfunded and unable to meet the growing needs, urgently needs $35 million to provide critical aid for the hundreds of thousands of displaced people.Inside Cameroon, he said many people are living in overcrowded conditions, without proper shelter or health and sanitation.

In a move towards transparency and accountability, the local government has announced that they will be broadcasting their meetings for the public to view. This decision comes after increased pressure from both citizens and the press to have greater access to government proceedings.The broadcasts will be available online and will include live streaming of meetings as well as archives for later viewing. This move is seen as a positive step towards keeping the government accountable and ensuring that decisions are made in a transparent manner.The press has welcomed this decision, with many journalists stating that they will be keeping a watchful eye on the government's actions during these meetings. This increased visibility is expected to lead to greater scrutiny and oversight of government operations.Overall, the decision to broadcast meetings is a positive development for the community and will hopefully lead to greater transparency and accountability in local government. The press will continue to monitor these meetings closely to ensure that the government is acting in the best interest of the public.

In a closely watched election, Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez has won a fourth term in office after defeating his opponent, Henrique Capriles, in a hotly contested race. Chavez, who has been in power since 1999, secured a victory with 54% of the vote compared to Capriles' 44%.The election results were met with both celebrations and protests across the country. Supporters of Chavez praised his socialist policies and efforts to reduce poverty, while opponents criticized his authoritarian style of governance and alleged human rights abuses.Chavez's victory ensures that his "Bolivarian Revolution" will continue in Venezuela for another six-year term. The president has promised to continue his socialist reforms and strengthen ties with other Latin American countries.As one of the most polarizing figures in Latin American politics, Chavez's re-election will undoubtedly have far-reaching implications for the region. It remains to be seen how his victory will impact Venezuela's relationship with the United States and other Western countries in the coming years.

The recent outbreak of the coronavirus in Greece has sparked a controversy between the church and the state. The Greek government's decision to suspend all public gatherings, including religious services, has not been well-received by the Greek Orthodox Church.The church, which plays a significant role in Greek society, has criticized the government's move as an infringement on religious freedom. The Archbishop of Athens has expressed concern over the impact of the suspension of services on the spiritual well-being of the faithful.On the other hand, the government has defended its decision as necessary to prevent the spread of the virus. Greece has seen a spike in cases in recent weeks, prompting authorities to take strict measures to contain the outbreak.The church-state controversy has raised questions about the balance between public health concerns and religious rights. Both sides are facing pressure to find a resolution that respects both the need for safety and the freedom of worship. The situation remains tense as the country continues to grapple with the challenges posed by the coronavirus pandemic.

U.S. President George Bush told legislators from the Democratic Party, which now controls Congress, that his new strategy for the war in Iraq is the best chance for success. VOA White House Correspondent Scott Stearns reports, Democrats are pushing Congressional resolutions opposing the president's plan to send more U.S. troops.Acknowledging opposition to his plans for Iraq, President Bush said he and Democrats do agree on some things, including the need for Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki's government to show strong leadership.At a meeting of House Democrats, Mr. Bush thanked their leader, Nancy Pelosi, for briefing him on her recent trip to Iraq."She said loud and clear, 'Mr. President, you have got to make it clear to the Iraqi people that their government has got to perform.' And I understand that. I agree, Madame Speaker," he said. "There has got to be success, not only on the military front. In other words, the Iraqis have got to be taking the lead in Baghdad to secure its capital, but there has also got to be success on the political front."This was the president's first-ever appearance before an informal meeting of House Democrats. The party won control of both houses of Congress last year in an election decided largely over voter displeasure with the war.In a private session with legislators, Mr. Bush took questions on Iraq, veterans' benefits, the budget, Hurricane Katrina, immigration, and global warming.Speaker Pelosi said Democrats were encouraged by the president's remarks and believe they can work with him on immigration reform and alternative energy. But she made clear there are still sharp divisions over the war."

The US military has come forward to clarify that a statement made by one of its officials was "mischaracterized" by South Korean media outlets. The statement in question was regarding a joint military exercise between the two countries. The US military emphasized that the official's remarks were taken out of context and did not accurately reflect the nature of the exercise. This incident serves as a reminder of the importance of responsible reporting and accurate representation of statements made by officials. The US military is working closely with South Korean authorities to address any misunderstandings and ensure clear communication moving forward.

In a significant development, the former deputy leader of the militant group Al-Shabab has been arrested in Somalia. The suspect, who goes by the name of Hassan Dahir Aweys, was apprehended by security forces in a raid on a hideout in the capital city of Mogadishu.Aweys, a prominent figure within Al-Shabab, is known for his role in the group's operations and ideology. He was previously a senior member of the organization before reportedly falling out of favor with its leadership.The arrest of Aweys is seen as a major blow to Al-Shabab and a significant victory for the Somali government in its efforts to combat extremism and terrorism in the region. Security officials are hopeful that the capture of such a high-profile figure will help disrupt the group's activities and weaken its influence.The Somali government has not yet released any further details about the circumstances of Aweys' arrest or any charges that may be brought against him. However, it is expected that he will face prosecution for his alleged involvement in terrorist activities.Al-Shabab, which has been responsible for numerous deadly attacks in Somalia and neighboring countries, remains a threat to peace and stability in the region. The group has been designated as a terrorist organization by the United States and other countries.The arrest of Aweys marks a significant milestone in the fight against terrorism in Somalia and serves as a reminder of the ongoing challenges faced by the government and security forces in combating extremist groups.

Floods in northern Venezuela have killed at least 13 people and forced more than 5,000 to leave their homes.Heavy rains have caused floods and landslides, forcing authorities to declare a state of emergency in the capital city of Caracas and the country's northern provinces.Flooding rivers and landslides have blocked traffic in northern Venezuela's coastal areas.Minister of Education Aristolbulo Isturiz announced Wednesday that classes across the country have been canceled to allow flood victims to seek refuge in the schools.Weather forecasts say more rain is likely to hit northern Venezuela over the next three days.Some information for this story provided by AP and AFP.
The World Health Organization has confirmed that two more Indonesians have died of bird flu, bringing the country's death toll from the virus to 32.

Popular music acts Imagine Dragons and Lorde have emerged as the frontrunners in the list of finalists for the upcoming Billboard Music Awards. The awards show, which recognizes achievements in the music industry based on Billboard charts, will see the two artists competing in multiple categories.Imagine Dragons, known for hits like "Believer" and "Thunder," have been nominated in several categories including Top Duo/Group and Top Rock Artist. Similarly, Lorde, who gained fame with songs like "Royals" and "Green Light," is a finalist in categories such as Top Female Artist and Top Alternative Artist.Other notable finalists include Ed Sheeran, Taylor Swift, and Kendrick Lamar, who are also vying for awards in various categories. The Billboard Music Awards ceremony is scheduled to take place in the coming months, where winners will be announced and honored for their contributions to the music industry.Fans of Imagine Dragons, Lorde, and other nominated artists are eagerly awaiting the awards show to see who will take home the prestigious trophies. Stay tuned for updates on the Billboard Music Awards and the latest news on your favorite music artists.

The first lady of Uganda calls AIDS a threat to the populations of entire countries, especially in sub-Saharan Africa. Janet Museveni, the wife of President Yoweri Museveni, was the keynote speaker at an international conference in Washington on AIDS.She spoke on the final day of a four-day conference in Washington D.C. on Christianity and AIDS. Nearly 1,000 people have attended from 87 countries suffering from the HIV/AIDS pandemic. Uganda is one of a very few African countries that took on the AIDS crisis early, addressing the problem as a public health crisis. Uganda's first lady, Janet Museveni, has been a leader in the country's AIDS campaign.Mrs. Museveni founded a program to provide education and support for Uganda's 1.7 million AIDS orphans. The first lady stressed the disease has not only robbed children of their parents, but of much of their extended family as well, endangering the fabric of Ugandan society. "The able-bodied uncles and aunties have also succumbed to the same disease," she said, "often leaving only the grandmothers to care for their grandchildren."Mrs.

Queen Elizabeth II is set to make a rare televised address to the nation on Sunday in response to the ongoing coronavirus pandemic. This will only be the fifth time in her 68-year reign that the Queen has made such an address, with the last one being during the Gulf War in 1991.The address is expected to be pre-recorded and will air at 8 pm local time.

IPIALES, COLOMBIA - More than 200 Venezuelans crossed the border illegally into Ecuador on Tuesday, fleeing a deepening economic and political crisis at home in a desperate race to get to Peru before new entry restrictions kick in Saturday.This year 423,000 Venezuelans have entered Ecuador through the Rumichaca border near the southwestern Colombian town of Ipiales. Alarmed, Ecuador last Saturday put in place rules requiring Venezuelans to show passports, rather than just national identity cards. Peru will do the same this Saturday.Hundreds of migrants who had begun traveling days ago by bus and on foot through Colombia from Venezuela before the policy change crossed the Rumichaca checkpoint Tuesday. More than a dozen Ecuadorean police watched them, but did nothing to stop them."We only want Ecuador to support us to keep moving forward to Peru where we can work," said Yorian Alcides Gamez, as fellow migrants sang the national anthem.Venezuelan migrants walk along the Ecuadorean highway to Peru before new rules requiring they hold a valid passport kick in, at Tulcan, Ecuador, Aug. 21, 2018.They plan to walk and hitchhike 840 kilometers (522 miles) in freezing conditions to the crossing at Huaquilla in Peru."We're walking to Peru, we're on our way.

SINGAPORE - Pang Kin Keong is dreaming of a paradise city, where the grass is green and the people are -- well, sitting in driverless cars.Pang is also Permanent Secretary at Singapore’s Transport Ministry, so this is more than a dream. The government wants a city with fewer surface streets, making room for more parks, and more commuters using self-driving cars. Singapore would build roads underground and use the pods to shuttle people from the subway to their final destination.“You must dream, you must dream,” Pang said in an interview at his office. “I want to live in a place much like the holidays and resorts that I go to once or twice every year, where the living environment is dominated by greenery. It’s safe, there are no cars zooming past me, I can go anywhere I want.”From Silicon Valley to South Australia, companies are tinkering with driverless cars. But what sets Singapore apart is that the government now funds this research and plans to use the cars for public transit.A few subway stops from the transport ministry, engineers have retrofitted a Toyota Alphard Hybrid to drive, sans human. On a recent, cloudless morning, the minivan navigated a public, but unclogged road, its steering wheel spinning left and right without the touch of a human hand. Outside, a toy robot rolled in front of the vehicle, testing whether it could dodge an intrusion. The robot was unharmed.If Singapore eventually brings driverless cars online, the rest of the world could ride on its technological coattails.

In a recent statement, President Trump announced that the United States and its allies are targeting the use of chemical weapons. The President expressed his concern over the use of such weapons and emphasized the need for a strong response to deter their use in the future.The United States has been working closely with its allies to address the threat posed by chemical weapons, particularly in conflict zones around the world. Trump's statement comes in the wake of several high-profile incidents involving the use of chemical weapons, including the recent attack in Syria.The President stressed the importance of holding those responsible for the use of chemical weapons accountable and stated that the United States will not tolerate the use of such weapons under any circumstances. He also emphasized the need for a united front among the US and its allies in order to effectively combat this threat.The targeting of chemical weapons by the United States and its allies reflects a broader commitment to promoting peace and security around the world. Trump's statement serves as a reminder of the dangers posed by these weapons and the importance of working together to prevent their use in the future.

In the latest update on the situation in Liberia, reports indicate that clashes between government forces and rebel groups have intensified in the capital city of Monrovia. The fighting has resulted in a significant number of casualties and has caused widespread panic among the civilian population.The United Nations and other international organizations have expressed grave concern over the escalating violence and have called for an immediate ceasefire to allow for the delivery of humanitarian aid to those affected by the conflict. The UN Security Council is set to convene an emergency meeting to discuss the crisis in Liberia and explore possible avenues for peace.Meanwhile, neighboring countries have stepped up efforts to mediate a peaceful resolution to the conflict and prevent further bloodshed. The African Union and ECOWAS have both pledged their support for a peaceful resolution to the crisis in Liberia and have urged all parties to prioritize dialogue and negotiation.As the situation continues to deteriorate, the international community is closely monitoring developments in Liberia and stands ready to provide assistance to help resolve the conflict and alleviate the suffering of the civilian population. The coming days will be crucial in determining the future of Liberia and the well-being of its people.

In recent years, tech startups in Africa have been gaining momentum and making significant progress. With a growing number of entrepreneurs and investors focusing on the continent, the tech industry in Africa is experiencing a surge of innovation and development.Countries such as Nigeria, Kenya, South Africa, and Ghana have emerged as hubs for tech startups, with a vibrant ecosystem that is conducive to growth and success. From fintech and e-commerce to health tech and agri-tech, African startups are leveraging technology to address local challenges and create impact in their communities.One key factor driving the growth of tech startups in Africa is the increasing access to mobile technology and the internet. With the majority of the population now owning a smartphone, there is a huge potential for reaching a large and diverse customer base. This has paved the way for innovative solutions in areas such as mobile banking, e-commerce platforms, and digital healthcare services.Furthermore, the support from local governments and international organizations has also played a significant role in fostering the growth of tech startups in Africa. Initiatives such as tech hubs, funding opportunities, and mentorship programs have provided a conducive environment for entrepreneurs to thrive and succeed.As tech startups in Africa continue to move forward, the future looks promising for the continent's digital economy. With a young and dynamic population eager to embrace technology, the potential for innovation and growth in Africa's tech industry is boundless.

Abebe Gellaw, journalist and visiting scholar at Stanford University, was one of two Ethiopians named Young Global Leaders weeks ago by Geneva-based World Economic Forum.The World Economic Forum selected 197 Young Global Leaders this year from a pool of nearly 5,000 under-40-year-old nominees from around the world for their “professional accomplishments, commitment to society and potential to contribute to shaping the future of the world,” the forum announced. The other honored Ethiopian is supermodel Liya Kebede.In his interview with VOA Amharic’s Cultural Mosaic, Abebe said that he wants to continue to work as advocate for the poor and a group he calls "voiceless" in Ethiopia and elsewhere. He also says that he will continue on his dedication to address issues of human right, economic inequality and justice.

Two Russian servicemen were killed and a third wounded in a mortar attack in Syria, the Russian Defense Ministry announced Tuesday."Russian professional servicemen who were in a unit of Syrian forces as instructors ... came under a mortar attack from a group of rebel fighters," the Defense Ministry said in a statement quoted by Interfax news agency. "As a result of a mortar explosion, two Russian servicemen died."

The United States has announced that the agenda for talks with North Korea will be "quite narrow". This announcement comes as tensions between the two countries continue to escalate.According to a statement from the US State Department, the talks will focus on denuclearization and the possibility of a peace treaty to formally end the Korean War. The US has made it clear that any talks with North Korea will only proceed if they are focused on these specific issues.The narrow agenda for the talks reflects the Trump administration's commitment to resolving the North Korean nuclear crisis through diplomatic means. However, it remains to be seen whether North Korea will be willing to engage in discussions that are limited to these issues.The announcement of the narrow agenda comes after months of escalating rhetoric between the US and North Korea, with both sides exchanging threats and insults. It remains to be seen whether the talks will lead to any concrete progress towards resolving the long-standing tensions between the two countries.

This winter in the United States will be colder than normal. That's what the classic American publication the Old Farmers's Almanac is predicting."We expect heavier than normal snow in the Intermountain region, which is in the vicinity of the Rocky Mountains, and just colder than normal temperatures throughout most of the U.S.," said Janice Stillman, the Almanac's editor. She claims the forecasts made by the Old Farmer's Almanac are 80 percent accurate.To make those predictions, the Almanac has traditionally used a secret formula based on sunspot activity developed in 1792 by its founder, Robert B. Thomas. Today, it also employs what it calls state-of-the-art technology and modern scientific calculations."But you have to remember that we predict temperature and precipitation as deviations from the norm," said Stillman. "We're not going to tell you what the temperatures going to be, nor how much rain or snow is going to fall."Some scientists claim the vagueness of the Almanac's predictions makes it difficult to determine their accuracy.

The rebellion in the Central African Republic last year turned into the worst looting spree the country has ever seen. The Seleka rebels and criminal gangs pillaged businesses, homes, government offices, religious missions, health centers and public utilities. The CAR's national union of business leaders recently submitted a list of companies that suffered looting and vandalism after March 24 of last year, the day the Seleka rebel alliance seized power in Bangui. The list of 23 companies includes most of the important businesses in the country. While the scale of their losses has yet to be calculated, visiting just a few sites around Bangui gives an indication of how damaging the recent upheavals have been.Road construction firm looted VOA visited the main depot of the country's biggest road construction firm, the China International Fund. One of the managers, Cyriaque Motoumba, says looters took vast amounts of building materials and equipment. He says they emptied the containers of cement and also they took the steel reinforcement bars that the Chinese had stocked in eight of the containers, so now there is nothing. Motoumba says 400 tons of steel bars were loaded onto trucks by the Seleka and driven away - to Chad, he believes, because that is where many Seleka mercenaries came from. Several vehicles were stolen, he says, and it's clear some of the other vehicles were taken apart. He shows the inside of a tractor engine.

The ongoing crisis in Myanmar's Kachin State has forced many residents to embark on perilous jungle treks in order to escape the escalating violence and conflict in the region. With clashes between government forces and ethnic armed groups intensifying, civilians are resorting to dangerous journeys through dense forests and rugged terrain to seek safety.The conflict in Kachin State, which has been ongoing for decades, has recently seen a resurgence in violence, displacing thousands of people and leaving many villages in ruins. The lack of access to humanitarian aid and services has further exacerbated the situation, pushing residents to flee their homes in search of refuge.These treacherous jungle treks are often the only option for those fleeing the violence, as many roads are blocked or controlled by armed groups. The journey is fraught with risks, including the threat of encountering landmines, wild animals, and harsh weather conditions. Many who attempt the journey are elderly, sick, or have young children with them, adding to the difficulties they face along the way.Humanitarian organizations and local aid groups are working tirelessly to provide assistance to those affected by the crisis, but the challenges they face in reaching remote and conflict-affected areas are immense. The international community has called for an end to the violence in Kachin State and for all parties involved to respect the rights and safety of civilians caught in the crossfire.As the conflict in Myanmar's Kachin State continues to escalate, the plight of those forced to undertake dangerous jungle treks to escape the war remains a stark reminder of the human cost of ongoing violence and instability in the region.

Public schools across the United States are facing a dilemma over whether to recognize Muslim holidays in their academic calendars. As the Muslim population in the US continues to grow, many Muslim families are advocating for schools to acknowledge important religious holidays such as Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha.Currently, the academic calendar in most public schools primarily recognizes Christian holidays such as Christmas and Easter. This has left Muslim students and their families feeling marginalized and excluded from school celebrations. In some cases, Muslim students have had to miss school in order to observe their religious holidays, leading to concerns about academic performance and attendance.In response to these concerns, some school districts have started to consider adding Muslim holidays to the academic calendar. This move has sparked debate among parents, educators, and community members, with some arguing that schools should be inclusive of all religious traditions, while others express concerns about the impact on scheduling and curriculum.The issue of recognizing Muslim holidays in public schools is a complex one that requires careful consideration and dialogue. As the US continues to become more diverse, it is important for schools to ensure that all students feel respected and included, regardless of their religious beliefs. It remains to be seen how schools will navigate this dilemma in the future.

Former Ugandan Supreme Court Justice George Kanyeihamba has raised concerns over the recent treason charges brought against opposition leader Kizza Besigye. In a statement released today, Kanyeihamba questioned the legitimacy of the charges, citing lack of concrete evidence and a potential political motive behind the arrest.Besigye, a prominent critic of President Yoweri Museveni's government, was arrested last week on allegations of plotting to overthrow the government. The charges have been widely criticized by human rights groups and political opponents as an attempt to silence dissent and intimidate the opposition.Kanyeihamba, known for his outspoken views on justice and democracy in Uganda, called for a transparent and fair trial for Besigye. He urged the government to uphold the rule of law and ensure that the rights of all citizens, including political opponents, are respected.The trial of Besigye is set to begin next week, and many are closely watching the proceedings to see how the case unfolds. The outcome of this trial could have significant implications for the future of democracy and political freedom in Uganda.
This week's most eye-catching and news-relevant pictures.

Turkey's central bank has vowed to take action after the country's inflation rate surged to 18%, the highest level in nearly two decades. The rapid increase in prices has been fueled by a combination of factors, including the sharp depreciation of the Turkish lira and rising global commodity prices.In response to the alarming inflation figures, the central bank has announced that it will implement measures to stabilize the economy and curb inflation. This could include raising interest rates or implementing other monetary policy tools to control the flow of money in the economy.The surge in inflation is a major concern for Turkey, as it threatens to erode the purchasing power of its citizens and undermine economic stability. The government has come under pressure to address the issue and take decisive action to combat the rising prices.Analysts warn that high inflation could have damaging effects on the Turkish economy, leading to higher costs for businesses and consumers, and potentially triggering a cycle of inflation that is difficult to control.The central bank's promise to take action is seen as a step in the right direction, but it remains to be seen whether their measures will be enough to bring inflation under control and stabilize the economy. Investors and citizens alike will be watching closely to see how the situation unfolds in the coming months.

The Democratic National Committee (DNC) has recently undergone a significant transformation with the introduction of new media technologies. With the rise of social media platforms and digital tools, the DNC has embraced a new era of communication and engagement with the public.One of the key changes brought about by new media is the ability to reach a wider audience in real-time. The DNC now has the ability to connect with supporters and voters across the country instantly through platforms like Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram. This has allowed the party to communicate their message more effectively and engage with a diverse range of individuals.Additionally, new media has allowed the DNC to create interactive and engaging content that resonates with younger generations. Videos, memes, and live streams have become integral parts of the party's communication strategy, helping to reach a broader demographic and mobilize support.Furthermore, the use of data analytics and digital advertising has enabled the DNC to target specific demographics and tailor their messages accordingly. This targeted approach has proven to be effective in reaching undecided voters and persuading them to support the Democratic party.Overall, the integration of new media technologies has revolutionized the way the DNC communicates with the public and mobilizes support. As the party continues to adapt and evolve in the digital age, it is clear that new media will play a crucial role in shaping the future of the Democratic National Committee.

A recent report from the World Health Organization (WHO) has revealed that 75% of asymptomatic COVID-19 patients in China eventually develop symptoms. This finding highlights the importance of monitoring and testing individuals who may have been exposed to the virus, even if they are not showing any signs of illness.The study, which was conducted in several regions of China, found that a significant percentage of individuals who initially tested positive for COVID-19 but were asymptomatic later went on to develop symptoms such as fever, cough, and shortness of breath. This suggests that even individuals who do not show any signs of illness should be closely monitored for the virus.These findings have important implications for public health officials and policymakers as they work to control the spread of COVID-19. It is crucial to identify and isolate individuals who may be carrying the virus, even if they do not appear to be sick. This will help to prevent further transmission and protect vulnerable populations.As the global pandemic continues to evolve, studies like these provide valuable insights into the behavior of the virus and the ways in which it can spread. It is essential that we continue to monitor and study COVID-19 in order to better understand and combat the disease.

BEIJING - A court in Canada has released tech giant Huawei's chief financial officer Meng Wanzhou on bail as she awaits possible extradition to the United States over bank fraud allegations linked to Iran sanctions. However, in a new twist to the case that has quickly mushroomed far beyond its initial scope, U.S. President Donald Trump has said that he might intervene. “Whatever's good for the country, I would do," Trump told Reuters in an interview, shortly after the ruling. "If I think it's good for what will certainly be the largest trade deal ever made, which is a very important thing. What's good for national security, I would certainly intervene if I thought it was necessary." The United States has 60 days from the day of Meng’s arrest to issue a formal extradition request and provide Canadian courts with evidence. Meng, the daughter of Huawei’s founder, was taken into custody on December 1 while transiting planes in Canada. While her legal fate is worked out, Meng agreed to post $7.5 million in bail, hand over her passports and remain in British Columbia. She will also wear an ankle bracelet and be under 24-hour surveillance, barred from leaving a home she owns in Vancouver between 11 at night and six in the morning. Huawei Technologies is one of the world's biggest manufacturers of mobile phones.

In a historic move, the Vietnam Parliament has elected the Communist Party chief, Nguyen Phu Trong, as the country's President. This decision marks the first time in over two decades that a party leader has taken on the role of President in Vietnam.Trong, who has been the General Secretary of the Communist Party since 2011, was the only candidate nominated for the position. The 74-year-old leader is known for his anti-corruption efforts and his strong stance on maintaining the party's control over the government.With the backing of the Communist Party, Trong's election to the presidency solidifies the party's grip on power in Vietnam. However, some critics have raised concerns about the lack of opposition and the consolidation of power in the hands of one party.Despite these concerns, Trong's supporters believe that his leadership will bring stability and continuity to Vietnam, a country that has seen rapid economic growth in recent years. As the newly elected President, Trong is expected to continue pursuing the party's agenda and further strengthen ties with other countries in the region.

The White House has announced a new initiative aimed at assisting cities in the United States with addressing the impacts of climate change.
