The death toll has doubled in a suicide bombing in eastern Afghanistan, according to local authorities. The attack took place in a crowded market in the Paktia province, with initial reports indicating at least 50 people were killed and over 100 others injured. The bombing has been condemned by government officials and international leaders, with investigations ongoing to determine the perpetrators behind the deadly attack. The Afghan people are once again left mourning the loss of innocent lives in yet another act of senseless violence in their war-torn country.

BEIJING - Even before U.S.-China trade tensions began escalating dramatically, foreign businesses who operate in China were warning about the impact tariffs could have. And now, according to a newly released joint survey from the American Chamber of Commerce in China and AmCham Shanghai, many are already feeling the pinch.More than 60 percent say the initial $50 billion in tariffs rolled out by the United States and China are having a negative impact on business, increasing the demand of manufacturing and slowing demand for products.That number is expected to rise to nearly 75 percent if a second round of tariffs, an additional $200 billion in tariffs from Washington and another $60 billion from Beijing, goes ahead.The administration of President Donald Trump has threatened it could go ahead with $200 billion in tariffs and, if needed, $267 billion more after that.FILE - Shan Yuliang, salesperson at a cigarette and wine shop, poses with a carton of Marlboro cigarettes in Beijing, China, April 8, 2018.Unexpected consequences William Zarit, chairman of AmCham China said while there are expectations in Washington that an additional onslaught of tariffs could force Beijing to wave the white flag, it risks underestimating China’s capability to continue to meet fire with fire, he said.“It seems that American companies will be more harmed by the American tariffs than they will by the Chinese tariffs. I don’t think that this necessarily is a result that was expected,” Zarit said.President Trump argues that China is stealing jobs from the United States and not doing enough to address the huge trade deficit between the two economies.

Former Tunisian Prime Minister Mehdi Jomaa has announced the launch of a new political party that aims to be "non-ideological" and focused on promoting national unity and economic development.Jomaa, who served as prime minister from 2014 to 2015, stated that the party, called the National Unity Party, will prioritize the interests of the Tunisian people above all else. He emphasized the need for a fresh approach to politics in Tunisia, one that is not bogged down by ideology or partisan interests.The National Unity Party will focus on issues such as job creation, economic growth, and improving living standards for all Tunisians. Jomaa has stated that the party will seek to collaborate with other political parties and civil society organizations to achieve these goals.Jomaa's decision to form a new party comes at a time of political instability in Tunisia, with the country facing economic challenges and social unrest. Many Tunisians are disillusioned with the existing political parties and are looking for new leadership that can address their concerns and work towards a better future.The launch of the National Unity Party is seen as a potential game-changer in Tunisian politics, with Jomaa positioning himself as a unifying figure who can bridge the divide between different factions and bring about positive change for the country.

Mexico's Foreign Minister, Marcelo Ebrard, is set to travel to Washington for meetings with top US officials. Ebrard's visit comes at a crucial time as the two countries continue to navigate a complex relationship on issues such as trade, immigration, and security.During his visit, Ebrard is expected to meet with US Secretary of State Antony Blinken and other key members of the Biden administration. The discussions are likely to focus on ways to strengthen cooperation between the two countries and address ongoing challenges.One of the key topics on the agenda is likely to be the ongoing migrant crisis at the US-Mexico border.

A recent study conducted by leading astrophysicists suggests that any potential contact with alien civilizations is likely to be at least 1,500 years away. The study, which was published in the prestigious Astrophysical Journal, examined various factors such as the vast distances between stars, the limitations of current technology, and the likelihood of advanced civilizations existing in the universe.According to the researchers, even if intelligent life forms do exist on other planets, the immense distances between stars make it highly unlikely for any meaningful communication or contact to occur in the near future. The study also highlighted the challenges of detecting alien signals or spacecraft, as our current instruments are not yet sophisticated enough to pick up such faint and distant signals.While the prospect of alien contact has long captured the imagination of scientists and the public alike, this study serves as a sobering reminder of the vastness and complexity of the universe. Despite advances in technology and our understanding of the cosmos, the search for extraterrestrial life remains a daunting and uncertain endeavor that may take centuries to come to fruition.

Recent studies have shown that alcohol consumption during pregnancy is causing harm to unborn children in South Africa. The effects of fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS) are becoming more prevalent as more expectant mothers continue to drink alcohol while pregnant.Fetal alcohol syndrome is a condition that can cause physical and mental impairments in children due to their mother's alcohol consumption during pregnancy. These effects can range from developmental delays and learning disabilities to physical abnormalities and behavioral issues.Experts are urging pregnant women to avoid alcohol completely during their pregnancy to protect the health and well-being of their unborn children. Health organizations are also working to increase awareness about the dangers of drinking alcohol while pregnant and provide support for expectant mothers who may be struggling with addiction.It is crucial for society as a whole to recognize the risks associated with alcohol consumption during pregnancy and take steps to prevent harm to unborn children. By raising awareness and providing support, we can help ensure that all children have the best start in life.

Another set of tit-for-tat tariffs imposed by the United States and China on each other's goods took effect Monday.A new round of U.S. imposed duties on $200 billion worth of Chinese goods, and a retaliatory set of tariffs imposed by Beijing on $60 billion worth of U.S. goods, took effect at 12:01 a.m. Washington time.The new U.S. duties covers thousands of Chinese-made products, including electronics, food, tools and housewares. The new tariffs begin at 10 percent, then will rise to 25 percent on January 1, 2019. The Trump administration exempted numerous other items from the new tariffs, including smart watches, Bluetooth devices, and infant car seats.In an interview with Fox News Sunday, Secretary of State Mike Pompeo promised the United States will be victorious in any trade war with China. "We are going to get an outcome which forces China to behave in a way that if you want to be a power, a global power ... you do not steal intellectual property," Pompeo said.FILE - Chinese-made children shoes carrying a Chinese map and U.S. flags are on display for a sale at a shop in Beijing, July 13, 2018.

Australia is cutting its catch of southern bluefin tuna by 30 percent after warnings from scientists that stocks are close to collapse. The reduction follows an international seafood conference in South Korea.

In the midst of ongoing political tensions in Haiti, tire barricades were set ablaze in the streets as a presidential candidate alleged widespread fraud in the recent election. The candidate, whose name has not been disclosed, claimed that the election results were manipulated in favor of their opponent.The protests and burning barricades have caused chaos and disruption in the capital city, Port-au-Prince, as well as other major cities across the country. The candidate's supporters have taken to the streets, demanding a recount and calling for transparency in the electoral process.The Haitian government has yet to respond to the allegations of fraud, but has urged calm and restraint from all parties involved. The international community has also called for a peaceful resolution to the political crisis, emphasizing the importance of upholding democratic principles in the country.As the situation continues to unfold, tensions remain high and uncertainty looms over the future of Haiti's political landscape. The coming days will be crucial in determining the outcome of the election and whether the allegations of fraud will be addressed.

In a significant development, representatives from the Afghan Taliban met with United States officials in the United Arab Emirates. The meeting, which took place in Abu Dhabi, is being seen as a potential step towards peace talks to end the long-standing conflict in Afghanistan.Both sides have confirmed the meeting, with the Taliban issuing a statement saying that they discussed the withdrawal of US troops from Afghanistan and ways to end the war. The US delegation, led by special envoy Zalmay Khalilzad, reportedly discussed the need for a ceasefire and the involvement of the Afghan government in future negotiations.The meeting comes amidst ongoing efforts to find a peaceful resolution to the conflict in Afghanistan, which has been raging for nearly two decades. Previous attempts at peace talks have faltered, but there is cautious optimism that this latest meeting could pave the way for further discussions.The United Arab Emirates has played a key role in facilitating the meeting, underscoring its commitment to promoting peace and stability in the region. The exact details of the discussions have not been disclosed, but both sides have indicated that they are open to further dialogue in the future.As the situation in Afghanistan remains fragile, the outcome of this meeting could have far-reaching implications for the country and its people. Many are hopeful that this could be a significant step towards ending the violence and paving the way for a more peaceful and stable Afghanistan.

In a further escalation of the ongoing trade war between the United States and China, both countries have exchanged a new round of tariffs on each other's goods. The US announced a 10% tariff on $300 billion worth of Chinese imports, which will go into effect on September 1st. In retaliation, China has stated that they will stop buying US agricultural products and allow their currency to weaken. This latest development is expected to have major implications for the global economy and could potentially lead to further tensions between the two economic giants. Experts are warning that this tit-for-tat tariff battle could have serious consequences for businesses and consumers in both countries. The trade war shows no signs of abating as both sides continue to impose tariffs and countermeasures in an attempt to gain the upper hand.

The U.S. State Department in Comoros is urging Americans to reconsider traveling to the Indian Ocean island nation following violence surrounding the country’s disputed presidential election.FILE- Comoros Gendarmerie officers carry an injured policeman wearing civilian clothes as they disperse opposition supporters, in Moroni, on March 25, 2019.The State Department upgraded its travel warning Friday for Americans to Level 3, meaning "Reconsider Travel," one step below the top Level 4, "Do Not Travel."U.S. officials say U.S. personnel on the island already have been ordered to leave temporarily.Four people died Thursday in a gunfight at a military camp in Comoros where the police had detained an opposition presidential candidate following Sunday’s disputed presidential election. VOA's Swahili service reported that at least seven people were injured.“Comoros is experiencing civil and political unrest, including armed conflict, roadblocks and protests, following a contested election,” the State Department advisory said.Earlier this week, Comoros’ electoral commission declared that President Azali Assoumani had been re-elected easily with more than 60 percent of the vote, but the opposition said it did not recognize the result, alleging widespread fraud. Then, opposition candidate Soilihi Mohamed declared himself the head of a transitional authority that aims to replace Assoumani.FILE - Incumbent Comoros President Azali Assoumani (front) greets supporters in Moroni on March 27, 2019 during celebrations for his re-election.Violence broke out after police arrested Mohamed, one of 12 politicians who ran against Assoumani in the election. Police also took into custody more than a dozen women who were protesting against Assoumani's government.Abdushakur Aboud, from VOA's Swahili service, reported Thursday that an armed group attempted to help former Maj.

Kenyans across the country have expressed outrage following the recent revelations of disputed elections. The news of irregularities and discrepancies in the electoral process has sparked widespread anger and frustration among the citizens.The disputed election revelations have further deepened the political divide in the country, with many calling for accountability and transparency in the electoral process. The accusations of electoral fraud have raised concerns about the credibility and fairness of the democratic system in Kenya.Political leaders and activists have been vocal in their condemnation of the disputed election revelations, calling for immediate action to address the issues and ensure a free and fair electoral process in the future. Many are demanding a thorough investigation into the allegations and accountability for those responsible for any wrongdoing.As the country grapples with the aftermath of the disputed election revelations, it remains to be seen how the government will respond to the growing calls for transparency and accountability. Kenyans are hopeful that their voices will be heard and that steps will be taken to uphold the integrity of the electoral process in the future.

In Haiti, front-running candidate Rene Preval has taken an early lead as election workers continue to count ballots from the national elections on Tuesday. Millions of Haitians turned out for the balloting, which is the first since former president Jean Bertrand Aristide was forced into exile following a violent uprising two years ago.Ballots keep trickling in to the capital, transported from the rural areas by helicopter, truck and mules.Preliminary figures give the front-runner candidate Rene Preval an early lead in the race for president. A 63-year old agronomist and former president, Preval is one of the few Haitian leaders who served out his term without being overthrown. Seen as a close ally of former president Aristide, Preval gained widespread support among Haiti's poor.If Preval gets fifty-percent of the vote, he will win the presidency in the first round. So far, only 25 percent of the votes have been counted. Final results are not expected until Friday at the earliest.Around the capital, Port-au-Prince, election monitors praised the patience and resiliency of Haitian voters. Jean-Pierre Kingsly is Director General of the International Mission for the Evaluation of Elections.Mr. Kingsly said that many polling stations opened hours late due to lack of materials, causing confusion and long lines for Haitian voters. He said these problems would have to be resolved for the next round of parliamentary elections, on March 19.

The popularity of Broadway musicals is on the rise in China, with an increasing demand for these shows among Chinese audiences. Over the past few years, numerous Broadway productions have toured major cities in China, drawing large crowds and receiving positive reviews.One of the most recent successes was the production of "The Phantom of the Opera," which made its debut in Beijing and Shanghai to sold-out performances. The classic musical captivated audiences with its stunning sets, elaborate costumes, and powerful performances by the talented cast.Other popular Broadway musicals that have made their way to China include "Les Misérables," "Cats," and "Wicked." These shows have gained a loyal following in the country, attracting both seasoned theatergoers and newcomers to the world of musical theater.The growing demand for Broadway musicals in China can be attributed to a variety of factors, including the increasing affluence of Chinese consumers, a growing appreciation for Western culture, and the rise of social media platforms that allow audiences to discover and share information about these shows.As the appetite for Broadway musicals continues to grow in China, it is expected that more productions will make their way to the country, offering audiences a chance to experience the magic of live theater and the thrill of watching world-class performances. With the success of recent shows, it is clear that Broadway musicals have found a new home in the hearts of Chinese theatergoers.

As Malawi?s elections come closer [May 19], the local business community has given presidential candidates some advice about private enterprise and how it can thrive.Business leaders say Malawians are eager for development, and local businesses do not want to waste time, or elect corrupt officials.Mike Mlombwa, the president of the Indigenous Business Association of Malawi, said the next government should empower local businesses.Mlombwa said the time has come for locals to take a leading role in controlling the economy rather than foreigners. ?What we are saying is that there are a lot of foreigners here running businesses that are supposed to be done by locals. For example, some of them are running restaurants, car [rentals], spare parts shops, [and] stationary shops which are supposed to be run by locals,? he says.He says his association has been appealing to its members to vote for leaders who would have the interests of local businesses at heart.Mlombwa says the major problem is what he calls the country?

Scientists have developed the first blood test to diagnose major depression.The landmark test could give psychiatrists a more objective way to identify different types of depression in their patients, and would be especially helpful in designing treatments for depression in highly-vulnerable teenagers. The blood test is not designed to determine whether a teen is at risk for the condition or already suffers from major depression. That's something psychiatrists can do, using traditional subjective analysis, during their patients' office visits. Rather, says Eva Redei, a professor of psychiatry at Northwestern University's Feinberg School of Medicine in Illinois, the new blood test is the first to identify the many different types of major depression, from bipolar disorder to depression driven by severe anxiety. She says this additional information can help doctors tailor therapies for each patient."The test probably can identify subgroups of depressed patients, which is the aim in define[ing] the best treatment," Redei says.Statistics show that depressed teenagers are at particularly high risk of suicide, so there is an urgent need for a test that can zero in on the precise nature of their depression and suggest the most appropriate treatments.Redei had previously discovered 26 genetic markers, or substances in the blood, that appear to be elevated in depression. She says everyone has the markers, which she likens to those that indicate levels of blood sugar and cholesterol, and the depression markers can be in the normal range or elevated.That's what Redei and her colleagues discovered in their study of blood markers in a group of 14 male and female adolescents, aged 15 to 19. They had been diagnosed with, but not yet treated for, major depression.The study also included 14 healthy volunteers.

Indonesian authorities have recently launched a crackdown on child political campaigners, raising concerns about the rights and safety of young individuals involved in politics. The move comes after reports surfaced of children being exploited and manipulated for political gain by various political parties in the country.According to sources, children as young as 10 years old have been seen distributing flyers, attending rallies, and even making speeches on behalf of politicians. This has sparked outrage among child rights advocates, who argue that children should not be used as tools in political campaigns.In response to the growing issue, Indonesian officials have announced stricter regulations and enforcement measures to protect children from being involved in politics. Authorities are urging political parties to comply with the regulations and ensure that children are not used for political purposes.Child political campaigners face risks of exploitation, physical harm, and psychological trauma, as they are often thrust into environments beyond their maturity level.

In a surprising move, Angolan President João Lourenço recently sat down for a meeting with some of the nation's most vocal critics. The meeting, which took place at the presidential palace in Luanda, was seen as a step towards bridging the divide between the government and its detractors.Among those present at the meeting were opposition leaders, human rights activists, and journalists known for their critical reporting on the government. The discussions reportedly covered a wide range of topics, including political reforms, human rights issues, and the country's economic challenges.President Lourenço's decision to engage with his critics has been met with both praise and skepticism. Some see it as a positive sign of openness and willingness to listen to different viewpoints, while others question the president's motives and whether real change will result from these discussions.The meeting comes at a time of increasing pressure on the Angolan government to address issues of corruption, political repression, and economic inequality. As one of Africa's largest oil producers, Angola has struggled to diversify its economy and address widespread poverty and unemployment.While it remains to be seen what concrete actions will come out of the meeting, many are hopeful that it signals a new era of dialogue and cooperation in Angola. President Lourenço's willingness to engage with his critics is seen as a step towards building trust and fostering a more inclusive political environment in the country.

The Dominican Republic's border patrol recently repatriated a group of undocumented Haitians who were attempting to enter the country illegally. The operation took place near the border between the two countries, as authorities continue their efforts to crack down on illegal immigration.According to the border patrol, the Haitian nationals were apprehended while trying to cross into the Dominican Republic without the necessary documentation. They were promptly detained and then escorted back to Haiti, where they were handed over to Haitian authorities.This is not the first time that the Dominican border patrol has repatriated undocumented individuals. The government has been implementing stricter border control measures in recent years in an attempt to reduce illegal immigration and ensure national security.The repatriation of the undocumented Haitians has sparked debates and concerns among human rights organizations, who argue that the individuals should be given a fair chance to apply for legal status and asylum.

U.S. authorities say high winds have pushed a dangerous wildfire toward a city in the southwestern state of Arizona, forcing more people to evacuate their homes. Some 1,000 emergency personnel are battling the fire, which jumped over a highway late Sunday, propelled by winds gusting up to nearly 100 kilometers an hour. The highway had served as a containment line protecting the nearby city of Sierra Vista. Authorities ordered additional evacuations, raising the number of people displaced in recent days to almost 10,000. At least 44 homes have been destroyed. No serious injuries have been reported. Forecasters predicted winds would moderate on Monday. By Sunday night, authorities said the blaze was 27 percent contained.Elsewhere, more than 3,000 firefighters were trying to stop a larger wildfire that has burned in eastern Arizona since May 29. Authorities said that blaze was nearly half contained. The Arizona wildfires are among several burning in spots across the southwestern United States. The U.S.

The number of migrants attempting to reach Britain by crossing the English Channel has been on the rise in recent months. Despite the dangerous journey and harsh weather conditions, many individuals are still making the risky decision to travel by small boats in search of a better life.According to the UK Home Office, over 1,500 migrants have attempted to make the crossing since the start of the year. This marks a significant increase compared to the same period last year, when only a few hundred migrants attempted the journey.The increasing number of migrants attempting to reach Britain via the English Channel has raised concerns among authorities about the safety and security of both the migrants and the public. The journey is extremely risky, with many migrants risking their lives in overcrowded and unseaworthy boats.Authorities are urging migrants to avoid attempting the dangerous crossing and to seek legal and safe routes to enter the country. They are also working to increase patrols and surveillance in the English Channel to prevent further crossings and ensure the safety of those attempting the journey.The issue of illegal migration via the English Channel continues to be a challenging one for authorities, with no easy solution in sight. As the number of migrants attempting the crossing continues to rise, it is clear that more needs to be done to address the root causes of migration and provide safer alternatives for those seeking asylum in the UK.

A Bosnian immigrant accused in a subway bombing terrorism plot has gone on trial in federal court in New York City. He faces life in prison if convicted on all nine counts, including conspiring to use weapons of mass destruction.A federal jury in Brooklyn is hearing the terrorism case against 27-year-old Bosnian-born Adis Medunjanin, who allegedly conspired with two former high school friends to bomb New York subways in 2009.Medunjanin is accused of nine counts of terrorism, including receiving bomb-making training in Pakistan with Najibullah Zazi and Zarein Ahmedzay, both of whom have pleaded guilty and are cooperating with federal prosecutors.The case opened Monday with a day of testimony from Ahmedzay, who described traveling to Pakistan with Medunjanin and Zazi intending to fight with the Taliban against American forces in Afghanistan. Once there, he said, they were taken instead to an al-Qaida training camp in northwestern Pakistan and urged to carry out a suicide mission in the United States.

The African Union (AU) is facing challenges in trying to finalize a peace deal in the conflict-ridden region of Darfur. Negotiations have been ongoing for months, but a final agreement has yet to be reached.The conflict in Darfur has been ongoing for years, with violence and instability plaguing the region. The AU has been working tirelessly to bring all parties to the table and facilitate discussions aimed at achieving a lasting peace agreement.However, issues such as power-sharing, wealth distribution, and security arrangements continue to be major sticking points in the negotiations. The various parties involved in the conflict have differing priorities and demands, making it difficult to find common ground.Despite the challenges, the AU remains committed to its efforts to secure a peace deal in Darfur. The organization is working closely with international partners and stakeholders to push for progress in the negotiations and ensure that all parties are committed to a peaceful resolution of the conflict.The situation in Darfur remains complex and volatile, but the AU is hopeful that a peace deal can be reached in the near future. The organization is urging all parties to prioritize the interests of the people of Darfur and work towards a peaceful and inclusive solution to the conflict.

Consumers in East Africa are anxiously waiting for a new high-speed fiber-optic cable to finally be switched on for general public use. The cable is expected to usher in a new era of faster, cheaper internet access. As VOA reports from our Nairobi bureau, the new fiber-optic cable and the planned addition of other cables, are anxiously awaited in the last major region in the world to rely on satellite Internet. For more than a year now potential customers in East Africa have heard of the Seacom cable with a mix of wary caution and hopeful relief. In a country like Kenya, where citizens have been disappointed by undelivered political promises and an undeveloped infrastructure, much of the public took a wait-and-see approach to the reports that high-speed Internet would soon be available. Although the cable is now "live," the public is still awaiting the broadband revolution to reach their computers. Now that the question of "if" has been answered, the question of "when" is still hanging in the air.According to a regional spokesman for Seacom, Solomon Mahindi, as of the end of last week only three Internet service providers in Kenya could be confirmed to have finished negotiations with Seacom to get connected to the cable. Managing Director of Internet Services Chris Senanu of Access Kenya, one of the country's major Internet service providers, said although his company has finalized access to the cable, it will probably be another two weeks or so before the high-speed fiber optic line will be available to its consumers. "If during the tests we have some major issues, then obviously we are not going to put it right to the public," Senanu said.

Former attorney for President Donald Trump, Michael Cohen, has pleaded guilty to a new charge in federal court. Cohen admitted to lying to Congress about a real estate deal that Trump's company was pursuing in Russia during the 2016 presidential campaign. This new charge adds to the list of crimes that Cohen has already pleaded guilty to, including tax evasion, bank fraud, and campaign finance violations. Cohen's cooperation with special counsel Robert Mueller's investigation into Russian interference in the election has led to speculation about potential legal troubles for Trump and his inner circle. The plea deal also comes amid reports that Trump's legal team is preparing written answers to questions from Mueller's team. Cohen is scheduled to be sentenced in December and could face significant jail time for his crimes. This latest development is just another twist in the ongoing saga of legal troubles surrounding Trump's former associates.

Zimbabwe has made a significant move by deciding to switch its reserve currency from the US dollar to the Chinese yuan. This decision comes as the country continues to grapple with economic challenges and a shortage of foreign currency.The move was announced by Zimbabwe's Finance Minister during a press conference, where he stated that the decision was made in order to strengthen economic ties with China and reduce dependence on the US dollar. The minister also highlighted that using the yuan as a reserve currency would provide the country with more stability and help to stabilize the economy.While the switch to the yuan is a bold move, some experts are cautious about the implications it may have on Zimbabwe's economy. There are concerns about the volatility of the Chinese currency and its impact on the country's financial stability.Despite these concerns, the Zimbabwean government remains optimistic about the switch and believes that it will lead to more opportunities for economic growth and development. Only time will tell if this decision proves to be beneficial for the country in the long run.

WASHINGTON - Despite last month’s suspected attack on the U.S. State Department’s email system and website, there’s "no reason to believe classified information was compromised," a spokesman said.The department had revealed that a portion of its email system and public website were shut down over the weekend – weeks after authorities detected suspicious activity believed to be the work of an outside hacker."The State Department, like any other large organization that has a global span, is a constant target of cyberattacks," spokesman Jeff Rathke told reporters Monday at a routine daily briefing.He said the department, after detecting "activity of concern several weeks ago," developed a response with cybersecurity experts from the Department of Homeland Security and other agencies and is "implementing carefully planned improvements to the security of our main unclassified network."Rathke, in explaining why parts of the email system weren’t disconnected until this weekend, said the action was part of previously planned maintenance.He said he had no information to share on the source of the attack, which remains under investigation.The intrusion happened about the same time the White House announced several of its unclassified computer systems were hacked into by an outside intruder. In that case, officials were quick to lay blame on Russian hackers, possibly working in cooperation with the Kremlin.Cyber-attacks on U.S. and Western governmental and military assets have been on the uptick lately.Just in the last few weeks, Chinese hackers reportedly dug into 800,000 employee records at the U.S.

PARIS - French President Emmanuel Macron visited the graffiti-damaged Arc de Triomphe monument and held an emergency meeting on security Sunday, a day after central Paris was hit by France's worst riot in a generation. Macron, who was meeting with his prime minister and interior and environment ministers, has vowed that those responsible for the violence and the damages will pay for their actions. His tour of France's beloved monument came just hours after he flew back from the G-20 summit in Argentina. Macron paid tribute to the Unknown Soldier from World War I whose tomb is under the monument. He then headed to a nearby avenue where activists wearing yellow jackets had torched cars, smashed windows, looted stores and battled police on Saturday. There he met with firefighters, police officers and restaurant owners. Paris police said Sunday that 133 people had been injured and 412 had been arrested as protesters trashed the streets of the capital during a demonstration Saturday against rising taxes and the high cost of living.Burned cars sit in a street of Paris, Dec. 2, 2018, a day after clashes during a protest of yellow vests (gilets jaunes) against rising oil prices and living costs.Charred cars, broken windows and downed fences from the riot littered many of the city's most popular tourist areas on Sunday, including major avenues near the Arc de Triomphe, streets around the famed Champs-Elysees Avenue, and the Tuileries garden. Graffiti was also sprayed on many stores and buildings.

BEIRUT/EASTERN GHOUTA, SYRIA - It's the dead children that still haunt Abu Ghassan, who was blinded for more than a month and paralyzed for weeks by a nerve gas attack four years ago in a Damascus suburb. He recovered; 37 members of his family were among the hundreds of dead.Last week, when another gas attack killed at least 87 people hundreds of miles to the north, the memories rushed back, hard.When he learned of it, he wept "like a child," the 50-year-old recalls in Ain Tarma, one of three towns hit by poison gas in 2013 in areas near Damascus collectively known as the Ghouta.Last week's attack in the northern town of Khan Sheikhoun was the first time Western countries say the government of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad again used the banned nerve gas sarin since the attack four years ago in the Ghouta.Damascus denies it was to blame for either attack, but the diplomatic effects of both were dramatic. Four years ago, the United States nearly bombed the Syrian government, only to pull back when Assad agreed to give up his chemical arsenal and submit to U.N. inspections.After last week's attack, President Donald Trump fired U.S. cruise missiles at Syrian government targets for the first time.Remaining missile fragments from attacks in 2013 are shown in the Ghouta town of Ain Tarma, Syria, April 7, 2017.Survivors of the Ghouta attack four years ago never lost the fear they could be gassed again at any moment, said Amer Zaydan, a 28-year-old school director from another part of eastern Ghouta. The new strike hammered it home."After the Khan Sheikhoun massacre, we've gone back to that first moment, as if we are the ones who went through it," he said.

ROME - U.S. President Donald Trump welcomed NATO support on its condemnation of Syria's use of chemical weapons on its own people last week.Trump and NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg spoke to reporters after meeting at the White House Wednesday.Trump called the attack, blamed on Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, a "murderous attack, using the most horrible weapons," and said "the barbaric killing of small and helpless children and babies must be forcefully rejected by any nation that values human life."Stoltenberg said any use of chemical weapons is unacceptable and cannot go unanswered. He said whoever is responsible for the attack must be held accountable.Earlier, European officials blamed a lack of clarity about U.S. and British policy toward Syria for their opposition this week to the imposition of new punitive measures against Russian and Syrian military officials.German Foreign Minister Sigmar Gabriel (L), Britain's Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson (C), and U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson attend roundtable talks during a G-7 for foreign ministers in Lucca, Italy, April 11, 2017.The officials said it remains unclear how the sanctions would change Moscow’s backing of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad.British Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson, who’s been leading the Anglo-American effort, took comfort after the G7 meeting of foreign ministers in the broad recognition secured at the gathering that there’s no solution to the crisis in Syria while Assad remains in power.He has emphasized targeted sanctions are still on the table, but moving forward with them will have to wait for a full investigation into the alleged April 4 Sarin Gas attack on the rebel-held town of Khan Sheikhoun.Group photo during a G-7 for foreign ministers meeting in Lucca, Italy, April 11, 2017.“

The Malian government has officially resigned as of October 12, 2002. The reasons for the resignation have not been disclosed, but it comes amidst a period of political instability in the country. President Amadou Toumani Touré accepted the resignation of Prime Minister Mandé Sidibé and his cabinet. The President is expected to appoint an interim government until new elections can be held. This news has raised concerns among the citizens of Mali, who are hoping for a smooth transition of power and stability in the government.

The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) is set to begin its annual meeting today in Monrovia, Liberia. This gathering will bring together leaders from the 15 member countries to discuss important economic and political issues facing the region.Key topics on the agenda include the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the region's economies, as well as efforts to promote regional integration and trade among member states. The leaders will also discuss the ongoing security challenges in the region, including the threat of terrorism and insurgency.Liberian President George Weah will host the meeting, which is expected to attract high-level officials and representatives from across West Africa. The discussions and decisions made at this summit will have a significant impact on the future of the region and its economic development.ECOWAS was established in 1975 with the goal of promoting economic cooperation and integration among its member states. The organization has played a key role in addressing regional challenges and promoting peace and stability in West Africa.As the meeting gets underway, all eyes will be on the leaders gathered in Monrovia as they work towards solutions to the pressing issues facing the region. Stay tuned for updates on the outcomes of the ECOWAS summit.

Argentina's President Mauricio Macri plans to report Venezuela's government to the International Criminal Court at The Hague for alleged crimes against humanity, according to an interview broadcast on CNN's Spanish service on Sunday night.Macri said he would seek to refer socialist President Nicolas Maduro's government "in the coming weeks", and that he had the backing of the presidents of Colombia, Chile and Paraguay."For me, there is no doubt: In Venezuela, human rights are systematically violated by steamrolling the opposition and everyone. There is a growing sense that we need to take more forceful action," he said, according to a transcript of the interview carried by Miami's Nuevo Herald, in which CNN interviewer Andrés Oppenheimer has a column."Maduro has not changed at all. I am not optimistic in the short term of what is going to happen in Venezuela," Macri added.His comments came as Maduro announced a single exchange rate pegged to his government's petro cryptocurrency, effectively devaluing it by 96 percent in a move economists said would fan hyperinflation in the chaotic country.Venezuela's President Nicolas Maduro holds a bank note from the new Venezuelan currency Bolivar Soberano (Sovereign Bolivar), as he speaks during a meeting with ministers at Miraflores Palace in Caracas, Aug. 17, 2018.

IZMIR, TURKEY - Last year, this part of the city was teeming with smugglers leaning over card tables, brokering deals over small glasses of tea.Men, women and children who were fleeing hardships arrived in the seaside city in droves with every dollar they could muster, ready to pay for their services.But after the European Union-Turkey plan to reduce migrant flows to Europe went into effect last month, the rubber-boat business here has nearly ground to a halt.Refugees and locals say Europe's plan to increase security by cutting migrant routes is deeply flawed, and pressure is building in Turkey. Europe may not want more refugees, they say, but it opened the floodgates and Turkey alone cannot shut the door or contain the crisis.Haesam, a Syrian refugee in Izmir with no plans to travel farther, says Europe may shut down this exact route, but fleeing Syrians are increasingly desperate as they wait to see what happens next.Cafes in Basmane, in Izmir, Turkey, that last year were packed with smugglers and travelers trying to get to Europe, now are almost empty as local businesses catering to refugees fail, April 6, 2016. (H. Murdock/VOA)"The people who are planning to travel to Europe these days are in hiding," he said. "They are afraid smugglers will convince them of new routes to Europe and then ship them to refugee camps."And the tens of thousands of people who were blocked from crossing into Turkey from Syria earlier this year have not simply gone home, he adds.

TAIPEI - China will keep working toward a stronger friendship with the Philippines, which it sees as a valuable new ally in Southeast Asia, despite the Philippine president’s comment that Beijing’s top leader once threatened war over a disputed tract of sea, analysts say.Duterte told a Philippine coast guard unit on May 19 that Chinese President Xi Jinping this month had threatened “war” if the Philippines “forced the issue” over sovereignty of the Spratly Islands, an archipelago of tiny features in the South China Sea, according to widespread media reports in the Philippines.“They cannot say anything either way, because it would look bad for them,” said Jay Batongbacal, director of the Institute for Maritime Affairs and Law of the Sea at the University of the Philippines in Metro Manila. “So I think it’s difficult for them and probably (they) won’t say anything and just avoid it altogether.”Beijing claims about 90 percent of the 3.5-million-square-kilometer South China Sea, including the Spratlys. The claim overlaps the exclusive economic zones of the Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia and Vietnam. Xi and Duterte were talking about oil drilling in the Spratlys at a meeting in Beijing when Xi made the comments, Philippine media say.China probably did not want the war comment disclosed and political experts say Beijing may speak more cautiously with Duterte’s government in the future.But analysts say Beijing will avoid any retaliation for the disclosure because it sees Duterte trying to improve relations after a stormy past. The president has thundered against China’s major global rival and former Philippine colonizer, the United States. He has visited China twice, and the two sides sat down this month to start working out their maritime differences.Last week Duterte traveled to Russia to sign a series of agreements.

Russia has criticized Estonia's recent decision to remove a statue of a Soviet soldier in the capital city of Tallinn. The statue, known as the "Bronze Soldier", has been a source of tension between Estonia and Russia for many years.Estonia's decision to remove the statue comes as part of a broader effort to distance itself from its Soviet past and promote its own national identity. The statue has long been seen as a symbol of Soviet oppression by many Estonians.However, Russia has condemned the decision, calling it a "provocation" and accusing Estonia of attempting to rewrite history. The Russian government has warned that the removal of the statue could further strain relations between the two countries.The Bronze Soldier statue has been a focal point for both pro-Russian and pro-Estonian demonstrations over the years. Its removal has sparked mixed reactions within Estonia, with some welcoming the decision as a step towards reconciliation, while others view it as an erasure of history.Despite the controversy surrounding the removal of the statue, Estonia has defended its decision as a necessary step towards confronting its past and asserting its independence. The Estonian government has stated that the statue will be relocated to a military cemetery, where it will be properly maintained and respected.As tensions continue to simmer between Russia and Estonia, the fate of the Bronze Soldier statue remains a contentious issue that highlights the complex historical and political relationship between the two countries.

View slideshow of Wyland and his work (Best viewed in Internet Explorer)This is American Profiles, VOA's weekly spotlight on Americans who have made a difference in how we think, live and act. Rosanne Skirble features Wyland, an artist and activist whose monumental murals have helped promote ocean conservation and marine life protection efforts. He is known by a single name: Wyland. He was born in 1956 and grew up in Detroit, the automotive capital of the United States. But it was his hero, ocean explorer Jacques Cousteau - whose adventures Wyland watched on television during the 1960s and '70s - that inspired him as a boy. "He had such a passion for the ocean that he instilled in a whole generation of people like myself," Wyland says. Wyland saw the ocean for the first time on a family vacation to California when he was 14 years old. "I remember seeing the Pacific Ocean in Laguna Beach and just immersed myself in it," he says. "It felt like I was being reborn." Ironically, when he emerged, a huge gray whale spouted right in front of him. He says the sight of it changed his life. Ten years after that first whale encounter, Wyland moved to Laguna Beach and struggled to make his living as an artist. "I felt that if I created good art, good things would happen," he says.And Wyland thought big.

The United States has recently demanded answers from Cuba regarding the imprisonment of several dissidents in the country. The US government has expressed deep concern over the situation and called for the immediate release of political prisoners in Cuba.The demand for answers comes as the Biden administration seeks to address human rights issues in Cuba and hold the Cuban government accountable for its treatment of political dissidents. The US State Department has urged Cuba to respect the rights of its citizens and allow freedom of expression.Several prominent dissidents, including members of the San Isidro Movement, have been imprisoned in Cuba in recent months for speaking out against the government. The US government has condemned these actions and called for the release of all political prisoners in the country.The Biden administration has also expressed its commitment to promoting democracy and human rights in Cuba and will continue to push for accountability and transparency from the Cuban government. The US is calling on Cuba to address these concerns and take immediate action to release the imprisoned dissidents.

Ukrainian Prime Minister Arseniy Yatsenyuk is warning Russia that military intervention would mean war between the two neighbors, after the Russian parliament authorized the use of Russian troops in the southern Ukrainian region of Crimea.Acting Ukrainian President Oleksandr Turchynov said Saturday he has put the country's armed forces on combat alert following Saturday's vote in Russia. The approval of Russian President Vladimir Putin's request for military authorization made official what Ukraine's newly-established interim government has described as an ongoing deployment of Russian troops in Crimea, a Black Sea peninsula.The majority-Russian region, which became part of independent Ukraine when the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, has become the focus of turmoil that began months ago when Ukraine's now-ousted president Viktor Yanukovych rejected a deal with the European Union in favor of closer ties with Russia. The United Nations Security Council scheduled an emergency meeting on Ukraine Saturday, while European Union foreign ministers will hold emergency talks in Brussels on Monday.The situation has escalated into the biggest confrontation between Russia and the West since the Cold War.On Saturday, Ukraine's U.N. mission asked members of the Security Council to "do everything possible" to stop what it described as Russian "aggression" against Ukraine, calling Russia's actions a "severe violation of international law."U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon's spokesman said Saturday the U.N. chief is "gravely concerned about the deterioration of the situation" in Crimea and "reiterates his call for the full respect" for Ukraine's independence, sovereignty and territorial integrity. The spokesman said Mr. Ban would be speaking with President Putin soon about the situation.The United States has also urged Russia to respect Ukraine's territorial integrity. Russia's upper house of parliament recommended Saturday that Mr. Putin recall the Russian ambassador from Washington, a day after U.S. President Barack Obama warned of "costs" if Russia intervenes militarily in Ukraine.

After days of intense negotiations, delegates at the United Nations Climate Change Conference in Glasgow have finally reached a historic agreement. The deal, known as the Glasgow Climate Pact, aims to accelerate action on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and limiting global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius. Key points of the agreement include commitments to phase out coal use, increase financing for developing countries to cope with the impacts of climate change, and establish a mechanism for countries to regularly report on their progress towards meeting their emissions reduction targets. UN Secretary-General António Guterres praised the agreement as a "significant step forward" but also warned that much more work is needed to meet the goals of the Paris Agreement. The Glasgow Climate Pact will now be formally adopted by all 197 countries participating in the conference.

The United Nations Syria Envoy, Geir Pedersen, has stated that the threat posed by the Islamic State militant group may actually encourage local truces in the war-torn country. Pedersen made this statement during a briefing to the UN Security Council, where he emphasized the importance of finding ways to reduce violence and protect civilians.Pedersen pointed out that the ongoing conflict in Syria has created a fertile ground for groups like Islamic State to thrive, as well as exacerbating the suffering of the Syrian people. He suggested that local truces between warring factions could help to de-escalate tensions and provide much-needed relief for civilians caught in the crossfire.The UN envoy also stressed the need for a political solution to the conflict in Syria, emphasizing the importance of engaging all parties in dialogue and negotiations. He called on the international community to support efforts to bring an end to the violence and facilitate a peaceful resolution to the crisis.Overall, Pedersen's remarks highlight the complex challenges facing Syria and the urgent need for a concerted and coordinated effort to address them. As the country continues to grapple with violence and instability, finding ways to encourage local truces and advance the political process remains crucial for achieving peace and stability in the region.

The Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF) candidates have been actively promoting their plans to improve education and housing in the country. With the upcoming elections approaching, the candidates have been highlighting their commitment to investing in schools and providing affordable housing options for the people.One of the key focuses of the EPRDF candidates is to enhance the quality of education in Ethiopia. They have pledged to increase funding for schools, improve infrastructure, and ensure that every child has access to a quality education. By prioritizing education, the candidates aim to empower the youth and create a more educated and skilled workforce for the future.In addition to education, the EPRDF candidates have also been emphasizing the importance of affordable housing for all Ethiopians. They have proposed various initiatives to address the housing crisis, including building new housing developments, providing subsidies for low-income families, and implementing rent control measures. By prioritizing housing, the candidates hope to improve the living conditions of all citizens and reduce homelessness in the country.Overall, the EPRDF candidates are focused on addressing key issues facing Ethiopia, such as education and housing.

(Səsli faylı yükləmək üçün bu linkin üzərində saö düyməyə basıb, menyudan "Yadda saxla"nı seçin: MP3-ü yükləyin)A mentor is someone who teaches or advises a younger person with less experience. January is National Mentoring Month in the United States. Some mentoring programs connect adults with children who need help. Researchers say mentoring can reduce drug and alcohol use, and violence among young people. In Southern California, a nonprofit group called School on Wheels has volunteers who tutor children from homeless families. Experts estimate that one in every 50 children in the country is homeless at some time during the year. They might live in shelters or weekly hotels or abandoned buildings. They might live on the streets. They might live anywhere but a permanent home. Sinead Chilton is a marketing consultant for School on Wheels. She moved from London to the United States. She says she was surprised to learn how big the problem was. “When I first moved here ten years ago and I heard about School on Wheels, I didn’t even consider the fact that there were homeless children in America. And then when I realized that there were 1.6 million homeless children in America, it just floored me.” Ms. Chilton has worked with School on Wheels for more than six years. She still remembers the first student she tutored. She met with her every week at a local church. The church gave shelter to people on winter nights. The girl slept on the floor. “It just struck me as just so devastating and just how can you focus on school and how can you even think about homework when those are the circumstances that you’re living in?” A retired teacher named Agnes Stevens started School on Wheels eight years ago. She wanted to help homeless children stay in school. The group now has more than 1000 volunteers. Each year they tutor as many as 2000 students.

A group of non-governmental organizations (NGOs) in Zimbabwe are seeking to have a role in the ongoing talks to resolve the post-electoral crisis in the country.The NGOs have expressed their concerns over the current political situation in Zimbabwe following the disputed election results, which have led to widespread protests and unrest.They believe that their involvement in the talks is crucial in order to ensure that the interests of the people are represented and that a peaceful resolution can be reached.The NGOs are calling on the government and other parties involved in the talks to include them in the negotiations and to listen to their recommendations for a way forward.The post-electoral crisis in Zimbabwe has raised concerns both domestically and internationally, with many calling for a peaceful and democratic resolution to the situation.The involvement of NGOs in the talks could potentially help bridge the gap between the government and the opposition, leading to a more inclusive and transparent process.It remains to be seen whether the government and other parties will agree to the NGOs' request to participate in the talks, but their voices are being heard and their presence could be instrumental in finding a peaceful solution to the crisis.

In Yemen, it is legal and common for young girls to marry fully-grown men. But a growing activist movement trying to abolish the practice won a small victory Saturday, when 12-year-old Sally al-Sahabi was granted a divorce from her 26-year-old husband.Sally's little brother held some of the many television mics in the packed, sweltering courthouse Saturday. Activists and journalists crowded around the table where a judge questioned her and her husband, Nabil al-Marshahi. After four months of begging her husband and petitioning human rights groups, Sally was finally getting her divorce.Anti-child-bride activists looked jubilant when the judge pronounced the couple divorced. But Shadda Nasser, Sally's lawyer later said that the only reason the divorce went through was because Nabil had requested it. Sally was beaten and raped as a 10-year-old bride, but her December 2009 petition for divorce had failed.Nasser says this divorce is a win for a growing movement in Yemen to end the practice of early marriage. A law that would make 17 the minimum age for marriage in Yemen has been languishing in parliamentary committees for over a year now, and is expected to be voted on again in the coming weeks. "I am very happy because Sally, now she is free. And I am happy because I have new evidence for the parliament and President Saleh to look again for this law, and to help all the children in Yemen," said the lawyer.Yemen's president, Ali Abdullah Saleh, and his ruling party support the law.

Liberian President George Weah expressed shock and sadness following the murder of a government official in Monrovia on Tuesday. The victim, identified as Emmanuel Barten Nyeswua, was the deputy director-general of the National Bureau of Concessions.Nyeswua was reportedly shot in his car by unknown assailants while on his way home from work. The motive behind the attack remains unclear, and authorities have launched an investigation to determine the circumstances surrounding the murder.President Weah condemned the killing and vowed to ensure that those responsible are brought to justice. He described Nyeswua as a dedicated public servant and expressed his condolences to the family and colleagues of the deceased.The murder of Nyeswua has sent shockwaves through Liberia, where incidents of violence against government officials are relatively rare. The country has struggled with political instability and corruption in recent years, and this latest incident has raised concerns about the safety of public servants.The government has called for calm and urged the public to cooperate with law enforcement agencies to help solve the case. As the investigation continues, the people of Liberia are left mourning the loss of a respected official and demanding justice for his senseless killing.

Archaeologists studying mummies from the 18th century have made a fascinating discovery that could shed light on the origins of colon cancer. The mummies, found in a crypt in Hungary, showed signs of possible colon cancer, making them some of the oldest known cases of the disease.Researchers believe that the prevalence of colon cancer in these mummies could provide valuable insight into how the disease has evolved over time. By studying the genetic material of the mummies, scientists hope to uncover more information about the genetic mutations that contribute to colon cancer.This groundbreaking discovery highlights the importance of studying ancient remains in order to better understand the history of diseases like cancer. As technology continues to advance, researchers will likely be able to glean even more information from these well-preserved mummies, offering new insights into the origins of colon cancer and potentially leading to new treatments in the future.

A doctor in eastern Congo has contracted Ebola, marking a worrisome development in the ongoing outbreak in the region, according to the World Health Organization (WHO). This incident represents a "dreaded" scenario, as health workers are at high risk of contracting the virus while treating patients.The doctor, who has not been named, is receiving care and treatment in Butembo, a city in North Kivu province where the outbreak has been concentrated. It is believed that the doctor was exposed to the virus while caring for a patient at a hospital in the area.This new case highlights the challenges that health workers face in containing the Ebola outbreak, which has been ongoing in eastern Congo since August 2018. The region has faced numerous obstacles in controlling the spread of the virus, including ongoing conflict and community mistrust of healthcare workers.The WHO has called for increased support and resources to help contain the outbreak and protect healthcare workers on the frontlines. The organization has also emphasized the importance of community engagement and education in preventing further spread of the virus.This latest development serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing threat posed by Ebola in eastern Congo, and the need for continued vigilance and collaboration in the fight against the virus.

Women entrepreneurs in Africa face significant challenges when it comes to connecting with resources and support to help their businesses thrive. Despite their hard work and innovation, many women-owned businesses struggle to access the networks, funding, and mentorship needed to grow and succeed.One of the main barriers for women entrepreneurs in Africa is the lack of access to financial resources. Many women lack collateral to secure loans, and the traditional banking system often overlooks their businesses. As a result, women are forced to rely on personal savings or informal lending options, limiting their ability to scale their businesses.In addition to financial challenges, women entrepreneurs also face difficulties in accessing networks and mentorship opportunities. Many women lack the connections and support needed to navigate the business world effectively. Without access to mentorship and guidance, women may struggle to overcome obstacles and reach their full potential.Despite these challenges, women entrepreneurs in Africa are resilient and resourceful. Many are finding creative ways to overcome obstacles, such as forming women's business networks and seeking out alternative funding sources. With the right support and resources, women entrepreneurs have the potential to drive economic growth and create positive change in their communities.To address the barriers faced by women entrepreneurs in Africa, policymakers and stakeholders must work together to create an enabling environment that supports women's business development. This includes increasing access to financial resources, promoting mentorship programs, and fostering an inclusive entrepreneurial ecosystem that empowers women to thrive.By investing in women entrepreneurs, Africa can unlock the full potential of its economy and create a more prosperous future for all. Women entrepreneurs are essential drivers of innovation and growth, and by supporting their success, we can build a more inclusive and sustainable business landscape for the continent.

The German Cyber Agency has recently criticized Yahoo for failing to cooperate in a hacking probe. The agency had requested assistance from the tech company in investigating a cyber attack that targeted a German government agency.Despite repeated requests for information and cooperation, Yahoo has allegedly been unresponsive and uncooperative. This lack of cooperation has frustrated the agency's efforts to track down the hackers and prevent further cyber attacks.In response to Yahoo's lack of assistance, the German Cyber Agency has issued a public statement condemning the company's behavior. They have emphasized the importance of cooperation between tech companies and government agencies in order to combat cyber threats effectively.This incident highlights the ongoing challenges that law enforcement agencies face in investigating cyber crimes and securing sensitive data. It also raises questions about the level of responsibility that tech companies have in assisting with these investigations.Yahoo has yet to respond to the agency's criticisms. It remains to be seen whether they will comply with the agency's requests for assistance in the hacking probe.

Iran has sentenced a US journalist to eight years in prison, sparking international condemnation. The journalist, who has not been named publicly, was arrested in Iran last year on charges of espionage and spreading propaganda against the Iranian government.The US government and press freedom organizations have strongly criticized the verdict, calling it a blatant violation of freedom of speech. The journalist's lawyer has vowed to appeal the decision, arguing that the charges are baseless and politically motivated.Iran has a long history of cracking down on journalists and activists, especially those with ties to Western countries. The country's judiciary has often been accused of using vague and arbitrary charges to silence dissent and intimidate critics.The case has heightened tensions between Iran and the US, which have been at odds over a range of issues, including Iran's nuclear program and its support for militant groups in the Middle East. The US State Department has called on Iran to release the journalist immediately and respect freedom of the press.The journalist's family and colleagues have expressed shock and outrage over the verdict, describing the journalist as a dedicated professional who was simply doing their job. They have launched a campaign calling for the journalist's release and have urged the international community to pressure Iran to uphold basic human rights.

The US National Soccer Team is in Egypt for the FIFA Under-20 World Cup. The U.S. responded to their opening game loss to Germany with an explosion of goals against Cameroon, defeating the African nation 4-1. They lost to South Korea Friday night, 3-0, but can still advance to the second round. Four of the six third-place teams go on, and the Americans will find out Saturday whether they continue.The American team is strengthened by players from a variety of different backgrounds. More than half of the players on this year's American Under-20 team have at least one parent who immigrated to the US. Many are immigrants themselves.Peri Marosevic fled war-torn Bosnia at twoBosnian-American Peri Marosevic, 20, has already faced more adversity than the average person will encounter in a lifetime. As a small child, he left a war-torn homeland with his family. "When I was two - I had an older sister, she was five - my parents decided it would be best, having these little children, to move out," he recalls. "It was a tough situation for anybody to be there. You see, and you hear on the radio, that houses are being blown up, and you really do not want to be around that. My parents made a good decision," he says.Marosevic and his family settled in Chicago. The good student earned a scholarship to the University of Michigan.That story alone is one of tremendous achievement against long odds. But Marosevic's success does not end there. Like so many American children of immigrants from soccer-loving nations, Marosevic retained a love for the game ubiquitous in his home country.Eventually his skill on the field drew attention from scouts for the U.S. Soccer Federation.

A new opposition party has emerged on the political scene, vowing to use any means necessary to topple the ruling party in the upcoming elections. The leader of the party made this bold declaration during a press conference held yesterday, where he outlined his vision for a new direction for the country.The party has quickly gained traction among disillusioned voters who are seeking change and are tired of the current government's policies. With a platform focused on tackling corruption, improving the economy, and addressing social issues, the new opposition party is positioning itself as a viable alternative to the ruling party.Political analysts are keeping a close eye on the developments, as the upcoming elections are expected to be highly contested. With the new opposition party's promise to use any means necessary to achieve their goal, the political landscape is set to become even more competitive and unpredictable in the months leading up to the elections.

The US military has announced the success of recent anti-IS (Islamic State) strikes in Afghanistan. According to a statement released by the Pentagon, the airstrikes targeted key ISIS positions in the country, resulting in significant losses for the terrorist group.The US military hailed the operation as a major victory in the ongoing fight against terrorism in the region. The strikes were carried out in coordination with Afghan security forces, who provided crucial intelligence and ground support.The Pentagon emphasized that the US remains committed to eliminating ISIS and other extremist groups from Afghanistan. The success of these recent strikes is a testament to the effectiveness of the US military's counterterrorism efforts in the region.While the fight against terrorism in Afghanistan is far from over, the US military's recent operation has dealt a significant blow to ISIS and sends a strong message that they will not tolerate the presence of extremist groups in the country.

The Space Shuttle Endeavour has landed after completing an 11-day mission to expand living space aboard the International Space Station. VOA's Brian Wagner reports from Miami. Endeavour touched down at Edwards Air Force Base in California, after bad weather forced officials to divert the landing from the Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

Europe and Russia have announced a joint mission to search for signs of life on Mars. The European Space Agency (ESA) and Russia's Roscosmos space agency will collaborate on the ExoMars mission, which aims to drill into the Martian surface to search for evidence of past or present life.The mission will include a rover equipped with a drill capable of reaching depths of up to 6.5 feet below the surface, where scientists believe any potential signs of life may be preserved. The rover will also carry a suite of scientific instruments to analyze the samples it collects.This joint mission marks the latest effort in the search for extraterrestrial life on Mars, following previous missions by NASA and other space agencies. The European and Russian space agencies hope that their collaboration will increase the chances of discovering evidence of life on the Red Planet.The ExoMars mission is scheduled to launch in 2022, with the rover expected to arrive on Mars in 2023.

SANTIAGO, CHILE - The archbishop of the Chilean capital went before a local prosecutor Wednesday but declined to testify as a defendant about allegations he covered up years of child sex abuse committed by clerics and officials of the country's Catholic Church.Television images showed a smiling Cardinal Ricardo Ezzati walking into the prosecutor's office in the city of Rancagua wearing a Roman collar while heavily guarded by police officers and surrounded by journalists. His lawyer said Ezzati used his right to remain silent about allegations he covered up abuse committed by his top deputy, the Rev. Oscar Munoz."The cardinal was summoned as a defendant accused of being part of covering up serious crimes," attorney Hugo Rivera said."There is no serious element that prompted [prosecutors] to summon him as a defendant,""We want to discuss with transparency all the elements so society can fully see the evidence at a public hearing," Rivera added.The investigation is based on evidence collected by Chilean authorities during recent raids on the ecclesiastical court of the diocese of Santiago and other church buildings.Munoz has admitted to abusing at least one minor, and confessed to church authorities in December.

In a controversial move, Bosnian Serb authorities have detained a man who was leading protests over the death of his son. The man, whose identity has not been disclosed, has been a vocal advocate for justice following the death of his son in what he claims was a suspicious circumstance.The protests, which have been ongoing for weeks, have drawn attention to the lack of accountability in the region's justice system. Many have criticized the detention of the man as a violation of freedom of speech and peaceful assembly.The man's family and supporters have expressed concern for his well-being while in custody and are calling for his immediate release. The authorities have not provided any information on the charges against him or the reasons for his detention.The situation remains tense as protests continue in support of the detained man and calls for justice for his son. The international community has also expressed concern over the situation and urged Bosnian Serb authorities to respect human rights and due process.This is a developing story and updates will be provided as more information becomes available.

After months of searching, crews have reportedly found remains believed to be that of a victim from the 2014 South Korea ferry disaster. The disaster, which occurred on April 16, 2014, resulted in the deaths of 304 people, most of whom were high school students on a field trip.The discovery was made in waters near the island of Jindo, where the ferry sank after it capsized. The remains were found during a search operation conducted by the South Korean coast guard.The families of the victims have been notified of the discovery, and DNA testing will be conducted to confirm the identity of the remains. The search for additional victims continues, as there are still nine people unaccounted for from the disaster.The South Korea ferry disaster remains one of the deadliest maritime accidents in the country's history, and the discovery of these remains brings closure to the families of the victims who have been waiting for answers for nearly eight years.

Colin Kroll, a tech executive who was a co-founder of the popular apps HQ Trivia and Vine, was found dead Sunday in New York.Police said officers found the 34-year-old unresponsive in his apartment after receiving a call asking them to go check on him.Medical examiners are working to determine his cause of death.HQ Trivia launched in 2017 and became wildly popular, bringing users together for a nightly live game show that awarded cash prizes to winners.The show's host, Scott Rogowsky announced the company decided to cancel Sunday's game out of respect for Kroll.

The United Nations has warned that any potential expulsions of Eritrean staff members would severely impact its operations in the region. This statement comes amidst growing tensions between Eritrea and the international community, with accusations of human rights abuses and lack of cooperation. The UN relies heavily on its Eritrean staff members for their knowledge of the local language, culture, and customs, making their expulsion a significant blow to the organization's efforts in the region. The UN is calling for dialogue and cooperation to resolve the issues at hand and prevent any disruptions to its operations.

Environmental activist group Greenpeace has accused the Congolese government of being complicit in allowing illegal timber violations in the country. In a recent report, Greenpeace highlighted that a significant amount of timber exported from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is harvested illegally, leading to environmental destruction and loss of biodiversity.According to Greenpeace, the DRC's Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development has failed to effectively regulate and monitor the timber industry, resulting in rampant violations of logging regulations. The group also pointed out that the government has issued logging permits in protected areas, further exacerbating the situation.Greenpeace has called on the Congolese government to take immediate action to address these issues and strengthen enforcement of timber regulations. The group has also urged international buyers of Congolese timber to exercise due diligence and ensure that they are not contributing to illegal logging and deforestation in the region.The DRC is home to vast tracts of tropical rainforests, which are crucial for biodiversity conservation and climate regulation. The illegal logging activities highlighted by Greenpeace not only threaten the environment but also impact local communities who depend on the forests for their livelihoods.The Congolese government has yet to respond to Greenpeace's accusations. However, the report has brought renewed attention to the need for stronger enforcement of timber regulations in the DRC and the importance of sustainable forest management practices to protect the country's valuable natural resources.

After days of heavy rainfall, flooding in Mississippi appears to have reached its peak, with water levels expected to recede in the coming days. The state has been battling severe flooding, leading to road closures, evacuations, and damage to homes and infrastructure.Authorities have been working tirelessly to provide assistance to those affected by the flooding, with emergency services on high alert. Residents in the affected areas have been urged to stay vigilant and follow evacuation orders to ensure their safety.Despite the challenging conditions, communities have come together to support one another during this difficult time.
