The floodwaters may be receding, but the impact of the flooding will continue to be felt for some time as residents work to recover and rebuild.As the waters begin to recede, residents are encouraged to exercise caution and avoid any remaining floodwaters. The cleanup and recovery process will be ongoing, and support from local, state, and federal agencies will be crucial in helping the affected communities recover from this natural disaster.

It's clear at this point that even if the international climate accord being negotiated in suburban Paris becomes legally binding, it won't include punitive measures like trade sanctions or embargoes on straggler countries that fail to meet their commitments."The only penalty for falling short on efforts to fight global warming would be failing in front of the whole world to achieve their goals. "And that's not necessarily a bad thing, many analysts say. In international diplomacy, peer pressure and the risk of losing face can be powerful motivations for a country to keep a promise, particularly on a high-profile issue like climate change. "Meeting national emissions pledges will emerge as a key measure of international moral and diplomatic standing after a Paris agreement, with countries reluctant to flout their targets and risk being treated as pariahs,'' said Paul Bledsoe, a former Clinton White House climate adviser. Seeking transparency Some countries led by the European Union still insist that governments accept legally binding targets to reduce greenhouse gas pollution in the Paris agreement, which is supposed to be adopted at the end of this week. But that's a no-go for the U.S. for political reasons. So the negotiations are increasingly focused on creating transparency rules to determine whether countries actually follow through on their pledges. The idea is to ensure that even if the targets aren't binding internationally, countries would have a binding obligation to report on whether they are achieving their national goals, which could turn into a potentially humiliating experience if they're not. "Three words: name and shame,'' said Li Shuo, a climate policy expert at Greenpeace China.

China's top advisor on rural issues says protests and riots fell last year, but he warns discontent in the countryside is still a serious problem. As Daniel Schearf reports from Beijing, much of the problem is caused by local officials taking farmland for development.Chen Xiwen, director of China's office of the central leading group on rural work, said Tuesday that government spending had helped reduce demonstrations and uprisings against local governments.Chen says in 2006 incidents of unrest went down sharply, but he provided no figures on the number. The government said there were 87,000 such incidents in 2005, up six percent from the year before.He says rural poverty is declining and the situation for farmers has improved since 2005.However, he told journalists, clashes between farmers and local governments remain a serious problem."Now in fact it is still the case that issues that arose several years ago have not been properly resolved or not resolved to farmers' satisfaction. And, so farmers continue to express their complaints."

Several Democratic members of the U.S. House of Representatives on Thursday reintroduced a bill that would expand immigration eligibility for immediate family members of U.S. citizens and allow same-sex married couples to join spouses in the United States.Representative Mike Honda (D-Calif.), who sponsored the Reuniting Families Act (RFA), says the immigration reform legislation is designed to cut backlogs that separate some 4.4 million family members from U.S. citizens and green card holders."The act will cut red tape and get rid of bureaucracy that results in lost visas and lost opportunities," Rep. Honda announced at a Capitol Hill news conference, explaining that the RFA will utilize thousands of visas that have gone unused over the past two decades.Bill faces opposition in CongressThe bill is likely to face stiff opposition from lawmakers advocating increased restrictions on U.S. immigration, which has been at top issue in the 2016 presidential race.According to current U.S. immigration law, immediate family members of U.S. citizens include spouses, unmarried children under 21 years of age and parents 21 years of age or older.The bill, originally introduced in 2013, classifies the spouse and minor children of green card holders as immediate relatives and separately, increases visa allocations for siblings, and ensures that same-sex, interfaith and other couples unable to wed in their home countries are treated the same as opposite-sex couples."If you have more people coming into the category of immediate families, they would automatically be put into the queue for getting the visa actually granted," Rep.

Flags over the American capitol flew at half staff Sunday as a tribute to the 132 victims of Friday's deadly attacks in Paris at a nightclub, sports stadium and several restaurants.Washingtonians also paid their respects at a makeshift memorial in front of the French embassy, leaving flowers and bottles of wine in tribute – and lighting candles. Similar scenes took place at French embassies around the world in Istanbul, Berlin, Moscow and Prague, among others.As many as 20,000 people gathered in front of the French embassy in Copenhagan, Denmark to show their respect for the victims of the attacksAmong the dead in Paris was at least one American as well foreign nationals from at least 13 other countries around the world. A number of the people killed were from Muslim countries, including Algeria, Morocco and Tunisia.Portugal lost two of its citizens. One was a driver who had just dropped off passengers at the Stade de France. South Americans from Chile and Mexico died in the attacks. Two Brazilians were wounded. Two Romanians were killed as well as people from Belgium, Britain, Germany, Italy, Spain and Sweden.The world mourned with the French on Sunday.In Jerusalem, the old city walls appeared in red, white and blue, the colors of the French flag. Similarly, the world’s tallest building in Dubai – the Burj Khalifa – was capped with red, white and blue lights. Bratislava castle wore horizontal red, white and blue stripes. Those on Tokyo’s Skytree and Bogota’s Colpatria tower were stacked up vertically.But the iconic Eiffel Tower in Paris was a black tower as its lights were turned off.

Nature is sending us messages all the time, but most of us can’t read them. Tristan Gooley says he can.The British explorer has led expeditions on five continents, climbed mountains, and studied navigation methods in remote regions on Earth. He also founded a school for natural navigation to help people decode these messages. And in his new book, "The Lost Art of Reading Nature’s Signs", Gooley shares more than 850 different clues from nature that can help people find their way, predict the weather and get more out of their time outdoors.For example, figuring out which way is north, without a compass.“One of the first things to look for is the sun, if it’s out,” Gooley says. “If you’re north of the tropics, then the sun is due south in the middle of the day. That’s the best place to start.”The Lost Art of Reading Nature’s Signs by Tristan Gooley shares more than 850 different clues from nature that can help people find their way, predict the weather and get more out of their time outdoors.Trees can be helpful in navigation because their shape and color can indicate compass directions. “Trees grow bigger, more bushy, there is just more tree on the southern side, because that’s the side that gets most of the light.” That's the same reason leaves are smaller and lighter in color on the south side than one the north side, when they're more in the shade.Nature also provides clues that can help predict weather.“I think a lot of people are still familiar with the most popular weather law, ‘Red sky at night, sailors delight,’” he says. “There is a lot of truth in that.

Argentina's President Mauricio Macri has announced plans to report Venezuela to the International Criminal Court (ICC) over alleged human rights abuses. The decision comes after a damning report by the United Nations which highlighted widespread violations in the country under President Nicolas Maduro's leadership.Macri, who has been a vocal critic of Maduro's government, stated that the evidence of human rights abuses in Venezuela is overwhelming and cannot be ignored. He also called on other countries in the region to join Argentina in condemning the actions of the Venezuelan government.The ICC is the world's first permanent international criminal court and is responsible for prosecuting individuals for crimes such as genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity. While Venezuela is not a member of the ICC, Macri's decision to report the country could lead to further scrutiny of the human rights situation there.The move by Argentina to report Venezuela to the ICC is likely to escalate tensions between the two countries, which have had strained relations in recent years. It also signals a more aggressive stance by Latin American countries towards the Maduro government, which has faced widespread condemnation for its authoritarian practices.It remains to be seen how the ICC will respond to Argentina's report and whether other countries in the region will follow suit. However, Macri's decision to take action against Venezuela sends a strong message that human rights abuses will not be tolerated in Latin America.

The 2018 Grammy Awards are fast approaching, and many in the music industry are wondering if this year will finally be the year that hip-hop takes center stage and receives the recognition it deserves.Despite being one of the most influential and popular genres in music today, hip-hop has historically been underrepresented at the Grammys. While artists like Jay-Z and Kendrick Lamar have been nominated for numerous awards over the years, they have often been overlooked in favor of more traditional genres like pop and rock.This year, however, there is hope that things may be different. With Jay-Z leading the pack with eight nominations, including Album of the Year for "4:44," it seems that the Recording Academy may finally be giving hip-hop the respect it has long been denied.In addition to Jay-Z, artists like Kendrick Lamar, Childish Gambino, and Cardi B have also received multiple nominations, indicating a shift towards recognizing the talent and influence of hip-hop artists in the industry.While it remains to be seen whether hip-hop will finally "even the score" at the 2018 Grammys, one thing is for certain - the genre's influence and impact on the music industry can no longer be denied. Whether it's through thought-provoking lyrics, infectious beats, or powerful social commentary, hip-hop has become a driving force in music and culture, and it's about time it received the recognition it deserves.

Jazz fusion band Spyro Gyra is celebrating their 25th anniversary with the release of a new CD. The band, formed in 1974, has been a staple in the jazz world for decades. Known for their smooth sound and captivating performances, Spyro Gyra has garnered a loyal fan base over the years.The new CD, titled "25th Anniversary Celebration," features a collection of the band's greatest hits along with a few new tracks. Fans can expect to hear classics such as "Morning Dance" and "Shaker Song" along with fresh new tunes that showcase the band's continued evolution.Spyro Gyra has truly stood the test of time, with their innovative blend of jazz, pop, and R&B resonating with audiences around the world. The band's ability to push boundaries and explore new musical territories has set them apart in the jazz genre.To mark this significant milestone, Spyro Gyra will be embarking on a tour later this year, bringing their music to fans old and new. The band continues to inspire and captivate audiences, proving that their music is timeless and enduring.Fans of Spyro Gyra can look forward to celebrating the band's 25th anniversary with the release of their new CD and upcoming tour dates.

The recent arrests of suspected Turkish spies in Iran have escalated tensions between the two neighboring countries. Iranian authorities announced on Wednesday that they had detained several individuals accused of espionage activities on behalf of Turkey.The arrests come at a time when relations between Turkey and Iran have been strained over various geopolitical issues, including the conflict in Syria and their differing alliances in the region. Turkish officials have denied any involvement in espionage activities and have called for the immediate release of their citizens.Iranian officials, on the other hand, have accused Turkey of meddling in their internal affairs and have warned of potential consequences if such activities continue. Both countries have summoned each other's ambassadors to discuss the situation, further worsening diplomatic relations.The spy arrests have added another layer of complexity to the already tense relationship between Turkey and Iran. It remains to be seen how the two countries will navigate these latest developments and whether they can find a way to de-escalate the situation before it spirals out of control.

The upcoming ASEAN Summit in Myanmar is not expected to bring about any major breakthroughs, according to analysts and officials. While the summit will address important regional issues such as the ongoing crisis in Myanmar's Rakhine state and the South China Sea dispute, there are low expectations for any significant progress on these matters. The focus is likely to be on maintaining dialogue and fostering cooperation among member countries. Critics argue that more concrete actions are needed to address these pressing issues, but officials maintain that the summit will serve as a platform for discussion and diplomacy. Myanmar is hosting the summit for the first time since assuming the ASEAN chairmanship in 2021, and all eyes will be on the country to see how it navigates these challenging issues.

Tensions have escalated between Sudan and the United Nations peacekeeping mission in Darfur after a recent clash in the region. The Sudanese government has accused the peacekeepers of overstepping their mandate and interfering in the country's internal affairs.The incident, which occurred in the town of Nertiti, reportedly involved peacekeepers attempting to arrest a local militia leader accused of committing human rights abuses. The Sudanese government condemned the actions of the peacekeepers and demanded an immediate withdrawal of the mission from the country.The United Nations, on the other hand, has defended the actions of its peacekeepers, stating that they were carrying out their mandate to protect civilians and maintain peace in the region. The mission has called for a thorough investigation into the incident and urged all parties to exercise restraint and work towards a peaceful resolution.The situation in Darfur remains tense, with ongoing violence and instability threatening the lives of civilians in the region. Both Sudan and the United Nations must find a way to de-escalate tensions and cooperate in order to ensure the safety and security of the people of Darfur.

Colin Kroll, the co-founder of HQ Trivia and Vine, was found dead in his New York City apartment on Sunday. The 34-year-old entrepreneur was discovered by police after his girlfriend asked them to check on him. Kroll's cause of death has not yet been officially determined, but it is suspected to be a drug overdose.Kroll was known for his work in the tech industry, having also co-founded the video looping app Vine, which was later acquired by Twitter. He most recently served as the CEO of the popular live trivia game show app HQ Trivia.The news of Kroll's sudden passing has shocked his colleagues and friends in the tech community. Tributes have been pouring in on social media, with many remembering Kroll as a talented and innovative entrepreneur.Authorities are continuing to investigate the circumstances surrounding Kroll's death. His passing is a tragic loss for the tech world, and he will be deeply missed by those who knew him.

Kevin Tsujihara, the CEO of Warner Bros., has announced his resignation amidst a scandal involving an affair with an actress who was seeking roles in the company's films. Tsujihara, who has been with Warner Bros. for over 25 years, released a statement acknowledging his mistake and apologizing for any harm caused.The scandal came to light after text messages between Tsujihara and actress Charlotte Kirk were made public. In these messages, Tsujihara promised to help Kirk secure roles in Warner Bros. productions. The revelation led to accusations of misconduct and a subsequent investigation by the company.In his resignation statement, Tsujihara expressed regret for his actions and stated that he believes stepping down is in the best interest of the company. Warner Bros. has not yet announced a successor to Tsujihara, but industry insiders speculate that an internal candidate will likely be chosen to fill the position.The scandal has sent shockwaves through Hollywood and raised questions about the power dynamics within the entertainment industry. It remains to be seen how Warner Bros. will move forward in the wake of Tsujihara's resignation.

Different cultures have different ways of coping with extreme heat. In Houston, Texas, when the mercury rises, office workers head underground rather then go outdoors. Their refuge is an elaborate network of tunnels, which has expanded over the years without any central planning. Malcolm Brown paid a visit.Looking at Houston's high-rise landscape, you might imagine that the streets would be packed at lunchtime. But they're not.That is because many office workers are down here, in a network of pedestrian tunnels around 11 kilometers long.Sandra Lord, also known as 'the tunnel lady,' provides tours of this labyrinth, which can be hard to navigate. "People who walk around it, if they see a tourist or someone who looks lost, they'll walk up to them and say, 'Can I help you?' They know that to be polite you walk on the right, so that people can come the other way on the right. There's a passing lane."The popularity of these air-conditioned tunnels stems from the heat outside. Top temperatures in summer average around 33 degrees centigrade. High humidity makes it feel even hotter.Down here in the cool there are over 100 places to eat, as well as a host of service providers; including banks, doctors, dentists, and florists. Some people even walk the corridors for exercise.Sandra Lord also runs above-ground tours, but she became fascinated by the world beneath. "It's the oldest and largest, tunnel-skywalk, mostly private, completely pedestrian system in the world! (Laughs)"It is mostly private, because each section belongs to a building above. The patchwork ownership accounts for the uncoordinated growth, as developers and property owners add to the system.Susan Speck, from Tarantino Properties, shows off an area that will soon be a new food court.

The International Labour Organization (ILO) has released a new report detailing the economic crisis currently facing the Middle East region. The report, published on May 8, 2002, highlights the significant challenges that countries in the Middle East are currently facing, including high unemployment rates, low wages, and limited job opportunities.According to the report, the economic crisis in the Middle East is largely driven by political instability, regional conflicts, and economic sanctions. These factors have resulted in a stagnation of economic growth and have had a detrimental impact on the labor market in the region.The ILO report calls for urgent action to address the root causes of the economic crisis in the Middle East, including the need for increased investment in job creation, skills development, and social protection measures. The report also emphasizes the importance of promoting social dialogue and inclusive growth to ensure a more sustainable and equitable recovery from the crisis.As countries in the Middle East continue to grapple with economic challenges, the ILO report serves as a timely reminder of the urgent need for coordinated efforts to address the underlying issues and support the region's economic recovery.

After weeks of negotiations, officials in Ethiopia have reportedly agreed on new rules for the upcoming 2010 elections. The agreement comes amid mounting tensions and concerns over the fairness and transparency of the electoral process.According to sources close to the negotiations, the new rules include provisions for increased oversight and monitoring of the voting process, as well as measures to ensure the independence of the electoral commission. Additionally, there are reports that the agreement includes provisions for greater representation of opposition parties in the electoral process.The news of the agreement has been met with cautious optimism by many in Ethiopia, who have long expressed concerns about the lack of political freedoms and democratic processes in the country. The upcoming elections are seen as a crucial test of the government's commitment to political reform and transparency.However, some opposition parties have raised concerns about the implementation of the new rules and have called for further assurances that the elections will be free and fair. They have also called for the release of political prisoners and an end to harassment and intimidation of opposition members.The coming months will be crucial in determining whether Ethiopia can successfully navigate the challenges of organizing a free and fair election. With the eyes of the international community on the country, the stakes are high for all involved.

Peter Jok’s long journey that began in war-torn Sudan and detoured through Ugandan and Kenyan refugee camps has not landed him on an NBA basketball court — yet.Jok, a University of Iowa standout for four years, had been predicted to be chosen in the first two rounds of the National Basketball Association draft in New York.While he wasn’t chosen by an NBA team in the draft, Jok did agree to compete in Las Vegas with the summer league team of the New Orleans Pelicans.The 198-centimeter-tall (6 feet 6 inches) Jok told VOA's South Sudan in Focus that his future once looked bleak, but since he began to play competitive basketball, he has set his sights on achieving a bright future."I have had a lot of ups and downs, but it has made me into a better man and a better player," he said. "Everything I have been through has given me more edge, more motivation to go harder. The process is hard work, but you keep the faith and believe in God."Jok, who is originally from South Sudan's Lakes state, scored an average of 19.9 points per game in his senior year at Iowa.

Multiple shots were fired at a convoy of United Nations monitors in Syria earlier today, in a troubling attack on the peacekeeping mission in the war-torn country.The incident occurred as the monitors were on their way to investigate reports of violence and human rights abuses in the region. No injuries have been reported so far, but the attack underscores the dangers faced by those working to bring peace and stability to Syria.The United Nations has condemned the attack and called for an immediate investigation to identify the perpetrators. The monitors play a crucial role in documenting violations of the ceasefire agreement and helping to de-escalate tensions in the conflict-ridden country.This latest attack serves as a stark reminder of the challenges faced by those trying to bring an end to the violence in Syria, and highlights the urgent need for all parties involved to commit to a peaceful resolution to the ongoing conflict.

A Pakistani woman, who has been accused of blasphemy in her home country, is seeking asylum in France. The woman, whose identity has not been disclosed for safety reasons, is facing serious allegations that could put her life at risk if she remains in Pakistan.According to reports, the woman has been targeted by religious extremists in Pakistan after she was accused of insulting the Prophet Muhammad. Blasphemy is a highly sensitive issue in Pakistan and carries severe penalties, including the possibility of death.The woman's case has attracted international attention, with human rights organizations calling for her protection and urging the French government to grant her asylum. France has a history of offering asylum to individuals facing persecution for their beliefs or actions in their home countries.It remains to be seen whether the Pakistani woman will be granted asylum in France, but her case highlights the ongoing challenges faced by individuals who are targeted for their beliefs or actions in countries where freedom of expression is restricted.

WASHINGTON - A new device can reveal in a matter of seconds whether that drink of water you desperately need is contaminated with disease-causing microorganisms or ready to drink.The device, called Duo Fluor, can in about 30 seconds test for organic material like decaying plants and animals, and bacteria, including pathogens that cause cholera and dysentery. John Bridgeman is with the University of Birmingham’s Department of Civil Engineering in England who led a team that developed the device. “It does not require any chemical, any reagents," Bridgeman says, "and will give you an indication of water quality within just a few seconds.” The device works by shining a high frequency light into a water sample. If microbes are present in the water, they absorb the photons and emit a weak light back. A thin optical LED instrument detects the fluorescence, which is then processed by a laptop. The method is especially sensitive to fecal matter and results are almost immediate, compared to the 12 hours it now takes to analyze drinking water. Its application is evident to the 663 million people the United Nations says don't have ready access to safe drinking water.

The United States is ramping up its efforts to combat the Islamic State (IS) with a renewed focus on special operations and intelligence. The US military has announced plans to increase the use of elite special operations forces and intelligence capabilities to target and eliminate key leaders of the terrorist organization.Special operations forces, such as Navy SEALs and Army Rangers, will be deployed to conduct covert missions against high-value IS targets in Iraq and Syria. These highly trained troops have the skills and expertise needed to carry out precision strikes and capture or kill terrorist leaders.In addition to special operations forces, the US military will also leverage its intelligence assets to gather information on IS operatives and their activities. By analyzing data and conducting surveillance, intelligence agencies will be able to identify and track down IS leaders, disrupt their operations, and ultimately degrade the organization's ability to carry out terrorist attacks.This intensified focus on special operations and intelligence is part of the US government's broader strategy to accelerate efforts to defeat IS and eliminate the threat it poses to global security. By combining the strengths of special operations forces and intelligence capabilities, the US aims to gain a tactical advantage over the terrorist group and dismantle its leadership structure.As the fight against IS continues, the use of special operations and intelligence will play a crucial role in disrupting and defeating the terrorist organization. The US government is committed to using all available resources and capabilities to confront the threat of terrorism and protect the safety and security of the American people and its allies.

WASHINGTON - Five years before he held 18 people hostage inside a Sydney cafe, Iranian-born Man Haron Monis attracted the attention of the FBI as someone who had “the potential to incite others to violence,” according to documents obtained by The Associated Press.A November 2009 memorandum from an FBI office in Australia to the agency's counterterrorism division said that while Monis was not believed to be a threat to national security, “his ongoing offensive and nuisance behavior has the potential to incite others to violence.”A shotgun-wielding Monis took customers and workers captive in a December 2014 siege at the Lindt Cafe in Sydney and demanded to be delivered an Islamic State flag, an incident that fueled anxiety about the extremist group's expanding influence across the globe. Police stormed the cafe 16 hours later, after Monis fatally shot one of the hostages. Monis was shot dead by police and another hostage was killed in the crossfire.Though it's been established that Australian authorities failed to detect that Monis was a threat, despite years of warnings, the memo shows how the FBI was itself concerned about his rhetoric even before the Islamic State group emerged as a prominent international force and well ahead of the siege.

The Senate continued its second day of debate on Supreme Court nominee Sonia Sotomayor yesterday, with both Democrats and Republicans sharing their views on her qualifications for the highest court in the land. Sotomayor, currently a federal appeals court judge, has faced scrutiny from conservatives for her past rulings and statements on issues such as gun rights and affirmative action.Democrats praised Sotomayor's judicial record and personal background, highlighting her experience as a prosecutor and her time on the bench. They emphasized her commitment to upholding the rule of law and ensuring equal justice under the law for all Americans.Republicans raised concerns about Sotomayor's views on gun rights and her approach to interpreting the Constitution. Some senators questioned whether she would be impartial and fair in her decision-making as a Supreme Court justice.The Senate is expected to vote on Sotomayor's nomination later this week. If confirmed, she would be the first Latina and only the third woman to serve on the Supreme Court. Stay tuned for more updates on this developing story.

The global financial crisis has pushed millions of people around the world out of work. But in China, some cities are offering incentives to attract both overseas Chinese and foreign talent to fill high-level jobs. Guangzhou in southern China is offering cash incentives to mid- and upper-level professionals overseas to get them to move there.And Shanghai recently announced it would offer tax incentives to overseas professionals who agree to work at financial institutions in the city.Both Guangzhou and Shanghai want to attract overseas Chinese as well as foreigners. Guangzhou recently held a job fair for overseas Chinese, while Shanghai, which hopes to lure laid-off financial industry workers, has recruited overseas Chinese at job fairs in London, Chicago and New York.Harley Seyedin is president of the South China American Chamber of Commerce in Guangzhou. He says the nearby city of Shenzhen also has an incentive program. "Because there's tremendous demand for talent all over China. And Guangzhou certainly needs to get its share of that talent as it begins to move up the higher value-added chain of manufacturing and production," said Seyedin.Guangzhou has a $30 million fund to attract top talent in the manufacturing, technology, trade and finance sectors. It will give cash awards of up to $146,000 to those who move there - the amount recently was increased to compete with other cities.As China's economy boomed over the past three decades, the country struggled to find experienced managers and professionals.

STATE DEPARTMENT - The United States is urging Turkey to de-escalate tensions over its military operations in Syria and focus on the fight to defeat the Islamic State militants, amid a growing distrust between two NATO allies.Meanwhile, Turkey is pressing the U.S. to stop its support for Kurdish fighters or risk confronting Turkish forces on the ground in Syria.U.S.-Turkish relations have suffered another blow, with Ankara and Washington disputing each other’s version of a telephone call Wednesday between the U.S. and Turkish presidents aimed at defusing tensions over Turkish-led forces’ intervention in Syria.Ankara has rejected Washington’s account of what U.S. President Donald Trump said to his Turkish counterpart, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, denying there was any call by Trump to de-escalate the military operation against the Syrian Kurdish militia the YPG in Syria’s Afrin enclave.But Washington said it stands by Trump's "firm" and "tough" stand during his phone call with Erdogan."We stand by the president’s assertion of cautioning Turkey about the escalation of tensions in Afrin area," said State Department spokesperson Heather Nauert on Thursday."We certainly hope they [Turkish leaders] will listen," said Nauert, "that would be a good thing."The White House reported Trump said the Turkish operation "risks undercutting our shared goals in Syria," and "urged Turkey to de-escalate, limit its military actions, and avoid civilian casualties and increases to displaced persons and refugees."Turkey's President Recep Tayyip Erdogan gestures to supporters of his ruling Justice and Development Party at a rally in Bingol, Turkey, Jan. 13, 2018.The dispute over the contents of the telephone call is exacerbating a lack of trust between the two NATO allies.The United States has supported the YPG militia in the war against Islamic State.

WASHINGTON - U.S. government searches of travelers' cellphones and laptops at airports and border crossings nearly quadrupled since 2015 and are being conducted for reasons beyond customs and immigration enforcement, according to papers filed Tuesday in a federal lawsuit that claims going through electronic devices without a warrant is unconstitutional.The government has vigorously defended the searches, which rose to 33,295 in fiscal 2018, as a critical tool to protect America. But the newly filed documents claim the scope of the warrantless searches has expanded to enforce tax, bankruptcy, environmental and consumer protection laws, gather intelligence and advance ongoing law enforcement investigations.Agents with U.S. Customs and Border Protection and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement consider requests from other government agencies in determining whether to search travelers' electronic devices, the court papers said. They added that agents are searching the electronic devices of not only targeted individuals but their associates, friends and relatives.The new information about the searches was included in a motion the Electronic Frontier Foundation and the American Civil Liberties Union filed Tuesday in U.S. District Court in Massachusetts."The evidence we have presented the court shows that the scope of ICE and CBP border searches is unconstitutionally broad,'' said Adam Schwartz, senior staff attorney for the EFF, based in San Francisco."ICE and CBP policies and practices allow unfettered, warrantless searches of travelers' digital devices and empower officers to dodge the Fourth Amendment when rifling through highly personal information contained on laptops and phones,'' he said.The Department of Homeland Security did not respond to a request for comment.

Amid rising political tensions and concerns over human rights violations in Cambodia, European countries are considering cutting aid to the Southeast Asian nation. The European Union and individual member states have expressed deep concerns over the deteriorating political situation in Cambodia, including the crackdown on opposition parties and activists.European officials have warned the Cambodian government that aid may be suspended or reduced if the current political climate does not improve. The European Union is one of Cambodia's largest donors, providing millions of euros in aid each year for development projects and humanitarian assistance.The European concerns come as Cambodia is facing increasing international criticism for its recent actions, including the dissolution of the main opposition party and the arrest of its leader, Kem Sokha. The Cambodian government has been accused of cracking down on political dissent and stifling freedom of expression.In response to the threats of aid cuts, Cambodian officials have defended their actions, claiming that they are necessary to maintain stability and national security. However, critics argue that the government is using these tactics to consolidate power and silence opposition voices.The European Union and its member states are expected to continue monitoring the situation in Cambodia closely and may take further action if the government does not address their concerns. The potential cuts in aid could have significant repercussions for Cambodia's economy and development projects, putting pressure on the government to address international criticisms and improve the country's human rights record.

In a significant development, various factions in Libya have come to an agreement in principle on the establishment of a unified oil structure in the country. This agreement marks a positive step towards stabilizing the nation's oil industry, which has been plagued by conflict and disorganization in recent years.The agreement, reached after months of negotiations facilitated by international mediators, outlines a framework for the management and distribution of Libya's vast oil resources. This unified structure aims to streamline the process of oil production and export, ultimately benefiting the country's economy and its people.Libya, home to Africa's largest proven oil reserves, has seen its oil industry suffer from disruptions caused by rival factions vying for control. The agreement among these factions to work together towards a common goal demonstrates a willingness to put aside differences for the greater good of the country.While this agreement is a significant step forward, there are still challenges to overcome in implementing and maintaining a unified oil structure in Libya. However, the willingness of the factions to cooperate and compromise is a hopeful sign for the future of the country's oil industry.The international community has welcomed this agreement as a positive development for Libya and its people. With continued cooperation and support, Libya's oil industry could once again become a key driver of economic growth and stability in the region.

A US court has put a stop to a ruling that would have prevented victims of abuse and gang violence from seeking asylum in the United States. The ruling, which was issued by the Trump administration in 2018, sought to restrict asylum claims based on fear of persecution from non-governmental actors.The recent decision by the court comes as a relief to many asylum seekers who have faced violence and persecution in their home countries. Advocates for immigrants' rights have hailed the decision as a step towards upholding the US's commitment to protecting those fleeing from harm.The ruling had been challenged by several civil rights organizations and immigrant advocacy groups, who argued that it violated both US and international law. The court's decision to halt the ruling is a victory for those fighting for the rights of vulnerable individuals seeking refuge in the US.As the debate over immigration policy continues to be a hot-button issue in the US, this latest development serves as a reminder of the importance of upholding human rights and protecting those in need. The court's decision sends a strong message that asylum seekers, including victims of abuse and gang violence, deserve a fair chance at seeking safety and protection in the United States.

Former Malaysian Prime Minister Najib Razak has been hit with 25 new charges in connection with the 1MDB looting scandal. The charges include money laundering, abuse of power, and criminal breach of trust. This latest development comes as part of the ongoing investigation into the multi-billion dollar scandal, in which billions of dollars were allegedly siphoned off from the state fund 1MDB.Najib, who was ousted from power in the 2018 general elections, has maintained his innocence throughout the investigation. However, the new charges indicate that authorities believe he played a central role in the looting of the state fund.The 1MDB scandal has been described as one of the biggest financial scandals in Malaysian history, and has also implicated several high-profile individuals and institutions around the world. The scandal has raised questions about the role of corruption in Malaysia's political and economic system, and has led to calls for greater transparency and accountability in government.Najib is currently facing multiple charges related to the scandal, and if convicted, he could face a lengthy prison sentence. The trial is set to continue in the coming months as authorities seek to bring those responsible for the looting of 1MDB to justice.

World number-one women's golfer Anika Sorenstam of Sweden will try to become the first LPGA player since 1986 to win the first two major titles of a season when she tees it up Thursday at the LPGA Championship in Havre de Grace, Maryland.Sorenstam is the tournament's two-time defending champion and will need the victory to reach her 2005 goal of winning golf's Grand Slam. The Swede won the first of the four major women's events at the Kraft Nabisco Championship in March.Among those in the field are 15-year-old Michelle Wie and 18-year-old Paula Creamer of the United States. Creamer became the youngest player in 53 years to win a tour event when she captured the Sybase Classic last month in New York.Wie, still an amateur, is in the normally professionals-only LPGA Championship by virtue of a special invitation. Playing against the pros this year she has finished as high as second, 12th and 14th.Wie's scoring average of 71.06 would be fourth on the tour.

The Catholic hierarchy in Europe has issued warnings about the rise of populism ahead of key elections in several countries. Church leaders are concerned about the increasing influence of populist and nationalist movements, which they argue can lead to division, intolerance, and a rejection of fundamental Christian values.Pope Francis has been vocal in his criticism of populist leaders and movements, warning against the dangers of closed borders and xenophobia. In a recent address, the Pope urged European countries to embrace solidarity and unity in the face of growing populism.Cardinal Reinhard Marx, the president of the Commission of the Bishops' Conferences of the European Union, echoed the Pope's sentiments, stating that populism undermines the principles of democracy and human rights. He called on Catholic voters to reject politicians who promote fear and division.The warnings from the Catholic hierarchy come as Europe prepares for a series of important elections, including the French presidential election in April and the German federal election in September. With populist parties gaining ground in many countries, church leaders are urging voters to consider the values of compassion, tolerance, and solidarity when casting their ballots.As the Catholic Church continues to speak out against the dangers of populism, it remains to be seen whether their warnings will resonate with voters across Europe.

U.N. peacekeeping troops working with Haitian police have traded heavy gunfire with gangs in a slum of the capital, Port-au-Prince.Officials say at least nine people were killed Friday and several others were wounded during the early morning raid in the volatile slum of Cite Soleil. All the casualties were believed to be civilians.The U.N. force said it launched the operation with Haiti's national police to stop a surge in kidnappings and other crimes by gangs in the slum.U.N. peacekeepers have been in Haiti since the ouster of former President Jean-Bertrand Aristide in February 2004.Haiti is the Western Hemisphere's poorest country and has been plagued by civil and political violence.

Sukaina Mohammed Ali put her own life at risk during the military campaign to liberate Mosul, Iraq, in 2017, rescuing children left wandering the streets after they were separated from their families by the fierce fighting between U.S.-backed Iraqi forces and Islamic State (IS) militants. Since then, Ali has made it her mission to save these abandoned children to help them overcome the trauma and horror of war.FILE - Babies toddle around in walkers at the state-run al-Zuhour Orphanage in Mosul, Iraq, Aug. 19, 2018. Orphanages are where wars play out: children abandoned in the chaos of battle, children born of rape and sexual slavery, babies left behind in the hospital in the hopes of a fresh start, and those left in state care after their parents are sentenced to death.Thousands of children have been traumatized by war, but Ali believes she is making a difference by providing some of these children with the opportunity to rebuild their lives. Some of the children she rescues, however, have IS parents, she said. "I have a message to deliver to IS and to their supporters: You took our children to use them as soldiers, as slaves; you turned them into extremists, slaughtered our sons and daughters. Today, I take your children to care for them, to teach them how to be children again, because Islam is not what extremist groups like IS and AQ [al-Qaida] represent," Ali told VOA. Through her work, she has risked her life and reputation, Ali said. She said she also had faced backlash by helping IS children. But she said she was passionate about wanting to erase the traces of hatred and despair that IS has left behind in her country.

The European Union has announced its support for limited sanctions on Iran, in response to the country's recent actions that have raised concerns among EU member states. These sanctions are aimed at targeting specific individuals and entities that are believed to be involved in activities that pose a threat to regional security and stability.The decision to impose limited sanctions on Iran comes after a series of incidents, including the recent attacks on oil tankers in the Gulf of Oman and the downing of a US drone by Iranian forces. The EU has expressed its concern over Iran's provocative actions and its failure to comply with international agreements, particularly the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) regarding its nuclear program.While the EU has emphasized that these sanctions are targeted and limited in scope, they serve as a clear signal of the international community's growing frustration with Iran's behavior. The EU has called on Iran to adhere to its international obligations and engage in constructive dialogue to de-escalate tensions in the region.The decision to back limited sanctions on Iran has received mixed reactions from EU member states, with some expressing support for a tougher stance against Iran while others cautioning against further escalation. The EU will continue to monitor the situation closely and coordinate with its international partners to address the ongoing tensions in the region.

NAIROBI - The U.S. State Department has offered $5 million rewards for information about two American citizens who have joined ranks with the Somali militant group al-Shabab. One of the militants is the media savvy al-Amriki, known for his recruitment videos, jihadi rap songs and Twitter battles with al-Shabab’s leadership. In an undated video by Amriki, the long-haired young American man who would become the western face of al-Shabab leads what he says is an ambush on Ethiopian soldiers. Born Omar Hammami, he is now better known by the moniker Abu Mansour al-Amriki - “the American.” Originally from the southern U.S. state of Alabama, he moved to Somalia in 2006 and received training from the al-Qaida linked militants. In the years since, he has become one of the group’s leading propagandist, appearing speaking English and Arabic in recruitment videos. At some point along the way, he recorded a jihadi rap song. Now, the U.S. State Department is offering $5 million for information that can lead to his arrest, and that of another American citizen in Somalia, Jehad Mostafa, a former resident of California. In a telephone conference Thursday, U.S. Diplomatic Security official Kirk Rice said the rewards program is part of the U.S. strategy targeting al-Shabab’s Somali and foreign commanders. “So we have done this before, we continue to try and get information on al-Shabab key leaders," said Rice."We try to get information on al-Shabab key recruiters and leaders of foreign fighter cadres, and we will continue to look at and try to get information on al-Shabab into the future.” In recent months, al-Amriki had a very public falling out with the leadership of al-Shabab which he narrated on his Twitter feed and in his videos.

Travis Kalanick, the CEO of Uber, has announced his resignation following pressure from investors. Kalanick, who co-founded the ride-sharing company in 2009, has faced numerous controversies during his tenure, including allegations of workplace harassment and a toxic corporate culture.The pressure for Kalanick to step down increased after a series of scandals rocked the company, leading to a decline in Uber's reputation and stock value. Investors, including major shareholders, have been pushing for new leadership to restore trust and stability within the company.In a statement, Kalanick acknowledged the need for new leadership at Uber and stated that he believed it was time for him to step aside. He will remain on the board of directors.The resignation of Kalanick comes at a crucial time for Uber as the company continues to face challenges in the competitive ride-sharing market. The search for a new CEO is now underway, with many speculating on who will take over the helm of one of the world's most valuable startups.Despite his departure, Kalanick's influence on Uber and the tech industry as a whole cannot be underestimated. His aggressive leadership style and ambitious vision helped propel the company to become a global powerhouse, but also contributed to its downfall.As Uber looks towards the future, the company will have to navigate a new era under fresh leadership as it seeks to regain the trust of both investors and customers alike.

BRUSSELS - Eurozone ministers on Monday held back granting Greece debt relief because the government failed to implement reforms promised during the massive bailout that ended last year, officials said.Greece exited its third and final international bailout in August, a turning point in its progress out of the catastrophe that engulfed the country during the financial crisis.The Greek government has still failed to complete housing insolvency rules that have raised fears in Greece for families threatened with foreclosure on their homes.European officials, however, played down the delay, not wanting to rekindle memories of the eurozone debt crisis that nearly destroyed Europe's single currency."It's too early to decide formally on the disbursement today," said EU Economics Affairs Commissioner Pierre Moscovici ahead of a Eurogroup meeting of eurozone finance ministers.FILE - European Commissioner for Economic and Financial Affairs Pierre Moscovici attends a news conference in Brussels, Belgium, Dec. 4, 2018."

The recent incident involving tennis star Serena Williams at the US Open has sparked a larger conversation about the treatment of black women in professional sports. Williams was penalized and fined during the championship match for her actions and words towards the umpire, leading many to believe that she was unfairly targeted.Black women across the country have expressed their support for Williams, citing the incident as just one example of the discrimination and double standards they face in various industries, including sports. Many feel that Williams' treatment was influenced by factors such as race and gender, highlighting the need for greater equality and respect for black women in all aspects of society.The conversation surrounding Williams' treatment has also prompted discussions about the representation of black women in the media and the importance of amplifying their voices. As a trailblazer in the world of tennis, Williams has long been an advocate for equality and justice, and her experience at the US Open has only further solidified her impact on the fight for social change.Overall, the support for Serena Williams among black women serves as a reminder of the ongoing challenges they face and the need for continued advocacy and solidarity. As the conversation continues to unfold, many hope that it will lead to greater understanding and progress towards a more inclusive and equitable society for all.

NASA has announced the development of a new spacecraft that will provide advanced warning of solar outbursts, which can potentially disrupt communication and navigation systems on Earth. The spacecraft, named SunRISE (Sun Radio Interferometer Space Experiment), will be equipped with a radio telescope to monitor solar activity and provide real-time data on solar storms.Solar outbursts, also known as solar flares and coronal mass ejections, can release massive amounts of energy and radiation into space. When these solar events reach Earth, they can interfere with satellite communication, GPS navigation, and power grids. By providing early warning of these events, SunRISE will help minimize the impact on these critical systems.The new spacecraft is expected to launch in 2023 and will operate in a geostationary orbit around the Earth, allowing it to continuously monitor the Sun and provide timely alerts to space weather forecasters. This will improve our ability to predict and prepare for solar storms, ultimately enhancing the resilience of our technological infrastructure.NASA's SunRISE mission is a collaboration between the agency's Science Mission Directorate, the Heliophysics Division, and the Space Weather Follow On-Lagrange 1 (SWFO-L1) mission. The spacecraft is part of NASA's efforts to better understand and mitigate the effects of space weather on Earth and its technological systems.Overall, the development of SunRISE represents a significant step forward in our ability to monitor and respond to solar outbursts, ultimately enhancing our ability to protect critical infrastructure from the impacts of space weather.

U.S. Ambassador Michael McFaul, long an advocate of democracy in Russia, arrived in Moscow two months ago, and he walked straight into a groundswell of anti-Americanism. Prime Minister Vladimir Putin was running for president and his supporters accused the new ambassador of supporting anti-Putin street protests. Putin won the election easily three weeks ago, and now McFaul talks about where he thinks U.S.-Russia relations go from here.In February, McFaul said it felt like he was back in the Cold War when he moved into Spaso House, the Moscow residence of American ambassadors since 1933."It has been surprising that there was so much anti-Americanism, because we thought we were building a different kind of relationship, and it makes some people nervous that it could so quickly and reflexively go back to - in terms of rhetoric - an era that we thought was behind us," said McFaul.There were video attacks on the Internet, demonstrations outside the embassy, and charges by Putin supporters that the new American ambassador was backing the street protests for democracy.After Russia?s March 4 election, McFaul went back to Washington last week and met with Vice President Joe Biden. Biden's new marching orders, he said, were that he should stay the course."'Keep to your guns. Keep to your guns, McFaul.' He likes to call me McFaul. He said, 'Keep to your guns. You are doing exactly what our policy is [to] keep the engagement both with the government and with society out here in Russia,'" said McFaul.Now, three weeks after the Russian political campaign concluded, how much of the anti-Americanism was campaign rhetoric? And how much was a new policy toward Washington by President-elect Putin? The ambassador weighs in."We have no interest in going back to some kind of Cold War - bitter, bickering rhetoric. We don't think that serves the American national interest.

In a surprising move, Formula One teams have announced a change to the numbering system for the upcoming season. Starting in 2001, each team will now be assigned a specific number that will remain consistent throughout the season, regardless of the driver lineup. This new system aims to provide fans with a more straightforward way to identify and follow their favorite teams, as well as adding an additional layer of consistency to the sport. The change has received mixed reactions from fans and experts alike, with some praising the move for simplifying the viewing experience, while others express concerns about the potential loss of tradition and individuality. Only time will tell how this change will impact the world of Formula One racing.

U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon sharply rebuked Iran's supreme leader and president Friday, describing their latest anti-Israel rhetoric as "offensive and inflammatory."At a rally Friday, Iranian President Mahmoud Ahamadinejad told thousands of screaming worshipers at Tehran University that a new Middle East would have no trace of Americans or Israelis.Earlier this week, Iranian media reported that Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei said Israel would one day be returned to the Palestinian nation and would cease to exist.Secretary-General Ban's statement said that all leaders in the region should use their voices to lower, rather than to escalate tensions.President Ahmadinejad's remarks Friday were made during Quds day (Jerusalem), on the last Friday of the holy month of Ramadan.Israel considers Iran to be a looming threat because of its nuclear and missile programs.

The Lima Group made up of mostly Latin American countries called on the United Nations on Monday to "take action" to prevent an escalation of Venezuela's humanitarian crisis.The group of 14 countries, which also includes Canada, exhorted U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres, the General Assembly and the Security Council to "take measures to avoid the progressive deterioration of peace and security, and to provide urgent humanitarian aid to the population of migrants coming from Venezuela."Monday's meeting in Santiago came as Canada announced new sanctions against 41 members of Venezuela President Nicolas Maduro's government it holds "responsible for the deterioration of the situation" in the South American country.The sanctions included a freeze on the individuals' assets and a ban on them conducting business with Canada, which previously sanctioned another 70 top government officials.The Lima Group was created in 2017 to try to find a solution to Venezuela's economic meltdown.More than four years of recession have left Venezuela in crisis, with the country's poorest residents suffering from shortages of basic necessities such as food and medicine.Despite this the South American country sits on the world's largest proven oil reserves.Most Lima Group members refused to recognize Maduro's second term, which began on January 10, due to alleged fraud during his reelection last year.The speaker of the National Assembly, Juan Guaido, launched a challenge to Maduro's authority in January and has since been backed by more than 50 countries, led by the United States, that recognize him as Venezuela's interim president.Guaido wants to force Maduro, whom he deems to be illegitimate, from office and set up a transitional government ahead of new elections.U.S.

The ongoing dispute in the South China Sea has been partly blamed on depleted fish stocks in the region. Experts believe that the overfishing of marine resources has not only caused ecological damage but has also fueled tensions between countries claiming sovereignty over the area.The South China Sea is a critical fishing ground that supports the livelihoods of millions of people in the region. However, years of unsustainable fishing practices have led to a significant decline in fish populations, making it increasingly difficult for fishermen to make a living.In addition to the economic impact, the depletion of fish stocks has also exacerbated territorial disputes in the South China Sea. Countries such as China, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Malaysia have competing claims over various islands and reefs in the area, leading to heightened tensions and occasional confrontations.Efforts to address the issue of overfishing and depleted fish stocks have been challenging due to the complex nature of the territorial disputes in the region. However, experts stress the importance of finding sustainable solutions to ensure the long-term health of the marine environment and prevent further escalation of conflicts in the South China Sea.

The death toll from a devastating earthquake near the Indonesian island of Sulawesi has risen to over 430, with hundreds more injured and missing. The 6.2 magnitude earthquake struck in the early hours of Friday, causing significant damage to buildings and infrastructure in the city of Mamuju. Rescue teams are working tirelessly to search for survivors buried under the rubble, but the chances of finding more people alive are diminishing as time passes. The Indonesian government has declared a state of emergency in the region and has mobilized resources to provide aid and support to those affected by the disaster. This earthquake comes just a few years after a powerful earthquake and tsunami hit the island of Sulawesi in 2018, which left thousands dead and displaced. The people of Indonesia are once again facing immense challenges and hardships, and the international community is urged to come together and offer assistance in any way possible.

Tuesday looms as a major day in the U.S. presidential race, as voters in five large states head to the polls for primaries that could go a long way toward determining the presidential nominees for both political parties.The primaries in Florida, Illinois, Missouri, North Carolina and Ohio. Voting is taking place amid a backdrop of protests and some violence at rallies for the Republican front-runner, billionaire real estate mogul Donald Trump.Trump cancelled a rally Friday night in Chicago after clashes between protesters and supporters. Several other Trump rallies have been interrupted numerous times by demonstrators, and on Saturday Trump was quickly surrounded by U.S. Secret Service agents after a man leapt from the crowd and dashed toward the stage, only to be tackled by security and led away.Trump later told another rally in Missouri he was ready to defend himself had the protester made his way on stage. “I don’t know if I would have done well, but I would have been boom, boom, boom,” he said, pounding his right fist into his left hand.What to Watch for in Tuesday's US Presidential PrimariesPablo Ramos, of Orlando, holds a campaign sign as he waits for Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump to speak prior to a campaign event in Tampa, Fla., March 14, 2016.Trump leads in Florida; Ohio race tightDespite the chaos at recent Trump rallies, the Republican front-runner is positioned to do well in the five states voting on Tuesday. Three new polls (NBC News-Wall Street Journal-Marist/Quinnipiac University/Monmouth University) give Trump a double-digit lead in Florida over home state Senator Marco Rubio. A Rubio loss in Florida likely would lead to calls from other Republicans that he abandon his White House ambitions.Trump expressed confidence during a rally in Florida.

As Malawi gears up for the upcoming presidential elections, small businesses in the country are making their voices heard. With the aim of improving the business environment and boosting economic growth, small business owners have come together to offer advice and recommendations to the presidential candidates.One of the key issues raised by small businesses is the need for better access to financing. Many small businesses in Malawi struggle to secure loans from traditional financial institutions, hindering their ability to grow and expand. The presidential candidates are being urged to address this issue by implementing policies that make it easier for small businesses to access capital.Another major concern for small businesses is the high cost of doing business in Malawi. From high taxes to costly permits and licenses, small businesses are burdened with numerous expenses that eat into their profits. The presidential candidates are being urged to streamline the business registration process, reduce taxes, and eliminate unnecessary red tape in order to make it easier for small businesses to operate.In addition to these economic issues, small businesses are also calling for improved infrastructure and utilities in order to support their operations.

In response to the growing number of H1N1 cases in Afghanistan, authorities have made the decision to close schools in an effort to curb the spread of the virus. The Ministry of Health announced that the closure will take effect immediately and will remain in place until further notice.The decision comes as the country has seen a surge in H1N1 cases in recent weeks, with many individuals requiring hospitalization due to severe symptoms. Health officials are urging the public to take precautions such as frequent hand washing, wearing masks, and avoiding crowded places to help prevent further spread of the virus.Parents and students are advised to stay updated on the situation and follow guidelines from health authorities. The government is also working to provide resources and support to those affected by the closures, including online learning options for students to continue their education during this time.The closure of schools is just one of the measures being taken to address the growing health crisis in Afghanistan. Authorities are working diligently to contain the spread of H1N1 and ensure the safety and well-being of the population.

At least 14 people have been stabbed in an attack on a campus of Lone Star State College near Houston, Texas. Four of the victims were critically wounded and taken by helicopter to a hospital. Police near Houston say they have a suspect in custody. They are giving little information on him or the type of weapon that was used. Three people were shot and wounded on a separate Lone Star College campus in January. That shooting came just weeks after 26 students and teachers were massacred at a Connecticut elementary school, leading to the renewed debate about gun control in the United States.

Four men who attacked a British family in Thailand have been sentenced to two years in prison.The incident occurred last year when the family, consisting of a father, mother, and their two young children, were visiting the popular tourist destination of Phuket. The attackers, who were identified as local men, approached the family and began assaulting them without provocation.The father suffered serious injuries trying to protect his wife and children during the attack. The family was left traumatized by the incident, which received widespread media attention both in Thailand and the UK.After a lengthy trial, the four attackers were found guilty of assault and sentenced to two years in prison. The judge emphasized that violence against tourists will not be tolerated in Thailand and that the attackers must face the consequences of their actions.The British family expressed relief at the verdict and hope that the sentence will serve as a deterrent to others who may consider committing similar crimes in the future.Overall, the incident serves as a reminder of the importance of ensuring the safety and security of tourists in popular travel destinations around the world.

Former President Barack Obama paid tribute to the US military over Memorial Day weekend, honoring the men and women who have served and sacrificed for their country. In a statement released on Monday, Obama expressed his gratitude for the brave individuals who have given their lives in service to the United States.Obama highlighted the importance of remembering and honoring those who have made the ultimate sacrifice, stating that Memorial Day is a time to reflect on the true meaning of patriotism and service. He also urged Americans to continue supporting and caring for veterans and their families.The former president's tribute comes as the nation commemorates Memorial Day, a day dedicated to honoring the military personnel who have died in the line of duty. Obama's message serves as a reminder of the sacrifices made by members of the armed forces and their families, and the importance of honoring their legacy.

The Eastern Democratic Republic of Congo has once again been plagued by violence and bloodshed, with a series of brutal massacres in the Beni region leaving many dead and sparking fear and outrage among the local population.The question on everyone's mind is: who is behind these horrific killings?The area has long been a hotbed of armed conflict and instability, with various armed groups vying for control and wreaking havoc on the civilian population. The Allied Democratic Forces (ADF), a notorious rebel group with roots in neighboring Uganda, is believed to be responsible for the recent spate of massacres.The ADF, which has been active in the region for over two decades, is known for its brutal tactics and has been accused of carrying out numerous atrocities against civilians. The group is believed to be driven by a mix of religious extremism and criminal interests, with a history of targeting civilians, particularly in the Beni area.The Congolese government and international peacekeeping forces have vowed to take action against those responsible for the massacres and bring them to justice. However, the complex and volatile situation in the region makes it difficult to root out the perpetrators and put an end to the violence.As the people of Beni mourn the loss of their loved ones and demand answers, the search for the perpetrators behind the massacres continues. The world watches with concern as the cycle of violence and bloodshed in the DRC shows no signs of abating.

South Korean Prime Minister Kim Hwang-sik has urged North Korea to work for peace on the Korean peninsula and stop what he called "reckless military provocation".Speaking Saturday during a ceremony in Seoul marking the 61st anniversary of the start of the Korean War, Kim invited the North to join the path "where 70 million Korean people can live together."Meanwhile, about 10 activists, mostly North Korean defectors, gathered near the border city of Paju to release balloons filled with anti-Pyongyang propaganda leaflets across the demilitarized zone into the North. They said the balloons carried 100,000 leaflets, one-dollar bills, books, radios and DVDs.The Korean War started on June 25, 1950, when communist North Korean troops launched a surprise attack across the 38th parallel into South Korea.The United Nations, particularly the United States, came to the aid of South Korea in repelling the invasion. A rapid U.N. counter-offensive drove the North Koreans past the 38th Parallel, almost to the Yalu River, and the People's Republic of China entered the war on the side of the North.The Chinese launched a counter-offensive that pushed the U.N. forces back across the 38th Parallel. The Soviet Union materially aided the North Korean and Chinese armies.In 1953, the war ceased with an armistice that restored the border between the Koreas near the 38th Parallel and created the Korean Demilitarized Zone, a 4-kilometer wide buffer zone between the two Koreas.

President Trump has once again expressed his desire to find ways to bypass Congress in order to push through his agenda. With tensions running high between the executive and legislative branches, Trump is seeking more workarounds to achieve his goals.Trump has made it clear that he is frustrated with the slow pace of progress in Congress and is determined to find alternative routes to get things done. This comes after multiple failed attempts to pass legislation through Congress, particularly in areas such as immigration and healthcare.One strategy that Trump is considering is to use executive orders to implement policies without the need for congressional approval. This tactic has been met with criticism from Democrats and even some Republicans who argue that it undermines the democratic process.Despite the pushback, Trump appears undeterred and continues to explore various options to circumvent Congress. It remains to be seen how successful he will be in his efforts to achieve his policy objectives without the support of lawmakers.

An airplane crash in Colombia has killed all 12 people aboard the aircraft."Unfortunately... there were no survivors," Colombia's civil aviation authority said.The Laser Aero flight made a distress call before the crash Saturday morning, officials said.The flight originated in San Jose del Guaviare and was headed for Villavicencio.Authorities said the remains of the plane, a DC-3, were found near Villavicencio.

MEXICO CITY - Mexican leftist presidential hopeful Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador has maintained the leading position in the race to win the July election, opening a 20-point lead ahead of his nearest competitor, a poll by newspaper El Financiero showed on Monday.Lopez Obrador, the former Mexico City mayor, is the first choice of 46 percent of voters surveyed while his nearest rival, Ricardo Anaya, who heads a right-left coalition, has 26 percent support.The result marks slight growth for both Lopez Obrador and Anaya compared to a March survey by El Financiero, while it showed a 4-point decline for Jose Antonio Meade, the candidate of the ruling Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI), who comes in with just 20 percent support.The survey was conducted between April 26 and May 2 and based on 1,000 in-person interviews with voters, with a margin of error of 3.1 percent.The poll's results stripped out the 38 percent of respondents who said they were undecided in the five-candidate field.In his third consecutive bid to win the presidency, Lopez Obrador, 64, has capitalized on widespread disenchantment with the PRI over political corruption, rising levels of violence and sluggish economic growth.The survey showed independent candidate Margarita Zavala, wife of former President Felipe Calderon, at 5 percent.Meanwhile, support for the former governor of the state of Nuevo Leon, Jaime Rodriguez Calderon, another independent hopeful, stood at 3 percent.

CAPITOL HILL - One day after the U.S. Senate blocked four gun control measures, a proposal emerged Tuesday that could ease the partisan furor over the chamber’s failure to act in the aftermath of the attack on an Orlando, Florida, nightclub, America's deadliest mass shooting.Forged by a bipartisan group that included some of the Senate’s most centrist members, the measure would deny gun sales to terror suspects who are barred from boarding commercial flights and subject to enhanced security screening at U.S. airports. It also would allow those flagged for terrorist ties to contest the government’s findings and recover legal fees if a judge determines they had been placed on the terror watch list by mistake.“Our goal is simple and straightforward. We want to make America safer,” said Senator Susan Collins, a Maine Republican who was the proposal’s lead author. “Surely the terrorist attacks in San Bernardino [California] and Orlando that took so many lives are a call for compromise, a plea for bipartisan action.”“I owe it to the people of Orlando to get something done,” said Senator Bill Nelson, a Florida Democrat who co-sponsored the measure.Senator Bill Nelson, a Florida Democrat, speaks during a news conference on Capitol Hill in Washington on a compromise proposal on gun control measures, June 21, 2016.The proposal’s nine sponsors — four Republican, four Democratic and one independent — appeared together at a news conference as partisan fallout continued after Monday’s votes in which Republicans blocked Democratic gun reform measures and Democrats blocked Republican ones.Minority Leader Harry Reid, a Nevada Democrat, accused Republicans of being pawns of America’s most powerful gun rights lobbying group.“

Seoul, South Korea - In a massive display of protest, tens of thousands of South Koreans took to the streets of Seoul today to voice their opposition to the importation of US beef. Demonstrators carried signs and chanted slogans denouncing the controversial trade deal, which has sparked concerns about food safety and health risks.The protest comes amid growing fears over the safety of American beef, following reports of outbreaks of mad cow disease in the US. South Korean citizens are demanding that their government take action to protect public health and safety by banning the importation of US beef.The demonstration was peaceful, with participants from all walks of life coming together to make their voices heard. Many expressed their frustration and anger at what they see as a disregard for the well-being of the Korean people in favor of economic interests.The South Korean government has yet to respond to the demands of the protesters, but the pressure is mounting for officials to take action.

The arrest of a village chief in China has sparked protests among the local community. The village chief, known for his efforts to advocate for the rights of the villagers and improve their living conditions, was taken into custody by authorities last week.The reason for the chief's arrest remains unclear, leading to speculation and anger among the villagers. Many believe that the arrest is politically motivated and aimed at silencing dissent.In response to the arrest, hundreds of villagers took to the streets to protest, demanding the release of their chief and calling for an explanation from the authorities. The protests have been met with a heavy police presence, with reports of clashes between protesters and law enforcement.The situation remains tense as villagers continue to demand justice and transparency from the authorities. The arrest of the village chief has highlighted growing tensions between the government and grassroots activists in China.

WHITE HOUSE - President Donald Trump is accusing China of meddling in upcoming U.S. elections."Regrettably, we found that China has been attempting to interfere in our upcoming 2018 election coming up in November against my administration," Trump said during an address Wednesday morning as he chaired his first U.N. Security Council meeting."They do not want me, or us, to win because I am the first president ever to challenge China on trade. And we are winning on trade. We are winning at every level."Shortly after Trump's remarks, China's foreign minister rejected his accusations of election meddling.Foreign Minister Wang Yi told reporters that Beijing follows the principle of non-interference in other countries' affairs and refuses to accept "unwarranted accusations" against China.Later Wednesday, Trump said in a tweet: "China is actually placing propaganda ads in the Des Moines Register and other papers, made to look like news."China's state media has been placing paid supplements in major American newspapers for at least several years.
