Writing in the Financial Times, Pimco's Mohamed El-Erian warns the deal does little to reduce the country's actual deficits and postpones decisions on key issues.The major credit rating agencies are also being cautious.Moody's Investor Services said late Tuesday it will maintain the United States government's top AAA rating, but warns the outlook for the country is negative. Fitch Ratings said the risk of the U.S. defaulting on its debts remains extremely low but added it wants to see a credible plan to further reduce the U.S. budget deficit.The debt deal signed Tuesday by President Barack Obama allows the government to continue borrowing money through 2012 in exchange for spending cuts of almost $1 trillion over the next 10 years. The package would also create a bipartisan budget committee that will seek up to another $1.5 trillion in deficit reduction.

Former President Barack Obama faced discontent and criticism during the recent Americas Summit, as leaders from various countries expressed frustration with his administration's policies and actions.Many attendees were particularly vocal about Obama's foreign policy decisions, including his approach to handling conflicts in the Middle East and Latin America. Some leaders accused the former president of prioritizing American interests over the needs of other nations, leading to strained relationships and diplomatic tensions.In addition to foreign policy concerns, Obama was also criticized for his handling of economic issues and trade agreements. Many leaders pointed to what they perceived as unfair trade practices and economic policies that disproportionately benefitted the United States at the expense of other countries in the region.Despite the discontent expressed at the summit, Obama remained composed and defended his administration's decisions, arguing that they were made in the best interest of the American people. He emphasized the importance of collaboration and dialogue between nations to address global challenges and build a more equitable and prosperous future for all.Overall, the Americas Summit highlighted the complexities of international relations and the challenges faced by leaders in navigating diverse interests and priorities. As discussions continue, it is clear that finding common ground and fostering cooperation will be essential for addressing the pressing issues facing the region and the world at large.

Russian President Vladimir Putin says a "NATO legion" is fighting against rebel forces in eastern Ukraine, and that its goal is to contain Russia. “We often say: Ukrainian army, Ukrainian army. But who is really fighting [in eastern Ukraine]? There are official divisions of the armed forces but to a great extent there are so-called voluntary nationalist battalions. This is not even an army, it's a foreign legion. In this case it's a foreign NATO legion,” Putin said, speaking before university students in the city of St. Petersburg.Separatists Area of Control in Ukraine“[They are there] with the aim of geopolitically containing Russia, which is absolutely not in the national interests of the Ukrainian people,” he said. Putin also accused Kyiv of refusing to settle the conflict in eastern Ukraine peacefully. NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg dismissed Putin's claim as "nonsense." "The statement that there is a NATO legion in Ukraine is nonsense. There is no NATO legion," Stoltenberg said Monday, speaking to reporters following an extraordinary meeting of the NATO-Ukraine Commission. "The foreign forces in Ukraine are Russian," he added.‘Stop destabilizing Ukraine’ Stoltenberg also called on Russia to stop destabilizing Ukraine and join efforts to seek a peaceful solution to the crisis in the country’s east. “We call on Russia to stop its support for the separatists immediately, to stop destabilizing Ukraine and to respect its international commitments,” said he.Ukrainian servicemen guard their position in the village of Luhanska, Luhansk region, Jan. 24, 2015.

Amidst the upcoming elections, a prominent opposition figure has made a bold claim of establishing an alternative leadership in the country. The figure, who remains unnamed, has stated that this alternative leadership will serve as a viable option for voters who are dissatisfied with the current government.This announcement comes at a crucial time as the country prepares for a highly anticipated election that will determine the future direction of the nation. The opposition figure has cited concerns about corruption, lack of transparency, and economic stagnation as reasons for the need for a new leadership.While the details of this alternative leadership remain unclear, the opposition figure has promised to reveal more information in the coming days. This move has sparked both support and criticism from various sectors of society, with some viewing it as a much-needed change and others questioning the legitimacy and feasibility of such a proposition.As the election date draws near, the political landscape in the country continues to evolve, with new players entering the fray and challenging the status quo. It remains to be seen how this claim of alternative leadership will impact the electoral process and whether it will resonate with voters looking for change.

While the world tries to go green, South Africa still invests in coal. Last Friday, President Jacob Zuma visited what is soon to become the fourth largest coal power-station in the world. The welcome was warm. Gathered against the gates, hundreds of workers greeted South Africa's President Jacob Zuma as he made his way through the construction site. In Lephalale, a wind-blown desert area 350 kilometers north of Johannesburg, the huge power station is being built. And on Friday, the South African president came to unveil the first unit which has been completed. In his speech, he reminded the crowd about the purpose of the gigantic project. "These new power stations will provide the electricity capacity needed to grow the economy, attract investment, and create jobs," said Zuma. The Medupi power station is part of a $41 billion project to build several power plants across the country. The project is run by Eskom, South Africa's electricity company. Since construction started in 2005, 17,000 people worked daily on the Medupi site to build what is seen as part of the solution to South Africa's power woes.Keeping up with demand As its economy keeps growing fast, the country has been struggling to keep up as power demand outstripped supply. It came close to catastrophe in 2008 when a series of power shortages hit the country. According to some estimates, the demand in terms of power should double by 2030. South Africa tackles the problem by using mainly coal to produce electricity. It has decades of coal reserves in the ground, according to Eskom chief executive Brian Dames. "Coal is very important for us to use.

In a shocking turn of events, Eritrea has arrested two staff members of the US Embassy in the capital city of Asmara. The reason for their arrest remains unclear, with Eritrean authorities providing little information on the matter.The US State Department has confirmed the arrests and is working to ensure the safety and well-being of its employees. The department has also called on Eritrea to provide more details on the charges against the detained staff members.Tensions between the United States and Eritrea have been strained in recent years, with the two countries having a tumultuous relationship. The arrests of the US Embassy staff will likely further strain relations between the two nations.The families of the detained employees are anxiously awaiting more information on the situation and are hoping for a swift resolution to the matter. The US government is working diligently to secure the release of its staff members and is calling on Eritrea to respect their rights and ensure their safety during this challenging time.

Israel has officially started the process of forming a new government after weeks of political negotiations. The current Prime Minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, is facing a tough challenge as opposition parties have been working together to form a coalition government that would see him removed from power.The negotiations have been ongoing for weeks, with opposition leader Yair Lapid working hard to secure the support of various political parties in order to form a majority coalition. If successful, this would mark the end of Netanyahu's 12-year tenure as Prime Minister.The new government would likely be a diverse coalition, with parties from across the political spectrum coming together to oust Netanyahu. This would be a significant shift in Israeli politics and could have far-reaching implications for the country's future.As the negotiations continue, the political landscape in Israel remains uncertain. Netanyahu is fighting to hold on to power, while opposition parties are determined to bring about change. It remains to be seen how this political drama will unfold in the coming days and weeks.

In a bold move, officials in Prague have decided to remove a statue of a controversial Russian general from a public square in the city. The statue, which depicted General Ivan Konev, had been a source of tension between Czech residents and Russia due to Konev's role in the Soviet Union's suppression of the Hungarian Revolution in 1956 and his involvement in the construction of the Berlin Wall.The decision to remove the statue comes after years of debate and discussion within the city council. Many residents and local politicians felt that the presence of the statue was a painful reminder of Czechoslovakia's past under Soviet rule and did not reflect the values of a democratic and independent Czech Republic.While some have criticized the decision as erasing history, others see it as a step towards acknowledging and coming to terms with the country's complicated past. The statue will be relocated to a museum where it can be viewed in a more historical context.The removal of the statue has sparked mixed reactions, with some praising the decision as a symbol of progress and reconciliation, while others see it as an attempt to rewrite history.

The devastating aftermath of Hurricane Michael continues to unfold as the death toll from the powerful storm has now climbed to 17. The storm, which made landfall in Florida as a Category 4 hurricane last week, has left a trail of destruction in its path, causing widespread flooding, power outages, and destruction of homes and infrastructure.Rescue and recovery efforts are still ongoing as authorities work tirelessly to search for missing individuals and provide assistance to those affected by the hurricane. The hardest-hit areas, including the Florida Panhandle and parts of Georgia, have been left reeling from the impact of the storm.As communities come together to support one another during this difficult time, it is clear that the road to recovery will be long and challenging. Our thoughts are with all those affected by Hurricane Michael, and we hope for a swift and effective response to help rebuild and recover from this tragic event.

The defending Women's World Cup champion has successfully advanced to the next round of the tournament after a hard-fought victory in their latest match. The team showed great determination and skill on the field, earning a well-deserved spot in the quarter-finals.Fans of the defending champions are thrilled with their team's performance and are looking forward to seeing them continue their winning streak in the upcoming matches. The players have been praised for their teamwork and resilience, demonstrating why they are considered one of the top teams in the world.The Women's World Cup has been full of excitement and surprises, but the defending champion's advancement is a clear indication of their strength and determination to retain their title. As they prepare for their next match, the team will be looking to showcase their talent and prove once again why they are the reigning champions.

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA - In 2018, technology firms such as Facebook and Google faced more scrutiny and negative press over their handling of data breaches and online speech. The issue may mean new rules and more regulations in the future.The question of who can access personal user data through technology caused many people to rethink how much they trust these companies with their private information. At a recent hearing, House Republican Majority Leader Kevin McCarthy grilled Google over accusations it uses algorithms to suppress conservative voices."Are America's technology companies serving as instruments of freedom? Or instruments of control? Are they fulfilling the promise of the digital age? Are they advancing the cause of self-government? Or are they serving as instruments of manipulation used by powerful interests and foreign governments to rob the people of their power, agency, and dignity?," he said.At the hearing, Google CEO Sundar Pichai said he runs the U.S. technology giant without political preference.Google CEO Sundar Pichai testifies at a House Judiciary Committee hearing "examining Google and its Data Collection, Use and Filtering Practices" on Capitol Hill in Washington, Dec. 11, 2018.In October, Google acknowledged that several months earlier, it had discovered a data breach involving its Google Plus service, which the company said would be shut down.Pantas Sutardja, chief executive of data storage company LatticeWork Inc., says such scandals are forcing the companies to take a closer look at how they manage and protect user content."2018 has been a challenging year for tech companies and consumers alike. Company CEOs being called to Congress for hearings and promising profusely to fix the problems of data breach but still cannot do it," said Sutardja.Also this year, Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg faced tough questions from U.S. lawmakers over a breach that allowed a political consulting firm, Cambridge Analytica, to exploit the data of millions of Facebook users.

A recent report has shown a significant increase in the number of searches of phones and laptops by US authorities at airports. These searches, often conducted without a warrant, have sparked controversy and raised concerns about privacy rights.The lawsuit, filed by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), alleges that the searches are on the rise and often target travelers based on their race, religion, or national origin. The ACLU claims that these searches violate the Fourth Amendment, which protects against unreasonable searches and seizures.According to the report, the number of electronic device searches at US airports has doubled in the past few years. Many travelers have reported having their devices seized and searched without any explanation or justification.Critics argue that these searches are invasive and unnecessary, especially when there is no suspicion of criminal activity. They are calling for more transparency and oversight in the way these searches are conducted.In response to the lawsuit, the US Customs and Border Protection agency has stated that they have the authority to search electronic devices at the border without a warrant. However, they have also emphasized that they must have reasonable suspicion to conduct such searches.As the debate over the legality and necessity of these searches continues, travelers are advised to be aware of their rights and take precautions when crossing US borders.

The United Nations Security Council is considering a recommendation by Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon to create two forces in Mali -- one, a peacekeeping operation, and a separate military force to confront Islamist militants. Former U.S. ambassador to Mali Vicki Huddleston says neighboring Algeria must play a key role in any long-term stabilization plan. In an interview on VOA's Press Conference USA, Huddleston said Algeria is the only country in the region with the military capacity and the air and land mobility to take the lead in ridding Mali of Islamist militants. She noted these particular militants have their roots in Algeria, and contributed to unrest there before becoming active in Mali.Veteran jihadist Mokhtar Belmokhtar speaks in this undated still image taken from a video released by Sahara Media, Algeria, Jan. 21, 2013."Sooner or later Algeria will have to do what it takes. Algeria is responsible in that al-Qaida in the Islamic Maghreb, [Mokhtar] Belmokhtar, [Abdelhamid] Abou Zeid, [Abdelmalek] Droukdel, who is even now the leader, is in Algeria. This is an Algerian, Salafist, militant movement. So, they went in to Mali and restarted the Algerian civil war in Mali," said Huddleston. Huddleston said Algeria has shown some "hesitancy" over its involvement in Mali's conflict. But she added, she does not believe Algeria can ignore the unrest at its southern border. "United Nations [troops] generally don’t fight. They are peacekeepers, not peacemakers. So if that is going to happen, then Algeria should be the lead because Algeria has the capacity to fight in that area, to bring Chad, and Niger, and Libya, and Mauritania together," said Huddleston.Analysts say Algeria has been working in the background since the start of the French-led intervention in Mali in January.

Shark numbers along the Queensland coast have declined by more than 90 percent for some species in the past five decades, according to new research that calls for better protection for threatened species in Australian waters.Australia has 170 types of sharks out of about 440 species globally. Great white and grey nurse sharks are listed as threatened in Australian waters.Researchers from the University of Queensland and Griffith University have analyzed data from shark control programs to gauge changes in populations over the past five decades. Nets and baited hooks have been used in Queensland to reduce the risk of attacks on swimmers and surfers.The research teams looked at the number of white, tiger, hammerhead and whaler sharks caught from the early 1960s to 2016.This handout photo from the Queensland Police Service taken on March 30, 2017, shows a bull shark washed up on a road near the town of Ayr.What they found was a shock, according to George Roff from the University of Queensland, who was one of the authors of the study."The results are quite striking,” he said. “What we found over the last 50 years is a dramatic decline in the numbers of apex sharks on the Queensland coastline. So the number of wild sharks on our beaches have declined by between 74 and 92 percent.“(

The confirmation hearings for Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh have escalated into chaos as senators on both sides of the aisle engaged in a heated verbal and physical altercation. The contentious proceedings took a violent turn when Senator John Smith from the Democratic Party and Senator Jane Johnson from the Republican Party got into a physical brawl on the Senate floor.The incident began when Senator Johnson accused Senator Smith of intentionally misrepresenting Kavanaugh's record and attempting to smear his character. Senator Smith retaliated by pushing Senator Johnson, leading to a full-blown physical altercation between the two lawmakers. Other senators quickly intervened to break up the fight, but tensions remained high as both sides continued to argue and hurl insults at each other.The intense confrontation reflects the deep divisions and strong emotions surrounding Kavanaugh's nomination. Critics of Kavanaugh have raised concerns about his conservative judicial record and his past rulings on issues such as abortion rights and executive power. Supporters, on the other hand, praise Kavanaugh's qualifications and believe he will uphold the Constitution as a fair and impartial judge.As the Senate prepares for a final vote on Kavanaugh's confirmation, the brawl has underscored the bitter partisan divide that has characterized the nomination process. It remains to be seen how this incident will impact the outcome of the confirmation vote and the future of the Supreme Court.

ANCHORAGE, ALASKA - A team of federal accident investigators is expected to arrive in Alaska Tuesday to try to piece together what caused a deadly midair collision between two sightseeing planes.Four people were killed after the floatplanes carrying cruise ship tourists collided Monday near the southeast Alaska town of Ketchikan, the Coast Guard said. Two others were missing, said Petty Officer Jon-Paul Rios, a Coast Guard spokesman.The Washington, D.C.-based investigative team from the National Transportation Safety Board is expected to arrive in Ketchikan Tuesday afternoon, agency spokesman Peter Knudson said. He said board member Jennifer Homendy also is traveling with the so-called "Go Team,'' which investigates major accidents.The floatplanes collided under unknown circumstances, Federal Aviation Administration spokesman Allen Kenitzer said in an email to The Associated Press. Floatplanes have pontoons mounted under the fuselage so they can land on water.The passengers were from the cruise ship Royal Princess and were on sightseeing flights, one of which was operated by flightseeing company Taquan Air.Eleven people were inside Taquan's single-engine de Havilland Otter DHC-3 when it went down as it returned from Misty Fjords National Monument, which is part of the Tongass National Forest, the nation's largest. Ten people were taken to a Ketchikan hospital.All patients were in fair or good condition, according to Marty West, a spokeswoman for PeaceHealth Ketchikan Medical Center.Three people who died were among five people aboard the second plane, a single-engine de Havilland DHC-2 Beaver, according to Coast Guard Lt. Brian Dykens.

In preparation for the upcoming elections in Nigeria, state governors have requested for increased troop presence to ensure security during the electoral process. With tensions running high in the country due to previous incidents of election-related violence, the governors are taking proactive measures to maintain peace and order.The Nigerian Governors' Forum has called for additional troops to be deployed to states across the country to prevent any potential unrest or disruptions. The security forces will work in conjunction with local police and election officials to safeguard polling stations and protect voters.In a statement released by the Forum, the governors emphasized the importance of a peaceful and transparent electoral process. They reiterated their commitment to ensuring free and fair elections and urged citizens to exercise their democratic rights responsibly.The request for increased troop presence comes as Nigeria prepares for its next round of general elections, scheduled to take place in the coming months. The governors are hopeful that the security measures will help prevent any incidents of violence and ensure a smooth and successful electoral process.As the country gears up for the elections, all eyes will be on the security forces to see how they handle the increased troop presence and maintain peace and order throughout the electoral period.

CROZET, VIRGINIA - Every day, at Our Lady of the Angels Monastery in the foothills of Virginia's Blue Ridge Mountains, the 13 resident nuns gather for prayer. Theirs is a simple Christian life of worship and meditation. The sisters also follow the Benedictine tradition of combining prayer with work. For the past 20 years, they've made their own version of Gouda, the flavorful cheese which originated in the Netherlands. Sister Barbara Smickel, who co-founded the monastery 25 years ago, says that while it is not the most important part of their life, work is still very important. "I just think working for a living is a good thing…our life is pretty intense; there’s a lot of silence, a lot of prayer, a lot of meditation in it and I think the balance of using our bodies in a healthy way is very important for that,” she says. "We use our bodies and the gifts God has given us of mind and heart and body to support ourselves and we find a special satisfaction in making a wholesome product to do that.”Making Gouda They support themselves by making the Dutch-style Gouda, which they produce in a cheese barn just down the hill from the monastery. It's an enterprise they inherited from the farm’s previous owner.Sister Maria Gonzalo-Garcia checks the curds during the cheese-making process. (VOA/J. Taboh)The nuns start the cheese-making process with a truckful of warmed, pasteurized milk - more than 6,000 pounds [2,700 liters] - that’s piped into a giant stainless steel vat. Cheese cook Sister Maria Gonzalo-Garcia adds a starter culture, which helps speed the process of turning the milk into cheese.

Two Italian marines who were charged with murder in India have returned to the country to face trial after nearly eight years. Massimiliano Latorre and Salvatore Girone were accused of killing two Indian fishermen off the coast of Kerala in 2012 while providing security on an Italian oil tanker.The case has strained diplomatic relations between Italy and India, with the Italian government arguing that the marines should be tried in Italy. However, the Indian Supreme Court ruled that the trial should take place in India.After a lengthy legal battle, the marines agreed to return to India in order to face trial. They arrived in New Delhi on Friday and were taken into custody by Indian authorities. The trial is expected to begin soon.The families of the victims have been calling for justice and have expressed relief that the marines are finally back in India to face trial. The case has garnered widespread attention in both countries, with many hoping for a resolution that will bring closure to all parties involved.

Pope John Paul II is to preparing to celebrate his annual Easter vigil Mass Saturday, at Saint Peter's Basilica, one day after presiding over a solemn service at Rome's ancient Coliseum on Good Friday.Amid exceptionally tight security, the pope Friday led thousands of Roman Catholics in prayer at the ancient stadium where early Christians were first persecuted nearly 2,000 years ago.John Paul presided over The Stations of the Cross procession also known as "Via Crucis", which symbolically traces Jesus' path to his crucifixion. He said the suffering of Jesus, after 20 centuries, continues to move Catholics.On Sunday, Pope John Paul will deliver his traditional blessing and message "Urbi et Orbi," which in Latin means "to the city and the world."The 83-year-old pontiff spoke in a relatively clear voice Friday despite concerns about his health. He suffers from the neurological illness, Parkinson's Disease, as well as arthritis and other ailments.Roman Catholics and other Christians who use the Julian calendar, and Orthodox congregations who use the Gregorian calendar are all celebrating Easter this year on April 11.Christians believe Jesus is the son of God, and that he rose from the dead on Easter Sunday after his death by crucifixion to save the world from sin.

The Nigerian military has launched a major offensive to retake a military barracks that was captured by the terrorist group Boko Haram. The barracks, located in the northeastern region of the country, fell into the hands of the insurgents after a fierce battle that left several soldiers dead or injured.The Nigerian military has deployed ground troops, air support, and heavy artillery in an effort to reclaim the strategic location. The operation is ongoing, with reports of intense fighting and casualties on both sides.Boko Haram, which has been waging a violent insurgency in Nigeria for over a decade, has carried out numerous attacks on military and civilian targets in the region. The group is known for its brutal tactics, including suicide bombings, kidnappings, and mass killings.The retaking of the barracks is seen as a crucial victory for the Nigerian military in its ongoing battle against Boko Haram. The outcome of the operation will have significant implications for the security situation in the region and the fight against terrorism in Nigeria.

NAIROBI - Police and security forces are responsible for nearly 300 gun-related killings and dozens more injuries in the past two years, including cases of summary execution and stray bullets striking bystanders, a Kenyan rights group says.Jeremy Mutiso, 28, was chatting with friends near his home in Mathare one Sunday evening in June 2011 when a demonstration started. Police arrived to break it up."From where I was watching,” Mutiso said, “I heard four bullets, gunshots, and the fourth one was so loud. … when I was trying to run to our house, my hand couldn't move.”One hand was in his back pocket, he said, and he was unable to pull it out. People running behind him told him that he had been shot and was bleeding.Mutiso said it took hours to get to the hospital, where he stayed for two weeks.While in the hospital, Mutiso had asked if his father had followed up the case. He had not."He can't do that,” Mutiso recalled learning. “He was afraid he will not be able to win the case. … he is afraid the cops might fight back in terms of harassing us."

The United Nations is seeking $21 million in funding to address food insecurity in Zimbabwe, particularly in light of the ongoing lockdown measures in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.The lockdown has had a severe impact on the economy of Zimbabwe, with many people losing their livelihoods and struggling to access food. The UN estimates that 60% of the population in Zimbabwe is now food insecure.The funds sought by the UN would go towards providing food assistance to those most in need, as well as supporting small-scale farmers to ensure food production can continue despite the challenges posed by the lockdown.UN officials have emphasized the urgent need for action to prevent a worsening of the food security situation in Zimbabwe. They have called on the international community to step up and provide the necessary funding to support the country's most vulnerable populations during this difficult time.

President Barack Obama won praise from Cuba's leader Saturday for moving to restore diplomatic relations between Washington and Havana, but President Raul Castro told his nation that the change did not mean the end of communist rule in Cuba.In a speech to Cuba's National Assembly that was televised throughout the island, Castro said he was open to discussing a wide range of issues with U.S. officials who are expected to visit Havana next month. He also confirmed that he would attend the Summit of Americas in Panama in April, where he is expected to meet with Obama.Asked in Washington about his move to restore diplomatic ties with Cuba, Obama said Friday that he did not expect dramatic changes in Cuba immediately, but that the U.S. would now have a much greater opportunity for dialogue with the Cuban government and people.Castro's speech Saturday was his first public comment since the diplomatic breakthrough was announced simultaneously by the two countries' presidents.Castro said Cuba would accelerate its economic reform program, with the goal of creating a form of “prosperous and sustainable communism.” But he emphasized that changes would be gradual. Reports from Havana said Castro ended his speech with the words "Viva Fidel," hailing his brother, who led the revolutionary force that seized power in Cuba in 1959. Fidel Castro, 88, who relinquished the presidency to his brother, 83, in April 2011, has not been seen or spoken in public this week.

Mexican leftist candidate Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador has surged ahead in the presidential race, opening up a significant 20-point lead in the latest opinion poll.According to the poll conducted by a leading research firm, Obrador currently holds the support of 45% of likely voters, compared to his closest rival who has just 25% of the vote.Obrador, who is running for the third time for the presidency, has been a vocal critic of the current government's policies and has promised to bring about significant changes if elected.His populist message and promises to tackle corruption and inequality have resonated with many voters, leading to his growing popularity in the polls.With the election just a few weeks away, Obrador's lead in the polls has solidified his status as the frontrunner in the race.Political analysts predict that if Obrador maintains his lead, he could become Mexico's next president and bring about a new era of change in the country.

Turkey's President Abdullah Gul has called a fatal bomb attack on the town of Gaziantep a "crime against humanity" and lamented the deaths of the nine victims, including four children. Gul said in a statement Tuesday that he shares the pain of the families who lost loved ones. He said he condemns those responsible for the car bomb that exploded near a police station in southeastern Turkey, near the country's border with Syria. In addition to the nine deaths, 69 other people were wounded. Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdogan is expected to travel to the town on Wednesday. There was no immediate claim of responsibility for the bombing. Rebels from the outlawed Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) are active in Turkey's Kurdish-majority southeast.

The White House has declared its opposition to a bill that would give Washington, D.C. a vote in Congress.A White House spokesman Friday said the Constitution is clear that only residents of states, not districts, can elect people to Congress.The bill now being considered by the House of Representatives would add two votes to the 435-member chamber - one for the nation's capital, which traditionally votes Democratic. The other seat would go to the majority-Republican western state of Utah.Democratic leaders say the proposal is expected to pass when it goes to the House floor, perhaps as early as next week. But analysts say the bill is likely to die in the Senate.The District of Columbia has a non-voting delegate in the House of Representatives.

Three Ethiopian journalists debated the development journalism that has been practiced by government media for the last several decades. Some are afraid of it, others say it is not good journalism.Three experienced journalists expressed their views of the practice in a roundtable discussion.The term is popularly used in developing countries where the state controls the media and discourages objective reporting of voices critical of government policies and practices. The assistant dean of Addis Ababa University School of Journalism, Tadesse Zinaye, said,"There is indeed this fear of it limiting press freedom not only in Ethiopia but also in other countries promoting the practice." He believes that developmental journalism is very good and can only be dangerous "if used to limit the press freedom.""I know historically it started in the 60's and how it started, I still have difficulty understanding it," says Tamerat G. Giorgis, Managing Editor of Addis Fortune."Journalism is just like any other profession,” said Tamerat Giorgis, editor-in-chief of Addis Fortune. Tamerat argued that there are no development dentists or development lawyers. “I don't believe on the idea of developmental journalism.”"I think the first ten years of the State media in the current government was in state of loss of direction," Tamerat claims that it's while they were trying to find direction this idea of developmental journalism came up. He also admits that so far in Ethiopia no one has imposed it on the private media. It's limited only to the state media.Abiy Teklemariam, former Editor-in-Chief of Addis Neger, said the media a nation promotes reflects its political philosophy. “In Ethiopia, it’s rather an extension of the politics."He asks, "What is development anyway? Who defines it?

U.S. sanctions are biting harder into the Iranian economy, with new signs that Iran’s key oil exports are faltering, while its trade figures with Europe’s biggest economy and with the United States are shrinking.U.S. news service Bloomberg said it has compiled tanker tracking data showing that no oil tankers had been seen leaving Iran’s oil terminals for foreign ports in the first nine days of May. Crude oil is Iran’s main revenue source.Washington tightened its unilateral sanctions on Iranian oil exports May 2, ending waivers that it granted to several countries to keep importing crude from Iran and requiring them to reduce imports to zero. The U.S. sanctions are aimed at pressuring Iran to change its perceived malign behaviors.Bloomberg said the tracking data also revealed that four Iranian tankers were anchored off the coast of China as of May 9, with a fifth tanker on its way to the Chinese coast. It said most of the rest of Iran’s tanker fleet either was returning to the Persian Gulf after discharging cargoes or had been observed in or near the region in the previous two days. It said 10 Iranian tankers had not sent tracking signals for at least 16 days, keeping their transponders turned off in an apparent effort to hide their movements.In a report published Friday, Reuters said the four Iranian tankers off the Chinese coast and the fifth on its way to China had loaded Iranian crude in April, before China’s U.S. waiver to import the oil expired.

In a significant shift, climate talks have moved away from focusing on binding targets to a new tactic of "name and shame" to hold countries accountable for their actions in fighting climate change.This shift was evident at the recent United Nations Climate Change Conference, where several countries expressed frustration with the lack of progress in meeting the goals set in previous agreements. Instead of relying on strict targets and penalties, many countries are now advocating for a more transparent approach that highlights and publicizes the efforts – or lack thereof – of each nation in addressing climate change.By shining a spotlight on the actions (or inactions) of countries, advocates hope to pressure governments into taking more ambitious steps to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and transition to cleaner energy sources. This strategy is seen as a more effective way to encourage global cooperation and ensure that all countries are doing their part to combat the climate crisis.While some may argue that this new approach lacks teeth without binding targets, proponents believe that public pressure and scrutiny can be just as powerful in driving meaningful change. As the world grapples with the increasingly urgent need to address climate change, the shift towards "name and shame" tactics may prove to be a crucial tool in holding countries accountable and pushing for more ambitious climate action.
